topics.title: Introduction to Security - Security Goals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Security - Security Goals # MODULE 1 — INTRODUCTION TO SECURITY: SECURITY GOALS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is "Security" in the Computing Context? In the formal vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCITT601)** and in standard ISO/IEC 27000-aligned literature, **Computer / Network Security** is the collection of tools, protocols, policies, and engineered mechanisms designed to protect data, computational resources, and communication channels against unauthorized access, modification, disruption, or destruction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > *Security is the protection afforded to an automated information system in order to preserve the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the system, its data, and its services against adversarial threats.* Security is **not** a single product (e.g., an antivirus). It is a **holistic, multi-layered discipline** that spans: - **Hardware** (TPM chips, HSMs, secure enclaves) - **Software** (OS kernels, firewalls, cryptographic libraries) - **Communication** (TLS, IPsec, SSH) - **Human/Policy layers** (access control policies, audits, training) ### 1.2 The Triad of Security — The CIA Triad The cornerstone of every security architecture is the **CIA Triad**, a model first formalized in the orange-book era of TCSEC and now embedded in ISO/IEC 27001. It defines the **three primary security goals** that any system must satisfy: 1. **Confidentiality** — *Keeping secrets secret.* 2. **Integrity** — *Keeping data unaltered.* 3. **Availability** — *Keeping services reachable.* These three pillars are non-negotiable, and the failure of any one collapses the trust model of a system. ### 1.3 Extended Security Goals (Beyond CIA) Modern cryptographic engineering recognizes that the classical CIA triad, while foundational, is **insufficient on its own**. The following goals are now considered equally essential in the KTU 2024 syllabus: | Extended Goal | One-line Essence | |---|---| | **Authentication** | Proving *who you are* before access is granted. | | **Authorization / Access Control** | Deciding *what you are allowed to do* once authenticated. | | **Non-Repudiation** | Preventing a sender from *denying* they sent a message. | | **Accountability** | Tying every action to a unique, traceable identity. | | **Privacy** | Controlling *how your personal data* is collected and used. | | **Auditing** | Logging events for later forensic reconstruction. | > [!NOTE] > **Why CIA + Extensions?** The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) deliberately lists these six goals. A 14-mark KTU question frequently asks the student to *"explain the security goals of a system"* — the examiner expects the CIA triad **plus** Authentication, Non-Repudiation, and Access Control. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Story Imagine a bank's **central vault** that holds customers' gold: - **Confidentiality** → Only the *vault manager* (with the right key) and the *owner* can see/open the locker. Nobody else can peek inside. The bank's steel walls, biometric locks, and private viewing rooms are *physical confidentiality*. - **Integrity** → If a thief swaps the gold bars with painted lead, the owner is ruined. **Tamper-evident seals**, **weight checks**, and **periodic audits** ensure the contents remain exactly as deposited. - **Availability** → The vault must open during business hours. Even if the manager is on vacation, a *backup manager* with proper credentials must exist. A vault that is permanently locked is useless — *denial of service* is just as damaging as a break-in. - **Authentication** → Before opening a locker, the bank verifies the customer's *signature, photo ID, and fingerprint* — this is **"are you really who you claim to be?"** - **Access Control** → Even after authentication, a *read-only* customer can view contents but cannot move gold to another locker. Authorization defines **"what you can do."** - **Non-Repudiation** → Every transaction is video-recorded and signed. The customer can never later claim *"I never withdrew that gold."* The video, signature, and timestamp together form a *non-reputable audit trail.* > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for Exam:** *CIA* = **C**onceal, **I**mprison tampering, **A**lways serve. *AAAN* extensions = **A**uthenticate, **A**uthorize, **A**udit, **N**on-repudiate. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Six Security Goals ## 2.1 Goal 1 — Confidentiality ### Definition Confidentiality ensures that **information is accessible only to those authorized** to view it. It is the *privacy* dimension of security. ### Mechanism Classes - **Encryption (symmetric & asymmetric):** AES-256, ChaCha20, RSA-OAEP. - **Steganography:** Hiding existence of the message itself. - **Access Controls:** File permissions, ACLs, MAC labels. - **Physical & Environmental Controls:** Locked server rooms, Faraday cages. ### Threat Model (what confidentiality defends against) - Eavesdropping / Sniffing (e.g., Wireshark on open Wi-Fi). - Shoulder surfing. - Insider data exfiltration. - Traffic-flow analysis (even *encrypted* traffic reveals patterns). > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **confidentiality breaches** are also called *disclosure attacks*. --- ## 2.2 Goal 2 — Integrity ### Definition Integrity guarantees that data has **not been altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner** since the time it was created, transmitted, or stored. ### Two Sub-Forms 1. **Data Integrity** — the *bit-content* is unaltered (file, message, packet). 2. **System Integrity** — the *system itself* (OS, firmware, software) is unaltered (no rootkits, no malicious patches). ### Mechanism Classes - **Hash functions:** SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE3. - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC):** HMAC-SHA256, CMAC. - **Digital Signatures:** RSA-PSS, Ed25519. - **Versioning, checksums, replication, write-once storage.** ### Threat Model - Man-in-the-middle (MITM) bit-flipping. - Replay attacks (sending an old valid message again). - Database tampering. - Malware modifying binaries. > [!IMPORTANT] > *Confidentiality without integrity is dangerous.* A system that hides a message but lets an attacker flip its bits can still cause catastrophic damage (e.g., *"Pay $1"* → *"Pay $1,000,000"*). Hence integrity is **non-negotiable**. --- ## 2.3 Goal 3 — Availability ### Definition Availability ensures that **systems, data, and services are accessible to authorized users when needed**. ### Quantitative Metric — The **Availability Ratio** (used in KTU 2024 problem sets) $$ A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ Where: - $\text{MTBF}$ = **Mean Time Between Failures** (reliability metric). - $\text{MTTR}$ = **Mean Time To Repair** (recovery metric). This ratio $A \in [0, 1]$; the famous **"five nines"** availability $A = 0.99999$ allows only $\approx 5.26$ minutes of downtime per year. ### Mechanism Classes - **Redundancy:** RAID, multi-region replication, hot-standby failover. - **DDoS mitigation:** Rate limiting, scrubbing centers, anycast. - **Backups & Disaster Recovery (DR).** - **Fault-tolerant designs** and graceful degradation. ### Threat Model - Denial-of-Service (DoS), Distributed DoS (DDoS). - Ransomware encryption of backups. - Hardware failure, natural disasters. - Resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, bandwidth). --- ## 2.4 Goal 4 — Authentication ### Definition Authentication is the process of **verifying the identity of a user, process, or device** before granting access to a resource. ### Three "Factor" Classes | Factor | Example | |---|---| | **Knowledge** ("something you know") | Password, PIN, security question | | **Possession** ("something you have") | Smart card, OTP token, mobile phone | | **Biometric** ("something you are") | Fingerprint, iris, face, voice | > Combining $\geq 2$ factors = **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. ### Mechanism Classes - Password-based (with salted hashing: bcrypt, Argon2). - Challenge-response protocols. - Certificate-based (X.509, PKI). - Biometric matching. --- ## 2.5 Goal 5 — Access Control (Authorization) ### Definition After authentication, the system must decide **what operations the authenticated entity is permitted to perform**. This is authorization / access control. ### Three Classical Models (Board-Favorite Topic) 1. **DAC — Discretionary Access Control:** Owner decides. (Unix `chmod`). 2. **MAC — Mandatory Access Control:** System policy (e.g., Bell-LaPadula, Biba). 3. **RBAC — Role-Based Access Control:** Permissions tied to job roles. (Most enterprise systems.) --- ## 2.6 Goal 6 — Non-Repudiation ### Definition Non-repudiation ensures that **a party cannot deny having performed an action** (sending a message, signing a contract, executing a transaction). ### Mechanism Classes - **Digital signatures** (asymmetric cryptography). - **Trusted timestamping** (RFC 3161). - **Audit logs** with write-once storage. - **Certificate Authorities (CAs)** binding identity to keys. ### The Two Sub-Properties - **Non-repudiation of origin (NRO):** Sender cannot deny sending. - **Non-repudiation of receipt (NRR):** Receiver cannot deny receiving. > [!NOTE] > *Non-repudiation is what gives digital signatures their legal weight* in jurisdictions following the IT Act (India), eIDAS (EU), and the U.S. ESIGN Act. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Availability Ratio | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ | | 2 | Annual Downtime | $D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes per year | | 3 | "Five Nines" Downtime | $(1 - 0.99999) \times 525{,}600 \approx 5.26$ | Minutes/year | | 4 | Confidentiality Tool | Encryption $\rightarrow E_k(m) = c$ | $m$ plaintext, $c$ ciphertext, $k$ key | | 5 | Integrity Tool | Hash $H(m) = h$, length $\ell$ bits | e.g., SHA-256 $\to 256$ bits | | 6 | Authentication Tool | Compare $H(pwd) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{stored}}$ | One-way / salted | | 7 | Non-Repudiation Tool | $\text{Sign}(m) = E_{K_{priv}}(H(m))$ | Asymmetric, public key verifies | | 8 | Access Decision | $(\text{subject}, \text{object}, \text{right}) \to \{\text{allow}, \text{deny}\}$ | Boolean triplet | | 9 | Risk (qualitative) | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Composite metric | | 10 | Attack Probability | $P(\text{breach}) = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ for $n$ independent trials | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Availability formula** is the most-frequently tested equation in KTU Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize the *form* $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ and the **five-nines benchmark**. --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Confidentiality** is the legal basis of HIPAA (health), PCI-DSS (payments), and GDPR (privacy). - **Integrity** underpins software supply-chain security (Sigstore, code signing). - **Availability** is the SLA currency of cloud providers (AWS 99.99%, Azure 99.95%). - **Authentication** is the front-door of every modern zero-trust architecture (BeyondCorp, Zscaler). - **Access Control** is the operational heart of RBAC in Kubernetes, AWS IAM, and SAP. - **Non-Repudiation** is what makes blockchain transactions and e-signed contracts legally binding. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples This section contains the **worked numerical derivations** the KTU 2024 paper-setter prefers for Module 1. Each step is written exhaustively, in line with the KTU board's "show every transition" valuation policy. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Availability Calculation > **Problem (KTU-style 3-mark Part-A):** > A server has MTBF = 3000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute the availability and the expected annual downtime in minutes. ### Step 1 — Identify the Formula The availability of a repairable system is governed by the standard reliability expression. $$ A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values We are given: - $\text{MTBF} = 3000\ \text{hours}$ - $\text{MTTR} = 2\ \text{hours}$ Substituting directly: $$ A = \frac{3000}{3000 + 2} $$ ### Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator $$ A = \frac{3000}{3002} $$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the Decimal $$ A = 0.99933\ldots $$ ### Step 5 — Express as a Percentage $$ A \approx 99.933\% $$ ### Step 6 — Compute Annual Downtime Total minutes in a year: $$ T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600\ \text{minutes} $$ Downtime fraction is $(1 - A)$, so downtime in minutes is: $$ D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times T_{\text{year}} $$ ### Step 7 — Plug Numbers $$ D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.99933) \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ D_{\text{year}} = 0.000667 \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ D_{\text{year}} \approx 350.5\ \text{minutes per year} $$ ### Final Answer > **Availability $A \approx 99.933\%$, corresponding to $\approx 350.5$ minutes of downtime per year.** --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — How Many "Nines"? > **Problem:** A cloud provider advertises 99.95% availability. How many minutes of downtime per year does the customer tolerate under the SLA? ### Step 1 — Convert Percentage to a Ratio $$ A = 0.9995 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Downtime Fraction $$ 1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005 $$ ### Step 3 — Multiply by Annual Minutes $$ D_{\text{year}} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ D_{\text{year}} = 262.8\ \text{minutes} $$ ### Step 4 — Equivalence in Hours $$ D_{\text{year}} = \frac{262.8}{60} \approx 4.38\ \text{hours/year} $$ ### Final Answer > **The customer is contractually entitled to $\approx 262.8$ minutes ($\approx 4.38$ hours) of total annual downtime under a 99.95% SLA.** --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Probability of Successful Brute-Force > **Problem:** An attacker tries one password per second against a 4-digit PIN system. Each PIN attempt is independent. Compute the probability of *not* guessing the PIN in 100 attempts. ### Step 1 — Compute Probability of Failure on One Attempt The PIN space is $10^4 = 10{,}000$. The probability of a *single guess being wrong* is: $$ p_{\text{fail, 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{10{,}000} = \frac{9999}{10{,}000} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Independence over $n = 100$ Attempts $$ P_{\text{fail, 100}} = \left( \frac{9999}{10{,}000} \right)^{100} $$ ### Step 3 — Take the Natural Log for Numerical Evaluation $$ \ln P_{\text{fail, 100}} = 100 \cdot \ln\left( \frac{9999}{10{,}000} \right) $$ ### Step 4 — Series Approximation For small $x$, $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$. Here $x = 10^{-4}$: $$ \ln\left( \frac{9999}{10{,}000} \right) = \ln(1 - 10^{-4}) \approx -10^{-4} $$ Therefore: $$ \ln P_{\text{fail, 100}} \approx 100 \times (-10^{-4}) = -0.01 $$ ### Step 5 — Exponentiate $$ P_{\text{fail, 100}} \approx e^{-0.01} \approx 0.99005 $$ ### Step 6 — Probability of at Least One Success (Confidentiality Breach) $$ P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - P_{\text{fail, 100}} \approx 0.00995 \approx 0.995\% $$ ### Final Answer > **Probability of a successful PIN guess within 100 attempts $\approx 0.995\%$.** *This is why PINs are *often* throttled to 3 attempts before lockout — a confidentiality control.* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats to Security Goals The following table is a **board-exam favorite** and is essentially a cheat-sheet for "which goal defeats which attack": | Attack / Threat | Primary Goal Breached | Defensive Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Eavesdropping on Wi-Fi | **Confidentiality** | TLS, WPA3, VPN | | SQL Injection modifying rows | **Integrity** | Parameterized queries, input validation | | DDoS flooding a website | **Availability** | CDN, rate limiting, scrubbing | | Phishing (fake login page) | **Authentication** | MFA, FIDO2, password manager | | Privilege escalation exploit | **Access Control** | RBAC, least privilege, SELinux | | Sender denies signing a contract | **Non-Repudiation** | Digital signature, timestamped audit log | --- ## 3.5 Proof Sketch — Why Confidentiality and Integrity Are Independent This conceptual proof appears in 14-mark KTU questions as a sub-part. **Claim:** A system can have confidentiality without integrity, and integrity without confidentiality. **Proof (by counterexample):** *Confidentiality without integrity:* A doctor receives an encrypted message from a lab. The encryption preserves confidentiality (no one else reads it), but a MITM attacker who controls the network re-encrypts a tampered plaintext. The doctor decrypts and trusts the modified result. **Confidentiality preserved; integrity violated.** *Integrity without confidentiality:* A public bulletin board (e.g., a blockchain block explorer) — anyone can read the transactions (no confidentiality), but cryptographic hashes guarantee nobody can alter past blocks. **Integrity preserved; confidentiality absent.** Therefore the two goals are **logically independent**, and a complete security architecture must engineer **both** simultaneously. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Foundational Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[SENSITIVE DATA
INFORMATION ASSET] --> B[SECURITY ENGINEERING] B --> C[CONFIDENTIALITY
SEALED FROM OTHERS] B --> D[INTEGRITY
UNCHANGED TRUST] B --> E[AVAILABILITY
SERVABLE ON DEMAND] C --> F[SECURE SYSTEM OUTCOME] D --> F[SECURE SYSTEM OUTCOME] E --> F[SECURE SYSTEM OUTCOME] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style C fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff style D fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style E fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff style F fill:#6366f1,stroke:#312e81,color:#ffffff ``` > **Interpretation:** The diagram shows a single information asset (top) being subjected to security engineering, which fans out into the three classical CIA pillars. Their **union** (central node) is what yields a *secure system outcome*. If any pillar is missing, the system is *incomplete*, not *fully secure*. --- ## 4.2 Extended Security Goal Architecture (Full KTU 2024 View) ```mermaid flowchart LR REQ[SECURITY
REQUIREMENT] --> P1[CONFIDENTIALITY] REQ --> P2[INTEGRITY] REQ --> P3[AVAILABILITY] REQ --> P4[AUTHENTICATION] REQ --> P5[ACCESS CONTROL] REQ --> P6[NON REPUDIATION] P1 --> M1[TLS, AES, ACL] P2 --> M2[SHA256, HMAC, SIG] P3 --> M3[REDUNDANCY, DDOS MIT] P4 --> M4[PWD, MFA, BIOMETRIC] P5 --> M5[RBAC, MAC, DAC] P6 --> M6[DIGITAL SIG, TIMESTAMP] M1 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] M2 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] M3 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] M4 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] M5 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] M6 --> OUT[PROTECTED SYSTEM] style REQ fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff style OUT fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 The Authentication–Authorization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD U[USER OR PROCESS] --> Q1{IDENTITY
CLAIMED} Q1 -->|YES| V[VERIFY CREDENTIALS
PWD, OTP, BIOMETRIC] Q1 -->|NO| X1[DENY ACCESS] V --> Q2{CREDENTIALS
VALID} Q2 -->|NO| X2[DENY ACCESS] Q2 -->|YES| S[SESSION TOKEN ISSUED] S --> AC[ACCESS CONTROL CHECK
RBAC POLICY LOOKUP] AC --> Q3{REQUESTED OPERATION
PERMITTED} Q3 -->|NO| X3[DENY OPERATION] Q3 -->|YES| OK[GRANT OPERATION
LOG TO AUDIT TRAIL] style U fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style OK fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff style X1 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff style X2 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff style X3 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff ``` > **Interpretation:** *Authentication* and *Authorization* are **sequential, not synonymous**. The leftmost "claimed identity" is *who you say you are*; the middle "credentials valid" is *cryptographic verification*; the rightmost "operation permitted" is *policy-based access control*. KTU examiners reward students who explicitly call out the **two-step** nature of this pipeline. --- ## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth — Layered Mapping of Goals ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[LAYER 1
PHYSICAL SECURITY] --> G1[CONFIDENTIALITY PHYSICAL] L2[LAYER 2
NETWORK PERIMETER] --> G2[INTEGRITY AND AVAILABILITY NETWORK] L3[LAYER 3
HOST AND OS] --> G3[ACCESS CONTROL] L4[LAYER 4
APPLICATION] --> G4[AUTHENTICATION AND NON REPUDIATION] L5[LAYER 5
DATA AND CRYPTO] --> G5[CONFIDENTIALITY AND INTEGRITY] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 L5 --> RES[RESILIENT SYSTEM] style L1 fill:#475569,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style L2 fill:#475569,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style L3 fill:#475569,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style L4 fill:#475569,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style L5 fill:#475569,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style RES fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff ``` > **Note:** Defense-in-depth is the architectural principle that *no single layer is perfect*. Each layer contributes to a different security goal, so the **failure of one layer does not collapse the entire system**. --- ## 4.5 Threat-Goal-Mechanism Mapping Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference) ```mermaid flowchart LR THREATS[ADVERSARIAL THREATS] --> T1[SNIFFING] THREATS --> T2[TAMPERING] THREATS --> T3[DDOS] THREATS --> T4[IMPERSONATION] THREATS --> T5[PRIVILEGE ABUSE] THREATS --> T6[REPUDIATION] T1 --> G1[CONFIDENTIALITY GOAL] T2 --> G2[INTEGRITY GOAL] T3 --> G3[AVAILABILITY GOAL] T4 --> G4[AUTHENTICATION GOAL] T5 --> G5[ACCESS CONTROL GOAL] T6 --> G6[NON REPUDIATION GOAL] style THREATS fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > All questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern, with Bloom's cognitive levels tagged and examiner-style marks distribution. --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the CIA Triad. Why is it insufficient on its own? `[CO1, Bloom: Remember/Understand, 3 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]` **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three pillars: - **Confidentiality** — protection of data from unauthorized disclosure. - **Integrity** — assurance that data is not modified in an unauthorized way. - **Availability** — assurance that systems and data are accessible when needed. **Why insufficient:** Modern threats require **identity verification** (Authentication), **policy-based permissions** (Access Control), and **legally-binding accountability** (Non-Repudiation). The CIA triad alone does not address *who is accessing* or *whether they can later deny their action*. Hence the **extended model** with Authentication, Access Control, and Non-Repudiation is necessary. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between Authentication and Access Control. `[CO1, Bloom: Understand, 3 marks]` `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Authentication | Access Control | |---|---|---| | **Question Answered** | *Who are you?* | *What are you allowed to do?* | | **Mechanism** | Passwords, biometrics, MFA, certificates | RBAC, MAC, DAC, ACLs | | **When** | First, at session start | After authentication, on every request | | **Output** | Verified identity (session token) | Allow / Deny decision | | **Failure** | Login refused | Operation refused | Authentication is **identity proof**; Access Control is **permission enforcement**. Both are sequential and non-substitutable. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > The following **TWO** alternatives simulate KTU's ESE pattern: the student answers *one* full 14-mark question (with sub-parts (a) and (b) at 7 marks each). --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — The Classical "Security Goals + Calculation" `[CO1, CO2, Bloom: Apply/Analyze]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` #### (a) Explain in detail the six fundamental security goals of a secure information system. For each goal, give one real-world attack it defends against. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** The six fundamental security goals — also called the **security services** in ISO/IEC and KTU Module 1 — are: **1. Confidentiality (Privacy)** - *Definition:* Information is accessible only to those authorized. - *Attack defended:* Eavesdropping on a public Wi-Fi network. - *Mechanism:* Encryption (AES), access control lists. **2. Integrity** - *Definition:* Data is not altered in an unauthorized manner. - *Attack defended:* MITM bit-flipping in a financial transaction. - *Mechanism:* SHA-256 hash, HMAC, digital signatures. **3. Availability** - *Definition:* Systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed. - *Attack defended:* Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) on a banking website. - *Mechanism:* Redundancy, scrubbing, CDNs, failover clusters. **4. Authentication** - *Definition:* Verification of the identity of a user, device, or process. - *Attack defended:* Phishing (attacker impersonates a legitimate user). - *Mechanism:* Password + OTP, biometric, FIDO2 hardware keys. **5. Access Control (Authorization)** - *Definition:* Determination of which operations an authenticated entity may perform. - *Attack defended:* Privilege escalation by a low-privilege user gaining admin rights. - *Mechanism:* RBAC, MAC, capability-based security. **6. Non-Repudiation** - *Definition:* Guarantee that a party cannot deny having performed an action. - *Attack defended:* A sender denying a legally-binding email contract. - *Mechanism:* Digital signatures, trusted timestamps, write-once audit logs. *Diagrammatic summary (CIA + extensions):* > CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) + AAN (Authentication, Access Control, Non-Repudiation) form the complete KTU Module 1 service set. --- #### (b) A web server has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 5 hours. Compute (i) the availability ratio, (ii) the annual downtime in minutes, and (iii) identify which security goal is most relevant and why. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write the formula:** $$ A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 5} = \frac{5000}{5005} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ A = 0.999000\ldots \approx 99.90\% $$ **Step 4 — Annual downtime:** $$ D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 $$ $$ D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.999) \times 525{,}600 = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6\ \text{minutes} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the goal:** The most relevant security goal is **Availability**, because the entire computation measures *uptime versus downtime*. Improving availability requires reducing MTTR (faster repair) or increasing MTBF (more reliable hardware/software). > **Final Answer:** $A \approx 99.90\%$, annual downtime $\approx 525.6$ minutes ($\approx 8.76$ hours). Primary goal: **Availability**. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — "Threats and Counter-Measures" `[CO1, CO2, Bloom: Analyze/Evaluate]` `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` #### (a) For each of the following scenarios, identify (i) the security goal violated, (ii) the category of attack, and (iii) the appropriate counter-mechanism. (7 marks) **Scenarios and Model Answers:** **Scenario 1:** A hacker intercepts credit-card numbers transmitted between a browser and a shopping site. - (i) **Confidentiality** violated. - (ii) Attack = *Eavesdropping / Sniffing.* - (iii) Counter = **TLS/HTTPS** with strong cipher suites (AES-256-GCM), certificate pinning, HSTS. **Scenario 2:** An attacker changes the destination account number of a wire transfer in transit. - (i) **Integrity** violated. - (ii) Attack = *Man-in-the-Middle Tampering / Bit-Flipping.* - (iii) Counter = **Digital signatures** (Ed25519 or RSA-PSS) on the transaction payload; HMAC for session integrity. **Scenario 3:** A botnet sends millions of requests per second to a government portal, making it inaccessible to citizens. - (i) **Availability** violated. - (ii) Attack = *Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS).* - (iii) Counter = **Anycast CDN** (Cloudflare, Akamai), rate limiting, scrubbing centers, SYN cookies. **Scenario 4:** An employee accesses the HR salary database despite being only a junior accountant with no HR privileges. - (i) **Access Control** violated. - (ii) Attack = *Privilege Escalation / Authorization Bypass.* - (iii) Counter = **Strict RBAC policy** with least-privilege, mandatory access control (SELinux/AppArmor), audit logging. **Scenario 5:** A user authenticates to a banking app with username and password, but the app is fooled because the attacker stole the password from a phishing site. - (i) **Authentication** weakened (single-factor). - (ii) Attack = *Phishing / Credential Theft.* - (iii) Counter = **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** with FIDO2 hardware token; phishing-resistant authenticators. **Scenario 6:** A vendor digitally signs a contract, receives payment, then claims the signature was forged and demands a refund. - (i) **Non-Repudiation** violated (or, more precisely, *attempted to be subverted*). - (ii) Attack = *Repudiation of Origin.* - (iii) Counter = **Digital signature with trusted timestamping** from a Time Stamping Authority (TSA); signature verification log. **Scenario 7:** A user legitimately accesses the system but is then unable to reach the service for 18 hours during a cloud outage. - (i) **Availability** violated. - (ii) Attack = *Natural / Infrastructure Failure* (still a security concern under CIA). - (iii) Counter = **Multi-region active-active deployment**, regular DR drills, SLA-backed cloud contract. > **Examiners' note:** The student must state, for *each* scenario, all three of (i)–(iii). Skipping the counter-mechanism is the most common cause of partial-mark loss. --- #### (b) Explain the **Authentication–Authorization** pipeline with a labeled diagram. Why is authentication alone insufficient for security? (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Define the two operations:** - **Authentication** verifies the *claimed identity* of a user (e.g., username + password + OTP). - **Authorization** verifies the *permissions* attached to that identity (e.g., "user X may read file Y but not delete it"). **Step 2 — Describe the pipeline:** 1. **Claim:** User presents identity (e.g., username). 2. **Verify:** System challenges the user with a credential request (password, OTP, biometric). 3. **Validate:** System checks credentials against the authentication database (hashed and salted). 4. **Issue Token:** On success, the system issues a session token (cookie, JWT, OAuth bearer). 5. **Authorize Request:** For every subsequent request, the access-control module consults the policy store (RBAC/ACL) and compares the user's role/identity against the requested operation. 6. **Decide:** Allow or Deny. 7. **Audit:** Every decision is logged for accountability. **Step 3 — Diagram (as drawn in SECTION 4.3 of these notes):** User → Identity Claim → Credential Verify → Token Issue → Access Control Lookup → Allow / Deny → Audit Log. **Step 4 — Why authentication alone is insufficient:** - It does not specify *what* an authenticated user can do. - A valid login by a low-privilege insider is still a security event if that user reaches administrative endpoints. - Authentication does not prevent lateral movement, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration by *legitimately* authenticated accounts. - Hence **authorization is the operational gatekeeper**; authentication is the front door, but authorization is the *internal corridor control*. > **Closing Statement:** Security is not achieved by *either* step alone; the **chained** execution of "authenticate → authorize → audit" is what defines a zero-trust architecture in modern systems. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Security-Goals questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Listing CIA without explaining the mechanisms** — examiners want the *engineering tools* (encryption, hash, RBAC) tied to *each* goal, not just the names. > 2. **Mixing Authentication and Access Control** — these are *sequential*; treat them separately and explain the difference. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always state availability in **%**, downtime in **minutes**, not bare decimals. > 4. **Skipping the "real-world attack"** — KTU values *application-level* thinking; every security goal must be tied to a *concrete attack scenario* (DDoS, MITM, phishing, etc.). > 5. **Confusing integrity with confidentiality** — if the question is about *alteration*, you are in *integrity*; if about *disclosure*, you are in *confidentiality*. Mis-tagging costs 3–5 marks. > 6. **Not drawing a diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU valuation rubric awards 1–2 marks for a *labeled block diagram*; missing it caps your score. > 7. **Omitting Non-Repudiation** — this is a 2024-scheme emphasis; examiners explicitly look for it in any "list security goals" answer. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability. It is the *core* of information security. - **Extended KTU 2024 Goals** = Authentication, Access Control, Non-Repudiation (the "**AAN** extension"). - **Confidentiality** ↔ *Encryption* (AES, RSA, ChaCha20). - **Integrity** ↔ *Hash + MAC + Digital Signatures* (SHA-256, HMAC, Ed25519). - **Availability** ↔ *Redundancy + DDoS mitigation.* Formula: $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. - **Authentication** = "Who are you?" — knowledge / possession / biometric factors. - **Access Control** = "What can you do?" — DAC, MAC, RBAC. - **Non-Repudiation** = "You cannot deny it." — digital signatures + timestamps + audit. - **Authentication and Access Control are sequential, not synonymous.** - **CIA + AAN** is the **complete** Module 1 framework; examiners expect all six. - **Five-nines availability = 99.999%** ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year. - **Defense-in-Depth**: never rely on a single security layer. - **Always map** a real-world *threat* to a *goal* and a *mechanism* — the KTU answer must show the **threat–goal–mechanism** triplet. - **Audit + Accountability** close the loop, providing forensic traceability. - *Confidentiality and Integrity are independent* — you can have one without the other, but a secure system needs **both**.

topics.title: Security Services (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Access Control)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Services (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Access Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Security Service?

According to the **ITU-T X.800 Recommendation** (the foundational standard adopted by KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus), a **Security Service** is a processing or communication service that is provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to system resources. Security services achieve the security goals of an organization by leveraging one or more security **mechanisms** (such as encryption, digital signatures, access matrices).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Must Memorize):**
> A security service is a *capability* that protects against specific threats; it is **not** a one-to-one synonym for a mechanism. One mechanism (e.g., symmetric encryption) can provide multiple services (e.g., confidentiality + integrity if combined with MAC).

The five core security services mandated in **Module 1 of PCITT601** are:

1. **Data Confidentiality**
2. **Data Integrity**
3. **Authentication**
4. **Non-Repudiation**
5. **Access Control**

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogies for Each Service

| Security Service | Real-World Analogy | Threat Defended |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Sealing a letter inside a tamper-proof envelope before mailing it | Eavesdropping, snooping, traffic analysis |
| **Integrity** | A sealed notarial document — if the wax seal is broken, you know it was tampered | Modification, insertion, deletion, replay |
| **Authentication** | Showing your photo ID at airport security | Impersonation, masquerading |
| **Non-Repudiation** | A signed courier receipt — the sender cannot later deny having sent the parcel | Repudiation of origin or receipt |
| **Access Control** | A hotel key card that only opens your assigned room | Unauthorized use of a resource |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** The first letters form the acronym **CIA-NA** → **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication, **N**on-repudiation, **A**ccess Control. Do NOT confuse this with the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) — KTU examiners frequently test the distinction.

---

### 1.3 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Context)

The X.800 standard defines these services across the **OSI 7-layer model**, but the services in Module 1 are primarily applied at:

- **Application Layer** (e.g., PGP for confidentiality + non-repudiation)
- **Transport Layer** (e.g., TLS for confidentiality + integrity + authentication)
- **Network Layer** (e.g., IPsec for confidentiality + integrity + access control)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"At which layer is a given service typically implemented?"* Memorize: **Application = Non-repudiation**; **Transport = Authenticity + Integrity**; **Network = Access Control (via firewalls/VPNs)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Service 1 — Data Confidentiality

**Definition:** Confidentiality ensures that information is **not made available or disclosed to unauthorized** individuals, entities, or processes.

**Two levels of confidentiality** (KTU standard wording):

- **Connection confidentiality:** All user data on a connection is protected.
- **Connectionless confidentiality:** Protection of a single message block (e.g., one UDP datagram).

**Traffic-flow confidentiality** additionally protects the *pattern* of communication (who is talking to whom, when, and how much).

**Primary Mechanisms Used:**
- Symmetric encryption (AES, 3DES, ChaCha20)
- Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECC)
- Steganography (for hiding existence of communication)
- Access control lists (who is allowed to read what)

---

### 2.2 Service 2 — Data Integrity

**Definition:** Integrity assures that the data received is **exactly the same as the data sent** — no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay has occurred.

**Five sub-types of integrity** (per X.800):

- **Connection integrity with recovery:** Detects and attempts to recover.
- **Connection integrity without recovery:** Detects only.
- **Selective-field connection integrity:** Protects specific fields of each transmitted block.
- **Connectionless integrity:** Protects a single block, no sequence protection.
- **Selective-field connectionless integrity:** Selective fields, single block.

**Primary Mechanisms Used:**
- Message Authentication Codes (MAC)
- Hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3)
- Digital signatures (for integrity + non-repudiation)
- Sequence numbers + timestamps (against replay)

**The Math of Integrity Detection:**

A single-bit change in a message changes approximately **50%** of the output bits of a good hash function — this is the **avalanche effect** (a hard KTU exam favorite).

---

### 2.3 Service 3 — Authentication

**Definition:** Authentication is the **assurance that the communicating entity is the one that it claims to be**.

**Two fundamental sub-types:**

- **Peer Entity Authentication:** Confirms identity of a *connected* party (e.g., during a TLS handshake).
- **Data-Origin Authentication:** Confirms that a *specific message* originated from a claimed source.

**Primary Mechanisms Used:**
- Passwords / PINs
- Challenge-response protocols
- Digital certificates (X.509)
- Biometrics (fingerprint, retina — hardware layer)
- Multi-factor authentication (MFA)

**The Authentication Math (Challenge-Response):**

If Alice wants to prove to Bob that she is Alice, without revealing her secret key $K_A$:

$$C = E(K_A, N)$$

where $N$ is a fresh random **nonce** (number used once) chosen by Bob. Alice returns the encrypted nonce $C$, and Bob decrypts and compares.

---

### 2.4 Service 4 — Non-Repudiation

**Definition:** Non-repudiation provides protection against denial by one of the parties involved in a communication of having participated in all or part of the communication.

**Two Critical Variants:**

- **Non-repudiation of Origin (NRO):** Proves that a message *was sent* by the alleged sender.
- **Non-repudiation of Receipt (NRR):** Proves that a message *was received* by the alleged recipient.

**Primary Mechanism:** **Digital Signatures** (using asymmetric cryptography).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):** *Symmetric* MACs provide **integrity + authentication** but **NOT non-repudiation** (because both parties share the same key, so the receiver could have forged the tag). Only **asymmetric digital signatures** (RSA, ECDSA) provide true non-repudiation.

---

### 2.5 Service 5 — Access Control

**Definition:** Access Control is the **prevention of unauthorized use of a resource** — it determines *who can do what* to which resource.

**Access Control Models (KTU must-know):**

| Model | Core Idea | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **DAC** (Discretionary) | Owner decides who has access | File systems (Linux/Windows) |
| **MAC** (Mandatory) | System enforces classification labels (e.g., Top Secret) | Military / Government |
| **RBAC** (Role-Based) | Access based on user's role | Enterprise (admin, user, guest) |
| **ABAC** (Attribute-Based) | Access based on attributes of user, resource, environment | Cloud (AWS IAM policies) |

**Primary Mechanisms:**
- Access Control Matrix / Access Control Lists (ACLs)
- Capability lists
- Passwords and biometrics
- Firewalls (network-level)

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Service |
|---|---|---|
| $C = E(K, P)$ | Encryption of plaintext $P$ under key $K$ | Confidentiality |
| $P = D(K, C)$ | Decryption recovers plaintext | Confidentiality |
| $h = H(M)$ | Hash of message $M$, length $n$ bits | Integrity |
| $\text{MAC} = H(K \oplus M)$ | Message Authentication Code | Integrity + Auth |
| $S = \text{Sign}(K_{\text{priv}}, H(M))$ | Digital signature on hash | Integrity + Non-repudiation |
| $V = \text{Verify}(K_{\text{pub}}, S, M)$ | Signature verification | Non-repudiation |
| $C = E(K_A, N)$ | Challenge-response nonce | Authentication |
| $\text{Info Gain} = 1 - \frac{H(X \vert \text{observed})}{H(X)}$ | Information leakage (theoretical bound) | Confidentiality |

> [!NOTE]
> The probability of an undetected integrity violation in a message of $n$ independent bits is:
> $$P_{\text{undetected}} = \frac{1}{2^n}$$
> For $n = 128$ (e.g., MD5), $P_{\text{undetected}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-39}$ — vanishingly small. KTU expects you to know this for 14-mark problems.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Confidentiality** → HIPAA (medical records), PCI-DSS (credit cards).
- **Integrity** → Git commit hashes (SHA-1 → SHA-256), blockchain Merkle roots.
- **Authentication** → JWT tokens in REST APIs, Kerberos in Windows domains.
- **Non-repudiation** → Legally binding e-contracts under India's IT Act 2000 (Section 3A).
- **Access Control** → SELinux in Linux kernels, AWS IAM, Kubernetes RBAC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why XOR Provides Confidentiality (One-Time Pad Model)

The One-Time Pad (OTP) is the **provably secure** benchmark for confidentiality. We derive why it leaks zero information to an attacker who does not know the key.

**Setup:**

- Plaintext: $P \in \{0,1\}^n$ (n-bit string)
- Key: $K \in \{0,1\}^n$, chosen uniformly at random
- Ciphertext: $C = P \oplus K$ (bitwise XOR)

**Step 1 — Define the entropy of $K$ before observation:**

$$H(K) = n \text{ bits}$$

This is the maximum possible entropy for an $n$-bit key.

**Step 2 — Compute the conditional entropy of plaintext given ciphertext:**

$$H(P \mid C) = -\sum_{p \in \{0,1\}^n} \sum_{c \in \{0,1\}^n} \Pr(P=p, C=c) \cdot \log_2 \Pr(P=p \mid C=c)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that for every pair $(p, k)$ with $c = p \oplus k$, the joint probability is:**

$$\Pr(P=p, C=c) = \Pr(P=p) \cdot \Pr(K = p \oplus c)$$

**Step 4 — Since $K$ is uniform, $\Pr(K = p \oplus c) = 2^{-n}$, independent of $p$ and $c$.**

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$H(P \mid C) = H(P)$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:**

$$I(P; C) = H(P) - H(P \mid C) = 0$$

The **mutual information** between plaintext and ciphertext is **zero** — observing $C$ tells the attacker **absolutely nothing** about $P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The OTP achieves **perfect secrecy** (Shannon, 1949). The KTU-mandated key length is $n$ bits for an $n$-bit message, and the key **must never be reused** (hence "one-time").

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Undetected Integrity Violation

**Setup:** An integrity check uses a CRC or hash of length $n$ bits. An attacker wishes to modify the message without detection.

**Step 1 — Total possible hash values:** $2^n$.

**Step 2 — For a random modification, probability the corrupted message collides with the original hash:**

$$P(\text{collision}) = \frac{1}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 — For $n = 32$ (CRC-32):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2^{32}} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4 — For $n = 128$ (MD5):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-39}$$

**Step 5 — For $n = 256$ (SHA-256, modern standard):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2^{256}} \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-78}$$

**Step 6 — Implication:** A 256-bit hash provides stronger integrity protection than the number of atoms in the observable universe ($\approx 10^{80}$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Why Digital Signatures Provide Non-Repudiation

**Setup:** Alice signs message $M$ with her private key $K_{A,\text{priv}}$:

$$S = E(K_{A,\text{priv}}, H(M))$$

**Step 1 — Verification by Bob:**

$$M' = D(K_{A,\text{pub}}, S)$$

If $M' = H(M)$, Bob has cryptographic proof that the message was signed by Alice (because only Alice possesses $K_{A,\text{priv}}$).

**Step 2 — Property of asymmetric keys:** It is computationally infeasible to derive $K_{A,\text{priv}}$ from $K_{A,\text{pub}}$.

**Step 3 — Therefore:** Alice cannot later deny the signature without claiming her private key was compromised — which is a much stronger claim and can be ruled out by a **Certificate Authority (CA)** that issued her public-key certificate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Repudiation vs. MAC:** A MAC uses a **shared** key $K_{AB}$. If Bob produces a valid MAC, he could himself have created it. Hence, **MAC does not provide non-repudiation**. Only **digital signatures** do.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Services to OSI Layers

| Service | Application | Transport (TLS) | Network (IPsec) | Data Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | PGP / S/MIME | TLS record protocol | ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) | WPA3 |
| Integrity | S/MIME signatures | TLS HMAC | AH (Authentication Header) | — |
| Authentication | Kerberos | TLS handshake | IKE (Internet Key Exchange) | 802.1X |
| Non-Repudiation | Digital signatures | — | — | — |
| Access Control | App-level ACLs | TLS endpoint auth | IPsec Security Policy Database | 802.1X port-based |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Service → Mechanism

| Threat (X.800) | Required Service | Typical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Snooping | Confidentiality | Symmetric/AEAD encryption |
| Tampering | Integrity | MAC, digital signature |
| Masquerading | Authentication | Certificate, challenge-response |
| Replay | Integrity (+ freshness) | Nonce, timestamp, sequence number |
| Repudiation | Non-Repudiation | Digital signature |
| Unauthorized access | Access Control | ACL, RBAC, MAC labels |
| Denial of Service | Availability (not in this module) | Firewalls, rate limiting |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Five Security Services and Their Primary Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Security Services Module 1] --> B[Confidentiality]
    A --> C[Integrity]
    A --> D[Authentication]
    A --> E[Non-Repudiation]
    A --> F[Access Control]

    B --> B1[Symmetric Encryption AES]
    B --> B2[Asymmetric Encryption RSA]
    B --> B3[Traffic Flow Protection]

    C --> C1[Hash Function SHA-256]
    C --> C2[Message Authentication Code MAC]
    C --> C3[Digital Signature]

    D --> D1[Password Based]
    D --> D2[Challenge Response Nonce]
    D --> D3[Digital Certificate X509]
    D --> D4[Biometric Factor]

    E --> E1[Digital Signature Algorithm]
    E --> E2[Trusted Timestamp Authority]

    F --> F1[Discretionary DAC]
    F --> F2[Mandatory MAC]
    F --> F3[Role Based RBAC]
    F --> F4[Attribute Based ABAC]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Authentication via Challenge-Response (Zero-Knowledge Flavor)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Alice (Claimant)
    participant Bob as Bob (Verifier)

    Note over Bob: Step 1 Generate fresh nonce N
    Bob->>Alice: Step 2 Send challenge N
    Note over Alice: Step 3 Compute response C equals E of K_A and N
    Alice->>Bob: Step 4 Return ciphertext C
    Note over Bob: Step 5 Decrypt C and compare to N
    Bob->>Bob: Step 6 If match then Alice authenticated
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Digital Signature Workflow (Non-Repudiation + Integrity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender Side
        M1[Original Message M] --> H1[Compute hash h equals H of M]
        H1 --> S1[Sign with private key K_priv]
        S1 --> Sig[Digital Signature S]
    end

    subgraph Transmission
        M1 --> Tx[Channel]
        Sig --> Tx
    end

    subgraph Receiver Side
        Tx --> M2[Received Message M prime]
        Tx --> Sig2[Received Signature S]
        M2 --> H2[Compute hash h prime equals H of M prime]
        Sig2 --> V1[Decrypt S with public key K_pub to get h check]
        H2 --> Cmp[Compare h prime and h check]
        V1 --> Cmp
        Cmp --> Decision{Match?}
        Decision -->|Yes| Accept[Valid Integrity and Non-Repudiation]
        Decision -->|No| Reject[Discard Message]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Access Control Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Requests Access to Resource R]) --> Auth[Step 1 Authenticate Identity]
    Auth -->|Fail| Deny[Step 5 Deny Access]
    Auth -->|Pass| GetAttrs[Step 2 Retrieve Subject Attributes and Resource Attributes]
    GetAttrs --> EvalPolicy[Step 3 Evaluate Access Policy]
    EvalPolicy --> Decision{Step 4 Is action allowed?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Grant[Step 6 Grant Access and Log]
    Decision -->|No| Deny
    Grant --> End1([End])
    Deny --> End2([End])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is the **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)** model used in modern cloud systems. For RBAC, the "Retrieve Subject Attributes" step would be replaced with "Retrieve User's Roles" and the policy would be a static role-permission mapping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly define the five security services defined by the X.800 standard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The X.800 standard defines five core security services:

1. **Data Confidentiality (1 mark):** Protection of data from unauthorized disclosure during transmission or storage.
2. **Data Integrity (0.5 mark):** Assurance that the data received is exactly as sent, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay.
3. **Authentication (0.5 mark):** Verification that the communicating entity is the one it claims to be (peer-entity or data-origin).
4. **Non-Repudiation (0.5 mark):** Protection against denial by either party of having participated in the communication (origin or receipt).
5. **Access Control (0.5 mark):** Prevention of unauthorized use of a resource, including read, write, and execute permissions.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between **authentication** and **non-repudiation**. Why does a MAC not provide non-repudiation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Non-Repudiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Prove identity *at the time of communication* | Prove identity *with legal-grade evidence, even after communication ends* |
| **Mechanism** | Shared key (MAC) OR Public-key cert | Asymmetric digital signature only |
| **Dispute resolution** | Cannot be used as evidence in a dispute | Can be used in a court of law |

**Why MAC fails to provide non-repudiation (1.5 marks):**
A MAC uses a **shared symmetric key** $K_{AB}$. Since both Alice and Bob know $K_{AB}$, Bob himself could have generated a valid MAC. Hence, Bob cannot prove to a third party (e.g., a judge) that *Alice* and not he himself created the MAC. Only **asymmetric digital signatures** — where the private key is known solely to the signer — can provide non-repudiation.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Data Integrity** service. Discuss the role of **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** and **Hash Functions** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Authentication** service. With a neat diagram, describe the **Challenge-Response** mechanism using symmetric encryption. Discuss its strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Data Integrity** ensures that information is altered only in an authorized manner, and that any unauthorized modification can be detected (X.800 definition).

**Sub-types of integrity (1 mark):**
- Connection integrity with/without recovery
- Connectionless integrity
- Selective-field integrity

**Mechanism 1 — Hash Function (3 marks):**

A hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ maps an arbitrary-length message to a fixed-length $n$-bit digest. Properties:

$$H(M) = h, \quad \text{length}(h) = n \text{ bits}$$

For integrity, the hash is sent alongside the message:

$$\text{Transmit: } \{M, h = H(M)\}$$

Receiver computes $H(M)$ again and compares.

**Mechanism 2 — Message Authentication Code (3 marks):**

A MAC is a keyed hash. Using HMAC construction:

$$\text{MAC} = H((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M))$$

A MAC provides **integrity + authentication** (but not non-repudiation).

**Block diagram of HMAC-based integrity verification:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SenderMsg[M] --> SenderHash[H of M equals h]
    SenderKey[Shared Key K] --> HMAC[Compute HMAC tag]
    SenderHash --> HMAC
    HMAC --> Transmit[Transmit M and tag]
    Transmit --> ReceiverHash[H of M prime equals h prime]
    Transmit --> ReceiverMAC[Recompute HMAC tag prime]
    ReceiverKey[Shared Key K] --> ReceiverMAC
    ReceiverHash --> Compare{h equals h prime ?}
    ReceiverMAC --> Compare
    Compare -->|Yes| OK[Integrity Verified]
    Compare -->|No| Fail[Discard Message]
```

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Authentication (1 mark):** Assurance that an entity is the one it claims to be. Sub-types: **Peer-Entity** (during connection) and **Data-Origin** (per message).

**Challenge-Response Protocol using Symmetric Encryption (4 marks):**

1. Bob generates a fresh random **nonce** $N$.
2. Bob $\rightarrow$ Alice: $\text{Challenge} = N$.
3. Alice computes $C = E(K_{AB}, N)$ using shared key.
4. Alice $\rightarrow$ Bob: $\text{Response} = C$.
5. Bob computes $D(K_{AB}, C)$ and compares to $N$.
6. If match, Alice is authenticated.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant B as Bob
    participant A as Alice

    B->>B: Generate nonce N
    B->>A: Step 1 Send N
    A->>A: Compute C equals E of K_AB and N
    A->>B: Step 2 Return C
    B->>B: Decrypt C and compare to N
    B-->>B: Match = Authenticated
```

**Strengths (1 mark):**
- Password is never transmitted.
- Replay attack defeated by nonce freshness.

**Weaknesses (1 mark):**
- Requires pre-shared symmetric key.
- Vulnerable to **man-in-the-middle** if key exchange is compromised.
- Does not provide non-repudiation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Confidentiality** and **Non-Repudiation** services. With diagrams, describe how **symmetric encryption** achieves confidentiality and how **digital signatures** achieve non-repudiation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Access Control** service. Compare **DAC**, **MAC**, and **RBAC** models with one real-world example each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Confidentiality (1.5 marks):** Ensures that data is not disclosed to unauthorized parties. Achieved primarily through encryption.

**Symmetric Encryption for Confidentiality (2.5 marks):**

$$C = E(K, P), \quad P = D(K, C)$$

where $K$ is a shared secret. Algorithms: AES-128, AES-256, ChaCha20. Block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext P] --> E[Encryption E with Key K]
    E --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> Tx[Insecure Channel]
    Tx --> D[Decryption D with Key K]
    D --> P2[Recovered Plaintext P]
```

**Non-Repudiation (1 mark):** Provides unforgeable proof of the origin of a message.

**Digital Signature for Non-Repudiation (2 marks):**

$$S = E(K_{\text{priv}}, H(M)), \quad \text{Verify: } D(K_{\text{pub}}, S) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$$

Only the owner of $K_{\text{priv}}$ can produce $S$. Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.3** above.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Access Control (1 mark):** Determines and enforces who can perform what operations on which resources. Comprises **identification**, **authentication**, **authorization**, and **accounting (audit)**.

**Comparison Table (6 marks):**

| Feature | DAC (Discretionary) | MAC (Mandatory) | RBAC (Role-Based) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Policy Authority** | Resource owner | Central system admin | Central admin (role-based) |
| **Decision basis** | Owner's discretion | Security labels (e.g., Top Secret) | User's assigned role |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Security strength** | Lower (owner can make mistakes) | Highest (military-grade) | High (enterprise-grade) |
| **Example** | Linux file permissions (chmod 755) | SELinux, military classification | AWS IAM roles, hospital staff roles |
| **Best for** | Collaborative file systems | Government, defense | Enterprise, cloud |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the CIA Triad with CIA-NA** — Confidentiality-Integrity-Availability is NOT the same as the 5 services in this module. Availability is covered in a later module.
> 2. **Stating that MAC provides non-repudiation** — This is wrong and will cost 2–3 marks. Only digital signatures do.
> 3. **Forgetting sub-types** — X.800 lists 5 sub-types of integrity. Mentioning only "hash functions" without naming sub-types is a partial answer.
> 4. **No diagram = lose 1–2 marks** — KTU 14-mark answers must include a diagram (block/flow/mermaid) for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up MAC and HMAC** — HMAC is a *specific construction*; MAC is a *generic concept*.
> 6. **Access Control — omitting authentication step** — Access control assumes the user is *already authenticated*; failing to mention this loses a mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CIA-NA** is the order: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication, **N**on-repudiation, **A**ccess Control.
- **X.800** is the standard; **X.805** extends it to end-to-end network security.
- **Confidentiality** uses **encryption** (symmetric or asymmetric).
- **Integrity** uses **hash** (SHA-256) or **MAC** (HMAC).
- **Authentication** uses **passwords, nonces, certificates, biometrics**.
- **Non-repudiation** uses **digital signatures** ONLY (not MACs).
- **Access control** has four models: **DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC**.
- **One-Time Pad** provides **perfect secrecy** (Shannon).
- **Probability of undetected integrity violation** = $1/2^n$.
- **Avalanche effect** — 1-bit change $\rightarrow$ 50% output bit changes.
- **Nonce** = Number used ONCE (defeats replay).
- **Replay attack** is defeated by nonce + timestamp + sequence number.
- **TLS = Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication** (not non-repudiation).
- **PGP = Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication + Non-repudiation** (uses both symmetric and asymmetric).
- **MAC key must be shared**; hence no non-repudiation.
- **Digital signature private key** is unique to signer; hence non-repudiation holds.
- **Layer placement:** App = non-repudiation; Transport = confidentiality + integrity; Network = access control.
- **Three A's of access control:** **A**uthentication, **A**uthorization, **A**ccounting.
- **Bell-LaPadula** (confidentiality) and **Biba** (integrity) are formal MAC models.
- **Kerberos** uses tickets for authentication (mentioned in Module 2 but conceptually related).
- **Biometrics** is the only authentication mechanism that cannot be **revoked** (you cannot "reset" a fingerprint).
- **MFA** = something you **know** (password) + something you **have** (token) + something you **are** (biometric).
- **Salt** in password hashing defeats **rainbow table** attacks (not in this module but conceptually adjacent).

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU ESE, draw a **single comparative table** of all five services in your answer sheet. Examiners love it and award full marks instantly. Use the table in Section 1.2 as a template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Mechanisms (Encipherment, Data Integrity, Digital Signature, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization, Access Control)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Mechanisms (Encipherment, Data Integrity, Digital Signature, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization, Access Control)

# Module 1: Security Mechanisms — Foundational Pillar of Information Security

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A **Security Mechanism** is a process (or a device incorporating such a process) that is designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. According to the **ITU-T X.800 / ISO 7498-2** standard (which KTU 2024 follows), specific security mechanisms are universally defined as countermeasures against the classes of threats faced by modern open systems.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Intuition

Imagine a high-security bank building. To protect the vault (your data), the bank uses **multiple overlapping defenses**:
- A **locked metal door** (Encipherment)
- A **tamper-proof seal** on the deposit box (Data Integrity)
- A **signature on the cheque** (Digital Signature)
- An **ID check at the entrance** (Authentication Exchange)
- **Decoy corridors** to confuse intruders (Traffic Padding)
- **Multiple secret paths** to the vault (Routing Control)
- A **witness notary** who timestamps transactions (Notarization)
- A **list of authorized personnel** (Access Control)

Each one is a *mechanism* — a single mechanism alone is not enough. **Defense-in-depth** requires layering them. This is the core philosophy of the OSI Security Architecture.

### 1.2 The 8 KTU-Mandated Security Mechanisms (High-Level Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO1):** The student must be able to *identify, classify, and describe the specific security mechanisms defined under the OSI Security Architecture, and distinguish them from the broader class of Security Services.*

| # | Mechanism | Primary Purpose | OSI Layer (Typical) |
|---|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Encipherment** | Confidentiality via mathematical transformation | 1–7 (any) |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Detect alteration / insertion / deletion / replay | 2–7 |
| 3 | **Digital Signature** | Authentication + Integrity + Non-repudiation | 5–7 |
| 4 | **Authentication Exchange** | Verify identity of an entity via a handshake | 3–7 |
| 5 | **Traffic Padding** | Defeat traffic-flow analysis | 1–4 |
| 6 | **Routing Control** | Select/avoid specific secure routes | 3 |
| 7 | **Notarization** | Trusted third-party attestation of properties | 5–7 |
| 8 | **Access Control** | Restrict access to authorized users/resources | All |

### 1.3 Security Mechanism vs. Security Service (A Critical Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism = HOW** the protection is technically achieved (the algorithm/tool).
> **Service = WHY** the protection is needed (the goal from the user's perspective).
> A single *service* may be realized by *multiple mechanisms*, and a single *mechanism* may be used to provide *multiple services*.

**Example:** The *Digital Signature* mechanism provides the *Data Origin Authentication* service **and** the *Non-Repudiation* service simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism 1 — Encipherment

**Definition:** The use of mathematical algorithms to transform plaintext into ciphertext, making the data unintelligible to unauthorized parties.

**How it works:**
- Uses symmetric (shared key) algorithms — **AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20**
- Uses asymmetric (public key) algorithms — **RSA, ECC, ElGamal**
- The "key" parameterizes the transformation: $C = E_K(P)$, $P = D_K(C)$

**Why:** Confidentiality. The only mathematically proven way to protect data against eavesdropping on open networks.

**Real-world utility:** Used in **HTTPS (TLS 1.3)**, **WPA3 Wi-Fi**, **SSH tunnels**, **PGP email encryption**, and **full-disk encryption** (BitLocker, FileVault).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Encipherment is the *only* mechanism that can mathematically guarantee confidentiality against a computationally bounded adversary (assuming Kerckhoffs's principle holds).

### 2.2 Mechanism 2 — Data Integrity

**Definition:** A mechanism that guarantees that a message $M$ received is exactly the message $M$ that was sent — no bit was altered, inserted, deleted, or replayed.

**How it works:**
- Sender appends a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** or **cryptographic hash** to the message.
- Receiver recomputes the value and compares.
- The general verification relation:

$$
\text{Integrity Check} = \begin{cases} \text{VALID} & \text{if } h' = h \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } h' \neq h \end{cases}
$$

**Why:** Defends against active threats — modification, masquerade, replay, repudiation.

**Key algorithms (KTU 2024 Module 1/2):** **MD5** (deprecated), **SHA-256**, **SHA-3**, **HMAC-SHA256**, **CMAC**, **GMAC**.

**Real-world utility:** **TLS record-layer MAC**, **IPsec AH/ESP integrity**, **blockchain Merkle trees**, **signed software updates**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Integrity ≠ Confidentiality.** A hash alone is publicly visible — anyone can compute the hash. Integrity is typically *combined* with authentication (HMAC uses a key) to prevent forgery.

### 2.3 Mechanism 3 — Digital Signature

**Definition:** A cryptographic transformation that binds the identity of a signer to a piece of data, providing authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation.

**How it works:**
- Sender computes $h = H(M)$ (hash of message).
- Sender encrypts the hash *with their private key*: $\sigma = E_{K_{priv}}(h) = S_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$.
- Receiver decrypts using the sender's *public key*: $h' = D_{K_{pub}}(\sigma)$.
- Receiver compares $h'$ with freshly computed $H(M)$.

**The three properties provided:**
- **Authentication** — proves the sender.
- **Integrity** — proves the message is unaltered.
- **Non-repudiation** — the sender *cannot* later deny signing (because only they have $K_{priv}$).

**Algorithms (KTU 2024):** **RSA-PSS**, **ECDSA**, **Ed25519**, **Schnorr signatures**.

**Real-world utility:** **X.509 certificates**, **code signing** (Microsoft Authenticode), **blockchain transactions**, **electronic passports (eMRTD)**, **Aadhaar eSign**, **DSC for GST filing in India**.

### 2.4 Mechanism 4 — Authentication Exchange

**Definition:** A two-way mechanism by which entities prove their identity to each other through an information-exchange protocol.

**How it works:** Two types:
1. **Something the entity knows** — password, PIN, secret key.
2. **Something the entity possesses/is** — token, biometric.

Common protocols:
- **Challenge–Response** (mutual): $A \to B: r_B$ (challenge), $B \to A: E_{K_{AB}}(r_B)$ (response using shared key).
- **One-way authentication** (e.g., timestamped ticket using Kerberos TGT).
- **Zero-knowledge proofs** (e.g., Schnorr's identification protocol, ZK-SNARKs in privacy coins).

**Real-world utility:** **Kerberos** (used in Windows Active Directory), **RADIUS**, **OAuth 2.0**, **FIDO2/WebAuthn**, **TLS client certificates**.

### 2.5 Mechanism 5 — Traffic Padding

**Definition:** The insertion of spurious (decoy) data units into the data stream to obscure the actual traffic pattern, defeating *traffic-flow analysis* attacks.

**How it works:**
- Continuous stream of bits is transmitted at a constant rate, regardless of whether actual data exists.
- Idle periods are filled with **encrypted garbage frames**.
- The encryption of padding is critical — otherwise an attacker can easily identify padding.

**Mathematical model:** Let the actual traffic rate be $R(t)$. The padding mechanism enforces:

$$
R_{padded}(t) = R_{max} \quad \forall t \in [0, T]
$$

where $R_{max}$ is the maximum possible legitimate load.

**Why:** Hides *when* and *how much* data is being exchanged. An attacker cannot determine activity patterns.

**Real-world utility:** **Tor network** (constant-rate padding), **ISDN voice channels with silence suppression disabled**, **classified military/government links**, **WLAN with constant-rate beaconing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Traffic padding does *not* hide the *destination* (that's routing control) and does *not* hide the *content* (that's encipherment). It only hides the *pattern/timing*.

### 2.6 Mechanism 6 — Routing Control

**Definition:** A mechanism that enables the selection of *physically or logically secure routes* for certain data, and the avoidance of *insecure* ones.

**How it works:**
- Routes are selected based on security criteria (trust, encryption, redundancy).
- Allows dynamic re-routing if a node/hop is suspected compromised.
- May be combined with labels/secure domains: data marked with security attributes dictates permissible routes.

**Conceptual model:** The network is treated as a *trusted-edges graph* $G = (V, E, w)$ where $w(e)$ is a security weight. A secure path is the shortest path under the constraint $w(e) \geq w_{min}$.

**Why:** An attacker who controls a router on the path should not be able to read or modify the traffic.

**Real-world utility:** **BGP route filtering**, **MPLS LSPs with secure labels**, **SDN (Software-Defined Networking) flow rules**, **Tor's onion-routing overlay**, **multihomed corporate VPNs with fail-over routing**.

### 2.7 Mechanism 7 — Notarization

**Definition:** The use of a **trusted third party (TTP)** to attest to certain properties of a data exchange — typically *authenticity*, *time of origin*, and *integrity*.

**How it works:**
- Both sender and receiver send the message (or its hash) to the Notary.
- The Notary timestamps and signs the message.
- A later dispute is settled by consulting the Notary's record.
- Two forms: **Inline notarization** (Notary sees plaintext) vs. **Blind notarization** (Notary sees only encrypted/hashed values).

**Conceptual relation:**

$$
\text{Cert}_{notary} = S_{K_{priv}^{TTP}}\left( H(M) \,\|\, \text{TimeStamp} \,\|\, \text{Identity}_A \right)
$$

**Why:** Solves the *repudiation* problem when two parties later disagree on whether a transaction occurred.

**Real-world utility:** **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Certificate Authorities (CAs)**, **Trusted Timestamp Services (RFC 3161)**, **Blockchain consensus nodes** (as decentralized notaries), **e-Notary under the Indian IT Act 2000**.

### 2.8 Mechanism 8 — Access Control

**Definition:** A mechanism that enforces the rule that *only authorized entities* (users, processes, hosts) are allowed to access protected resources (files, services, ports), in *authorized ways* (read, write, execute, delete).

**How it works — The Three A's:**
1. **Authentication** — who are you?
2. **Authorization** — what are you allowed to do?
3. **Accounting (Audit)** — what did you actually do?

**Formal access matrix model:**

$$
M = [M_{ij}] \quad \text{where } M_{ij} = \text{set of rights of subject } i \text{ on object } j
$$

**Why:** Even with confidentiality, integrity, and authentication, without access control any authenticated user could do anything. Access control is the *final gatekeeper*.

**Real-world utility:** **Linux file permissions (rwx), ACLs, RBAC, ABAC, MAC (SELinux, AppArmor)**, **database GRANT statements**, **firewall rules**, **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)**, **IAM in AWS/Azure/GCP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Access control is implemented at multiple OSI layers — **MAC filtering at Layer 2**, **firewalls at Layer 3/4**, **RBAC at the application layer (Layer 7)**.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Summary Table

| Mechanism | Threat Defended | Service(s) Provided | Crypto Required? | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Encipherment | Snooping / Disclosure | Confidentiality | **Yes (always)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Data Integrity | Modification | Integrity | Hash or MAC | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Digital Signature | Forgery, Repudiation | Authenticity, Integrity, Non-repudiation | **Yes (asymmetric)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Authentication Exchange | Masquerade | Peer/Entity Authentication | Optional (recommended) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Traffic Padding | Traffic-Flow Analysis | Confidentiality of flow | Optional | ⭐⭐ |
| Routing Control | Misdelivery, Wiretapping | Confidentiality of route | Optional | ⭐⭐ |
| Notarization | Repudiation | Non-repudiation, Data Origin Auth | **Yes (signatures)** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Access Control | Unauthorized use | Access Control Service | No (but often combined) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Integration

In a modern enterprise architecture, the 8 mechanisms are layered as follows:
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Access control via MAC filtering, 802.1X.
- **Layer 3 (Network):** Routing control via secure BGP, IPsec tunneling.
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** Encipherment + integrity via TLS 1.3.
- **Layer 5–6 (Session/Presentation):** Authentication exchange via Kerberos.
- **Layer 7 (Application):** Digital signatures, notarization via PKI, access control via RBAC.
- **Cross-layer:** Traffic padding at the link or network layer (defense against traffic analysis).

This layered model directly maps to the **defense-in-depth** principle and is heavily tested in KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Analysis 1 — Formalizing the Digital Signature Verification Equation

A KTU ESE frequently asks: *"Prove that a digital signature provides both authentication and integrity. Show the verification mathematics."*

#### Step 1: The Sender's Signing Operation
Let $M$ be the message, $H(\cdot)$ a cryptographic hash function, and $S_{K_{priv}}(\cdot)$ a signing algorithm with sender Alice's private key.

$$
h = H(M)
$$

The signature is computed as:

$$
\sigma = S_{K_{priv}^{A}}(h) = S_{K_{priv}^{A}}(H(M))
$$

#### Step 2: The Transmitted Tuple
The sender transmits the pair $(M, \sigma)$ over the (insecure) channel.

#### Step 3: The Receiver's Verification
Bob, on receiving $(M', \sigma')$, performs:

$$
h_1 = V_{K_{pub}^{A}}(\sigma')
$$

That is, he decrypts the signature using Alice's publicly known verification key.

#### Step 4: Independent Hash Recomputation
Bob independently computes the hash of the received message:

$$
h_2 = H(M')
$$

#### Step 5: Equality Test
The signature is **accepted if and only if**:

$$
h_1 = h_2
$$

#### Step 6: Conclusion — Why Both Properties Hold
- **Authentication:** Only the holder of $K_{priv}^{A}$ could have produced a $\sigma$ such that $V_{K_{pub}^{A}}(\sigma)$ equals $H(M)$. Since $K_{pub}^{A}$ is bound to Alice's identity (via a certificate from a Notary/CA), Alice is proven to be the sender.
- **Integrity:** If even one bit of $M$ changed, then $h_2 \neq h_1$, and Bob rejects.
- **Non-repudiation:** Alice cannot later claim "I didn't sign it" because producing $\sigma$ requires $K_{priv}^{A}$, which only she has. (A symmetric MAC would *not* give non-repudiation, since Bob also has the key.)

### 3.2 Derivative Analysis 2 — Formalizing the Traffic Padding Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Question Type:** "Show mathematically how traffic padding eliminates information leakage about traffic patterns."

#### Step 1: Define the Attacker Observation
Without padding, the attacker observes a function of the real traffic $R(t)$:

$$
O(t) = f(R(t)) + \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is channel noise. The attacker can estimate $R(t)$ to learn *when* activity occurs.

#### Step 2: Define the Padding Function
The padding mechanism adds a compensatory term $P(t)$ such that:

$$
O'(t) = f(R(t) + P(t))
$$

#### Step 3: Constant-Rate Enforcement
The mechanism chooses $P(t)$ to make the observed output constant:

$$
R(t) + P(t) = R_{max} \quad \forall t
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(t) = R_{max} - R(t)
$$

#### Step 4: Attacker Information Leakage (Shannon's Information Theory)
The mutual information between the attacker's observation and the real traffic:

$$
I(O'; R) = H(R) - H(R \mid O')
$$

For constant $O'(t) = R_{max}$ (deterministic), the conditional entropy $H(R \mid O') = H(R)$. Hence:

$$
I(O'; R) = H(R) - H(R) = 0
$$

> **Conclusion:** Under ideal constant-rate padding, **zero** information is leaked about $R(t)$ — perfect defeat of traffic-flow analysis. This is the formal KTU expected proof.

### 3.3 Derivative Analysis 3 — The Access Control Matrix and its Decompositions

The **Access Matrix** $M$ is a fundamental concept in computer security theory (Lampson 1971 — part of KTU Module 1 background).

#### Step 1: Formal Definition
Let $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$ be the set of *subjects* (users, processes). Let $\mathcal{O} = \{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_m\}$ be the set of *objects* (files, devices, services).

The access matrix is:

$$
M = [m_{ij}]_{n \times m} \quad \text{where } m_{ij} \subseteq \{r, w, x, d, \ldots\}
$$

representing the *rights* subject $S_i$ has on object $O_j$.

#### Step 2: Row Decomposition — Capability List
The $i$-th row of $M$, denoted $C_i$, is the *capability list* of subject $S_i$:

$$
C_i = [m_{i1}, m_{i2}, \ldots, m_{im}]
$$

A capability is a "ticket" — possessing it grants access. Used in **distributed systems** and **Unix file descriptors**.

#### Step 3: Column Decomposition — Access Control List (ACL)
The $j$-th column of $M$, denoted $L_j$, is the *access control list* of object $O_j$:

$$
L_j = [m_{1j}, m_{2j}, \ldots, m_{nj}]^T
$$
