$$\log_2(5) + \log_2(7) \equiv 16 + 6 = 22 \pmod{18} \equiv 4 \pmod{18}$$

**Step D — Verify directly**:
$$5 \times 7 = 35 \equiv 35 - 19 = 16 \pmod{19}$$

So we need $\log_2(16)$. From our table, $2^4 \equiv 16 \pmod{19}$. Thus $\log_2(16) = 4$.

Since $35 \equiv 16 \pmod{19}$:
$$\log_2(35) \equiv \log_2(16) = 4 \pmod{18}$$

The identity holds: $16 + 6 \equiv 4 \pmod{18}$. $\checkmark$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Change of Base Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Given $\log_2(5) \equiv 16 \pmod{18}$, compute $\log_3(5)$ using the change of base formula.

We first need $\log_2(3) = 13$ (from the table in Example 2). Then:

$$\log_3(5) \equiv (13)^{-1} \cdot \log_2(5) \pmod{18}$$

Compute the modular inverse of $13$ mod $18$:
$$13 \times k \equiv 1 \pmod{18}$$

By the Extended Euclidean Algorithm or direct testing:
$$13 \times 7 = 91 = 5 \times 18 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{18}$$

So $13^{-1} \equiv 7 \pmod{18}$.

Therefore:
$$\log_3(5) \equiv 7 \times 16 = 112 \pmod{18} \equiv 112 - 6 \times 18 = 112 - 108 = 4 \pmod{18}$$

**Verification**: $3^4 = 81 = 4 \times 19 + 5 \equiv 5 \pmod{19}$. $\checkmark$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When is DLP Hard or Easy?

| Condition | DLP Difficulty | Algorithm to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $p-1$ has only small prime factors (B-smooth) | **EASY** | Pohlig-Hellman algorithm |
| $p-1$ has one large prime factor | **HARD** | Index Calculus / Number Field Sieve |
| $p$ is small ($< 2^{60}$) | **EASY** | Brute force / baby-step giant-step |
| $p \ge 2^{2048}$ with $p-1 = 2q$, $q$ prime | **HARD (secure)** | None known in polynomial time |
| Group is elliptic curve $\mathbb{E}(\mathbb{F}_p)$ | **VERY HARD** | Pollard's rho ($O(\sqrt{n})$ only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (from KTU 2024 syllabus)**: For a prime $p$ such that $p-1$ has no factors smaller than $2^{160}$, the DLP is considered secure for 80-bit security level. Modern systems use primes where $p-1 = 2q$ with $q$ a Sophie Germain prime.

### 3.5 Computation Using Square-and-Multiply (Forward Direction)

To compute $\alpha^x \bmod p$ efficiently (the easy direction), we use the **square-and-multiply** algorithm in $O(\log_2 x)$ multiplications:

**Example**: Compute $2^{13} \bmod 19$

Binary of $13$ = $1101_2$ (MSB to LSB).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } a &= 2 \quad (\text{from leftmost bit } 1) \\
\text{Step 2: } a &= 2^2 = 4 \quad (\text{square; next bit } 1 \Rightarrow \text{multiply: } 4 \times 2 = 8) \\
\text{Step 3: } a &= 8^2 = 64 \equiv 7 \pmod{19} \quad (\text{square; next bit } 0 \Rightarrow \text{no multiply}) \\
\text{Step 4: } a &= 7^2 = 49 \equiv 11 \pmod{19} \quad (\text{square; next bit } 1 \Rightarrow \text{multiply: } 11 \times 2 = 22 \equiv 3 \pmod{19}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Final result: $2^{13} \equiv 3 \pmod{19}$. $\checkmark$ (matches our earlier table)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Discrete Logarithm — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: prime p, generator alpha, target h] --> B{DLP is Hard?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Used in Public-Key Cryptography]
    B -- No --> D[Insecure System - Pohlig-Hellman Risk]
    C --> E[Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange]
    C --> F[ElGamal Encryption]
    C --> G[Digital Signature Algorithm]
    C --> H[Elliptic Curve Cryptography]
    D --> I[Regenerate Prime with Safe Properties]
    I --> J[Require p-1 to have only large factors]
    J --> K[New Secure System]
    A --> L[Forward Operation]
    L --> M[alpha power x mod p = h]
    M --> N[Square and Multiply Algorithm]
    N --> O[Easy: O log x steps]
    A --> P[Reverse Operation]
    P --> Q[Find x given alpha, h, p]
    Q --> R[Sub-exponential Difficulty]
    R --> S[Hard for Large Cryptographic Primes]
    M --> T[Output h]
    Q --> U[Output x = dlog alpha of h]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of DLP in a Cryptographic Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENDER[Sender Alice] --> SA[Private Key xA in 1 to p-2]
    SA --> CA[Compute YA = alpha power xA mod p]
    CA --> PUBKEYA[Public Key YA]
    PUBKEYA --> CHAN[Insecure Public Channel]
    CHAN --> RECV[Receiver Bob]
    RECV --> SB[Private Key xB in 1 to p-2]
    SB --> CB[Compute YB = alpha power xB mod p]
    CB --> PUBKEYB[Public Key YB]
    PUBKEYB --> CHAN2[Insecure Public Channel]
    CHAN2 --> SENDER
    CA --> SECRET1[Shared Secret K = YB power xA mod p]
    CB --> SECRET2[Shared Secret K = YA power xB mod p]
    SECRET1 --> MATCH[Both compute same K]
    SECRET2 --> MATCH
    MATCH --> SECURE[Secure Symmetric Channel Established]
    EVE[Eavesdropper Eve] -.-> CHAN
    EVE -.-> CHAN2
    EVE -.-> DLP[DLP Instance: find xA from YA]
    DLP -.-> HARD[Computationally Infeasible for Large p]
    HARD -.-> SECURE
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Algorithm Hierarchy for Solving DLP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Discrete Logarithm Problem]
    TOP --> EXH[Exhaustive Search: O p]
    TOP --> BSGS[Baby-Step Giant-Step: O square-root p]
    TOP --> PH[Pohlig-Hellman: O sum of p-i]
    TOP --> IC[Index Calculus: sub-exponential]
    TOP --> NFS[Number Field Sieve: L p format]
    EXH --> LIMIT1[Feasible only for p under 2 to 30]
    BSGS --> LIMIT2[Feasible for p under 2 to 60]
    PH --> COND1[Effective when p-1 is smooth]
    IC --> COND2[Effective in Z asterisk p]
    NFS --> COND3[Best classical algorithm for large p]
    COND1 --> SAFECHK[Safe Prime Requirement]
    SAFECHK --> SOPHIE[p-1 = 2q with q prime]
    SOPHIE --> SEC[Secure DLP-based System]
    COND2 --> SEC
    COND3 --> LARGE[Required p over 2 to 2048]
    LARGE --> SEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide**: In Diagram 4.1, the asymmetry between the **forward direction** (easy, polynomial time) and the **reverse direction** (hard, sub-exponential) is the foundation. In Diagram 4.2, observe that an eavesdropper sees the public keys $Y_A$ and $Y_B$ but cannot recover $x_A$ or $x_B$ without solving DLP. In Diagram 4.3, the **safe prime condition** ($p - 1 = 2q$, $q$ prime) is what neutralizes the Pohlig-Hellman attack and is the modern standard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. State the three parameters involved and explain why DLP is considered a one-way function.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Discrete Logarithm Problem states that given a finite cyclic group $\mathbb{G}$ of order $n$, a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{G}$, and an element $h \in \mathbb{G}$, we must find the unique integer $x$ (where $0 \le x \le n-1$) such that $g^x \equiv h \pmod{n}$. The three parameters are: (i) the **prime modulus** $p$, (ii) the **generator** $\alpha$, and (iii) the **target element** $\beta$.

DLP is a **one-way function** because computing $g^x \bmod p$ in the forward direction is computationally easy (polynomial-time using square-and-multiply), but finding $x$ from $(g, h, p)$ in the reverse direction is believed to be computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes, as no polynomial-time classical algorithm is known. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** State and explain any **three properties** of discrete logarithms in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Multiplicative-to-Additive Conversion**: $\log_\alpha(\beta \gamma) \equiv \log_\alpha(\beta) + \log_\alpha(\gamma) \pmod{p-1}$. Multiplication in the group becomes addition in the exponent space. **(1 mark)**
2. **Power Rule**: $\log_\alpha(\beta^k) \equiv k \cdot \log_\alpha(\beta) \pmod{p-1}$. Scalar multiplication in the exponent is preserved. **(1 mark)**
3. **Change of Base**: $\log_{\alpha^k}(\beta) \equiv k^{-1} \cdot \log_\alpha(\beta) \pmod{p-1}$, where $k^{-1}$ is computed modulo $p-1$ and requires $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently reduce exponents modulo $p$ instead of modulo $p-1$. The correct reduction is **always mod $p-1$** because $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ has order $p-1$ (Fermat's Little Theorem). Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — DLP Computation and Properties

**(a)** Given $p = 23$, $\alpha = 5$ (which is a primitive root modulo 23), compute the **discrete logarithms** $\log_5(3)$ and $\log_5(7)$ modulo 23. Show all your working. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

First, generate the table of powers of $5 \bmod 23$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^1 &\equiv 5 \pmod{23} \\
5^2 &\equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\
5^3 &\equiv 10 \pmod{23} \\
5^4 &\equiv 50 \equiv 4 \pmod{23} \\
5^5 &\equiv 20 \pmod{23} \\
5^6 &\equiv 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \times 23 = 8 \pmod{23} \\
5^7 &\equiv 40 \equiv 17 \pmod{23} \\
5^8 &\equiv 85 \equiv 85 - 3 \times 23 = 16 \pmod{23} \\
5^9 &\equiv 80 \equiv 80 - 3 \times 23 = 11 \pmod{23} \\
5^{10} &\equiv 55 \equiv 9 \pmod{23} \\
5^{11} &\equiv 45 \equiv 22 \pmod{23} \\
5^{12} &\equiv 110 \equiv 110 - 4 \times 23 = 18 \pmod{23} \\
5^{13} &\equiv 90 \equiv 90 - 3 \times 23 = 21 \pmod{23} \\
5^{14} &\equiv 105 \equiv 105 - 4 \times 23 = 13 \pmod{23} \\
5^{15} &\equiv 65 \equiv 19 \pmod{23} \\
5^{16} &\equiv 95 \equiv 95 - 4 \times 23 = 3 \pmod{23} \quad \longleftarrow \text{Found } 3! \\
5^{17} &\equiv 15 \pmod{23} \\
5^{18} &\equiv 75 \equiv 6 \pmod{23} \\
5^{19} &\equiv 30 \equiv 7 \pmod{23} \quad \longleftarrow \text{Found } 7! \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results:**
- $\log_5(3) = 16 \pmod{22}$ **(1 mark for each step, 4 marks total for table)**
- $\log_5(7) = 19 \pmod{22}$ **(3 marks for results)**

---

**(b)** Using the values from part (a), verify the **multiplicative property** of discrete logarithms by computing $\log_5(21)$ in **two different ways**: (i) directly from the table, and (ii) using the identity $\log_5(21) \equiv \log_5(3) + \log_5(7) \pmod{22}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Method 1 — Direct Lookup**:
From the table: $5^{13} \equiv 21 \pmod{23}$.
Therefore, $\log_5(21) = 13 \pmod{22}$. **(3 marks)**

**Method 2 — Multiplicative Property**:
Note that $3 \times 7 = 21$. Using the identity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_5(21) &\equiv \log_5(3 \times 7) \pmod{22} \\
&\equiv \log_5(3) + \log_5(7) \pmod{22} \\
&\equiv 16 + 19 \pmod{22} \\
&\equiv 35 \pmod{22} \\
&\equiv 13 \pmod{22}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks for derivation steps)**

**Verification**: Both methods yield $\log_5(21) = 13 \pmod{22}$, confirming the multiplicative property. **(1 mark for concluding statement)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Computational Hardness and Cryptographic Applications

**(a)** Explain the **Pohlig-Hellman algorithm** for computing discrete logarithms. Under what condition on the prime $p$ does this algorithm become effective, and how is this condition addressed in modern cryptographic systems? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Pohlig-Hellman algorithm** is a method to compute discrete logarithms in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ that exploits the factorization of $p-1$. **(1 mark)**

**Working Principle**:
1. Factor $p-1$ into prime powers: $p-1 = p_1^{e_1} \cdot p_2^{e_2} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$.
2. For each prime factor $p_i$, compute the discrete logarithm modulo $p_i^{e_i}$ using the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)**.
3. Combine the partial results via CRT to obtain $\log_\alpha \beta \bmod p-1$.

The complexity is dominated by the largest prime factor of $p-1$, namely $O\left(\sum_i e_i (\log p + \sqrt{p_i})\right)$. **(3 marks)**

**Effective Condition**:
The algorithm is **highly effective** when $p-1$ has only small prime factors (i.e., $p-1$ is **B-smooth** for some small bound $B$). In such cases, the DLP becomes tractable even for large $p$. **(1 mark)**

**Modern Mitigation**:
Cryptographic systems use **safe primes**, where $p$ is chosen such that $p-1 = 2q$ with $q$ also prime. This forces the largest prime factor of $p-1$ to be on the order of $p/2$, making Pohlig-Hellman computationally infeasible. Examples include the primes used in **Diffie-Hellman** (RFC 3526) and **DSA**. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b)** Describe the **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol** based on the discrete logarithm problem. List all messages exchanged and show mathematically that both parties derive the same shared secret. Explain why an eavesdropper cannot compute the shared secret. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Public Parameters**: A large prime $p$ and a primitive root $\alpha$ (generator) modulo $p$ are agreed upon and made public. **(0.5 marks)**

**Protocol Steps**:

1. **Alice** chooses a private key $x_A$ where $1 \le x_A \le p-2$, and computes her public key: $y_A = \alpha^{x_A} \bmod p$. **(0.5 marks)**

2. **Bob** chooses a private key $x_B$ where $1 \le x_B \le p-2$, and computes his public key: $y_B = \alpha^{x_B} \bmod p$. **(0.5 marks)**

3. Alice sends $y_A$ to Bob over the (insecure) public channel. Bob sends $y_B$ to Alice. **(0.5 marks)**

4. **Alice computes the shared secret**:
$$K_A = y_B^{x_A} \bmod p = (\alpha^{x_B})^{x_A} \bmod p = \alpha^{x_A x_B} \bmod p$$
**(1.5 marks)**

5. **Bob computes the shared secret**:
$$K_B = y_A^{x_B} \bmod p = (\alpha^{x_A})^{x_B} \bmod p = \alpha^{x_A x_B} \bmod p$$
**(1.5 marks)**

Since $K_A = K_B = \alpha^{x_A x_B} \bmod p$, both parties derive the **same shared secret**. **(0.5 marks)**

**Why an Eavesdropper Cannot Compute the Secret**:
An eavesdropper Eve observes $p$, $\alpha$, $y_A$, and $y_B$. To compute the shared secret, she needs to determine $x_A$ or $x_B$. From $y_A = \alpha^{x_A} \bmod p$, finding $x_A$ is precisely the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**, which is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large $p$ (e.g., 2048-bit prime) under current classical computing capabilities. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B)**: Two common 2-mark deductions in this type of question:
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the range of private keys** ($1 \le x \le p-2$, not $[0, p-1]$ — otherwise $x = 0$ gives trivial secret = 1).
> 2. **Not justifying the security claim** with reference to DLP hardness — merely stating "Eve cannot compute" without referencing DLP loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Discrete Logarithms**

- ✅ **DLP Definition**: Find $x$ such that $\alpha^x \equiv \beta \pmod{p}$ in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
- ✅ **Group Order**: $|\mathbb{Z}_p^*| = p - 1$ (cyclic for prime $p$).
- ✅ **Order of Element**: Smallest $k$ such that $a^k \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, and $k \mid p-1$.
- ✅ **Primitive Root (Generator)**: An element of order $p-1$.
- ✅ **Three Critical Properties**: (1) $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$ (mod $p-1$), (2) $\log(a^k) = k \log a$ (mod $p-1$), (3) Change of base formula.
- ✅ **Reduction Modulo**: Always reduce exponents modulo $p-1$, **never** modulo $p$.
- ✅ **Pohlig-Hellman Vulnerability**: Exploits smooth $p-1$; mitigated by **safe primes** ($p - 1 = 2q$, $q$ prime).
- ✅ **Best Classical Attack**: Number Field Sieve — sub-exponential time.
- ✅ **Recommended Key Size**: $\ge 2048$ bits for $p$ (128-bit security).
- ✅ **Computational Asymmetry**: Forward ($O(\log x)$ square-and-multiply) is easy; reverse is hard.
- ✅ **Cryptographic Applications**: Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, DSA, ECC.
- ✅ **Fermat's Little Theorem**: $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for $\gcd(a,p) = 1$ — the algebraic foundation for all mod-$p-1$ reductions.
- ✅ **Square-and-Multiply**: Standard $O(\log_2 x)$ algorithm for forward computation.
- ✅ **Verification Habit**: Always verify $x$ by recomputing $\alpha^x \bmod p$ (catches arithmetic errors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip**: In KTU 2024 scheme, questions on this topic often appear as **Part A 3-markers** (definitions, properties) or **Part B 14-markers** (computation + application). For computational questions, present tables clearly with column headers; for theoretical questions, always state assumptions (e.g., $p$ is prime, $\alpha$ is generator) explicitly at the start.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Cryptography - Kerckhoff's Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Cryptography - Kerckhoff's Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerckhoff's Principle (1883):** *A cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system — except the secret key — is publicly known.* In other words, the security of a cryptosystem must depend **solely on the secrecy of the key**, not on the secrecy of the algorithm itself.

This principle was first articulated by the Dutch cryptographer **Auguste Kerckhoffs von Nieuwenhof** in his landmark 1883 paper *"La Cryptographie Militaire"* published in the *Journal des Sciences Militaires*. Kerckhoffs proposed **six axioms of military cryptography**, of which the second axiom became immortalized as **Kerckhoff's Principle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reformulation (Shannon's Maxim):** Claude Shannon later restated the same idea as — *"The enemy knows the system."* This is often called **Shannon's Maxim** and is the cornerstone of modern cryptographic design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **house with a very strong lock**.

- The **algorithm** = the *design* of the lock (how the bolt, pins, and tumblers are arranged).
- The **key** = the *physical key* that opens the lock.

Now consider two approaches:

| Approach | House Analogy | Cryptographic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bad Design (Security through Obscurity)** | You hide the lock inside a sealed box, hoping burglars never find it. | You keep the algorithm secret, hoping attackers never reverse-engineer it. |
| **Kerckhoff's Approach** | You publish the lock's full blueprint in a newspaper. Even if a burglar knows *exactly* how the lock works, they still cannot open the door **without the key**. | You publish the algorithm. Security rests *only* on the key. |

**Why is publishing the algorithm actually *safer*?**
Because the algorithm then gets *peer-reviewed* by thousands of expert cryptanalysts worldwide. Bugs and weaknesses are discovered early. A secretly-designed lock that nobody has tested might look impressive — but is it really unbreakable? You don't know. A publicly-tested lock is *proven* reliable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world examples that obey Kerckhoff's Principle:**
> - **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — algorithm is a public FIPS standard (FIPS 197).
> - **RSA** — algorithm is openly published and used everywhere.
> - **TLS 1.3** — the entire handshake protocol is a public RFC.
> - **Bitcoin's SHA-256** — hashing function is fully public, yet the system is secure.

### 1.3 Original Six Principles of Kerckhoffs (1883)

For historical completeness, here are all six axioms Kerckhoffs proposed for a military cryptosystem:

1. The system must be **practically, if not mathematically, indecipherable**.
2. **It must not be required to be secret**, and it must be able to fall into the hands of the enemy without inconvenience.
3. Its key must be **communicable and retainable** without the help of written notes, and changeable or modifiable at the will of the correspondents.
4. It must be **applicable to telegraphic correspondence**.
5. It must be **portable**, and its usage and function must not require the concourse of several people.
6. Finally, given the circumstances that condition its application, the system must be **easy to use**, requiring neither mental strain nor the knowledge of a long series of rules to observe.

> The **second principle** is what is universally known today as *Kerckhoff's Principle*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Algorithm** $\mathcal{A}$ — the public mathematical transformation function.
- **Key** $k \in \mathcal{K}$ — the secret parameter drawn from the keyspace $\mathcal{K}$.
- **Keyspace** $|\mathcal{K}|$ — total number of possible keys (size in bits).
- **Ciphertext** $c = E_k(m)$ — encryption of plaintext $m$ under key $k$.
- **Plaintext** $m = D_k(c)$ — decryption of ciphertext $c$ under key $k$.
- **Work Factor** — the minimum computational effort (e.g., $2^{128}$ operations) required to break the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposing the Principle into Logical Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the components of a cryptosystem.**
A modern cryptosystem $\Pi = (\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{K}, E, D)$ is composed of:
- An algorithm family $\mathcal{A}$ (publicly known).
- A keyspace $\mathcal{K}$ (set of all possible keys).
- An encryption function $E_k : \mathcal{M} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$.
- A decryption function $D_k : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$.

**Step 2 — Distinguish what is public vs. secret.**
- **Public domain** (per Kerckhoff): $\mathcal{A}, E, D, \mathcal{K}$ structure.
- **Secret domain** (per Kerckhoff): the specific key $k$ in use during a session.

**Step 3 — State the security condition formally.**
For an attacker (eavesdropper / cryptanalyst) who knows:
- The plaintext-ciphertext pairs $(m_i, c_i)$,
- The full algorithm specification,
- Even the source code,

… the system remains secure **iff** recovering $k$ (or any $m$ not in the known set) is computationally infeasible.

**Step 4 — Translate to engineering reality.**
This means the algorithm must be **openly published**, audited, standardized, and subjected to years of public cryptanalysis. Examples: **AES** standardized by NIST, **SHA-256** standardized by NSA/NIST, **ChaCha20** standardized by IETF RFC 8439.

### 2.2 The "Why" — Engineering Rationale

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Verifiability** | Public algorithms can be peer-reviewed. A secret algorithm has *no* external validation. |
| **Longevity** | Algorithms are deployed for decades. Secret algorithms risk obsolescence if reverse-engineered. |
| **Interoperability** | Multiple parties need to communicate using the *same* algorithm — it must be standardized. |
| **Key Management** | Keys can be rotated, revoked, and distributed; algorithms cannot (without breaking compatibility). |
| **Trust** | You trust an algorithm that has survived 20 years of public attack, not a closed proprietary one. |

### 2.3 The "How" — Implementation Requirements

To honour Kerckhoff's Principle, a system must:

1. **Publish** the algorithm in a formal specification (RFC, FIPS, IEEE standard).
2. **Generate keys** from a high-entropy source (e.g., CSPRNG).
3. **Rotate keys** periodically to limit exposure.
4. **Use key lengths** large enough to resist brute force (e.g., **128 bits** for symmetric, **2048+ bits** for RSA).
5. **Subject the algorithm** to continuous public cryptanalysis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encryption function | $c = E_k(m)$ | Plaintext $m$ becomes ciphertext $c$ under key $k$ | — |
| Decryption function | $m = D_k(c)$ | Reverse transformation | — |
| Correctness property | $D_k(E_k(m)) = m$ | Fundamental identity of any cipher | — |
| Keyspace size | $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | Number of possible keys | $2^{128}$ for AES-128 |
| Brute-force work factor | $W = 2^{\vert k \vert}$ | Avg. trials to find key by exhaustive search | $2^{127}$ for AES-128 |
| Avalanche effect | $\Delta H \geq 50\%$ | 1-bit input change → 50% output change | SHA-256 property |
| Shannon entropy of key | $H(k) = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ bits | Information content of the key | $\geq 128$ bits |
| Effective key bits (RSA) | $\text{equiv} \approx \log_2(n)$ | RSA-2048 $\approx$ 112-bit symmetric | NIST SP 800-57 |
| Public-Key strength (EC) | $\text{equiv} \approx n/2$ | secp256r1 $\approx$ 128-bit symmetric | NIST curves |
| One-time pad work factor | $W = \infty$ (perfect secrecy) | Unbreakable if key is truly random & used once | Shannon 1949 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"What does Kerckhoff's Principle state?"*, always give the **two-part** answer: *(1) the algorithm is public, AND (2) only the key is secret*. Many students lose marks by stating only one half.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kerckhoff's Principle is the **governing philosophy** behind:
- **Open Standards** — TLS, IPsec, SSH, S/MIME, PGP.
- **NIST Competitions** — AES (1997–2001) and SHA-3 (2007–2012) were *open competitions*.
- **Post-Quantum Cryptography** — NIST's PQC standardization (2022–2024) selected Kyber, Dilithium, etc., all publicly evaluated.
- **Open-source crypto libraries** — OpenSSL, BoringSSL, libsodium — code is public, security is in the keys.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Brute-Force Work Factor Under Kerckhoff's Assumption

**Goal:** Quantify the effort an attacker needs to break a cipher when the algorithm is *known* (per Kerckhoff) and only the key is secret.

**Given:**
- Cipher is a *substitution cipher* on a keyspace of size $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$.
- Attacker has ciphertext $c$ and known plaintext-ciphertext pair $(m_0, c_0)$.
- Algorithm $E_k(\cdot)$ is fully public.

**Step 1 — Express the brute-force attack as a search problem.**

The attacker iterates over all candidate keys $k_i \in \mathcal{K}$:

$$k^* = \underset{k \in \mathcal{K}}{\arg\min} \; \text{dist}\big(E_k(m_0), \, c_0\big)$$

i.e., the attacker finds the key that maps $m_0$ to $c_0$ correctly.

**Step 2 — Determine the search space cardinality.**

If keys are binary strings of length $n$ bits, the keyspace size is:

$$\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^n$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average number of trials.**

On average, an attacker finds the correct key after checking half the keyspace:

$$W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}{2} = 2^{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to known secure key lengths.**

| Algorithm | Key length $n$ (bits) | Work factor $2^{n-1}$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| DES | 56 | $2^{55} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{16}$ | **Broken** (broken in 1998 EFF) |
| 3DES | 168 (effective 112) | $2^{111}$ | Legacy / Deprecated |
| AES-128 | 128 | $2^{127}$ | **Secure** (recommended) |
| AES-256 | 256 | $2^{255}$ | **Secure** (high-grade) |
| RSA-2048 | 2048 (asymmetric equiv $\sim 112$) | $\sim 2^{112}$ (best known: GNFS) | **Secure** (until $\sim$ 2030) |

**Step 5 — Conclude with the security condition.**

Kerckhoff's Principle is satisfied **iff** the work factor $W_{\text{avg}}$ is computationally infeasible (e.g., $W_{\text{avg}} \geq 2^{128}$) for any attacker with realistic resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** Under Kerckhoff's assumption (algorithm is public), the *only* parameter controlling security is the **key length $n$**. This is why modern ciphers obsess over key size — it is the *single* knob available to increase security.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Kerckhoff vs. Security-through-Obscurity

| Dimension | Kerckhoff's Approach (Open) | Security-through-Obscurity (Closed) |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm** | Public | Secret |
| **Key** | Secret | Secret |
| **Audit** | Public cryptanalysis (years) | Internal testing (limited) |
| **Reverse-engineering risk** | None — already public | High — single leak = total compromise |
| **Trust model** | Trust the math & community | Trust the vendor |
| **Failure mode** | Slow erosion of key strength | Catastrophic single-point failure |
| **Examples** | AES, RSA, SHA-256, TLS | DVD CSS, proprietary "secure" phones, custom hash functions |
| **Longevity** | Decades (e.g., RSA since 1977) | Often years before being broken |
| **Standards body** | NIST, IETF, IEEE | None (vendor-controlled) |
| **Key rotation** | Easy & standard | Difficult, often impossible |
| **Compliance** | FIPS, Common Criteria | None |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A company uses an encryption algorithm with a 64-bit key. Per Kerckhoff's Principle, the algorithm is public. Compute the average brute-force work factor. Is it acceptable under 2024 NIST guidelines?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the key length.**

$$n = 64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the keyspace size.**

$$\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{64} = 1.844 \times 10^{19}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average work factor.**

$$W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{64}}{2} = 2^{63} \approx 9.22 \times 10^{18}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NIST SP 800-131A (2024 revision).**

NIST recommends a **minimum of 112 bits** of security for legacy use and **128 bits** for new systems. A 64-bit key provides only $2^{63}$ work factor, far below the threshold.

**Step 5 — Verdict.**

> [!WARNING]
> **NOT acceptable.** 64-bit keys are brute-forceable on commodity hardware in days. Migrate to AES-128 or AES-256 (or RSA-2048+ for asymmetric).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kerckhoff's Cryptosystem Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext m] -->|Input| B[Public Algorithm E]
    K[Secret Key k] -->|Parameter| B
    B -->|Transform| C[Ciphertext c]
    C -->|Intercepted| E[Attacker / Eavesdropper]
    E -.->|Knows algorithm E| E
    E -.->|Does NOT know key k| E
    C -->|Received| D[Public Algorithm D]
    K2[Same Secret Key k] -->|Parameter| D
    D -->|Inverse transform| F[Recovered Plaintext m]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style K fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style K2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **green blocks** (B and D) represent the *public algorithm* — known to the attacker.
- The **red blocks** (K and K2) represent the *secret key* — known only to Alice and Bob.
- The **orange block** (C) is the ciphertext — visible to the attacker in transit.
- The **red block** (E) is the attacker, who knows everything *except* the key.
- Per Kerckhoff, even with full knowledge of B, D, and C, attacker E cannot recover m without k.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kerckhoff vs. Obscurity Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KP["Kerckhoff Principle: OPEN ALGORITHM"]
        A1[Algorithm published] --> A2[Peer reviewed]
        A2 --> A3[Long-term trust]
        A2 --> A4[Key is the ONLY secret]
        A4 --> A5[Security measured in key bits]
    end
    subgraph OB["Security by Obscurity: CLOSED ALGORITHM"]
        B1[Algorithm hidden] --> B2[No external review]
        B2 --> B3[Single leak = total compromise]
        B2 --> B4[Vendor trust required]
        B4 --> B5[No independent validation]
    end
    style KP fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style OB fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style A1 fill:#b9d7a9,stroke:#6a9c45
    style A2 fill:#b9d7a9,stroke:#6a9c45
    style A3 fill:#b9d7a9,stroke:#6a9c45
    style A4 fill:#b9d7a9,stroke:#6a9c45
    style A5 fill:#b9d7a9,stroke:#6a9c45
    style B1 fill:#e6b3b3,stroke:#a04545
    style B2 fill:#e6b3b3,stroke:#a04545
    style B3 fill:#e6b3b3,stroke:#a04545
    style B4 fill:#e6b3b3,stroke:#a04545
    style B5 fill:#e6b3b3,stroke:#a04545
```

    P5 -->|High grade| P6[256 bits]
    P6 -->|Maximum standard| P7[Beyond 256 bits PQC era]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State and explain Kerckhoff's Principle. Why is it considered the foundation of modern cryptography?* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Kerckhoff's Principle, formulated by Auguste Kerckhoffs in 1883, states that *"a cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is publicly known."* In modern terminology (Shannon's Maxim), the security of a cipher must rest *entirely* on the secrecy of the cryptographic key — not on the secrecy of the algorithm.

**Justification (1.5 marks):** It is the foundation of modern cryptography because:
1. **Open algorithms** can be peer-reviewed by the global cryptographic community, exposing weaknesses before deployment.
2. **Standardization** (e.g., AES, RSA) requires public specifications for interoperability.
3. **Keys can be rotated**; algorithms cannot be easily changed after deployment.
4. **Trust** is placed in mathematically-proven schemes, not in vendor promises.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *"the algorithm is public"* without adding *"and security depends only on the key."* Examiners will deduct 1 mark if the *key-secret* condition is missing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Kerckhoff's Principle and "Security through Obscurity." Give one example for each approach.* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Kerckhoff's Principle | Security through Obscurity |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm secrecy | Publicly known | Kept secret |
| Key secrecy | Secret | Secret |
| Trust basis | Math + community review | Vendor's promise |
| Failure mode | Gradual key wear-out | Sudden total compromise |

**Examples (1.5 marks):**
- **Kerckhoff's approach:** AES (FIPS 197 public standard), RSA (published algorithm).
- **Security through obscurity:** DVD CSS encryption (broken in 1999, DeCSS); proprietary "military-grade" phones with closed algorithms (multiple such products compromised in under a year by academic researchers).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the six principles proposed by Auguste Kerckhoffs for military cryptography. Highlight which of them is universally known as "Kerckhoff's Principle" today. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A symmetric cipher uses a 128-bit key. Under Kerckhoff's Principle, the algorithm is public. Calculate:
   (i) The size of the keyspace.
   (ii) The average work factor for a brute-force key search.
   (iii) Comment on whether this key length is acceptable as per current NIST SP 800-131A guidelines. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Principles of Kerckhoffs (7 marks — 1 mark each, with 1 mark for identifying Principle 2 as the famous one):**

1. **Indecipherability:** The system must be *practically* (not just mathematically) indecipherable against known attack types.

2. **No Secrecy Required (Kerckhoff's Principle):** The system must not require secrecy, and it can fall into enemy hands without causing inconvenience. This is the famous one — *only the key needs to be secret.*

3. **Key Portability:** The key must be easily communicable, retainable, and changeable — without needing written notes or external help.

4. **Telegraphic Applicability:** The system must be applicable to telegraphic correspondence (i.e., suited to the communication medium of the time — modern: must work over digital networks).

5. **Single-Person Usability:** The system must be portable and operable by a single person, not require multiple operators.

6. **Ease of Use:** It must be simple to operate without mental strain or memorization of long rule sets.

**Identification (1 mark):** The **second principle** — that the system should remain secure even if publicly known — is the one universally known as *Kerckhoff's Principle*.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Keyspace size (2 marks):**

Given key length $n = 128$ bits, the keyspace is:

$$\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{128} = 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$$

**(ii) Average work factor (3 marks):**

A brute-force attacker tests keys until the correct one is found. On average, half the keyspace is checked:

$$W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{128}}{2} = 2^{127} \approx 1.7014 \times 10^{38}$$

This is $\approx 1.7 \times 10^{38}$ AES evaluations — far beyond the capability of any classical computer, even using all GPUs on Earth for the age of the universe.

**(iii) NIST compliance comment (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **128-bit symmetric key = fully compliant with NIST SP 800-131A (2024).** NIST mandates a *minimum* of 112 bits for legacy and 128 bits for new deployments. 128-bit AES is the *gold standard* for symmetric encryption as of 2024 and is expected to remain secure beyond 2050 against classical (non-quantum) attacks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain Kerckhoff's Principle in detail. Why should the algorithm be made public? List two real-world cryptographic systems that strictly follow this principle. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare and contrast Kerckhoff's Principle with Shannon's Maxim. How does the concept of *computational security* differ from *information-theoretic security*? Give one example of each. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kerckhoff's Principle — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Kerckhoff's Principle states that a cryptographic system should be designed such that *all details of the algorithm* — including its implementation, design, and even its source code — can be made public without compromising the system's security. The only component that must remain secret is the **cryptographic key** $k$.

**Why publish the algorithm? (3 marks — 1 mark each point):**

1. **Peer Review & Cryptanalysis:** Public algorithms are scrutinized by thousands of researchers. Weaknesses are discovered *before* widespread deployment, not after a catastrophic breach. AES survived 20+ years of such attacks.

2. **Standardization & Interoperability:** Open algorithms can be standardized by bodies like NIST, IETF, and IEEE, ensuring that vendors and users worldwide can interoperate seamlessly.

3. **Trust in Mathematics, Not Vendors:** Trust is placed in publicly-verified math, not in the goodwill of a company. Closed systems have failed catastrophically (DVD CSS, HDCP, proprietary cell-phone encryption).

**Two real-world examples (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — FIPS 197 (2001). The full algorithm, including the S-box, shift rows, and MixColumns operations, is a public standard. Yet AES-128 is considered unbreakable by classical means.

2. **RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977)** — The algorithm, including modular exponentiation $c = m^e \bmod n$ and the public/private key generation, is published in textbooks worldwide. Security rests on the difficulty of factoring large primes.

*(Honourable mentions: SHA-256, ChaCha20, Ed25519, TLS 1.3.)*

---

**(b) Comparison & Security Models (7 marks):**

**Kerckhoff vs. Shannon (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kerckhoff's Principle | Shannon's Maxim |
|---|---|---|
| Formulated by | Auguste Kerckhoffs (1883) | Claude Shannon (1949) |
| Domain | Cryptographic design philosophy | Information-theoretic security |
| Stated as | "Algorithm can be public" | "The enemy knows the system" |
| Focus | Open design & standardization | Adversarial information model |
| Relationship | **Shannon formalized Kerckhoff's idea** mathematically using entropy and redundancy. |

Both principles assert the *same* underlying idea: **security must not depend on the secrecy of the algorithm**. Shannon, however, provided the *mathematical framework* (entropy, perfect secrecy, redundancy) to reason about it rigorously.

**Computational vs. Information-Theoretic Security (4 marks — 2 each):**

- **Computational Security:** Security relies on *assumptions about computational hardness* (e.g., factoring large integers is hard). Breaking the system is *infeasible* with current technology, not impossible in principle. **Example:** AES-128, RSA-2048.

- **Information-Theoretic (Perfect) Security:** Security holds even against an attacker with *unbounded computational power*. The ciphertext reveals *zero* information about the plaintext. **Example:** One-Time Pad (OTP) with a truly random key as long as the message, used only once.

| Property | Computational | Information-Theoretic |
|---|---|---|
| Adversary power | Bounded (polynomial time) | Unbounded |
| Security basis | Hardness assumption (e.g., factoring) | Information theory (entropy) |
| Key length | Short (e.g., 128 bits) | At least as long as message |
| Practical use | Yes (AES, RSA) | Limited (OTP — key distribution hard) |
| Quantum threat | Vulnerable (RSA, ECC) | Still secure (OTP) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Kerckhoff's Principle Questions:**
>
> 1. **Half-defined principle (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing only *"the algorithm is public"* without specifying *"and only the key is secret."* Always include both halves.
> 2. **Confusing Kerckhoffs with Shannon (–1 mark):** They are *related* but not *identical*. Kerckhoffs gave the *design philosophy* (1883); Shannon gave the *mathematical formalism* (1949).
> 3. **Ignoring computational vs. information-theoretic distinction (–2 marks):** A 14-mark question on security models *always* tests this. If the question mentions Kerckhoff + security, discuss both models.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals (–1 mark):** Always state work factor as $2^{n-1}$ and convert to decimal form for clarity. Mention the comparison to NIST thresholds.
> 5. **Giving non-algorithmic examples (–1 mark):** Use *cryptographic* examples (AES, RSA, SHA), not generic ones (locks, safes).
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" (–1 mark):** Examiners want engineering rationale — *why* is open design better? Always list 2–3 reasons.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Kerckhoff's Principle**

- ⭐ **Year:** 1883, by **Auguste Kerckhoffs** in *"La Cryptographie Militaire"*.
- ⭐ **Core statement:** *Algorithm public, key secret* — both halves required.
- ⭐ **Shannon's Maxim (1949):** *"The enemy knows the system."*
- ⭐ **Six original principles:** only the **2nd** is the famous one (open design).
- ⭐ **Opposite philosophy:** *Security through obscurity* (e.g., DVD CSS — broken).
- ⭐ **Examples following it:** AES (FIPS 197), RSA, SHA-256, ChaCha20, TLS 1.3.
- ⭐ **Key equation:** $W_{\text{avg}} = 2^{n-1}$ (brute-force work factor).
- ⭐ **NIST minimum (2024):** 128 bits symmetric / RSA-2048+ / secp256r1+.
- ⭐ **DES (56-bit):** **BROKEN** — do not use in exam examples of "secure" ciphers.
- ⭐ **One-Time Pad:** Only cipher achieving *information-theoretic* security (Shannon 1949).
- ⭐ **Two security models:**
  - *Computational* (e.g., AES, RSA) — depends on hardness assumptions.
  - *Information-theoretic* (e.g., OTP) — secure against any attacker.
- ⭐ **Why publish?**
  1. Peer review
  2. Standardization
  3. Key rotation
  4. Trust in math, not vendor
- ⭐ **Exam mantra:** *"Algorithm public, key secret — the math must be strong enough to stand alone."*

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Cryptosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Cryptosystem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptosystem (Formal KTU Definition):** A **Cryptosystem** is a five-tuple *(P, C, K, E, D)* where:
> - **P** = Set of all possible **Plaintexts** (the original, intelligible message)
> - **C** = Set of all possible **Ciphertexts** (the scrambled, unintelligible output)
> - **K** = Set of all possible **Keys** (the secret parameter)
> - **E** = Set of **Encryption** transformations, where each $e_k \in E$ is a bijection from P to C
> - **D** = Set of **Decryption** transformations, where each $d_k \in D$ is a bijection from C to P
> - For every key $k \in K$, there must exist a corresponding decryption key such that:
>   $$d_{k'}(e_k(m)) = m \quad \text{for all } m \in P$$

In simpler KTU-board-friendly language: A cryptosystem is the complete recipe book of mathematics, rules, and secret keys used to scramble a message so that eavesdroppers cannot read it, while the intended receiver can unscramble it.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Conceptual Hook)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Padlock and the Mailbox**
> - **Plaintext** is a love letter written on paper.
> - **Ciphertext** is the same letter placed inside a sealed steel box.
> - **Key** is the unique combination (or physical key) that opens the box.
> - **Encryption Algorithm** is the act of closing/locking the box.
> - **Decryption Algorithm** is the act of unlocking the box.
> - **Cryptanalysis** is a thief trying to guess the combination by shaking the box or trying common codes.
> - **Cryptology** = Cryptography (making locks) + Cryptanalysis (breaking locks).

The **Classification of Cryptosystems** is simply the question: *"What kind of lock are we using?"* — Are we using a single key shared between two people, or two mathematically linked keys (one public, one private)?

---

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Cryptosystems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **KTU 2024 Syllabus (Module 2)**, cryptosystems are broadly classified based on the **type of keys** used during encryption and decryption. The two master categories are:

### A. Symmetric-Key Cryptosystems (Secret-Key / Private-Key / Conventional Cryptography)
- The **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption.
- Both sender and receiver must possess an **identical shared key**.
- It is further subdivided into:
  - **Classical Cryptosystems** (Character-level operations)
    - **Substitution Ciphers** (Caesar, Monoalphabetic, Polyalphabetic, Playfair, Hill, Vigenère, One-Time Pad)
    - **Transposition Ciphers** (Rail Fence, Columnar, Route Cipher)
  - **Modern Cryptosystems** (Bit-level / Block-level operations)
    - **Block Ciphers** (DES, 3DES, AES — process fixed-size blocks, e.g., 64 or 128 bits)
    - **Stream Ciphers** (RC4, Salsa20 — process one bit or byte at a time)

### B. Asymmetric-Key Cryptosystems (Public-Key Cryptography)
- Uses a **mathematically linked pair of keys**: a **Public Key** for encryption and a **Private Key** for decryption.
- The public key is freely published; the private key is kept secret.
- Examples: **RSA, Diffie-Hellman, Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), ElGamal, Digital Signature Standard (DSS)**.

### C. Other Classification Dimensions
- **Based on Plaintext Processing:** Block Cipher vs Stream Cipher
- **Based on Operation Type:** Substitution vs Transposition
- **Based on Key Usage:** Symmetric (1 key) vs Asymmetric (2 keys)
- **Based on Era:** Classical (paper-and-pencil) vs Modern (computer-based)

---

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The classification is **not academic trivia** — it directly governs:
> 1. **Performance** — Symmetric is ~1000× faster than Asymmetric (measured in **megabytes per second throughput**).
> 2. **Key Distribution** — Asymmetric solves the "key exchange problem" of symmetric systems.
> 3. **Use-Case Selection** — Block ciphers are used for file storage; stream ciphers are used for real-time voice/video (e.g., **TLS 1.3**, **WPA3**).
> 4. **Security Guarantees** — Asymmetric enables digital signatures and non-repudiation; symmetric cannot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Cryptosystem Class

### 2.1.1 Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem — Operational Logic

In a symmetric cryptosystem, the encryption and decryption keys are **identical** ($K_e = K_d = K$) or one is **trivially derivable** from the other.

**Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic):**
1. Alice and Bob agree on a secret key **K** over a **secure channel** (this is the famous *key distribution problem*).
2. Alice computes ciphertext $C = E_K(M)$ using an agreed algorithm **E** (e.g., AES).
3. Alice transmits **C** over an **insecure channel** (e.g., the Internet).
4. Bob, who already holds **K**, computes plaintext $M = D_K(C)$ using the inverse algorithm **D**.
5. An eavesdropper (Eve) sees **C** but cannot derive **M** without **K** within computationally feasible time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The *security* of the entire system rests entirely on **keeping K secret**. If K is leaked, the system collapses instantly — there is no fallback.

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric-Key Cryptosystem — Operational Logic

In a public-key cryptosystem, every user possesses a **key pair**:
- A **Public Key** $K_U^+$ (advertised to the world, e.g., uploaded to a certificate authority).
- A **Private Key** $K_U^-$ (kept secret, never transmitted).

**Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic):**
1. Bob generates a key pair and publishes $K_B^+$.
2. Alice fetches Bob's public key $K_B^+$ from a trusted directory.
3. Alice encrypts: $C = E_{K_B^+}(M)$ using Bob's public key.
4. Alice transmits **C** to Bob.
5. Bob decrypts: $M = D_{K_B^-}(C)$ using his private key.
6. Eve sees **C** but cannot decrypt it because deriving $K_B^-$ from $K_B^+$ requires solving a **computationally hard problem** (integer factorization for RSA, discrete logarithm for Diffie-Hellman, elliptic curve discrete logarithm for ECC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Trapdoor Concept:** The public key $K_B^+$ is the *easy* direction of a one-way function; the private key $K_B^-$ is the *trapdoor* that inverts it. Without the trapdoor, inversion is computationally infeasible (requires more than $10^{20}$ operations for a sufficiently large key).

### 2.1.3 Block Cipher vs Stream Cipher — A Critical Sub-Classification

| Dimension | Block Cipher | Stream Cipher |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing Unit** | Fixed-size block (typically **64 bits** for DES, **128 bits** for AES) | Single bit or single byte at a time |
| **Key Stream** | Does not generate a keystream; uses the same key for each block | Generates a pseudorandom **keystream** via a PRNG (Pseudo-Random Number Generator) |
| **Speed** | Slower per bit; high diffusion | Extremely fast, ideal for **real-time streaming** |
| **Error Propagation** | Errors propagate across the entire block | Limited propagation — single bit error affects only that bit |
| **Mode of Operation** | ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR, GCM (required for security) | Direct keystream XOR |
| **Classic Examples** | DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, Twofish | RC4, ChaCha20, Salsa20, SEAL |
| **Primary Use** | File encryption, database encryption, disk encryption | SSL/TLS sessions, VoIP (Voice over IP), wireless (WPA2/WPA3) |

### 2.1.4 Substitution vs Transposition — Classical Sub-Classification

- **Substitution Cipher:** Each element of the plaintext (bit, letter, group of bits) is **mapped to another element** of the ciphertext alphabet. The position remains unchanged; only the symbol changes. Examples: Caesar, Vigenère, Playfair, Hill.
- **Transposition Cipher:** The plaintext elements are **rearranged (permuted)** in position. The symbols themselves remain the same; only their locations change. Examples: Rail Fence, Columnar, Route Cipher.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern symmetric ciphers (e.g., AES) are built by **iteratively combining** substitution (S-boxes) and transposition (P-boxes) in multiple rounds — this is called a **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mathematical core** of cryptosystem classification. These formulas appear in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions every year.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Caesar Cipher Encryption | $C = (M + K) \mod 26$ | Shift cipher with key $K$ |
| 2 | Caesar Cipher Decryption | $M = (C - K) \mod 26$ | Recover plaintext from ciphertext |
| 3 | Multiplicative Cipher | $C = (M \cdot K) \mod 26$ | Requires $\gcd(K, 26) = 1$ for invertibility |
| 4 | Affine Cipher | $C = (aM + b) \mod 26$ | Combines additive and multiplicative; requires $\gcd(a, 26) = 1$ |
| 5 | Hill Cipher Encryption | $C = K \cdot P \pmod{26}$ | Matrix multiplication; $K$ is an $n \times n$ key matrix |
| 6 | Hill Cipher Decryption | $P = K^{-1} \cdot C \pmod{26}$ | Requires $\det(K)$ to be invertible mod 26 |
| 7 | Vigenère Cipher | $C_i = (M_i + K_i) \mod 26$ | Polyalphabetic; key repeats cyclically |
| 8 | One-Time Pad (OTP) | $C_i = M_i \oplus K_i$ | XOR with a truly random key of length $\vert M \vert$; **provably secure** |
| 9 | Stream Cipher (XOR) | $C_i = M_i \oplus \text{Keystream}_i$ | Bitwise XOR with pseudorandom keystream |
| 10 | Block Cipher Iteration | $C_j = E_K(P_j)$ | Each block encrypted independently (ECB) or chained (CBC) |
| 11 | RSA Encryption | $C = M^e \mod n$ | Public key $(e, n)$ |
| 12 | RSA Decryption | $M = C^d \mod n$ | Private key $d$ where $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| 13 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes $p$ and $q$ |
| 14 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | For RSA; counting integers coprime to $n$ |
| 15 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = \alpha^{X_A X_B} \mod q$ | Computed independently by both parties |
| 16 | Key Space (Brute-Force Resistance) | $\vert K \vert = 2^k$ | For a $k$-bit key, adversary must try $2^k$ keys on average |
| 17 | Kerckhoffs's Principle | Algorithm public, key secret | Security depends ONLY on the key, not the algorithm's secrecy |
| 18 | Information-Theoretic Security | $H(M \vert C) = H(M)$ | Perfect secrecy; ciphertext reveals zero information |
| 19 | Computational Security | Breaking requires $\geq 2^{80}$ operations | Practical standard for modern ciphers |
| 20 | Avalanche Effect | $\geq 50\%$ bits change in C | One-bit change in M or K must cascade through the cipher |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each class is used in industry:**
> - **AES (Symmetric Block)** — Used by **WhatsApp, Signal, TLS 1.3, BitLocker disk encryption, WPA3 Wi-Fi**.
> - **ChaCha20 (Symmetric Stream)** — Used by **Google QUIC protocol, OpenSSH, Tor network** for high-speed mobile encryption.
> - **RSA (Asymmetric)** — Used in **digital certificates (X.509), PGP email, SSH key exchange, JWT signing**.
> - **ECC (Asymmetric)** — Used in **Bitcoin/Ethereum, Apple iMessage, TLS 1.3 ephemeral keys** — provides equivalent security to RSA with **6× shorter keys**.
> - **Diffie-Hellman (Asymmetric)** — Used for **forward-secret key agreement** in TLS, IPSec, SSH.

The choice between symmetric and asymmetric is **never "either-or"** — modern systems use **hybrid cryptosystems** (e.g., RSA to securely exchange a temporary AES key, then AES encrypts the bulk data). This is the foundation of every HTTPS connection you make.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical walkthroughs** of the classification examples. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — **no skips, no shortcuts**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Caesar Cipher End-to-End

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext **"HELLO"** using Caesar Cipher with key **K = 3**.

### Step 1 — Map Letters to Numeric Values (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25)

| Plaintext Letter | H | E | L | L | O |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Numeric Value (M) | 7 | 4 | 11 | 11 | 14 |

### Step 2 — Apply Encryption Formula $C = (M + K) \mod 26$

For each plaintext character $M_i$, compute $C_i = (M_i + 3) \mod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (7 + 3) \mod 26 = 10 \mod 26 = 10 \\
C_2 &= (4 + 3) \mod 26 = 7 \mod 26 = 7 \\
C_3 &= (11 + 3) \mod 26 = 14 \mod 26 = 14 \\
C_4 &= (11 + 3) \mod 26 = 14 \mod 26 = 14 \\
C_5 &= (14 + 3) \mod 26 = 17 \mod 26 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each step:** We add the key value (3) to the plaintext value, then take the remainder when divided by 26. This ensures the result stays in the valid range 0–25 (i.e., wrapped around the alphabet).

### Step 3 — Map Numeric Ciphertext Back to Letters

| Ciphertext Number | 10 | 7 | 14 | 14 | 17 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ciphertext Letter | K | H | O | O | R |

### Step 4 — Result

$$\boxed{\text{Plaintext "HELLO"} \xrightarrow{K=3} \text{Ciphertext "KHOOR"}}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Decryption Round-Trip

Apply $M = (C - K) \mod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (10 - 3) \mod 26 = 7 \mod 26 = 7 \quad \rightarrow \text{H} \\
M_2 &= (7 - 3) \mod 26 = 4 \mod 26 = 4 \quad \rightarrow \text{E} \\
M_3 &= (14 - 3) \mod 26 = 11 \mod 26 = 11 \quad \rightarrow \text{L} \\
M_4 &= (14 - 3) \mod 26 = 11 \mod 26 = 11 \quad \rightarrow \text{L} \\
M_5 &= (17 - 3) \mod 26 = 14 \mod 26 = 14 \quad \rightarrow \text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✓ Decryption successfully recovers the original plaintext "HELLO" — confirming the cipher's correctness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hill Cipher (2×2 Matrix Key)

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext **"HI"** using the Hill cipher with key matrix:

$$
K = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The plaintext "HI" maps to numeric vector $P = \begin{pmatrix} H \\ I \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$.

### Step 1 — Confirm Key Invertibility mod 26

Compute $\det(K) = (3)(5) - (3)(2) = 15 - 6 = 9$.

Check $\gcd(9, 26) = 1$ ✓ (since 9 and 26 share no common factors other than 1). Therefore, the inverse exists.

### Step 2 — Apply Encryption $C = K \cdot P \pmod{26}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
  &= \begin{pmatrix} (3)(7) + (3)(8) \\ (2)(7) + (5)(8) \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
  &= \begin{pmatrix} 21 + 24 \\ 14 + 40 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
  &= \begin{pmatrix} 45 \\ 54 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply mod 26 to each element:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 45 \mod 26 = 45 - 26 = 19 \\
C_2 &= 54 \mod 26 = 54 - 52 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So $C = \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} T \\ C \end{pmatrix}$ (since T=19, C=2 in A=0 notation).

### Step 3 — Result

$$\boxed{\text{Plaintext "HI"} \xrightarrow{K} \text{Ciphertext "TC"}}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Inverse Key $K^{-1}$ for Decryption

The inverse of a 2×2 matrix $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ mod $n$ is:

$$
K^{-1} = (\det K)^{-1} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix} \pmod{n}
$$

Compute $(\det K)^{-1} = 9^{-1} \mod 26$. We need $x$ such that $9x \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$.

By the Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $9 \cdot 3 = 27 = 26 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. So $9^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod{26}$.

Now compute the inverse matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^{-1} &= 3 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
       &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 & -9 \\ -6 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
       &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 \mod 26 & -9 \mod 26 \\ -6 \mod 26 & 9 \mod 26 \end{pmatrix} \\
       &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify by multiplication** $K \cdot K^{-1} \pmod{26}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \cdot K^{-1} &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
               &= \begin{pmatrix} 45+60 & 51+27 \\ 30+100 & 34+45 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
               &= \begin{pmatrix} 105 & 78 \\ 130 & 79 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
               &= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Decrypt to Verify

$$
P = K^{-1} \cdot C = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= (15)(19) + (17)(2) = 285 + 34 = 319 \mod 26 = 319 - 12 \cdot 26 = 319 - 312 = 7 \\
P_2 &= (20)(19) + (9)(2) = 380 + 18 = 398 \mod 26 = 398 - 15 \cdot 26 = 398 - 390 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} H \\ I \end{pmatrix}$. ✓ Decryption successfully recovers "HI".

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RSA Key Generation and Encryption

**Problem:** Generate RSA keys using $p = 5$ and $q = 11$. Encrypt the message $M = 9$. (Toy example for KTU board.)

### Step 1 — Compute the Modulus $n$

$$
n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

$$
\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (5 - 1)(11 - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40
$$

**Reasoning:** $\phi(n)$ counts the number of integers between 1 and $n$ that are coprime to $n$. For $n = pq$ with $p,q$ prime, only multiples of $p$ and $q$ are excluded, giving $(p-1)(q-1)$.

### Step 3 — Choose Public Exponent $e$

Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.

Test $e = 3$: $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$ ✓ (valid). So $e = 3$.

**Public Key** = $(e, n) = (3, 55)$.

### Step 4 — Compute Private Exponent $d$

We need $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &= 13 \cdot 3 + 1 \\
3 &= 3 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substitute: $1 = 40 - 13 \cdot 3$, so $-13 \cdot 3 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, which means $d \equiv -13 \equiv 27 \pmod{40}$.

**Verify:** $3 \cdot 27 = 81 = 2 \cdot 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ ✓

**Private Key** = $(d, n) = (27, 55)$.

### Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 9$

$$
C = M^e \mod n = 9^3 \mod 55
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
9^2 &= 81 = 1 \cdot 55 + 26 \equiv 26 \pmod{55} \\
9^3 &= 9 \cdot 26 = 234 = 4 \cdot 55 + 14 \equiv 14 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{C = 14}$$

### Step 6 — Decrypt $C = 14$

$$
M = C^d \mod n = 14^{27} \mod 55
$$

We use successive squaring to compute $14^{27} \mod 55$:

Express 27 in binary: $27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1 = 11011_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
14^1 \mod 55 &= 14 \\
14^2 \mod 55 &= 196 \mod 55 = 196 - 3 \cdot 55 = 196 - 165 = 31 \\
14^4 \mod 55 &= 31^2 = 961 \mod 55 = 961 - 17 \cdot 55 = 961 - 935 = 26 \\
14^8 \mod 55 &= 26^2 = 676 \mod 55 = 676 - 12 \cdot 55 = 676 - 660 = 16 \\
14^{16} \mod 55 &= 16^2 = 256 \mod 55 = 256 - 4 \cdot 55 = 256 - 220 = 36
\end{aligned}
$$

Now combine: $14^{27} = 14^{16} \cdot 14^{8} \cdot 14^{2} \cdot 14^{1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
14^{27} \mod 55 &= (36 \cdot 16 \cdot 31 \cdot 14) \mod 55 \\
                &= (36 \cdot 16) \mod 55 = 576 \mod 55 = 576 - 10 \cdot 55 = 26 \\
                &= (26 \cdot 31) \mod 55 = 806 \mod 55 = 806 - 14 \cdot 55 = 806 - 770 = 36 \\
                &= (36 \cdot 14) \mod 55 = 504 \mod 55 = 504 - 9 \cdot 55 = 504 - 495 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 9} \quad \checkmark \text{ Original plaintext recovered}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strengths and Weaknesses by Classification

| Classification Type | Strengths | Weaknesses | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Substitution (Monoalphabetic)** | Simple to implement | Vulnerable to frequency analysis; key space only 26! | Caesar, Atbash |
| **Substitution (Polyalphabetic)** | Defeats frequency analysis | Vulnerable to Kasiski examination | Vigenère |
| **Transposition** | Hides statistical patterns | Weak alone; needs combination with substitution | Rail Fence, Columnar |
| **One-Time Pad** | Provably unbreakable (information-theoretic security) | Key must be truly random, as long as message, never reused | OTP |
| **Stream Cipher** | Fast, low latency, suitable for real-time | Requires secure synchronization; weak if keystream reused | RC4, ChaCha20 |
| **Block Cipher (ECB mode)** | Simple, parallelizable | Reveals patterns in identical plaintext blocks (the famous **ECB Penguin**) | AES-ECB |
| **Block Cipher (CBC mode)** | Hides patterns via IV and chaining | Sequential encryption; padding oracle attacks possible | AES-CBC |
| **Asymmetric (RSA)** | Solves key distribution, enables digital signatures | Very slow (~1000× slower than AES); large key sizes (2048+ bits) | RSA-2048 |
| **Asymmetric (ECC)** | Smaller keys, faster than RSA, lower power consumption | Still slower than symmetric; complex implementation | Curve25519, secp256k1 |
| **Hybrid (Real-World)** | Best of both worlds: speed + key exchange | More complex protocol; multiple failure points | TLS 1.3, PGP |

> **Reading Guide:** The root node `rootA` represents the universe of all cryptosystems. It branches into the two principal families (`rootB`, `rootC`) and an additional classification dimension (`rootD`). Each terminal leaf is a concrete algorithm you may be asked to identify in a KTU exam.

---

## 4.2 Symmetric Cryptosystem — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    aliceA[Alice - Sender] --> keyA[Shared Secret Key K]
    bobB[Bob - Receiver] --> keyA
    aliceA -->|Plaintext M| encA[Encryption Algorithm E]
    keyA -->|Key K| encA
    encA -->|Ciphertext C| chanA[Insecure Channel]
    chanA --> decA[Decryption Algorithm D]
    keyA -->|Key K| decA
    decA -->|Plaintext M| bobB
    chanA -.->|Eve intercepts C| eveA[Eve - Attacker]
    eveA -.->|Cannot compute K| failA[Attack Fails]
```

> **Key Takeaway:** The single secret key **K** is used on both sides. The eavesdropper can see the ciphertext on the channel, but without K, decryption is computationally infeasible.

---

## 4.3 Asymmetric Cryptosystem — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    bobB1[Bob - Receiver] --> keyPair[Key Pair Generation]
    keyPair --> pubB[Public Key of Bob]
    keyPair --> privB[Private Key of Bob - SECRET]
    pubB --> dirA[Public Directory]
    aliceA1[Alice - Sender] -->|Fetch Public Key| dirA
    aliceA1 -->|Plaintext M| encB[Encryption using Bob Public Key]
    pubB -->|Public Key| encB
    encB -->|Ciphertext C| chanB[Insecure Channel]
    chanB --> decB[Decryption using Bob Private Key]
    privB -->|Private Key| decB
    decB -->|Plaintext M| bobB1
```

> **Key Takeaway:** Encryption uses Bob's **public** key (which everyone can see), but decryption requires Bob's **private** key (which only Bob possesses). Eve, even if she intercepts the ciphertext, cannot decrypt it.

---

## 4.4 Block Cipher vs Stream Cipher — Processing Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    ptA[Plaintext] --> ptB{How to Process?}

    ptB -->|Fixed Size Blocks| bcA[Block Cipher]
    bcA --> bcB[Block 1 of 64 bits]
    bcA --> bcC[Block 2 of 64 bits]
    bcA --> bcD[Block N of 64 bits]
    bcB --> bcE[Encryption with Key K]
    bcC --> bcF[Encryption with Key K]
    bcD --> bcG[Encryption with Key K]
    bcE --> bcH[Ciphertext Blocks]
    bcF --> bcH
    bcG --> bcH

    ptB -->|Bit by Bit| scA[Stream Cipher]
    scA --> scB[Keystream Generator with Key K]
    scB --> scC[Keystream Bit 1]
    scB --> scD[Keystream Bit 2]
    scB --> scE[Keystream Bit N]
    ptA --> scF[Bit 1]
    ptA --> scG[Bit 2]
    ptA --> scH[Bit N]
    scF --> scI[XOR Operation]
    scC --> scI
    scI --> scJ[Ciphertext Bit 1]
    scG --> scK[XOR Operation]
    scD --> scK
    scK --> scL[Ciphertext Bit 2]
    scH --> scM[XOR Operation]
    scE --> scM
    scM --> scN[Ciphertext Bit N]
```

> **Key Takeaway:** Block ciphers encrypt **chunks of data** with the same key; stream ciphers generate a **pseudorandom keystream** and XOR it bit-by-bit with the plaintext.

---

## 4.5 Hybrid Cryptosystem — Real-World Architecture (TLS 1.3)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Client Wants Secure Session] --> asymA[Step 1 - Asymmetric Phase]
    asymA --> rsaA[Client generates random AES key]
    rsaA --> rsaB[Encrypts AES key using Server RSA Public Key]
    rsaB --> rsaC[Sends encrypted AES key to Server]
    rsaC --> symA[Step 2 - Symmetric Phase]
    symA --> aesA[Server decrypts using RSA Private Key]
    aesA --> aesB[Recovers shared AES key]
    aesB --> aesC[Both parties now share AES key K]
    aesC --> bulkA[Step 3 - Bulk Data Transfer]
    bulkA --> bulkB[All further data encrypted with AES-256 in GCM mode]
    bulkB --> bulkC[Fast, authenticated, low latency]
```

> **Key Takeaway:** Real systems like HTTPS use **asymmetric cryptography only to exchange a symmetric key**, then use that symmetric key for all bulk data. This is called a **hybrid cryptosystem** and it gives you the best of both worlds — secure key exchange *and* high-speed encryption.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. KTU expects a crisp 3–4 sentence answer with a defining formula or diagram.

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define a cryptosystem. List and briefly explain its three main classifications based on key type.**

**Model Answer:**

A cryptosystem is a five-tuple $(P, C, K, E, D)$ representing plaintext, ciphertext, key space, encryption transformations, and decryption transformations, such that $D_K(E_K(M)) = M$ for all $M \in P$.

The three main classifications based on key type are:

1. **Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem** — Same secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption. Examples: AES, DES. Fast but suffers from key distribution problem.

2. **Asymmetric-Key Cryptosystem** — Uses a mathematically linked key pair: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. Examples: RSA, ECC. Solves the key distribution problem and enables digital signatures, but is slow.

3. **Hybrid Cryptosystem** — Combines both: asymmetric cryptography securely exchanges a symmetric session key, which then encrypts bulk data. Examples: TLS 1.3, PGP, SSH.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Block Ciphers and Stream Ciphers with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Block Cipher | Stream Cipher |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing Unit** | Encrypts a fixed-size block (e.g., **64 bits for DES**, **128 bits for AES**) | Encrypts one bit or one byte at a time |
| **Key Stream** | No keystream; same key applied to each block | Generates a pseudorandom keystream via a PRNG |
| **Speed** | Slower per byte | Faster, suitable for real-time streams |
| **Error Propagation** | One bit error corrupts the entire block | Single bit error affects only that bit |
| **Examples** | DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish | RC4, ChaCha20, Salsa20 |

The fundamental mathematical operation in a stream cipher is the XOR: $C_i = M_i \oplus K_i$, while a block cipher applies a complex non-linear transformation $C = E_K(P)$ to each block.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. KTU provides an internal choice — attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. We present both options below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the classification of cryptosystems based on the type of keys used. Compare symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography in terms of speed, key distribution, and security.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification Based on Key Type:**

The classification of cryptosystems based on the type of keys used is broadly divided into two categories:

**(i) Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem (Private-Key / Secret-Key):**
In this system, the same key $K$ is shared between the sender and the receiver. The encryption function $E_K$ and decryption function $D_K$ use the same or trivially related key:

$$C = E_K(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_K(C)$$

Examples: **DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, RC4, ChaCha20**.

It is further sub-classified into:
- **Block Ciphers** (process 64/128-bit blocks): AES, DES
- **Stream Ciphers** (process bit-by-bit): RC4, ChaCha20

**(ii) Asymmetric-Key Cryptosystem (Public-Key):**
This system uses a pair of mathematically linked keys — a public key $K^+$ for encryption and a private key $K^-$ for decryption:

$$C = E_{K^+}(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_{K^-}(C)$$

Examples: **RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, ECC**.

**2. Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Keys** | 1 shared key | 2 keys (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (e.g., AES: ~1 Gbps on hardware) | Very slow (e.g., RSA-2048: ~1000 ops/sec) |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult — must be shared secretly | Easy — public key can be freely distributed |
| **Key Length** | 128–256 bits sufficient | 2048+ bits (RSA), 256+ bits (ECC) |
| **Number of Keys for $n$ Users** | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ pairs | $2n$ keys total |
| **Provides Confidentiality** | Yes | Yes |
| **Provides Authentication / Digital Signature** | No (since both share the same key, neither can prove uniqueness) | Yes (private key is unique) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Not possible | Possible |
| **Example Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman |

**3. Key Takeaway:**
- Symmetric is preferred for **bulk data encryption** due to speed.
- Asymmetric is preferred for **key exchange and digital signatures** due to security properties.
- Real-world systems use a **hybrid approach** (e.g., TLS) to get both benefits.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With suitable examples, explain the difference between substitution ciphers and transposition ciphers. Apply the Caesar cipher (K = 7) to encrypt the plaintext "NETWORK" and show the decryption process.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Substitution vs Transposition Ciphers:**

A **substitution cipher** replaces each element of the plaintext with another element, while keeping the **position unchanged**. In contrast, a **transposition cipher** rearranges the **positions** of plaintext elements, while keeping the symbols themselves unchanged.

| Parameter | Substitution Cipher | Transposition Cipher |
|---|---|---|
| **Operation** | Replace symbols with other symbols | Rearrange the order of symbols |
| **Preserves** | Position of characters | Identity of characters |
| **Examples** | Caesar, Vigenère, Playfair, Hill | Rail Fence, Columnar, Route |
| **Frequency Analysis** | Vulnerable (monoalphabetic) | Hides letter frequencies |
| **Modern Usage** | S-boxes in AES | P-boxes in AES, DES |

**2. Caesar Cipher Encryption of "NETWORK" with K = 7:**

**Step 1: Map letters to numbers (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25):**

| Letter | N | E | T | W | O | R | K |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number | 13 | 4 | 19 | 22 | 14 | 17 | 10 |

**Step 2: Apply encryption $C = (M + 7) \mod 26$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (13 + 7) \mod 26 = 20 \mod 26 = 20 \\
C_2 &= (4 + 7) \mod 26 = 11 \mod 26 = 11 \\
C_3 &= (19 + 7) \mod 26 = 26 \mod 26 = 0 \\
C_4 &= (22 + 7) \mod 26 = 29 \mod 26 = 3 \\
C_5 &= (14 + 7) \mod 26 = 21 \mod 26 = 21 \\
C_6 &= (17 + 7) \mod 26 = 24 \mod 26 = 24 \\
C_7 &= (10 + 7) \mod 26 = 17 \mod 26 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Map back to letters:**

| Number | 20 | 11 | 0 | 3 | 21 | 24 | 17 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Letter | U | L | A | D | V | Y | R |

**Ciphertext: "ULADVYR"**

**Step 4: Decryption using $M = (C - 7) \mod 26$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (20 - 7) \mod 26 = 13 \rightarrow N \\
M_2 &= (11 - 7) \mod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E \\
M_3 &= (0 - 7) \mod 26 = -7 \mod 26 = 19 \rightarrow T \\
M_4 &= (3 - 7) \mod 26 = -4 \mod 26 = 22 \rightarrow W \\
M_5 &= (21 - 7) \mod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O \\
M_6 &= (24 - 7) \mod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R \\
M_7 &= (17 - 7) \mod 26 = 10 \rightarrow K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decrypted Plaintext: "NETWORK"** ✓

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the concept of a hybrid cryptosystem. How does it overcome the individual weaknesses of symmetric and asymmetric systems? Describe a real-world example.**

**Model Solution:**

A **hybrid cryptosystem** combines the strengths of both symmetric and asymmetric cryptography to overcome their individual weaknesses. It is the **de facto standard** used in nearly all modern secure communication protocols (TLS, SSH, PGP, S/MIME).

**1. The Individual Weaknesses:**

- **Symmetric Cryptography** is fast but suffers from the **key distribution problem**: how do you securely share a secret key with the receiver over an insecure channel? If the channel is insecure, the key can be intercepted.
- **Asymmetric Cryptography** solves key distribution but is **computationally slow** — RSA is roughly 1000× slower than AES. Encrypting gigabytes of data with RSA is impractical.

**2. How a Hybrid System Works (Step-by-Step):**

**Phase 1 — Asymmetric Key Exchange (Slow but Secure):**
1. The receiver publishes their **asymmetric public key** (e.g., RSA-2048) in a public directory.
2. The sender generates a **random symmetric session key** $K_S$ (e.g., a 256-bit AES key).
3. The sender encrypts the session key using the receiver's public key: $C_{K_S} = E_{K_{R}^+}(K_S)$.
4. The sender transmits $C_{K_S}$ to the receiver.

**Phase 2 — Symmetric Bulk Encryption (Fast):**
5. The receiver decrypts the session key using their private key: $K_S = D_{K_R^-}(C_{K_S})$.
6. Both parties now share the same symmetric key $K_S$ without ever sending it in plaintext.
7. All subsequent bulk data $M$ is encrypted using AES (or ChaCha20) with key $K_S$.

**3. Why This Works:**

- **Confidentiality of the session key** is guaranteed by the asymmetric encryption (which is computationally hard to break).
- **Speed** is achieved by using the fast symmetric cipher for the bulk data.
- **Forward secrecy** can be added using ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (as in TLS 1.3) so that compromising the long-term private key does not compromise past sessions.

**4. Real-World Example — TLS 1.3 (HTTPS):**

Every time you visit `https://www.google.com`, your browser and Google's server execute the following hybrid protocol:
- **Handshake:** ECDHE (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral) is used to agree on a shared AES-256 key.
- **Authentication:** RSA or ECDSA is used to digitally sign the handshake, proving Google's identity.
- **Bulk Transfer:** All HTTP data is encrypted using **AES-256-GCM** (symmetric) for speed and authenticated encryption.

This is why HTTPS can secure a 4K video stream in real time without noticeable lag — the slow asymmetric step happens only once per session.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Generate the RSA public and private keys for $p = 7$, $q = 13$, and $e = 5$. Then encrypt the message $M = 10$ and verify the decryption.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the modulus $n$.**

$$n = p \cdot q = 7 \times 13 = 91$$

**Step 2: Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$.**

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (7-1)(13-1) = 6 \times 12 = 72$$

**Step 3: Verify the public exponent $e = 5$.**

Check that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = \gcd(5, 72) = 1$ ✓ (since 5 is prime and does not divide 72). Therefore $e = 5$ is a valid public exponent.

**Public Key** = $(e, n) = (5, 91)$.

**Step 4: Compute the private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.**

We need $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{72}$. Use the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
72 &= 14 \cdot 5 + 2 \\
5 &= 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \\
2 &= 2 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substitute to express 1 as a combination of 5 and 72:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 5 - 2 \cdot 2 \\
  &= 5 - 2 \cdot (72 - 14 \cdot 5) \\
  &= 5 - 2 \cdot 72 + 28 \cdot 5 \\
  &= 29 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 72
\end{aligned}
$$

So $29 \cdot 5 \equiv 1 \pmod{72}$, which gives $d = 29$.

**Verify:** $5 \cdot 29 = 145 = 2 \cdot 72 + 1 = 145$, and $145 \mod 72 = 1$ ✓

**Private Key** = $(d, n) = (29, 91)$.

**Step 5: Encrypt the message $M = 10$ using $C = M^e \mod n$.**

$$
C = 10^5 \mod 91
$$

Compute step-by-step using successive squaring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^1 \mod 91 &= 10 \\
10^2 \mod 91 &= 100 \mod 91 = 9 \\
10^4 \mod 91 &= 9^2 = 81 \mod 91 = 81
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $10^5 = 10^4 \cdot 10^1 = 81 \cdot 10 = 810 \mod 91$:

$$810 = 8 \cdot 91 + 82 = 728 + 82 = 810 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 810 \mod 91 = 82$$

**Ciphertext: $C = 82$**.
