# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List the different types of cryptanalytic attacks on DES. State the complexity of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
DES is vulnerable to three primary cryptanalytic attacks:
1. **Exhaustive Key Search (Brute Force)** — Tries all 2^56 possible keys. Average complexity is **2^55** DES operations. (1 mark)
2. **Differential Cryptanalysis** — A chosen-plaintext attack requiring **2^47 chosen plaintext–ciphertext pairs** with complexity **2^37 DES operations**. (1 mark)
3. **Linear Cryptanalysis** — A known-plaintext attack by Matsui (1993) requiring **2^43 known plaintext pairs** with complexity **2^43 DES operations**. (1 mark)

Additionally, DES has **4 weak keys** and **12 semi-weak keys** (and 48 possibly weak keys) — but these have astronomically low probability (1 in 2^52), so they are not practical threats.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is a Meet-in-the-Middle attack? Why does it break 2-DES?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A **Meet-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is a *time–memory tradeoff* attack in which the attacker:
- **Phase 1 (Forward):** Encrypts the known plaintext **P** with all 2^56 candidates of K1, producing intermediate values **X = E_{K1}(P)**, and stores them in a sorted table. (1 mark)
- **Phase 2 (Backward):** Decrypts the ciphertext **C** with all 2^56 candidates of K2, producing **Y = D_{K2}(C)**, and looks up **Y** in the table. (1 mark)

Any match **(X = Y)** reveals a candidate key pair **(K1, K2)**. The total time is **2^57** and storage is **2^56**, which means 2-DES has effective security of **only 2^57** — *not* the intuitive 2^112. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write "MITM has 2^56 time and 2^56 space, so 2-DES is as strong as 2^56." The correct answer is **2^57** because both phases are *sequential* and each takes 2^56 steps. Don't confuse "phases" with "effective key space."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-2 long-answer questions carry **14 marks** and offer *internal choice* between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)**, worth **7 marks each**. Students must answer *one* of the two choices.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **Meet-in-the-Middle attack** on **Double DES** with the help of a suitable diagram. Derive the time and space complexity.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, describe the working of **Triple DES with two keys in EDE mode**. Why is EDE used instead of EEE?

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Meet-in-the-Middle Attack on 2-DES (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Meet-in-the-Middle attack, proposed by Diffie and Hellman in 1977, is a known-plaintext cryptanalytic technique that reduces the effective security of 2-DES from the nominal 2^112 down to **2^57** by exploiting the algebraic structure C = E_{K2}(E_{K1}(P)).

**Attack Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Known Data:** The attacker is given a known plaintext–ciphertext pair (P, C) where C = E_{K2}(E_{K1}(P)).
2. **Algebraic Rearrangement:** Decrypt both sides with K2:
   $$D_{K_2}(C) = D_{K_2}(E_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P))) = E_{K_1}(P)$$
   So the *intermediate value* X = E_{K1}(P) = D_{K2}(C) is the meeting point.
3. **Phase 1 — Forward Encryption Table:** For each of the 2^56 values of K1, compute X = E_{K1}(P) and store the (X, K1) pair in a hash table indexed by X.
4. **Phase 2 — Backward Decryption Lookup:** For each of the 2^56 values of K2, compute Y = D_{K2}(C) and probe the table for Y.
5. **Match Detection:** Every match (X = Y) yields a candidate (K1, K2).
6. **False-Positive Filtering:** Verify candidates against a *second* known plaintext pair. Expected false positives per single PT = 2^48, which collapses to a single valid pair after 2 PT pairs.

**Complexity (2 marks):**

- **Time:** T = 2^56 (Phase 1) + 2^56 (Phase 2) = **2^57 DES operations**.
- **Space:** S = 2^56 entries × 64 bits = **2^62 bits = 2^59 bytes ≈ 512 PB**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** 2-DES is reduced to *single-DES-equivalent* security. Hence 2-DES is **never deployed** in production systems.

### Part (b) — Triple DES Two-Key EDE (7 Marks)

**Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The encryption of 2-key 3-DES is:
$$C = E_{K_1}\!\left(D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right)\right)$$

Operation chain (refer to mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):
- Stage 1: **Encrypt P with K1** → intermediate ciphertext A.
- Stage 2: **Decrypt A with K2** → intermediate plaintext-like B.
- Stage 3: **Encrypt B with K1** → final ciphertext C.

**Why EDE? (3 marks):**
1. **Backward Compatibility:** When K1 = K2 = K, the operation becomes E_K(D_K(E_K(P))) = E_K(P) = C, so 3-DES is *bit-for-bit identical* to single DES. EEE (Encrypt-Encrypt-Encrypt) would not have this property. (1 mark)
2. **Implementation Efficiency:** The decrypt function D in DES hardware/software is the *same circuit* as the encrypt function E, just with subkeys applied in reverse order. Hence EDE imposes *zero hardware cost* — only a flag-flip in the key schedule. (1 mark)
3. **Vendor Lock-in:** Enterprises with deployed single-DES hardware can upgrade *in place* to 3-DES without replacing their equipment. (1 mark)

**Security Properties (2 marks):**
- **Nominal key length:** 112 bits (K1, K2).
- **Effective security against MITM:** ~2^112 (MITM requires *3* known plaintexts to fully eliminate false positives, raising time to 2^112).
- **Block size:** Still 64 bits — vulnerable to **Sweet32 birthday-bound attack** after 2^32 blocks (~32 GB) under the same key.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 - Alternative Choice]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the various **cryptanalytic attacks on DES**, with emphasis on **differential cryptanalysis** and **linear cryptanalysis**. Compare their complexities.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the working of **Triple DES with three keys in EDE mode**. What is its effective security, and why has NIST deprecated 3-DES?

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Cryptanalytic Attacks on DES (7 Marks)

**Overview (1 mark):**
DES, though elegant, was designed in an era when 56-bit keys seemed unbreakable. Modern cryptanalysis has revealed multiple vulnerabilities.

**1. Exhaustive Key Search / Brute Force (2 marks):**
- Tries all 2^56 keys against a known plaintext–ciphertext pair.
- Average work: 2^55 ≈ 3.6 × 10^16 DES operations.
- The 1998 EFF "Deep Crack" achieved 90 billion keys/second, recovering a key in ~56 hours.
- Modern cloud-distributed attacks reduce this to hours.

**2. Differential Cryptanalysis (DC) (2 marks):**
- **Type:** *Chosen-plaintext* attack.
- **Inventors:** Biham and Shamir (1990).
- **Method:** Study how XOR *differences* ΔP = P1 ⊕ P2 of plaintext pairs propagate through DES rounds to produce predictable *differences* ΔC in ciphertext pairs. A *characteristic* traces a specific ΔP → ΔC path with high probability over many rounds.
- **Complexity:** 2^47 chosen plaintexts, 2^37 DES operations.
- **Key Insight:** DES's S-boxes were *deliberately* tuned by IBM/NSA in the 1970s to resist DC, raising the attack complexity to 2^47.

**3. Linear Cryptanalysis (LC) (2 marks):**
- **Type:** *Known-plaintext* attack.
- **Inventor:** Mitsuru Matsui (1993) — the *first publicly published* experimental break of DES.
- **Method:** Find a *linear approximation*:
  $$P_{a_1} \oplus P_{a_2} \oplus \dots \oplus C_{b_1} \oplus C_{b_2} \oplus \dots \oplus K_{c_1} \oplus K_{c_2} \oplus \dots = 0$$
  with probability ≠ 1/2 (the *bias* ε). Use the *Piling-Up Lemma* to chain such approximations over 16 rounds, recovering key bits statistically.
- **Complexity:** 2^43 known plaintexts, 2^43 DES operations.

**Comparison Summary (built into the above):**
| Attack | Type | Plaintexts | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | Known | 1 pair | 2^55 |
| Differential | Chosen | 2^47 | 2^37 |
| Linear | Known | 2^43 | 2^43 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** While DC and LC are *theoretically* faster than brute force, they require an *enormous* quantity of known/chosen plaintexts — which is often impractical. In real-world cloud environments, *exhaustive search* remains the dominant practical attack on DES.

### Part (b) — Triple DES Three-Key EDE Mode (7 Marks)

**Definition and Operation (2 marks):**
Three-key 3-DES uses three independent 56-bit keys, K1, K2, K3, applied in Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt order:
$$C = E_{K_3}\!\left(D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right)\right)$$

**Working Steps (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**
1. Encrypt plaintext P with key K1 (DES E-box).
2. Decrypt the result with key K2 (DES D-box).
3. Encrypt the result with key K3 (DES E-box) to produce ciphertext C.

**Effective Security Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Nominal key length:** 168 bits (3 × 56).
- **Best known attack:** Meet-in-the-Middle (Diffie–Hellman). With **3 chosen plaintexts** and **2^56 storage**, the attacker can reduce the effective security to 2^168 *time*, but the *space* requirement grows to 3 × 2^56 entries.
- **Effective security:** ~2^168, which is the highest among the 3DES family.
- When K1 = K2 = K3, three-key 3-DES collapses to single DES (backward compatibility).

**NIST Deprecation (3 marks):**
NIST formally *deprecated* 3-DES in **2017** (transition plan) and **prohibited its use in new applications after 2023** for the following reasons:
1. **Birthday-Bound (Sweet32) Attack:** The 64-bit block size means that after 2^32 blocks (~32 GB) under the same key, an attacker can detect ciphertext collisions with high probability, leading to plaintext recovery in CBC or other modes with predictable IVs. (1 mark)
2. **Throughput:** 3-DES is ~3× slower than single DES (~10–20 MB/s in software vs. ~50–100 MB/s). Modern ciphers like AES-128 achieve 1–10 GB/s. (1 mark)
3. **Brute-Force Threshold Reachable:** Though 168-bit keys are safe from classical brute force, quantum Grover's algorithm reduces security to ~2^84 — already in the "concerning" range for long-term confidentiality. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfalls (Frequently Tested Areas):**
> 1. **Confusing 2-key and 3-key 3-DES:** Students often write that 2-key 3-DES has 112-bit security under *all* attacks. The *correct* answer is that MITM reduces it to 2^57 *with one PT pair* but back to 2^112 *with three PT pairs*. The *effective* deployed security is 2^112 because real attackers cannot gather 2^56 storage easily. (1 mark lost in many scripts)
> 2. **Forgetting the Sweet32 birthday bound:** The 64-bit block size *is* a real vulnerability, and many students omit this. Always mention it. (1 mark)
> 3. **Writing EEE instead of EDE for 3-DES:** The default is EDE for backward compatibility; EEE is rare and non-backward-compatible. Examiners will deduct marks for ambiguity. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Critical):**

- **DES Key Length:** 56 bits is *broken* by modern brute force. The 1998 EFF DES Cracker was the historical proof.
- **Three Attack Families on DES:**
  - *Exhaustive Search* → 2^55 average, known-plaintext.
  - *Differential Cryptanalysis* → 2^47 chosen-plaintext (Biham–Shamir 1990).
  - *Linear Cryptanalysis* → 2^43 known-plaintext (Matsui 1993).
- **DES Key Weaknesses:**
  - **Weak keys:** 4 keys, all subkeys identical, P = 2^−52.
  - **Semi-weak keys:** 12 keys (6 pairs), 2 distinct subkeys, P = 2^−52.
  - **Possibly weak keys:** 48 keys, 4 distinct subkeys, P = 2^−48.
- **Double DES (2-DES):**
  - Encryption: C = E_{K2}(E_{K1}(P)).
  - **Intuitive security:** 2^112. **Actual security after MITM:** 2^57.
  - **MITM complexity:** T = 2^57, S = 2^56 (≈ 512 PB).
  - **Practical status:** *Never deployed* — security is no better than single DES.
- **Triple DES (3-DES):**
  - **Two-key EDE:** C = E_{K1}(D_{K2}(E_{K1}(P))). 112-bit nominal, 2^112 effective.
  - **Three-key EDE:** C = E_{K3}(D_{K2}(E_{K1}(P))). 168-bit nominal, 2^168 effective.
  - **EDE rationale:** Backward compatibility with single DES (when K1 = K2 = K) and zero hardware overhead (D-box = E-box with reversed subkeys).
  - **Drawbacks:** 64-bit block (Sweet32 attack), slow throughput, deprecated by NIST since 2023.
- **MITM Counter-Counter-Measure for 3-DES:** Three independent known/chosen plaintexts are required to force the attacker into 2^112+ time complexity. With 2-key 3-DES, MITM is *practically* infeasible at full key strength.
- **Sweet32 Threshold:** 2^32 blocks (~32 GB) under the same 3-DES key in CBC mode enables birthday-bound plaintext recovery.
- **Modern Replacement:** AES-128/192/256 (Rijndael, 128-bit block) — FIPS 197 standard since 2001.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Board-Ready Statements (use in 3-mark answers):**
> - *"DES has a 56-bit key and is broken by brute force; the EFF cracker demonstrated this in 1998."*
> - *"Meet-in-the-Middle attack reduces 2-DES security from 2^112 to 2^57, so 2-DES is never used."*
> - *"Triple DES uses EDE mode for backward compatibility with single DES and achieves 2^112 (2-key) or 2^168 (3-key) effective security."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) - Structure and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) - Structure and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric-key block cipher adopted by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS PUB 197** (November 2001) as the successor to the Data Encryption Standard (DES). AES operates on a fixed **block size of 128 bits** and supports three standardized key lengths: **128 bits, 192 bits, and 256 bits**, designated as **AES-128, AES-192, and AES-256** respectively.

AES is built upon a **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)** architecture and performs a sequence of well-defined linear and non-linear transformations on a 4×4 matrix of bytes (the **State** array). The number of transformation rounds varies with key length: **10 rounds for AES-128, 12 rounds for AES-192, and 14 rounds for AES-256**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AES is a mandatory topic under Module 2 of PCITT601. Students must be able to diagram the round structure, explain the four transformations (SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey), and derive the key schedule algorithm for full marks in ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a **sealed diplomatic pouch** through ten security checkpoints. At each checkpoint, the guard performs three actions:

1. **Substitution (SubBytes):** He rewrites every coded character using a secret bilingual dictionary — a non-English character never maps predictably to an English one.
2. **Shuffling (ShiftRows):** He rotates the rows of the document so that the spatial position of every word changes, breaking any positional patterns.
3. **Mixing (MixColumns):** He combines columns of the document so that every output character now depends on multiple input characters (diffusion).
4. **Stamping (AddRoundKey):** He XORs the entire document with a freshly generated one-time code from your shared master key schedule.

After ten rounds, the original document is mathematically unrecognizable. Only the recipient holding the **same key schedule** can reverse the process using the inverse operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** AES = "Substitute, Shift, Mix, and XOR — repeated 10/12/14 times depending on key size — on a 4×4 grid of bytes."

## 1.3 Standard Parameters and Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Block Size | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| Key Sizes | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** |
| Rounds (Nr) | **10 / 12 / 14** |
| State Matrix | **4 × 4 bytes** |
| Word Size | **32 bits (4 bytes)** |
| Key Schedule Words (Nb) | **4 columns** |
| Round Key Size | **128 bits per round** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AES Architecture Overview

AES processes data in a **4×4 column-major order byte matrix** called the **State**. Input plaintext bytes $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{15}$ are loaded column-by-column into the State:

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} P_0 & P_4 & P_8 & P_{12} \\ P_1 & P_5 & P_9 & P_{13} \\ P_2 & P_6 & P_{10} & P_{14} \\ P_3 & P_7 & P_{11} & P_{15} \end{pmatrix}
$$

The State undergoes an **initial round key addition** followed by **Nr − 1 identical rounds**, and concludes with a **final round** that omits the MixColumns step.

## 2.2 The Four AES Round Transformations

### 2.2.1 SubBytes (Non-linear Substitution Layer)

SubBytes applies a fixed **16 × 16 substitution box (S-Box)** independently to every byte of the State. The transformation involves two algebraic operations in **GF($2^8$)**:

1. **Multiplicative Inverse:** Compute $b = a^{-1}$ in GF($2^8$), where the irreducible polynomial is $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.
2. **Affine Transformation:** Apply the matrix operation $b' = b \oplus c$, where $c = \{63\}$ (hex).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The S-Box provides **confusion** — it destroys any statistical relationship between the key and ciphertext.

### 2.2.2 ShiftRows (Row-wise Permutation)

ShiftRows cyclically left-shifts the second, third, and fourth rows by **1, 2, and 3 byte positions** respectively. The first row remains unchanged.

$$
\begin{pmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{pmatrix}
\longrightarrow
\begin{pmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} & s_{1,0} \\ s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} & s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} \\ s_{3,3} & s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 2.2.3 MixColumns (Column-wise Diffusion)

Each column of the State is multiplied (in GF($2^8$)) by a fixed polynomial $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$. The matrix form is:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} s'_{0,c} \\ s'_{1,c} \\ s'_{2,c} \\ s'_{3,c} \end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} s_{0,c} \\ s_{1,c} \\ s_{2,c} \\ s_{3,c} \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 2.2.4 AddRoundKey (Key Mixing)

The round key is XORed byte-wise with the State. The round key is derived from the cipher key via the **Key Expansion** algorithm:

$$
S'_{i,j} = S_{i,j} \oplus K^{round}_{i,j}
$$

## 2.3 AES Key Schedule

The key expansion generates $Nb(Nr + 1)$ 32-bit words, where $Nb = 4$. For AES-128, this yields **44 words** ($W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{43}$), grouping into 11 round keys of 128 bits each.

For word index $i$:
- **If $i \bmod 4 \neq 0$:** $W_i = W_{i-1} \oplus W_{i-4}$
- **If $i \bmod 4 = 0$:** $W_i = \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W_{i-1})) \oplus \text{Rcon}_{i/4} \oplus W_{i-4}$

Where $\text{RotWord}$ performs a cyclic left shift of one byte, and $\text{Rcon} = (RC_j, 00, 00, 00)$ with $RC_j = \{02\}^{j-1}$ in GF($2^8$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Rule | Context of Use |
|---|---|---|
| AES-128 Rounds | $Nr = 10$ | Round count for 128-bit key |
| AES-192 Rounds | $Nr = 12$ | Round count for 192-bit key |
| AES-256 Rounds | $Nr = 14$ | Round count for 256-bit key |
| State Loading | Column-major order | $S_{r,c} = P_{r + 4c}$ |
| Key Expansion Words | $Nb \times (Nr + 1)$ | Total 32-bit words |
| MixColumns Constant | $\{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ | GF($2^8$) polynomial |
| Irreducible Polynomial | $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ | GF($2^8$) field definition |
| S-Box Affine Constant | $c = \{63\}_{hex}$ | SubBytes affine step |
| Rcon$_{j}$ | $(RC_j, 00, 00, 00)$ | Key schedule round constant |
| $RC_j$ Recurrence | $RC_1 = 01$, $RC_j = 02 \cdot RC_{j-1}$ | GF($2^8$) multiplication |
| GF Multiplication Rule | Shift-left and XOR with $\{1B\}$ if MSB=1 | Byte $\times 02$ operation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

AES is the **de facto global encryption standard**, deployed in:
- **TLS/SSL** (HTTPS web traffic) — typically AES-128-GCM or AES-256-GCM.
- **Wi-Fi Security** (WPA2/WPA3 protocols).
- **Full-disk encryption** (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS).
- **Government communications** (NSA Suite B cryptography uses AES-256).
- **Hardware Security Modules (HSMs)** and **Intel AES-NI** instruction set extensions.

Its efficiency on both software and hardware platforms, combined with provable resistance against known cryptanalytic attacks, makes AES the cornerstone of modern **confidentiality services**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough of a Single AES Round

We will trace the encryption of a single 128-bit block through one complete round to build intuition for the algebraic mechanics.

### 3.1.1 Initial State Loading

Suppose plaintext bytes in hexadecimal are $P = \{32\, 88\, 31\, E0\, 43\, 5A\, 31\, 37\, F6\, 30\, 98\, 07\, A8\, 8D\, A2\, 34\}$.

Loaded column-major into the State:

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} 32 & 43 & F6 & A8 \\ 88 & 5A & 30 & 8D \\ 31 & 31 & 98 & A2 \\ E0 & 37 & 07 & 34 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.2 AddRoundKey (Pre-round)

The initial round key $K_0$ is XORed with the State. For this example, assume $K_0$ consists of the first 16 bytes of the expanded key.

### 3.1.3 SubBytes — Detailed Derivation

For each byte $a$, the S-Box operation is:
- **Step 1:** Compute the multiplicative inverse $b = a^{-1}$ in GF($2^8$). Note that $\{00\}$ maps to $\{00\}$.
- **Step 2:** Apply the affine transformation:

$$
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
$$

where $c = \{63\}_{hex} = 01100011_2$.

**Worked Example:** Compute SubBytes$(\{32\})$.

- **Step A:** Find multiplicative inverse of $\{32\}$ in GF($2^8$).
  - $\{32\} = 00110010_2 = x^5 + x^4 + x$.
  - Using the extended Euclidean algorithm in GF($2^8$):

$$
\{32\}^{-1} = \{A2\}
$$

  - Verification: $\{32\} \otimes \{A2\} = \{01\}$. (Expansion omitted for brevity — see Section 3.2 for multiplication rule.)

- **Step B:** Apply affine transformation to $\{A2\} = 10100010_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b'_0 &= b_0 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus c_0 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
b'_1 &= b_1 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus c_1 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
b'_2 &= b_2 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus c_2 = 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
b'_3 &= b_3 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus c_3 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
b'_4 &= b_4 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus c_4 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
b'_5 &= b_5 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus c_5 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
b'_6 &= b_6 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus c_6 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
b'_7 &= b_7 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus c_7 = 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $b' = 00010000_2 = \{10\}_{hex}$. This matches the published AES S-Box value for row 3, column 2.

### 3.1.4 ShiftRows — Step-by-Step Application

After SubBytes, the State undergoes row-wise left circular shifts:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} d_{00} & d_{01} & d_{02} & d_{03} \\ d_{10} & d_{11} & d_{12} & d_{13} \\ d_{20} & d_{21} & d_{22} & d_{23} \\ d_{30} & d_{31} & d_{32} & d_{33} \end{pmatrix}
\longrightarrow
\begin{pmatrix} d_{00} & d_{01} & d_{02} & d_{03} \\ d_{11} & d_{12} & d_{13} & d_{10} \\ d_{22} & d_{23} & d_{20} & d_{21} \\ d_{33} & d_{30} & d_{31} & d_{32} \end{pmatrix}
$$

Row 0 (top): no shift. Row 1: shift left by 1. Row 2: shift left by 2. Row 3: shift left by 3 (equivalently, shift right by 1).

### 3.1.5 MixColumns — Detailed GF($2^8$) Derivation

For column $c$ of the post-ShiftRows State, we compute:

$$
s'_{0,c} = (\{02\} \otimes s_{0,c}) \oplus (\{03\} \otimes s_{1,c}) \oplus s_{2,c} \oplus s_{3,c}
$$

$$
s'_{1,c} = s_{0,c} \oplus (\{02\} \otimes s_{1,c}) \oplus (\{03\} \otimes s_{2,c}) \oplus s_{3,c}
$$

$$
s'_{2,c} = s_{0,c} \oplus s_{1,c} \oplus (\{02\} \otimes s_{2,c}) \oplus (\{03\} \otimes s_{3,c})
$$

$$
s'_{3,c} = (\{03\} \otimes s_{0,c}) \oplus s_{1,c} \oplus s_{2,c} \oplus (\{02\} \otimes s_{3,c})
$$

**GF($2^8$) Multiplication Rules Used:**

The expression $\{03\} \otimes x$ is expanded as:

$$
\{03\} \otimes x = (\{02\} \otimes x) \oplus x
$$

The xtime operation ($\{02\} \otimes x$) is implemented as:

$$
\text{xtime}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{leftShift}(x) & \text{if } x_7 = 0 \\ \text{leftShift}(x) \oplus \{1B\} & \text{if } x_7 = 1 \end{cases}
$$

where leftShift denotes a 1-bit left shift, and $\{1B\}$ is the reduction polynomial's low-order byte.

### 3.1.6 AddRoundKey — Final XOR of Round

The expanded round key is XORed byte-by-byte with the State matrix.

### 3.1.7 Inverse Cipher Round (Decryption)

The AES decryption uses the inverse operations in **reverse order**: InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey → InvMixColumns. Equivalently, a numerically equivalent decryption sequence can be constructed by reordering operations.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Round Operation Summary

| Round Operation | Type | Mathematical Basis | Diffusion/Confusion | Inverse Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SubBytes | Non-linear | GF($2^8$) inverse + affine | Confusion | InvSubBytes (affine inverse, then multiplicative inverse) |
| ShiftRows | Linear | Byte permutation | Diffusion | InvShiftRows (right shifts) |
| MixColumns | Linear | GF($2^8$) matrix mult. | Diffusion | InvMixColumns (matrix with constants $\{0E, 0B, 0D, 09\}$) |
| AddRoundKey | Linear | Bitwise XOR | Key mixing | AddRoundKey (XOR is its own inverse) |

## 3.3 Key Schedule — Step-by-Step Derivation for AES-128

Given a 128-bit cipher key (4 words: $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$), we derive $W_4, W_5, \ldots, W_{43}$.

**For $i = 4$ (a multiple of $Nk = 4$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Temp} &= W_3 = (k_0, k_1, k_2, k_3) \\
\text{RotWord}(\text{Temp}) &= (k_1, k_2, k_3, k_0) \\
\text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}) &= (\text{S-Box}(k_1), \text{S-Box}(k_2), \text{S-Box}(k_3), \text{S-Box}(k_0)) \\
\text{Rcon}[1] &= (01, 00, 00, 00) \\
W_4 &= \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W_3)) \oplus \text{Rcon}[1] \oplus W_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $i = 5, 6, 7$ (non-multiples):**

$$
W_i = W_{i-1} \oplus W_{i-4}
$$

This pattern repeats for every group of 4 words. For **AES-192** ($Nk = 6$) and **AES-256** ($Nk = 8$), the schedule complexity increases: AES-256 uses an additional SubWord step on even-indexed non-multiple words.

## 3.4 Security Analysis Foundation

The strength of AES arises from:

- **Wide Trail Strategy:** S-Box differential uniformity is 4 (best possible for non-APN 8-bit boxes), and MixColumns has a branch number of 5.
- **Algebraic Complexity:** No known attack breaks AES faster than brute force ($2^{128}, 2^{192}, 2^{256}$ for the three variants).
- **Side-Channel Resistance (with proper implementation):** Constant-time implementations defeat timing attacks; masking schemes defeat power analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AES-128 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["PLAINTEXT 128 BITS"] --> loadA["LOAD STATE MATRIX 4X4 BYTES COLUMN MAJOR"]
    loadA --> addK0["ADD ROUND KEY 0 INITIAL XOR"]
    addK0 --> loopStart{"ROUND COUNTER i LESS THAN 10"}
    loopStart -- YES --> subStep["SUBBYTES S BOX NON LINEAR SUBSTITUTION"]
    subStep --> shiftStep["SHIFTROWS CYCLIC LEFT SHIFTS BY 0 1 2 3"]
    shiftStep --> decisionMC{"IS THIS THE FINAL ROUND"}
    decisionMC -- NO --> mixStep["MIXCOLUMNS GF 2 POWER 8 MATRIX MULT"]
    decisionMC -- YES --> skipMix["SKIP MIXCOLUMNS"]
    mixStep --> addKr["ADD ROUND KEY i XOR WITH EXPANDED KEY"]
    skipMix --> addKr
    addKr --> incR["INCREMENT ROUND COUNTER"]
    incR --> loopStart
    loopStart -- NO --> outputC["CIPHERTEXT 128 BITS"]
    keyInput["CIPHER KEY 128 BITS"] --> keyExpand["KEY EXPANSION GENERATE 44 WORDS"]
    keyExpand --> k0["ROUND KEY 0"]
    keyExpand --> kMid["ROUND KEYS 1 TO 9"]
    keyExpand --> kLast["ROUND KEY 10"]
    k0 --> addK0
    kMid --> addKr
    kLast --> addKr
```

## 4.2 AES Round Structure — Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inState["INPUT STATE 4X4"] --> subNode["SUBBYTES BLOCK"]
    subNode --> outSub["S BOX LOOKUP PER BYTE"]
    outSub --> shiftNode["SHIFTROWS BLOCK"]
    shiftNode --> outShift["PERMUTED STATE"]
    outShift --> mixNode["MIXCOLUMNS BLOCK"]
    mixNode --> outMix["DIFFUSED STATE"]
    outMix --> addNode["ADDROUNDKEY BLOCK"]
    roundKey["ROUND KEY 128 BITS"] --> addNode
    addNode --> outState["OUTPUT STATE 4X4"]
```

## 4.3 AES Key Expansion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK["CIPHER KEY 4 WORDS"] --> assignW["ASSIGN W0 W1 W2 W3"]
    assignW --> checkI{"INDEX i MULTIPLE OF 4"}
    checkI -- YES --> rotOp["ROTWORD LEFT SHIFT BY 1 BYTE"]
    rotOp --> subOp["SUBWORD APPLY S BOX TO EACH BYTE"]
    subOp --> xorR["XOR WITH RCON i DIV 4"]
    xorR --> xorPrev["XOR WITH W i MINUS 4"]
    checkI -- NO --> xorSimple["W i = W i MINUS 1 XOR W i MINUS 4"]
    xorPrev --> emitW["EMIT WORD W i"]
    xorSimple --> emitW
    emitW --> nextI{"i LESS THAN 43"}
    nextI -- YES --> incCounter["i = i PLUS 1"]
    incCounter --> checkI
    nextI -- NO --> endK["KEY SCHEDULE COMPLETE 44 WORDS"]
```

## 4.4 AES Decryption Inverse Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cipherIn["CIPHERTEXT 128 BITS"] --> loadInv["LOAD STATE MATRIX"]
    loadInv --> addLast["ADD ROUND KEY 10 INITIAL XOR FOR DECRYPTION"]
    addLast --> invLoop{"ROUND COUNTER j LESS THAN 10"}
    invLoop -- YES --> invShift["INV SHIFTROWS RIGHT SHIFTS BY 0 1 2 3"]
    invShift --> invSub["INV SUBBYTES INVERSE S BOX LOOKUP"]
    invSub --> invAddK["ADD ROUND KEY FOR ROUND j"]
    invAddK --> decisionIMC{"IS THIS THE FINAL DECRYPTION ROUND"}
    decisionIMC -- NO --> invMix["INV MIXCOLUMNS MATRIX WITH 0E 0B 0D 09"]
    decisionIMC -- YES --> skipInv["SKIP INV MIXCOLUMNS"]
    invMix --> incJ["INCREMENT j"]
    skipInv --> incJ
    incJ --> invLoop
    invLoop -- NO --> plainOut["RECOVERED PLAINTEXT 128 BITS"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: List the four transformations performed in each round of AES and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer:**
The four transformations performed in each round of AES (except the final round, which omits MixColumns) are:

1. **SubBytes:** A non-linear byte substitution using the AES S-Box. It provides **confusion** by making the relationship between the key and ciphertext highly non-linear.
2. **ShiftRows:** A cyclic left-shift of the rows of the State by 0, 1, 2, and 3 byte positions for rows 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. It provides **diffusion** across columns.
3. **MixColumns:** A matrix multiplication of each column over the finite field GF($2^8$). It provides **diffusion** within each column.
4. **AddRoundKey:** A bitwise XOR of the State with the expanded round key. It incorporates the key material into the cipher.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the difference between AES and DES in terms of block size, key size, and round structure?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DES | AES |
|---|---|---|
| Block Size | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| Key Size | 56 bits | 128, 192, or 256 bits |
| Number of Rounds | 16 (Feistel) | 10, 12, or 14 (SPN) |
| Structure | Feistel Network | Substitution-Permutation Network |
| Primitive Operations | Bit permutations, XOR, S-Box | Byte substitutions, row shifts, column mixing, XOR |
| Cryptanalysis Status | Broken (brute-forceable) | Secure (no practical attacks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the AES key expansion algorithm for AES-128 with a neat diagram. How many round keys are generated, and what is the role of the Rcon array?**

**Model Solution:**

The AES-128 key expansion algorithm takes a 128-bit cipher key (treated as 4 words of 32 bits each: $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$) and expands it into **44 words** ($W_0$ through $W_{43}$), which collectively form **11 round keys** of 128 bits each ($Nr + 1 = 11$).

**Algorithm:**

$$
W_i = \begin{cases}
\text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W_{i-1})) \oplus \text{Rcon}_{i/4} \oplus W_{i-4} & \text{if } i \bmod 4 = 0 \\[4pt]
W_{i-1} \oplus W_{i-4} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Helper Functions:**

- **RotWord($[a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3]$) = $[a_1, a_2, a_3, a_0]$** — cyclic 1-byte left rotation.
- **SubWord($[a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3]$) = $[\text{S-Box}(a_0), \text{S-Box}(a_1), \text{S-Box}(a_2), \text{S-Box}(a_3)]$** — S-Box applied to each byte.
- **Rcon$_j = (RC_j, 00, 00, 00)$**, where $RC_j = \{02\}^{j-1}$ in GF($2^8$).

The Rcon array values are:
$$
\text{Rcon} = \begin{pmatrix} 01 & 02 & 04 & 08 & 10 & 20 & 40 & 80 & 1B & 36 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Round Key Grouping:** Round key $j$ (for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, 10$) consists of words $W_{4j}, W_{4j+1}, W_{4j+2}, W_{4j+3}$.

**Role of Rcon:** The Rcon array introduces **per-round variation** in the key schedule, ensuring that even if portions of the cipher key repeat, the round keys are distinct. This prevents **slide attacks** and related-key cryptanalysis.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    kIn["CIPHER KEY 128 BITS"] --> splitW["SPLIT INTO W0 W1 W2 W3"]
    splitW --> procW4["W4 = SUBWORD ROTWORD W3 XOR RCON1 XOR W0"]
    splitW --> procW5["W5 = W4 XOR W1"]
    splitW --> procW6["W6 = W5 XOR W2"]
    splitW --> procW7["W7 = W6 XOR W3"]
    procW4 --> procW8["W8 APPLY ROTWORD SUBWORD RCON2 TO W7"]
    procW5 --> procW8
    procW6 --> procW8
    procW7 --> procW8
    procW8 --> group["GROUP WORDS INTO 11 ROUND KEYS"]
    group --> outK["11 ROUND KEYS OF 128 BITS EACH"]
```

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: Describe the MixColumns transformation in AES. Show the matrix multiplication for one column and explain the GF($2^8$) arithmetic involved.**

**Model Solution:**

MixColumns operates on each 4-byte column of the State independently. The transformation matrix over GF($2^8$) is:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{pmatrix}
$$

For a column vector $\mathbf{s} = (s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3)^T$, the output column is $\mathbf{s'} = M \otimes \mathbf{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s'_0 &= \{02\} \otimes s_0 \oplus \{03\} \otimes s_1 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_2 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_3 \\
s'_1 &= \{01\} \otimes s_0 \oplus \{02\} \otimes s_1 \oplus \{03\} \otimes s_2 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_3 \\
s'_2 &= \{01\} \otimes s_0 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_1 \oplus \{02\} \otimes s_2 \oplus \{03\} \otimes s_3 \\
s'_3 &= \{03\} \otimes s_0 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_1 \oplus \{01\} \otimes s_2 \oplus \{02\} \otimes s_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**GF($2^8$) Arithmetic:**

The field GF($2^8$) uses the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$, represented in hexadecimal as $\{11B\}$.

- **Addition** = bitwise XOR (no carry propagation).
- **Multiplication** = polynomial multiplication modulo $m(x)$.

**Key Computation Rule (xtime):**

For multiplication by $\{02\}$:

$$
\text{xtime}(a) = \begin{cases} (a \ll 1) & \text{if } a_7 = 0 \\ (a \ll 1) \oplus \{1B\} & \text{if } a_7 = 1 \end{cases}
$$

For multiplication by $\{03\}$:

$$
\{03\} \otimes a = (\{02\} \otimes a) \oplus a
$$

**Worked Example:** Compute $s'_0$ for column $(s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3) = (\{87\}, \{6E\}, \{46\}, \{A6\})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{02\} \otimes \{87\} &= \text{xtime}(\{87\}) = (10000111 \ll 1) \oplus \{1B\} \\
&= 00001110_2 \oplus \{1B\} = 00001110 \oplus 00011011 \\
&= 00010101 = \{0E\} \\
\{03\} \otimes \{6E\} &= \{02\} \otimes \{6E\} \oplus \{6E\} \\
\{02\} \otimes \{6E\} &= (1101110 \ll 1) = 11011100 = \{DC\} \text{ (no XOR needed, MSB was 0 after shift analysis)} \\
&= \{DC\} \oplus \{6E\} = \{B2\} \\
s'_0 &= \{0E\} \oplus \{B2\} \oplus \{46\} \oplus \{A6\} = \{47\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Purpose:** MixColumns provides **intra-column diffusion** — after this step, every output byte depends on all four input bytes of that column, satisfying Shannon's principle of diffusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: With a block diagram, explain the complete AES-128 encryption algorithm. Mention the role of each transformation.**

**Model Solution:**

The AES-128 encryption algorithm processes a 128-bit plaintext block through the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Initial Round Key Addition:**

The plaintext is loaded into the 4×4 State matrix (column-major order), then XORed with Round Key 0 (the first 128 bits of the expanded key).

**Stage 2 — Iterative Rounds (Rounds 1 to 9):**

Each of the 9 middle rounds performs four transformations in fixed order:

1. **SubBytes:** S-Box substitution on each byte. Provides **confusion**.
2. **ShiftRows:** Left cyclic shifts of rows 2, 3, 4 by 1, 2, 3 bytes respectively. Provides **inter-column diffusion**.
3. **MixColumns:** GF($2^8$) matrix multiplication on each column. Provides **intra-column diffusion**.
4. **AddRoundKey:** XOR with the round-specific 128-bit subkey. Provides **key-dependent mixing**.

**Stage 3 — Final Round (Round 10):**

The final round is identical to the middle rounds except that **MixColumns is omitted**. This is because MixColumns is invertible but does not add cryptographic strength in the last round when followed only by an XOR with the final round key. Its omission simplifies implementation without reducing security.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pt["PLAINTEXT 128 BITS"] --> s0["LOAD INTO STATE MATRIX"]
    s0 --> ak0["ADD ROUND KEY 0"]
    ak0 --> r1["ROUND 1 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r1 --> r2["ROUND 2 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r2 --> r3["ROUND 3 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r3 --> r4["ROUND 4 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r4 --> r5["ROUND 5 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r5 --> r6["ROUND 6 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r6 --> r7["ROUND 7 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r7 --> r8["ROUND 8 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r8 --> r9["ROUND 9 SB SR MC ARK"]
    r9 --> r10["ROUND 10 SB SR ARK NO MIXCOLUMNS"]
    r10 --> ct["CIPHERTEXT 128 BITS"]
```

**Why These Specific Operations?**

- **SubBytes:** Non-linearity prevents the cipher from being broken by linear cryptanalysis.
- **ShiftRows + MixColumns:** Together they ensure that after a few rounds, every output bit depends on every input bit and key bit (**avalanche effect**).
- **AddRoundKey:** The only operation that uses the key, ensuring key-dependent ciphertext.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: Compare AES with 3DES in terms of security strength, performance, block size, and key flexibility. Which is preferred for new system designs?**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | AES | 3DES (Triple DES) |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm Type | Substitution-Permutation Network | Feistel Network (3× DES) |
| Block Size | **128 bits** | 64 bits |
| Key Sizes | **128, 192, 256 bits** | 112 or 168 bits (effective) |
| Rounds | 10 / 12 / 14 | 48 (16 per DES × 3) |
| Security Status | Secure (no practical attacks) | Deprecated; vulnerable to sweet32 birthday attacks |
| Effective Key Strength | $2^{128}, 2^{192}, 2^{256}$ | $2^{112}$ (meet-in-the-middle reduces 168 to 112) |
| Software Speed | **Fast** (especially AES-NI hardware) | Slow (3× DES execution) |
| Hardware Suitability | Excellent (compact S-Box, parallel ops) | Moderate |
| Cryptanalysis Resistance | Differential, linear, algebraic attacks mitigated | Linear and differential attacks mitigated, but block size weakness exposed |
| Standard Status | NIST recommended (FIPS 197) | NIST deprecated since 2023 |

**Conclusion:** **AES is strongly preferred for all new system designs.** Its larger block size eliminates birthday-bound collisions that plague 3DES, its variable key sizes allow scalability, and its SPN structure maps efficiently to modern CPU instruction sets. 3DES is retained only for legacy compatibility and will be fully disallowed after 2024 per NIST SP 800-131A Rev. 2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU AES Examinations:**
>
> 1. **MixColumns matrix wrong:** Students frequently write $\{02, 03, 01, 01\}$ as a **row vector** instead of properly applying the **4×4 matrix multiplication**. Always show the matrix form explicitly.
> 2. **ShiftRows direction confusion:** KTU expects **left shifts** of 0, 1, 2, 3 for encryption. Writing right shifts will lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Key expansion formula missing Rcon:** Many students forget to include the Rcon XOR when $i \bmod 4 = 0$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Final round missing:** The fact that the last round has **no MixColumns** is often overlooked. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Confusion between AES rounds vs. rounds per AES-128/192/256:** Writing "AES has 10 rounds" without specifying "AES-128" is considered incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- AES is a **symmetric block cipher** standardized as **FIPS 197** by NIST in 2001.
- **Block size = 128 bits; Key size = 128 / 192 / 256 bits; Rounds = 10 / 12 / 14.**
- The **State** is a **4×4 byte matrix** loaded **column-major** from the plaintext.
- Each round (except the last) executes **SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey**.
- The **final round** omits MixColumns: **SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey**.
- **SubBytes** uses an S-Box based on GF($2^8$) multiplicative inverse plus an affine transformation with constant $\{63\}$.
- **ShiftRows** performs cyclic left shifts of 0, 1, 2, 3 byte positions on rows 1–4.
- **MixColumns** multiplies each column by the matrix with constants $\{02\}, \{03\}, \{01\}, \{01\}$ over GF($2^8$).
- **AddRoundKey** is a bitwise XOR of the State with the round-specific subkey.
- The **key schedule** generates $Nb(Nr+1) = 4 \times 11 = 44$ 32-bit words for AES-128.
- For key schedule index $i$: if $i \bmod 4 = 0$, apply RotWord → SubWord → XOR with Rcon$_{i/4}$; otherwise, simply XOR $W_{i-1}$ with $W_{i-4}$.
- The GF($2^8$) field uses the irreducible polynomial $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ ($\{11B\}$ hex).
- AES provides **confusion** via SubBytes and **diffusion** via ShiftRows + MixColumns.
- **No practical cryptanalytic attack** is known against AES; brute-force complexity scales with key length.
- AES-128 is the **most widely deployed** variant; AES-256 is preferred for **top-secret** government use.
- The decryption cipher uses inverse transformations in reverse order: **InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey → InvMixColumns**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Hash Functions - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cryptographic Hash Functions - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Cryptographic Hash Function

A **Cryptographic Hash Function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical algorithm that accepts an input of arbitrary, variable length (the *message* $M$) and produces a fixed-size, condensed bit string known as the **message digest**, **hash value**, or **fingerprint** of the message.

Formally, it is expressed as the mapping:

$$
H: \{0, 1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}^{n}
$$

Where:
- $\{0, 1\}^{*}$ represents the set of all binary strings of **any length**.
- $\{0, 1\}^{n}$ represents the set of all binary strings of exactly **n** bits.
- $n$ is the **output length** (e.g., $n = 256$ bits for SHA-256, $n = 512$ bits for SHA-512).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A cryptographic hash function is the *backbone* of digital signatures, message authentication codes (MACs), password storage, and blockchain technology. Understanding its **three core security properties** is mandatory for board examinations.

### 1.2 The Three Mandatory Security Properties

For a hash function to be classified as *cryptographically secure*, it **must** satisfy three fundamental properties:

| # | Property Name | Alternate Name | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Pre-image Resistance** | One-Way Property | Remember / Understand |
| 2 | **Second Pre-image Resistance** | Weak Collision Resistance | Apply / Analyze |
| 3 | **Collision Resistance** | Strong Collision Resistance | Analyze / Evaluate |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Unique Digital Fingerprint"

Imagine a human being is a *message* of any size, and the government issues every citizen a unique **fingerprint** that is always exactly 256 squiggly lines long, no matter how tall or short the person is.

- **You cannot reverse-engineer the person from the fingerprint alone** → *Pre-image Resistance* (One-Way).
- **Given a specific person, an attacker cannot find a *different* person with the same fingerprint** → *Second Pre-image Resistance*.
- **An attacker cannot find any two *different* people who share the exact same fingerprint** → *Collision Resistance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Key Takeaway:** A good cryptographic hash is like a "black hole" — easy to fall into (compute $H(M)$) but practically impossible to climb out of (find $M$ from $H(M)$). And the "cave" has so many unique points that no two messages end up at the same spot.

### 1.4 Standard Output Metrics (KTU Board Favorites)

Common standardized cryptographic hash algorithms and their digest sizes:

| Algorithm | Output Size ($n$ in bits) | Block Size | Status |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------|--------|
| **MD5** | **128** | 512 | **Broken** ❌ |
| **SHA-1** | **160** | 512 | **Deprecated** ⚠️ |
| **SHA-256** | **256** | 512 | **Secure** ✅ |
| **SHA-384** | **384** | 1024 | **Secure** ✅ |
| **SHA-512** | **512** | 1024 | **Secure** ✅ |
| **SHA-3-256** | **256** | 1088 | **Secure** ✅ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Property 1: Pre-image Resistance (One-Way Property)

**Definition:** Given a hash value $h = H(M)$, it must be *computationally infeasible* to find any message $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.

$$
\text{Given: } y \in \{0,1\}^{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Find } x \text{ such that } H(x) = y
$$

- **Computational Infeasibility:** For an $n$-bit hash, the brute-force effort is of the order of $2^{n}$ operations.
- For **SHA-256** ($n = 256$), this means roughly $2^{256}$ trials — a number greater than the number of atoms in the observable universe.
- **Why it matters:** Protects password storage. Even if an attacker steals the hash database, they cannot easily recover the original passwords.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Pre-image resistance is often confused with confidentiality. A hash is **not encryption** — it is a *one-way* transformation. The original message $M$ is **mathematically destroyed** during hashing and cannot be decrypted.

### 2.2 Property 2: Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance)

**Definition:** Given a specific message $M_{1}$, it must be *computationally infeasible* to find a *different* message $M_{2} \neq M_{1}$ such that:

$$
H(M_{1}) = H(M_{2})
$$

- **Computational Infeasibility:** Brute-force effort is again $\mathcal{O}(2^{n})$.
- **Real-World Application:** Prevents an attacker from forging a malicious document that has the *same* hash as a legitimate, signed document.
- **Use Case:** Digital signatures — a sender signs $H(M)$; an attacker must not be able to swap $M$ for $M'$ while keeping the signature valid.

### 2.3 Property 3: Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance)

**Definition:** It must be *computationally infeasible* to find **any two distinct messages** $M_{1} \neq M_{2}$ such that:

$$
H(M_{1}) = H(M_{2})
$$

- **Computational Infeasibility:** By the **Birthday Paradox**, brute-force effort drops to approximately:

$$
\mathcal{O}\!\left(2^{n/2}\right) \text{ operations}
$$

- For **SHA-256**, this becomes $2^{128}$ — still computationally infeasible today, but *significantly weaker* than pre-image resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU Boards):**
> - Pre-image resistance requires $\approx 2^{n}$ trials.
> - Collision resistance requires only $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials (Birthday Attack).
> - Therefore, **collision resistance is the STRONGEST guarantee** — if a hash is collision-resistant, it is automatically second pre-image resistant. The reverse is **not** necessarily true.

### 2.4 The Birthday Paradox — Intuition and Derivation

The **Birthday Problem** states that in a room of just **23 people**, the probability that two people share the same birthday exceeds **50%**.

In hashing terms: To find a collision in an $n$-bit hash, an attacker needs to evaluate the hash function approximately $\sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2}$ times.

**Probability of at least one collision after $k$ trials:**

$$
P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1) / (2 \cdot 2^{n})}
$$

For a $50\%$ probability, set $P \approx 0.5$ and solve for $k$:

$$
k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — it solves 90% of the numerical questions asked in KTU boards for this module.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Practical Implication |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|
| Output Space Size | $N = 2^{n}$ | Total possible hash values |
| Pre-image Brute Force | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n})$ | Resistance to inverting the hash |
| Second Pre-image Brute Force | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n})$ | Resistance to forging alternate $M$ |
| Collision Brute Force (Birthday) | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ | Easiest attack to mount |
| Expected Trials for 50% Collision | $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^{n}}$ | $k \approx 2^{n/2}$ for large $n$ |
| Avalanche Effect Criterion | $\geq 50\%$ bits change | One-bit change in $M$ must drastically alter $H(M)$ |
| Fixed Output Length | $n$ bits (constant) | Independent of input message length |

### 2.6 Other Desirable (Non-Mandatory) Properties

While the three core properties above are *mandatory*, well-designed cryptographic hashes also exhibit:

- **Determinism:** The same message $M$ must always produce the same hash $H(M)$ on every run, on every platform.
- **Quick Computation:** Given $M$, computing $H(M)$ must be *fast* (microseconds), so hashing is practical for high-throughput systems.
- **Avalanche Effect:** A single bit flip in $M$ must change roughly **half** of the bits in $H(M)$ (typically $> 50\%$).
- **Puzzle Friendliness:** Given $h$ and a partial target prefix $p$, finding $M$ such that $H(M \mid \text{nonce}) = h$ (where $h$ starts with $p$) is computationally hard. This is essential for **Bitcoin mining** and **Proof-of-Work** systems.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Hash Properties |
|--------|----------------------------------|
| **Digital Signatures (RSA, ECDSA)** | Sign $H(M)$ instead of $M$ for efficiency and integrity. |
| **Password Storage** | Servers store $H(\text{password} + \text{salt})$; pre-image resistance protects users. |
| **Blockchain / Bitcoin** | Each block contains $H(\text{previous block})$, creating an immutable chain. |
| **Software Distribution** | Vendors publish $H(\text{executable})$; users verify integrity via collision resistance. |
| **TLS / SSL Handshakes** | Hashes of certificates are signed to authenticate web servers. |
| **Git Version Control** | Every commit is identified by its SHA-1 / SHA-256 hash. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why Collision Attacks Cost Only $2^{n/2}$

**Problem Setup:**
- A hash function $H$ produces $n$-bit outputs.
- The number of possible hash values is $N = 2^{n}$.
- An attacker collects $k$ random messages and computes their hashes.
- Find the smallest $k$ such that the probability of *at least one collision* is $\geq 50\%$.

**Step 1: Probability that NO collision occurs among $k$ samples.**

The first message has no prior match — probability = $1$.
The second message must avoid the 1 existing hash — probability = $1 - \frac{1}{N}$.
The third message must avoid the 2 existing hashes — probability = $1 - \frac{2}{N}$.
The $i^{th}$ message must avoid the $(i-1)$ existing hashes — probability = $1 - \frac{i-1}{N}$.

Multiplying all these:

$$
P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \left(1 - \frac{i-1}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Convert the product into a sum using the Taylor approximation.**

Recall that for small $x$, we have the identity:

$$
\ln(1 - x) \approx -x \quad \text{for } x \ll 1
$$

Therefore, the natural log of the probability becomes:

$$
\ln P(\text{no collision}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{i-1}{N}\right) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{k} -\frac{i-1}{N} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the arithmetic series.**

The sum of the first $(k-1)$ integers is:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i = \frac{k(k-1)}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\ln P(\text{no collision}) \approx -\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}
$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate to get the probability of no collision.**

$$
P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-k(k-1) / (2N)}
$$

**Step 5: Set the probability of AT LEAST ONE collision to $0.5$.**

$$
P(\text{collision}) = 1 - P(\text{no collision}) \approx 0.5
$$

$$
1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5
$$

**Step 6: Take the natural log of both sides.**

$$
-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693
$$

**Step 7: Substitute $N = 2^{n}$ and solve for $k$.**

$$
\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{n}} = 0.693 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k(k-1) \approx 1.386 \cdot 2^{n}
$$

**Step 8: For large $k$, approximate $k(k-1) \approx k^{2}$.**

$$
k^{2} \approx 1.386 \cdot 2^{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \approx \sqrt{1.386 \cdot 2^{n}} = \sqrt{1.386} \cdot \sqrt{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 9: Simplify the constants.**

$$
\sqrt{1.386} \approx 1.177 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \approx 2^{n/2} \text{ (for large } n\text{)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** An attacker needs only $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ trials to find a collision — squaring the speed of attack by exploiting the birthday paradox.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board-Style)

**Example 1: A hash function produces a 64-bit output. Estimate the number of trials required to find a collision with 50% probability.**

*Solution:*

$$
n = 64, \quad k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{32} \approx 5.06 \times 10^{9}
$$

The attacker must hash approximately **5 billion** messages — feasible on a modern desktop in hours.

**Example 2: For SHA-256 ($n = 256$), compare the cost of a pre-image attack vs a collision attack.**

*Solution:*

Pre-image attack: $\mathcal{O}(2^{256})$ trials.
Collision attack: $\mathcal{O}(2^{128})$ trials.

$$
\frac{2^{256}}{2^{128}} = 2^{128}
$$

**The collision attack is $2^{128}$ times faster than the pre-image attack.**

**Example 3: How many trials are needed for a 1% probability of collision on a 128-bit hash?**

*Solution:*

We want $P(\text{collision}) = 0.01$, so $P(\text{no collision}) = 0.99$.

$$
e^{-k^{2}/(2N)} = 0.99 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{k^{2}}{2N} = \ln(0.99) \approx -0.01005
$$

$$
k^{2} = 0.0201 \cdot N = 0.0201 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 2.71 \times 10^{36}
$$

$$
k \approx 1.65 \times 10^{18} \text{ trials}
$$

### 3.3 Security Level Comparison Matrix

| Hash Function | $n$ (bits) | Pre-image Security | Collision Security | Practical Status |
|---------------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| MD5 | 128 | $2^{128}$ | $2^{64}$ ❌ | Broken |
| SHA-1 | 160 | $2^{160}$ | $2^{80}$ ⚠️ | Deprecated |
| SHA-256 | 256 | $2^{256}$ ✅ | $2^{128}$ ✅ | Industry Standard |
| SHA-512 | 512 | $2^{512}$ ✅ | $2^{256}$ ✅ | High-Security Use |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: How Hashing Works

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Message M of arbitrary length"] --> B["Cryptographic Hash Function H"]
    B --> C["Fixed-length hash value h of n bits"]
    C --> D["Stored in database / Signed digitally / Used in blockchain"]
    E["Properties Required"] --> B
    E --> F["Pre-image Resistance"]
    E --> G["Second Pre-image Resistance"]
    E --> H["Collision Resistance"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram: The Three Core Properties of Cryptographic Hash Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Prop["Three Mandatory Properties of H"]
    Prop --> P1["Property 1: Pre-image Resistance"]
    Prop --> P2["Property 2: Second Pre-image Resistance"]
    Prop --> P3["Property 3: Collision Resistance"]
    P1 --> P1a["Given h find M such that H of M equals h"]
    P1 --> P1b["Effort required: 2 to the power n"]
    P2 --> P2a["Given M1 find M2 not equal to M1 such that H of M1 equals H of M2"]
    P2 --> P2b["Effort required: 2 to the power n"]
    P3 --> P3a["Find any M1 not equal to M2 such that H of M1 equals H of M2"]
    P3 --> P3b["Effort required: 2 to the power n by 2 via birthday paradox"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Security Hierarchy Among the Three Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top["Strongest Guarantee: Collision Resistance"]
    Top --> Mid["Implies Second Pre-image Resistance"]
    Mid --> Bot["Implies Pre-image Resistance One-way Property"]
    Bot --> Use["Application: Password Storage Digital Signatures"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Brute-Force Effort Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Attack Type"] --> B["Pre-image Attack: 2 to the power n"]
    A --> C["Second Pre-image Attack: 2 to the power n"]
    A --> D["Collision Attack Birthday: 2 to the power n by 2"]
    B --> E["Hardest to break"]
    C --> F["Same cost as pre-image"]
    D --> G["Easiest to break but still strong for n greater than 256"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hash-Based Authentication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User["User submits password P"] --> SaltGen["Server generates random salt S"]
    SaltGen --> Concat["Compute H of P plus S"]
    Concat --> Store["Store username salt and H of P plus S in database"]
    Store --> Login["User attempts login with P prime"]
    Login --> Verify["Compute H of P prime plus S and compare with stored hash"]
    Verify --> Match{"Match found?"}
    Match -->|"Yes"| Grant["Grant access to user"]
    Match -->|"No"| Deny["Deny access and log attempt"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a cryptographic hash function. List its three essential security properties.*

**Model Answer:**

A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a deterministic algorithm that takes an input of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-length output $h$ of $n$ bits.

The three essential security properties are:
1. **Pre-image Resistance** — Given $h$, it is infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance** — Given $M_{1}$, it is infeasible to find $M_{2} \neq M_{1}$ such that $H(M_{1}) = H(M_{2})$.
3. **Collision Resistance** — It is infeasible to find any $M_{1} \neq M_{2}$ such that $H(M_{1}) = H(M_{2})$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the significance of the "Birthday Paradox" in attacking cryptographic hash functions.*

**Model Answer:**

The Birthday Paradox states that the probability of two random values colliding becomes significant ($>50\%$) after approximately $\sqrt{N}$ samples, where $N$ is the total number of possible outputs.

For an $n$-bit hash with $N = 2^{n}$ outputs, an attacker requires only $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials to find a collision, not $2^{n}$ trials.

**Significance:** It reduces the effective security of a hash from $n$ bits to $n/2$ bits. For example, SHA-256 has a pre-image security of $2^{256}$ but a collision security of only $2^{128}$. Hence, NIST recommends hash outputs of at least **256 bits** to maintain a collision resistance of **128 bits**, which is considered secure against modern computational power.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the three security properties of cryptographic hash functions. Compare the computational effort required to break each property for an $n$-bit hash.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For a 160-bit hash function, calculate the approximate number of trials required to find (i) a pre-image with brute force and (ii) a collision with 50% probability using the birthday attack. Comment on the result.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 2.1, 2.2, and 2.3** of these notes. The three properties are:
1. Pre-image resistance — effort $2^{n}$.
2. Second pre-image resistance — effort $2^{n}$.
3. Collision resistance — effort $2^{n/2}$ (birthday paradox).

Comparison table:

| Property | Brute-Force Effort |
|----------|---------------------|
| Pre-image | $2^{n}$ |
| Second Pre-image | $2^{n}$ |
| Collision | $2^{n/2}$ |

Collision is the **easiest** to break due to the birthday paradox.

**(b)** Given $n = 160$:

**(i) Pre-image brute force:**

$$
\text{Effort} = 2^{160} \approx 1.46 \times 10^{48} \text{ trials}
$$

**(ii) Collision via birthday attack (50% probability):**

$$
k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{80} \approx 1.63 \times 10^{24} \text{ trials}
$$

**Comment:** The collision attack requires only $2^{80}$ trials — vastly less than $2^{160}$ — demonstrating that even a 160-bit hash (SHA-1) is **not collision-resistant** by modern standards. This is why SHA-1 is deprecated and SHA-256 is the new industry standard.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the formula for the expected number of trials required to find a collision in an $n$-bit hash function with 50% probability.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A digital signature scheme uses SHA-256. (i) What is the pre-image security? (ii) What is the collision security? (iii) An attacker claims to break collision resistance in $2^{100}$ operations. Is this feasible? Justify.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See the exhaustive step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} \approx 2^{n/2} \text{ (for large } n\text{)}}
$$

**(b)** SHA-256 has $n = 256$ bits.

**(i) Pre-image security:**

$$
\mathcal{O}(2^{256}) \text{ operations}
$$

**(ii) Collision security:**

$$
\mathcal{O}(2^{128}) \text{ operations}
$$

**(iii)** $2^{100}$ is **less than** $2^{128}$, so the attack **would be feasible** if achievable. However, in practice:
- No public attack currently breaks SHA-256 collisions in $2^{100}$ operations.
- The theoretical birthday bound of $2^{128}$ is still the best known.
- $2^{100}$ operations remains a *huge* computational task (exceeds the world’s combined computing power for any practical timeframe).
- Hence, while $2^{100} < 2^{128}$ reduces the **theoretical** safety margin, it is still **practically infeasible** with current technology.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing pre-image and collision security** — Many students write $2^{256}$ for *both*. The collision security is **$2^{n/2}$**, not $2^{n}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU boards.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $1.177$** in the birthday formula — The standard answer is $1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$. Dropping the constant loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing $2^{n/2}$ without explaining it is the birthday bound** — Always state *"By the Birthday Paradox, ..."* for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up MD5 and SHA-1 outputs** — MD5 is 128 bits, SHA-1 is 160 bits. Mixing these up is a 1-mark error.
> 5. **Not stating "computationally infeasible"** — KTU expects this phrase; otherwise, the answer is considered incomplete.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Cryptographic Hash Function Properties**

- **Definition:** $H: \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$ — arbitrary input, fixed $n$-bit output.
- **Three mandatory properties:**
  1. **Pre-image resistance** (one-way) — cannot reverse $h$ to find $M$.
  2. **Second pre-image resistance** (weak collision) — cannot find alternate $M'$ for given $M$.
  3. **Collision resistance** (strong collision) — cannot find any $M_{1} = M_{2}$ producing the same $h$.
- **Brute-force costs:** Pre-image = $2^{n}$, Collision = $2^{n/2}$ (birthday paradox).
- **Birthday formula:** $k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ trials for 50% collision probability.
- **Hierarchy:** Collision resistance ⇒ Second pre-image resistance ⇒ Pre-image resistance.
- **Standard algorithms:** MD5 (broken, 128 bits), SHA-1 (deprecated, 160 bits), SHA-256 (secure, 256 bits), SHA-512 (secure, 512 bits).
- **Avalanche effect:** A 1-bit change in $M$ must change at least 50% of bits in $h$.
- **Determinism:** Same input always yields same hash output.
- **Applications:** Digital signatures, password storage, blockchain, Git, TLS certificates, Bitcoin mining.
- **Puzzle friendliness:** Finding $M$ such that $H(M \mid \text{nonce})$ matches a target prefix is hard — vital for Proof-of-Work.
- **NIST recommendation:** Use at least 256-bit output to maintain 128-bit collision security.
- **Avoid in exams:** Writing "decryption of hash" (hashing is **not** encryption), confusing $2^{n}$ with $2^{n/2}$, omitting the Birthday Paradox justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Hash Algorithm (SHA-512) - Logic and Round Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secure Hash Algorithm (SHA-512) - Logic and Round Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SHA-512** is a member of the **SHA-2 (Secure Hash Algorithm 2)** family, standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS PUB 180-4**. It is a *cryptographic hash function* that accepts an input message of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-size **512-bit message digest** (hash value). The algorithm processes input data in **1024-bit (128-byte) blocks** through **80 sequential rounds** of bitwise logical operations, modular additions, and non-linear compression functions operating on **64-bit words**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SHA-512 is specifically emphasized in Module 3 alongside SHA-1 and MD5. Unlike SHA-1 (160-bit output) and MD5 (128-bit output), SHA-512 offers a **collision-resistant 512-bit hash**, making it suitable for high-security applications such as digital signatures, TLS/SSL certificates, and blockchain integrity.

### 1.2 Key Parameters of SHA-512

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Hash Output Size | **512 bits** |
| Message Block Size | **1024 bits** |
| Word Size | **64 bits** |
| Number of Rounds | **80** |
| Number of Working Variables | **8** (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h) |
| Number of Round Constants (K) | **80** 64-bit words |
| Maximum Message Length | **2^128 − 1 bits** |
| Initial Hash Value Size | **8 × 64 bits = 512 bits** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Fingerprint Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of SHA-512 as a *high-security shredder and mixer* in a bank vault. You feed in a stack of documents of **any size** (one page or a million pages). The shredder first chops them into uniform **1024-bit chunks**, then pushes each chunk through **80 rounds of cryptographic blending** using secret recipes (the round constants **K₀ to K₇₉**) and unpredictable mixing operations. At the end, you receive a unique **512-bit fingerprint** that:
> 1. Always has the same length regardless of input size.
> 2. Changes **completely and unpredictably** if even a single bit of the input changes (*avalanche effect*).
> 3. Cannot be reversed to recover the original documents (*pre-image resistance*).
> 4. Is statistically impossible for two different documents to produce the same fingerprint (*collision resistance*).

### 1.4 Why SHA-512 over SHA-1 and MD5?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Context:** MD5 and SHA-1 have both been **broken** in terms of collision resistance (researchers generated two distinct inputs producing the same hash). SHA-512, with its longer output and more complex round structure, remains **computationally secure** against known collision, pre-image, and second pre-image attacks as of the current cryptographic literature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Overall Algorithm Logic (Five-Stage Pipeline)

The SHA-512 computation is organized into the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Padding:**
The original message **M** is padded so that its bit-length becomes **congruent to 896 mod 1024**. This is done by:
- Appending a single **'1' bit** to the message.
- Appending **'0' bits** until the message length ≡ **896 (mod 1024)**.
- Appending the **64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length**.

After padding, the total padded message length is an integer multiple of **1024 bits**.

**Stage 2 — Parsing into Message Blocks:**
The padded message is divided into **N blocks** of 1024 bits each, denoted **M⁽¹⁾, M⁽²⁾, …, M⁽ᴺ⁾**.

**Stage 3 — Setting the Initial Hash Value (H⁽⁰⁾):**
Eight 64-bit initial hash values **H₀⁽⁰⁾, H₁⁽⁰⁾, …, H₇⁽⁰⁾** are set from the fractional parts of the square roots of the first eight primes (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19).

**Stage 4 — Message Schedule and Compression (For each block):**
- Construct the **80-word message schedule Wₜ** (t = 0 to 79).
- Initialize the **8 working variables** a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h.
- Execute **80 rounds** of the compression function.
- Compute the **intermediate hash value** H⁽ⁱ⁾.

**Stage 5 — Final Hash Value:**
After all N blocks are processed, the final 512-bit hash is:

$$H_0^{(N)} \Vert H_1^{(N)} \Vert H_2^{(N)} \Vert H_3^{(N)} \Vert H_4^{(N)} \Vert H_5^{(N)} \Vert H_6^{(N)} \Vert H_7^{(N)}$$

### 2.2 The Round Function — Detailed Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** The round function is the *heart* of SHA-512 and is the most frequently asked concept in ESE questions.

For each round **t** (where **0 ≤ t ≤ 79**), two temporary words **T₁** and **T₂** are computed, and the working variables are rotated.

The round function equations are:

$$T_1 = h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_t + K_t$$

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c)$$

$$h = g$$

$$g = f$$

$$f = e$$

$$e = d + T_1$$

$$d = c$$

$$c = b$$

$$b = a$$

$$a = T_1 + T_2$$

All additions are performed **modulo 2⁶⁴**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Function Name | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ch(e, f, g)** | **Ch**oice function | $(e \land f) \oplus (\neg e \land g)$ | Bit selection based on **e** |
| **Maj(a, b, c)** | **Maj**ority function | $(a \land b) \oplus (a \land c) \oplus (b \land c)$ | Output is majority of inputs |
| **Σ₀(a)** | Big Sigma 0 | $\text{ROTR}^{28}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{34}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{39}(a)$ | Diffusion for a, b, c |
| **Σ₁(e)** | Big Sigma 1 | $\text{ROTR}^{14}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{41}(e)$ | Diffusion for e, f, g |
| **σ₀(x)** | Small sigma 0 | $\text{ROTR}^{1}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{8}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{7}(x)$ | Message schedule (t = 16 to 79) |
| **σ₁(x)** | Small sigma 1 | $\text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{6}(x)$ | Message schedule (t = 16 to 79) |

### 2.4 Notation Definitions

> **ROTRⁿ(x):** Right rotation of 64-bit word **x** by **n** bit positions. Bits shifted off the right end re-enter from the left.
>
> **SHRⁿ(x):** Right shift of 64-bit word **x** by **n** bit positions. Bits shifted off the right end are **discarded** (zero-fill on the left).
>
> **⊕:** Bitwise XOR operation.
>
> **∧:** Bitwise AND operation.
>
> **¬:** Bitwise NOT (one's complement).
>
> **+ :** Addition modulo 2⁶⁴.

### 2.5 Message Schedule Construction

The 80-word message schedule W₀, W₁, …, W₇₉ is constructed as follows:

For **t = 0 to 15**: The word **Wₜ** is taken directly from the current 1024-bit message block. Since the block is 1024 bits = 16 × 64 bits, we have:

$$W_t = M^{(i)}_t \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15$$

For **t = 16 to 79**: The word is generated recursively:

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

SHA-512 is the cryptographic backbone of:
- **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** — signs certificates and code.
- **TLS 1.3 / HTTPS** — authenticates the handshake using SHA-384/SHA-512 cipher suites.
- **Bitcoin and Blockchain** — SHA-256 (a sibling of SHA-512) is used in **proof-of-work mining**.
- **File integrity verification** — Linux package managers (e.g., `sha512sum`).
- **Password hashing** (with salting and key-stretching) — PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Message Schedule for t ≥ 16

The message schedule extends the 16 64-bit words of the current message block into 80 words. This extension is required because SHA-512 has **80 rounds** but only 16 words of fresh data per block.

**Starting equation (given by FIPS 180-4):**

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$$

**Step 1 — Apply definition of σ₀:**

$$\sigma_0(W_{t-15}) = \text{ROTR}^{1}(W_{t-15}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{8}(W_{t-15}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{7}(W_{t-15})$$

**Step 2 — Apply definition of σ₁:**

$$\sigma_1(W_{t-2}) = \text{ROTR}^{19}(W_{t-2}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(W_{t-2}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{6}(W_{t-2})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the schedule equation:**

$$W_t = \big[\text{ROTR}^{19}(W_{t-2}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(W_{t-2}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{6}(W_{t-2})\big] + W_{t-7} + \big[\text{ROTR}^{1}(W_{t-15}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{8}(W_{t-15}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{7}(W_{t-15})\big] + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The four words W_{t−2}, W_{t−7}, W_{t−15}, W_{t−16} are spaced 13 and 9 positions apart in the schedule, ensuring that each new Wₜ depends on a wide window of previous words. This design is critical for achieving the **avalanche effect** — a single-bit change in the message block propagates rapidly across the schedule.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Working Variable Update Rule

Starting from the FIPS 180-4 round function specification:

$$T_1 = h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_t + K_t$$

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c)$$

**Step 1 — Compute new value of e:**

The variable **e** is updated by adding **T₁** to **d**:

$$e_{\text{new}} = d + T_1 = d + h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_t + K_t \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute new value of a:**

The variable **a** is updated by adding **T₁ + T₂**:

$$a_{\text{new}} = T_1 + T_2 = h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_t + K_t + \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Notice that **T₁** is reused in both the new **a** and the new **e**. This is the design pattern that creates **non-linearity and feedback**: a single round affects the next round through both **a** (via T₂) and **e** (via T₁), making the entire 80-round computation cryptographically strong and irreversible.

### 3.3 Derivation of Intermediate Hash Value Update

After the 80th round, the working variables are combined with the previous hash value:

$$H_0^{(i)} = H_0^{(i-1)} + a$$

$$H_1^{(i)} = H_1^{(i-1)} + b$$

$$H_2^{(i)} = H_2^{(i-1)} + c$$

$$H_3^{(i)} = H_3^{(i-1)} + d$$

$$H_4^{(i)} = H_4^{(i-1)} + e$$

$$H_5^{(i)} = H_5^{(i-1)} + f$$

$$H_6^{(i)} = H_6^{(i-1)} + g$$

$$H_7^{(i)} = H_7^{(i-1)} + h$$

All additions are performed **modulo 2⁶⁴**. This is the **feedback mechanism** that chains the N message blocks together, ensuring that any change in block **i** affects all subsequent blocks **i+1, i+2, …, N** through the final hash output.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Round Function Component Functions

| Function | Inputs | Formula | Output Bits Set to 1 When | Cryptographic Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ch(e, f, g)** | e, f, g | $(e \land f) \oplus (\neg e \land g)$ | e bit selects between f and g | Non-linear bit selector |
| **Maj(a, b, c)** | a, b, c | $(a \land b) \oplus (a \land c) \oplus (b \land c)$ | Majority of the three bits is 1 | Non-linear mixing |
| **Σ₀(a)** | a | $\text{ROTR}^{28} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{34} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{39}$ | Each output bit depends on 3 input bits | Diffusion (long range) |
| **Σ₁(e)** | e | $\text{ROTR}^{14} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{41}$ | Each output bit depends on 3 input bits | Diffusion (long range) |
| **σ₀(x)** | x | $\text{ROTR}^{1} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{8} \oplus \text{SHR}^{7}$ | Mix of rotations and shift | Message schedule expansion |
| **σ₁(x)** | x | $\text{ROTR}^{19} \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61} \oplus \text{SHR}^{6}$ | Mix of rotations and shift | Message schedule expansion |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Single Round (Symbolic)

**Given:** Round **t = 5**, with current working variables a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h (each 64 bits), schedule word W₅, and round constant K₅.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Choice function** on (e, f, g):

$$\text{Ch}(e, f, g) = (e \land f) \oplus (\neg e \land g)$$

**Step 2:** Compute **Big Sigma 1** on e:

$$\Sigma_1(e) = \text{ROTR}^{14}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{41}(e)$$

**Step 3:** Compute **T₁:**

$$T_1 = h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_5 + K_5 \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute **Big Sigma 0** on a:

$$\Sigma_0(a) = \text{ROTR}^{28}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{34}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{39}(a)$$

**Step 5:** Compute **Majority function** on (a, b, c):

$$\text{Maj}(a, b, c) = (a \land b) \oplus (a \land c) \oplus (b \land c)$$

**Step 6:** Compute **T₂:**

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 7:** Update working variables:

$$a_{\text{new}} = T_1 + T_2, \quad e_{\text{new}} = d + T_1$$

$$b_{\text{new}} = a, \quad f_{\text{new}} = e, \quad c_{\text{new}} = b, \quad g_{\text{new}} = f, \quad d_{\text{new}} = c, \quad h_{\text{new}} = g$$

**Conversion logic:** The rotation values (1, 7, 8, 14, 18, 19, 28, 34, 39, 41, 61) are carefully chosen by NIST so that each output bit of Σ₀, Σ₁, σ₀, σ₁ depends on multiple input bits, ensuring rapid diffusion. The choice of these constants is *not arbitrary* — it was the result of cryptographic analysis during the SHA-2 design process.
