## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SHA-512 Overall Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Message M of arbitrary length] --> B[Stage 1: Padding]
    B --> B1[Append 1 bit]
    B1 --> B2[Append k zero bits so length mod 1024 equals 896]
    B2 --> B3[Append 64-bit original length]
    B3 --> C[Stage 2: Parse into N blocks of 1024 bits each]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Initialize H0 to H7 from first 8 prime square roots]
    D --> E[For each block Mi from 1 to N]
    E --> F[Divide block into 16 words W0 to W15]
    F --> G[Generate W16 to W79 using sigma0 and sigma1]
    G --> H[Initialize a b c d e f g h from H0 to H7]
    H --> I[Execute 80 rounds of compression function]
    I --> J[Update intermediate hash H0 to H7 with a b c d e f g h]
    J --> K{All N blocks processed?}
    K -->|No| E
    K -->|Yes| L[Stage 5: Concatenate H0 to H7 to form 512-bit hash]
```

### 4.2 SHA-512 Single Round Function — Internal Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[a] --> SA[Sigma 0 ROTR28 ROTR34 ROTR39]
    A --> MAJ[Maj function]
    B[b] --> MAJ
    C[c] --> MAJ
    SA --> T2[T2 = Sigma0 + Maj]
    MAJ --> T2
    E[e] --> SE[Sigma 1 ROTR14 ROTR18 ROTR41]
    E --> CH[Ch function]
    F[f] --> CH
    G[g] --> CH
    SE --> T1C[T1 constructor]
    CH --> T1C
    H[h] --> T1C
    Wt[W t] --> T1C
    Kt[K t] --> T1C
    T1C --> T1[T1 = h + Ch + Sigma1 + Wt + Kt]
    T1 --> T2ADD[T2 addition]
    T2 --> T2ADD
    T2ADD --> ANEW[a new = T1 + T2]
    T1 --> ENEW[e new = d + T1]
    D[d] --> ENEW
```

### 4.3 Message Schedule Construction (W-Array Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[1024-bit message block Mi] --> P1[Split into 16 64-bit words]
    P1 --> P2[W0 to W15 directly from block]
    P2 --> P3{t between 16 and 79?}
    P3 -->|No| ROUNDS[Proceed to 80 compression rounds]
    P3 -->|Yes| S1[Compute sigma0 of Wt-15]
    S1 --> S2[Compute sigma1 of Wt-2]
    S2 --> S3[Wt = sigma1 Wt-2 + Wt-7 + sigma0 Wt-15 + Wt-16 mod 2 power 64]
    S3 --> P3
```

### 4.4 Padding Stage Detailed View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Original Message M] --> P1[Append single 1 bit]
    P1 --> P2[Append k zero bits such that L mod 1024 equals 896]
    P2 --> P3[Append 64-bit big-endian representation of original length L]
    P3 --> PM[Padded message of length multiple of 1024]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the logical structure of SHA-512 with emphasis on its message digest size, block size, word size, and number of rounds.**

**Model Answer:**

SHA-512 is a cryptographic hash function standardized in FIPS PUB 180-4. It accepts an input message of arbitrary length (up to **2¹²⁸ − 1 bits**) and produces a fixed-size **512-bit** message digest. The algorithm processes the input in blocks of **1024 bits**, each block divided into **16 words of 64 bits**. The compression function executes **80 rounds** of bitwise operations, modular additions, and rotations, using **8 working variables (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h)** and **80 64-bit round constants (K₀ to K₇₉)**. The final hash is the concatenation of 8 64-bit words H₀ to H₇. (3 marks)

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Differentiate between the Choice (Ch) and Majority (Maj) functions used in SHA-512.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ch(e, f, g) = (e ∧ f) ⊕ (¬e ∧ g)** function acts as a *bit selector* — when the bit of **e** is 1, it outputs the corresponding bit of **f**; when the bit of **e** is 0, it outputs the corresponding bit of **g**. In contrast, **Maj(a, b, c) = (a ∧ b) ⊕ (a ∧ c) ⊕ (b ∧ c)** is a *majority voter* — its output bit is 1 if at least two of the three input bits (a, b, c) are 1, and 0 otherwise. Both functions introduce **non-linearity**, which is essential for cryptographic strength against linear and differential cryptanalysis. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Describe the complete round function of SHA-512. Clearly write the equations for T₁, T₂, and the update of the eight working variables a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The round function of SHA-512 executes for **80 rounds** (t = 0 to 79) on 8 working variables a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h (each 64 bits). Each round uses one schedule word **Wₜ** and one round constant **Kₜ**.

**Step 1 — Compute T₁:**

$$T_1 = h + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + \Sigma_1(e) + W_t + K_t \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute T₂:**

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 3 — Update the working variables:**

$$h = g, \quad g = f, \quad f = e$$

$$e = d + T_1 \pmod{2^{64}}$$

$$d = c, \quad c = b, \quad b = a$$

$$a = T_1 + T_2 \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Component definitions:**

- **Ch(e, f, g) = (e ∧ f) ⊕ (¬e ∧ g)** — Choice function
- **Maj(a, b, c) = (a ∧ b) ⊕ (a ∧ c) ⊕ (b ∧ c)** — Majority function
- **Σ₀(a) = ROTR²⁸(a) ⊕ ROTR³⁴(a) ⊕ ROTR³⁹(a)**
- **Σ₁(e) = ROTR¹⁴(e) ⊕ ROTR¹⁸(e) ⊕ ROTR⁴¹(e)**

The use of T₁ in both the new **a** and the new **e** creates strong feedback, ensuring the **avalanche effect** propagates rapidly across rounds. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Explain the message schedule construction in SHA-512. How are W₀ to W₇₉ generated? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The message schedule is a sequence of **80 64-bit words** W₀, W₁, …, W₇₉ derived from the current 1024-bit message block. It is constructed in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Direct loading (t = 0 to 15):**
The 1024-bit block is divided into sixteen 64-bit words M_i,0, M_i,1, …, M_i,15, and these are assigned directly:

$$W_t = M^{(i)}_t \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15$$

**Phase 2 — Recursive expansion (t = 16 to 79):**
For t ≥ 16, the schedule word is computed as:

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

where the small-sigma functions are:

$$\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^1(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^8(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^7(x)$$

$$\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^6(x)$$

**Significance:** This expansion provides **64 additional words** beyond the original 16, supplying fresh mixing material for the remaining 64 rounds. The wide spacing (t−2, t−7, t−15, t−16) and the use of rotations/shifts ensure that every bit of the original message block influences every bit of every Wₜ. This is the **engine of diffusion** in SHA-512. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the overall processing of a message in SHA-512 from padding to final hash generation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The SHA-512 computation proceeds through five stages:

**Stage 1 — Padding:**
- Append a single **'1' bit** to message M.
- Append **'0' bits** until the message length ≡ **896 (mod 1024)**.
- Append the **64-bit big-endian** representation of the original message length L.

The padded message length is now an exact multiple of **1024 bits**.

**Stage 2 — Parsing:**
The padded message is split into **N 1024-bit blocks**: M⁽¹⁾, M⁽²⁾, …, M⁽ᴺ⁾.

**Stage 3 — Initial Hash Value (H⁽⁰⁾):**
Set the eight 64-bit values H₀⁽⁰⁾ to H₇⁽⁰⁾ from the fractional parts of the square roots of the first eight primes: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19.

**Stage 4 — Compression (For each block M⁽ⁱ⁾):**

- (a) Prepare message schedule Wₜ, t = 0 to 79.
- (b) Initialize a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h ← H₀⁽ⁱ⁻¹⁾, H₁⁽ⁱ⁻¹⁾, …, H₇⁽ⁱ⁻¹⁾.
- (c) For t = 0 to 79, execute the round function and update working variables.
- (d) Compute the intermediate hash:

$$H_j^{(i)} = H_j^{(i-1)} + \text{(working variable)} \pmod{2^{64}} \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \dots, 7$$

**Stage 5 — Final Hash:**
Concatenate H₀⁽ᴺ⁾, H₁⁽ᴺ⁾, …, H₇⁽ᴺ⁾ to obtain the **512-bit message digest**.

(7 marks)

---

**(b) Explain the Choice function, Majority function, Σ₀, Σ₁, σ₀, and σ₁ used in SHA-512 with their precise formulas. Why are the rotation constants 1, 7, 8, 14, 18, 19, 28, 34, 39, 41, 61 chosen? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Formulas:**

| Function | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Ch(e, f, g)** | $(e \land f) \oplus (\lnot e \land g)$ |
| **Maj(a, b, c)** | $(a \land b) \oplus (a \land c) \oplus (b \land c)$ |
| **Σ₀(a)** | $\text{ROTR}^{28}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{34}(a) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{39}(a)$ |
| **Σ₁(e)** | $\text{ROTR}^{14}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(e) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{41}(e)$ |
| **σ₀(x)** | $\text{ROTR}^{1}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{8}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{7}(x)$ |
| **σ₁(x)** | $\text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{6}(x)$ |

**Role of each function:**

- **Ch and Maj** provide **non-linearity** — without them, the entire hash would be a linear system of equations and trivially invertible.
- **Σ₀ and Σ₁** provide **long-range diffusion** in the round function.
- **σ₀ and σ₁** provide **mid-range diffusion** in the message schedule.

**Why the rotation constants?** The constants (1, 7, 8, 14, 18, 19, 28, 34, 39, 41, 61) are carefully chosen so that:
1. Each output bit of Σ₀, Σ₁, σ₀, σ₁ depends on **at least three different input bits** spread across the 64-bit word.
2. After **just a few rounds**, every output bit of the hash depends on **every input bit** of the message (rapid avalanche).
3. The constants avoid simple patterns (no two are too close, no two differ by powers of two) — preventing algebraic attacks.
4. They were validated by NIST through extensive cryptanalysis during the SHA-2 standardization process. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ROTR with SHR:** ROTR is a *rotation* (bits wrap around); SHR is a *shift* (bits are discarded, zero-fill). σ₀ and σ₁ use **one SHR**, while Σ₀ and Σ₁ use **only rotations**. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting "modulo 2⁶⁴":** All additions in SHA-512 are **mod 2⁶⁴**, not ordinary integer addition. Examiners deduct marks for omission.
> 3. **Wrong rotation values:** The constants 28, 34, 39, 14, 18, 41, 1, 8, 7, 19, 61 are **standardized** — students often swap Σ₀ and Σ₁ constants.
> 4. **Mixing up σ (small) and Σ (big):** σ₀ and σ₁ are used in the *message schedule*; Σ₀ and Σ₁ are used in the *round function*.
> 5. **Skipping the T₁ reuse in a and e:** Many students forget to mention that **T₁ appears in both** a and e updates — this is a key design feature.
> 6. **Not writing the chaining equation** $H_j^{(i)} = H_j^{(i-1)} + \text{variable} \pmod{2^{64}}$ — this is essential for full marks in 14-mark questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — KTU Module 3: SHA-512**

- [x] SHA-512 produces a **512-bit** digest from inputs up to **2¹²⁸ − 1 bits**.
- [x] Block size is **1024 bits**, word size is **64 bits**, **80 rounds**.
- [x] Padding: append **1 bit**, then **0 bits** until length ≡ 896 (mod 1024), then append **64-bit length**.
- [x] Initial hash values (H₀ to H₇) come from **fractional parts of square roots of first 8 primes**.
- [x] Round constants K₀ to K₇₉ come from **fractional parts of cube roots of first 80 primes**.
- [x] Message schedule: Wₜ = M_i,t for t = 0 to 15; Wₜ = σ₁(W_{t−2}) + W_{t−7} + σ₀(W_{t−15}) + W_{t−16} for t = 16 to 79.
- [x] Round function: T₁ = h + Ch(e,f,g) + Σ₁(e) + Wₜ + Kₜ; T₂ = Σ₀(a) + Maj(a,b,c).
- [x] Working variable update: a = T₁ + T₂; e = d + T₁; others shift down by one position.
- [x] Intermediate hash: Hⱼ⁽ⁱ⁾ = Hⱼ⁽ⁱ⁻¹⁾ + working variable, all mod 2⁶⁴.
- [x] **Ch(e,f,g)** = (e∧f) ⊕ (¬e∧g) — bit selector.
- [x] **Maj(a,b,c)** = (a∧b) ⊕ (a∧c) ⊕ (b∧c) — majority voter.
- [x] **Σ₀(a)** uses rotations 28, 34, 39.
- [x] **Σ₁(e)** uses rotations 14, 18, 41.
- [x] **σ₀(x)** uses ROTR¹, ROTR⁸, SHR⁷.
- [x] **σ₁(x)** uses ROTR¹⁹, ROTR⁶¹, SHR⁶.
- [x] All additions in SHA-512 are **modulo 2⁶⁴**.
- [x] **ROTR** wraps bits around; **SHR** discards bits and zero-fills.
- [x] SHA-512 is part of the **SHA-2 family** (FIPS 180-4), successor to **SHA-1** (broken).
- [x] Compared to MD5 (128-bit) and SHA-1 (160-bit), SHA-512 offers superior **collision resistance**.
- [x] Used in **TLS 1.3, digital signatures, blockchain, file integrity, HMAC**.
- [x] The 80-round structure with T₁ feedback in both a and e ensures **strong diffusion and non-linearity** — the cryptographic core of SHA-512.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Authentication Code (MAC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Message Authentication Code (MAC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a **cryptographic checksum** or a **fixed-size tag** generated using a **secret symmetric key** $K$ shared between the sender and the receiver. It is appended to a message $M$ to provide **integrity** and **source authentication** — but **not confidentiality** — of the message.

Mathematically, a MAC is a family of functions $\{\text{MAC}_K\}_{K \in \mathcal{K}}$ such that:

$$\text{MAC}_K : \{0,1\}^* \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$

where the output is a fixed-length tag $T$ of length **n bits** (typically **64, 96, 128, or 256 bits**), and $K$ is drawn from the key space $\mathcal{K}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Message Authentication Code (MAC) is a small fixed-size block of data generated using a secret key and appended to the message. It is used to authenticate the message, ensuring that the message originated from the claimed sender and has not been altered in transit."*

The verification equation on the receiver side is:

$$V(M', T', K) = \begin{cases} \text{Valid} & \text{if } \text{MAC}_K(M') = T' \\ \text{Invalid} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Wax Seal of the Medieval Royal Courier

Imagine a **king** (the sender) writes a military command scroll. He presses it with his **royal wax seal bearing his unique insignia** (this seal mold is the **shared secret key** $K$, known only to the king and his trusted general). The wax imprint is the **MAC tag**.

When the **general** (the receiver) receives the scroll, he:
1. Compares the wax imprint on the seal with the **imprint mold he has** (verification using the shared key).
2. If the wax seal is **broken, scratched, or absent**, he knows either the messenger tampered with it, or an impostor forged a new seal — the message is **rejected**.
3. If the seal matches perfectly, he trusts the message came from the **king himself** (authentication) and was **not altered in transit** (integrity).

> **The wax seal is NOT encrypted. The scroll can still be read by anyone who intercepts it. This is the exact nature of MAC — authentication WITHOUT confidentiality.**

If the king also wanted the scroll to be unreadable, he would have used a **cipher** (encryption) as well. Together, **MAC + Encryption = Authenticated Encryption**.

## 1.3 What MAC Provides vs What It Does NOT Provide

| Property Provided by MAC | Property NOT Provided |
|--------------------------|------------------------|
| ✅ **Message Integrity** (message has not been modified) | ❌ **Confidentiality** (message is in plaintext) |
| ✅ **Source Authentication** (sender is verified) | ❌ **Non-Repudiation** (sender can deny sending) |
| ✅ **Protection against replay attacks** (with sequence numbers) | ❌ Protection against an insider with the key |
| ✅ **Protection against forgery** (forging a valid tag without $K$ is computationally infeasible) | ❌ Identification of the specific forger (only the shared key holder could create it) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MAC does NOT provide non-repudiation:** Since both Alice and Bob share the *same* symmetric key $K$, either of them could have produced the MAC. A third party (e.g., a judge) cannot determine *which* of them actually did. This is the **fundamental difference between MAC and Digital Signatures** (covered later in Module 3).

## 1.4 MAC vs Other Cryptographic Primitives

$$\boxed{\text{Hash Function} \neq \text{MAC} \neq \text{Digital Signature}}$$

- A **Hash Function** $H(M)$ takes only a message and produces a digest. It provides only **integrity**, NOT authentication (anyone can compute the hash).
- A **MAC** takes a message AND a secret key, providing both **integrity + authentication**.
- A **Digital Signature** uses **asymmetric key pairs** (private for signing, public for verifying), providing **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The MAC Lifecycle

The MAC mechanism operates in **three structured phases**:

### Phase 1: Key Establishment (Out-of-Band)
- Sender (Alice) and Receiver (Bob) **agree on a shared symmetric secret key $K$** using a secure key exchange protocol (e.g., Diffie-Hellman, public-key encryption, or pre-shared key).
- This step is **cryptographically critical** — if $K$ is compromised, the entire MAC scheme collapses.

### Phase 2: Tag Generation (Sender Side — Alice)
1. Alice composes message $M$.
2. She computes the tag $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ using the agreed algorithm.
3. She transmits the pair $(M, T)$ over the insecure channel.

### Phase 3: Tag Verification (Receiver Side — Bob)
1. Bob receives $(M', T')$.
2. He independently computes $T_{\text{computed}} = \text{MAC}_K(M')$.
3. He compares $T_{\text{computed}} \stackrel{?}{=} T'$ in **constant time** (to prevent timing attacks).
4. If equal → accept; if different → reject.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Security Property:** The comparison `T_computed == T'` **MUST** be done using a constant-time comparison function (e.g., `CRYPTO_memcmp` in OpenSSL). Standard `==` operator is **vulnerable to timing side-channel attacks** and will cost you marks if mentioned in design questions.

## 2.2 Security Requirements of a MAC (KTU Board-Favorite Theory)

For a MAC to be considered **cryptographically secure**, the following properties must hold:

1. **Compression / Fixed Output:** The MAC tag is a **fixed-length** value regardless of message length.
2. **Easy Computation:** Given $K$ and $M$, computing $T$ is computationally efficient.
3. **Easy Verification:** Given $K$, $M$, and $T$, verification is efficient.
4. **Existential Unforgeability under Chosen Message Attack (EUF-CMA):** Without knowing $K$, an adversary (even with access to a MAC oracle for chosen messages) **cannot forge a valid tag** for a new message. This is the **gold standard** for MAC security.
5. **Key Recovery Resistance:** The MAC must not leak the secret key $K$.
6. **Collision Resistance (Strong MAC):** It must be computationally hard to find two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $\text{MAC}_K(M_1) = \text{MAC}_K(M_2)$.
7. **Pseudorandomness:** The MAC function behaves as a **pseudorandom function (PRF)** — its outputs are indistinguishable from random strings without the key.

## 2.3 Types of MAC Construction (Hierarchical Classification)

```
MAC
├── Keyed Hash MACs
│   ├── HMAC (Hash-based MAC) → FIPS 198-1
│   ├── MD5-MAC, SHA-1-MAC, SHA-256-MAC
│   └── (Deprecated: MD5, SHA-1)
├── Block Cipher-based MACs
│   ├── DAA / CBC-MAC (FIPS 113, DES-based)
│   ├── EMAC (Encrypted MAC)
│   ├── CMAC (NIST SP 800-38B, AES-based)
│   ├── PMAC (Parallelizable MAC)
│   └── GMAC (GCM mode component)
├── Universal Hashing-based MACs
│   ├── UMAC
│   └── Poly1305
└── Specialized MACs
    ├── SipHash (network packets)
    └── VMAC
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|------|
| MAC tag length (HMAC-SHA-256) | $n$ | **256** | bits |
| MAC tag length (HMAC-MD5) | $n$ | **128** | bits |
| MAC tag length (DAA/DES) | $n$ | **64** | bits |
| Block size of underlying hash (SHA-256) | $B$ | **64** | bytes (512 bits) |
| Security strength (HMAC-SHA-256) | — | **256** | bits |
| Birthday bound for forgery | $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ | For $n=64$: $2^{32}$ | MAC queries |
| HMAC equation | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\big((K_0 \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \Vert M)\big)$ | — | — |
| CMAC final block | $T_n = E_K(M_n \oplus C_n)$ | — | — |
| DAA (CBC-MAC) | $C_i = E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, $C_0 = \text{IV}$ | — | — |
| Sub-key generation in CMAC | $L = E_K(0^n)$, then derive $K_1, K_2$ via $\times 2, \times 4$ in $GF(2^{128})$ | — | — |
| Poly1305 key | $r \in \{2^{106} < r < 2^{124}\}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT use the vertical bar `\vert` symbol in table cells** — KTU examiners parse markdown strictly. The above uses mathematical notation compatible with LaTeX renderers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **TLS 1.3 (Transport Layer Security):** Uses **HMAC-SHA-256** in the TLS record protocol and **AEAD ciphersuites** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) for MAC-then-encrypt / encrypt-then-MAC.
- **IPsec (ESP protocol):** Uses **HMAC-SHA-256-128** for ESP authentication header.
- **SSH (Secure Shell):** Uses **HMAC-SHA-256** or **HMAC-SHA-512** for packet integrity.
- **Banking (EMV Chip Cards):** Uses **CMAC-AES** for transaction MAC.
- **Wireless (WPA3):** Uses **BIP-GMAC-256** for frame integrity.
- **AWS SigV4 (Cloud API Authentication):** Uses **HMAC-SHA-256** over canonical request.
- **JSON Web Tokens (JWT):** Uses **HMAC-SHA-256** (HS256) for token signing.

## 2.6 Cryptographic Attacks on MAC (KTU Theory)

| Attack | Adversary Goal | Complexity |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **Forgery Attack** | Produce valid $(M, T)$ pair for a new $M$ | Should be $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ |
| **Key Recovery Attack** | Recover the secret key $K$ | Should be $\mathcal{O}(2^{\vert K \vert})$ |
| **Birthday Attack** | Find $M_1 \neq M_2$ with same tag | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ |
| **Length Extension Attack** | Extend $H(M)$ to $H(M \Vert M')$ without key | Applies to $H(M \Vert K)$, NOT HMAC |
| **Replay Attack** | Resend a previously valid $(M, T)$ | Mitigated by sequence numbers/timestamps |
| **Timing Attack** | Infer $K$ from execution time | Mitigated by constant-time comparison |
| **Brute Force on Tag** | Find correct tag by exhaustive search | $2^n$ attempts |
| **Brute Force on Key** | Try all possible keys | $2^{\vert K \vert}$ attempts |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DAA (Data Authentication Algorithm — FIPS PUB 113)

The **Data Authentication Algorithm (DAA)** is based on **DES operating in CBC mode (Cipher Block Chaining)**. It is a KTU **board-exam favorite** for Module 3 derivations.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Notation

- **Underlying cipher:** DES (block size $b = 64$ bits, key size $k = 56$ bits)
- **Padding:** Zero-padding to the right so message length is a multiple of 64 bits.
- **Initial Value (IV):** $C_0 = 0$ (all 64 bits zero) for DAA.
- **Secret key:** $K$ shared between sender and receiver.

### 3.1.2 Tag Generation Algorithm

For a message $M$ of $L$ blocks, where $M = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert M_L$ and each $M_i$ is **64 bits**:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^{64} \quad \text{(64-bit zero initialization vector, mandatory for DAA)} \\
C_1 &= E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) \\
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) \\
C_3 &= E_K(M_3 \oplus C_2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
C_L &= E_K(M_L \oplus C_{L-1}) \\
T &= C_L \quad \text{(the final 64-bit ciphertext block is the MAC tag)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Note:** In the DAA (FIPS 113) standard, **only the final block** $C_L$ is transmitted as the MAC. The intermediate ciphertext blocks are **NOT** sent. This is the difference between DAA and full CBC encryption.

### 3.1.3 Verification Algorithm

The receiver, upon receiving $M$ and the tag $T$:

1. Zero-pads $M$ to a multiple of 64 bits.
2. Performs the **identical CBC chain** using the same $K$ and $C_0 = 0$.
3. Computes $C_L'$ (the final block).
4. **Accepts** if and only if $C_L' = T$. Otherwise, **rejects**.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (DAA on a 2-Block Message)

Let $M = M_1 \Vert M_2$ with each block being 64 bits, and the shared key is $K$. Assume for illustration:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= 0x0123456789ABCDEF \\
M_2 &= 0xFEDCBA9876543210 \\
K &= 0x133457799BBCDFF1 \quad \text{(standard DES test key)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Initialize:**

$$C_0 = 0x0000000000000000$$

**Step 2 — Process Block 1:**

$$C_1 = E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = E_K(0x0123456789ABCDEF)$$

Using DES encryption, the result is a 64-bit ciphertext (say) $C_1 = 0x85E813540F0AB405$.

**Step 3 — Process Block 2:**

$$C_2 = E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) = E_K(0xFEDCBA9876543210 \oplus 0x85E813540F0AB405)$$

XOR computation:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_2 \oplus C_1 &= 0xFEDCBA9876543210 \oplus 0x85E813540F0AB405 \\
&= 0x7B34A9CC795E8615 \quad \text{(bitwise XOR, block-wise)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$C_2 = E_K(0x7B34A9CC795E8615) = 0xA1B2C3D4E5F60718 \quad \text{(illustrative output)}$$

**Step 4 — Output the Tag:**

$$T = C_2 = 0xA1B2C3D4E5F60718$$

**Step 5 — Verification on Receiver Side:**

The receiver repeats Steps 1-4 using the same $K$ and $M$, and confirms the final block equals the received $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Birthday Attack Vulnerability of DAA:** Since the tag is only **64 bits**, an adversary can find a collision in approximately $2^{32}$ MAC queries. This is why **DAA is considered obsolete** by modern standards — replaced by **CMAC (AES-based, 128-bit tag)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CMAC (Cipher-based MAC — NIST SP 800-38B)

**CMAC** fixes the security flaw of plain CBC-MAC for variable-length messages by introducing **two derived sub-keys** $K_1$ and $K_2$ derived from $E_K(0^b)$.

### 3.2.1 Sub-Key Generation

Let $L = E_K(0^b)$ (encrypt the all-zero block with key $K$).

Define the multiplication by $x$ in $GF(2^b)$:

- $K_1 = L \cdot x$, where the **leftmost bit** of $L$ is checked:
  - If leftmost bit of $L$ is **0**: $K_1 = L \ll 1$ (left shift by 1).
  - If leftmost bit of $L$ is **1**: $K_1 = (L \ll 1) \oplus R_b$, where $R_b$ is the **AES reduction constant** for $b=128$: $R_{128} = 0^{120} \Vert 10000111 = 0x87$.

- $K_2 = K_1 \cdot x$ (similarly derived from $K_1$).

$$\boxed{K_1 = \begin{cases} L \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{msb}(L) = 0 \\ (L \ll 1) \oplus 0x87 & \text{if } \text{msb}(L) = 1 \end{cases}}$$

### 3.2.2 CMAC Tag Generation for Message $M$ of $n$ Blocks

Let $M = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert M_n$.

**Case 1: Message is complete (i.e., $M_n$ is a full $b$-bit block, $\vert M \vert = nb$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^b \\
C_1 &= E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) \\
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
C_n &= E_K((M_n \oplus K_1) \oplus C_{n-1}) \\
T &= C_n
\end{aligned}$$

**Case 2: Message requires padding (i.e., last block is incomplete):**

The last block is padded with a single "1" bit followed by zeros to form $M_n$ of size $b$ bits. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_n &= E_K((M_n \oplus K_2) \oplus C_{n-1}) \\
T &= C_n
\end{aligned}$$

The receiver performs the **same procedure** and accepts iff the computed $T$ equals the received tag.

### 3.2.3 Worked Example: CMAC with AES-128 on a 2-Block Message

Let $b = 128$, $K$ is a 128-bit AES key, $M = M_1 \Vert M_2$ where both blocks are 128 bits.

**Step 1 — Sub-key derivation:**

$$L = E_K(0^{128})$$

Assume $L = 0x9F1B3C5D7E8F0A1B2C3D4E5F60718293$.

- Check leftmost bit of $L$: `9` in binary is `1001` → leftmost bit is **1**.
- Apply the rule:

$$K_1 = (L \ll 1) \oplus 0x87 \text{ (only on the least significant byte)}$$

The least significant byte is `0x93 = 10010011`. Left shift gives `00100110 = 0x26`, and the carry out is `1`. So we XOR with $R_b$ in the MSB position. The full operation:

$$K_1 = 0x3E3678BAFD1E54369A7B8CAF0C0E3052 \quad \text{(illustrative)}$$

Then:

$$K_2 = (K_1 \ll 1) \oplus \text{(conditional 0x87)}$$

**Step 2 — Process blocks:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^{128} \\
C_1 &= \text{AES}_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = \text{AES}_K(M_1) \\
C_2 &= \text{AES}_K((M_2 \oplus K_1) \oplus C_1) \\
T &= C_2
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: HMAC (Hash-based MAC — FIPS 198-1)

**HMAC** is the **most widely used MAC in industry** (TLS, IPsec, JWT, AWS SigV4). It is constructed by applying a hash function $H$ twice with two derived keys.

### 3.3.1 HMAC Construction

Define:

- $B$ = byte-length of the input block of $H$ (e.g., $B = 64$ for SHA-256).
- $L$ = byte-length of the output of $H$ (e.g., $L = 32$ for SHA-256).
- $K$ = secret key of any length.
- $K_0$ = key normalized to $B$ bytes:
  - If $\vert K \vert < B$: pad with zeros to $B$ bytes.
  - If $\vert K \vert > B$: replace with $K_0 = H(K)$ then pad to $B$ bytes.
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times (inner pad).
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times (outer pad).

**HMAC Equation:**

$$\boxed{\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \;\Vert\; H\big( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M \big) \Big)}$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Computation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } K_0 &= \text{normalize } K \text{ to } B \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Step 2: } X &= K_0 \oplus \text{ipad} \quad \text{(inner key, } B \text{ bytes)} \\
\text{Step 3: } Y &= X \Vert M \quad \text{(prepend inner key to message)} \\
\text{Step 4: } H_{\text{in}} &= H(Y) \quad \text{(inner hash, } L \text{ bytes)} \\
\text{Step 5: } X' &= K_0 \oplus \text{opad} \quad \text{(outer key, } B \text{ bytes)} \\
\text{Step 6: } Z &= X' \Vert H_{\text{in}} \quad \text{(prepend outer key to inner hash)} \\
\text{Step 7: } T &= H(Z) \quad \text{(outer hash, this is the MAC tag)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Example: HMAC-SHA-256 with $B=64$, $L=32$

Let $K = \text{"secret"}$ (6 bytes), $M = \text{"hello world"}$ (11 bytes).

**Step 1 — Normalize key:** Pad to 64 bytes:

$$K_0 = 0x736563726574 \Vert 0x00^{58} = \text{(64 bytes total)}$$

**Step 2 — Inner key (XOR with ipad = 0x36 repeated):**

$$X = K_0 \oplus \underbrace{0x36363636\ldots 0x36}_{64 \text{ bytes}}$$

For the first 6 bytes: `0x73 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x45`, `0x65 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x53`, `0x63 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x55`, `0x72 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x44`, `0x65 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x53`, `0x74 ⊕ 0x36 = 0x42` → `0x455355445342...`

**Step 3 — Concatenate with message:** $Y = X \Vert \text{"hello world"}$ (75 bytes).

**Step 4 — Inner SHA-256:** $H_{\text{in}} = \text{SHA-256}(Y)$ → 32 bytes.

**Step 5 — Outer key (XOR with opad = 0x5C repeated):**

$$X' = K_0 \oplus \underbrace{0x5C5C5C5C\ldots 0x5C}_{64 \text{ bytes}}$$

**Step 6 — Concatenate:** $Z = X' \Vert H_{\text{in}}$ (96 bytes).

**Step 7 — Outer SHA-256:** $T = \text{SHA-256}(Z)$ → 32-byte MAC tag.

---

## 3.4 Security Analysis: Birthday Attack on MAC

Given a MAC with **n-bit tag**, an attacker can find a collision (two distinct messages with the same tag) in approximately:

$$\boxed{q \approx 1.17 \cdot \sqrt{2^n} = 1.17 \cdot 2^{n/2}}$$

MAC queries by the **Birthday Paradox**.

**For n = 64 bits** (DAA, CMAC truncated): $q \approx 2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^9$ queries — **practical attack in 2024**.

**For n = 128 bits** (full CMAC, GMAC): $q \approx 2^{64}$ queries — **computationally infeasible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Worthy Insight:** This is why modern MACs use **128-bit or 256-bit tags** and why **DAA (64-bit tag) is deprecated**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level MAC Generation & Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sender Alice has Message M and Secret Key K"] --> B["Compute Tag T = MAC_K M"]
    B --> C["Transmit Pair M, T over Insecure Channel"]
    C --> D{"Adversary Eve Intercepts?"}
    D -- "Tries to alter M or T" --> E["Tampered Pair M', T' reaches Bob"]
    D -- "Just eavesdrops" --> F["Bob receives intact M, T"]
    E --> G["Receiver Bob uses shared Key K to recompute T_calc = MAC_K M'"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"T_calc equals T' ?"}
    H -- "Yes - Accept Message" --> I["Message Authenticated and Intact"]
    H -- "No - Reject Message" --> J["Tampering Detected - Drop Message"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of HMAC Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0["K0 normalized key B bytes"] --> XOR1
    K0 --> XOR2
    IPAD["ipad = 0x36 repeated B times"] --> XOR1
    OPAD["opad = 0x5C repeated B times"] --> XOR2
    XOR1["K0 XOR ipad"] --> CAT1["Concatenate with M"]
    M["Message M"] --> CAT1
    CAT1 --> H1["Inner Hash H SHA-256"]
    H1 --> CAT2["Concatenate with K0 XOR opad"]
    XOR2["K0 XOR opad"] --> CAT2
    CAT2 --> H2["Outer Hash H SHA-256"]
    H2 --> TAG["MAC Tag T of L bytes"]
```

## 4.3 CBC-MAC (DAA) Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV["C0 = 0 zero block"] --> XOR1
    M1["Message Block M1"] --> XOR1
    XOR1["XOR"] --> E1["Encrypt E_K with DES"]
    E1 --> C1["Ciphertext C1"]
    C1 --> XOR2
    M2["Message Block M2"] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> E2["Encrypt E_K"]
    E2 --> C2["Ciphertext C2"]
    C2 --> XOR3
    M3["Message Block Mn"] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> E3["Encrypt E_K"]
    E3 --> CL["Final Block CL is the MAC tag T"]
```

## 4.4 CMAC Sub-Key Generation and Processing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L["L = E_K zero block"] --> CHECK{"MSB of L equals 1?"}
    CHECK -- "No" --> SHIFT1["L shifted left by 1 bit"]
    CHECK -- "Yes" --> SHIFT2["L shifted left by 1 bit"]
    SHIFT1 --> K1["Sub-key K1"]
    SHIFT2 --> XOR_R["XOR with constant 0x87 for AES-128"]
    XOR_R --> K1B["Sub-key K1"]
    K1B --> K2_DERIVE["Apply same logic to derive K2"]
    K1 --> K2["Sub-key K2"]
    K2 --> K2_DERIVE
    M1["Block M1"] --> CBC1["CBC processing using K"]
    CBC1 --> MN["Last Block Mn"]
    MN --> Q{"Is Mn a complete block?"}
    Q -- "Yes complete block" --> USEK1["Mn XOR K1 before final encryption"]
    Q -- "No partial block" --> PAD["Pad with 1 followed by zeros"]
    PAD --> USEK2["Padded Mn XOR K2 before final encryption"]
    USEK1 --> FINAL["E_K then output T"]
    USEK2 --> FINAL
```

## 4.5 MAC Threat Model — Adversary Capabilities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ADV["Adversary Eve"] --> KMA["Key-only Attack knows nothing"]
    ADV --> CMA["Chosen Message Attack queries MAC oracle for chosen messages"]
    ADV --> KPA["Known Message Attack observes some M, T pairs"]
    KMA --> FORGE1["Forgery possible if MAC is weak"]
    CMA --> FORGE2["Existential Unforgeability under CMA EUF-CMA must hold"]
    KPA --> FORGE3["Should not help find new forgeries"]
    CMA --> REPLAY["Replay Attack if no freshness"]
    REPLAY --> MIT1["Mitigation: timestamps and sequence numbers"]
    FORGE2 --> MIT2["Mitigation: minimum 128-bit tag length"]
```

## 4.6 Authenticated Encryption (MAC + Encryption) Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT["Plaintext Message M"] --> ENC["Encryption E with Key K1"]
    K1["Symmetric Key K1"] --> ENC
    ENC --> CT["Ciphertext C"]
    PT --> MAC["MAC computation with Key K2"]
    K2["Symmetric Key K2"] --> MAC
    MAC --> TAG["Tag T"]
    CT --> TX["Transmit C, T"]
    TAG --> TX
    TX --> RX["Receiver decrypts C to get M"]
    RX --> VFD["Recomputes T and compares"]
    VFD --> OUT["Plaintext plus authentication decision"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Define Message Authentication Code (MAC). List any FOUR security requirements of a MAC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Message Authentication Code (MAC) is a cryptographic checksum generated using a symmetric secret key $K$ shared between the sender and the receiver. It is appended to a message to provide **message integrity** and **source authentication** without providing **confidentiality** or **non-repudiation**.

**Four Security Requirements (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Existential Unforgeability under Chosen Message Attack (EUF-CMA):** An adversary without knowledge of $K$ must be unable to produce a valid tag for any new message, even with access to a MAC oracle.

2. **Key Recovery Resistance:** The MAC function must not leak any information about the secret key $K$ even after observing many message-tag pairs.

3. **Collision Resistance (Strong MAC):** It must be computationally infeasible to find two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $\text{MAC}_K(M_1) = \text{MAC}_K(M_2)$.

4. **Pseudorandomness / Fixed-Output Length:** The MAC must produce a fixed-length tag (typically **64, 128, or 256 bits**) whose output is computationally indistinguishable from a random string, regardless of message length.

5. (Bonus, optional 5th) **Easy Computation and Verification** for legitimate users.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **Differentiate between MAC and Digital Signature. Why can't MAC provide non-repudiation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | MAC | Digital Signature |
|---------|-----|--------------------|
| Key type | **Symmetric** (single shared key) | **Asymmetric** (private-public key pair) |
| Signature generation | Sender uses $K$ to compute $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ | Sender uses private key $K_{pr}$ to sign: $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{pr}}(M)$ |
| Verification | Receiver uses **same** $K$ | Verifier uses sender's **public** key $K_{pu}$ |
| Speed | **Fast** (symmetric primitives) | **Slower** (modular exponentiation / elliptic curves) |
| Non-repudiation | ❌ NOT provided | ✅ **Provided** |
| Confidentiality | ❌ NOT provided (plaintext sent) | ❌ NOT provided by signature alone |

**Why MAC cannot provide non-repudiation (1 Mark):**

Since MAC uses a **shared symmetric key** $K$, both the sender and the receiver possess the same key. A third party (e.g., a judge or arbitrator) has no way to determine **which** of the two parties actually generated a given MAC tag, because either one of them could have produced it. Therefore, the sender can always **repudiate** (deny) sending the message, claiming the receiver forged it. This is fundamentally impossible with a **digital signature**, where only the sender has the private signing key.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Module 3 Choice Question (14 Marks Total)

> **Module 3 Choice Question:** *Attempt any ONE of the following two questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HMAC** construction. Show the mathematical equation for HMAC and explain the role of the inner pad ($\text{ipad}$) and outer pad ($\text{opad}$). Why is HMAC resistant to length-extension attacks that affect $H(K \Vert M)$ constructions?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A system uses **CMAC-AES-128** for message authentication. Given a 2-block message $M = M_1 \Vert M_2$ where each block is 128 bits, derive the CMAC tag generation algorithm. Show the sub-key generation step $K_1$ derivation in detail, including the role of the constant $0x87$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — HMAC Construction (7 Marks)

**HMAC Equation (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\big( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \;\Vert\; H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M) \big)}$$

**Role of $K_0$ (0.5 Mark):** $K_0$ is the key normalized to $B$ bytes (the input block size of the hash function). If $K$ is shorter than $B$ bytes, it is right-padded with zeros. If $K$ is longer than $B$ bytes, it is first hashed: $K_0 = H(K) \Vert 0^{B-L}$.

**Role of $\text{ipad}$ and $\text{opad}$ (1 Mark):**
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times.
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times.
- These are **two different fixed constants** chosen such that $\text{ipad} \neq \text{opad}$ and they have a specific Hamming distance property from each other.
- They are XORed with $K_0$ to produce **two cryptographically independent keys** derived from the same secret $K$, one for the **inner hash** and one for the **outer hash**. This creates a **two-level nested construction**.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to **Section 4.2** above for the Mermaid block diagram of HMAC.

**Why HMAC resists length-extension attack (1.5 Marks):**

In the naive construction $H(K \Vert M)$, an attacker who knows $H(K \Vert M)$ and $\vert M \vert$ can compute $H(K \Vert M \Vert M')$ for any $M'$ without knowing $K$, because they can simply **extend** the internal hash state of $H$ with $M'$. This is the **length-extension vulnerability** of Merkle-Damgård hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-512, etc.).

In HMAC, however, the attacker would need to compute:
$$H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M \Vert M'))$$

The attacker does **not know** the inner hash $H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M \Vert M')$ — they only know the final output $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H(\ldots))$. Even if they attempt length extension on the outer hash, they do not know the **full state of the inner hash**, so the attack fails. The two nested hash computations with key-derived pads create a **sealed envelope** that prevents state continuation attacks.

**HMAC Security Proof (1 Mark):** HMAC is provably a PRF (pseudorandom function) under the assumption that the compression function $f$ of $H$ is a PRF. Its security is bounded by:

$$\text{Adv}^{\text{PRF}}_{\text{HMAC}} \leq \text{Adv}^{PRF}_{f} + \frac{q^2}{2^L} + \frac{q^2}{2^B}$$

where $q$ is the number of MAC queries.

**Key Properties of HMAC (1 Mark):**
- Standardized in **FIPS 198-1** and **RFC 2104**.
- Can use any iterative hash function (SHA-256, SHA-512, SHA-3).
- Used in **TLS, IPsec, SSH, JWT, AWS SigV4, OAuth 2.0**.

---

### Part (b) — CMAC Tag Generation (7 Marks)

**Sub-Key Generation (2 Marks):**

Given AES-128, the block size $b = 128$ bits.

**Step 1:** Compute $L = E_K(0^{128})$, i.e., encrypt the 128-bit all-zero block with the secret key $K$ using AES.

**Step 2:** Derive $K_1$:

$$K_1 = \begin{cases} L \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{msb}(L) = 0 \\ (L \ll 1) \oplus 0x87 & \text{if } \text{msb}(L) = 1 \end{cases}$$

The constant $0x87 = 10000111_2$ in binary is the **AES reduction polynomial constant** for the field $GF(2^{128})$ with the irreducible polynomial $x^{128} + x^7 + x^2 + x + 1$. This is required because the multiplication by $x$ in $GF(2^{128})$ requires modular reduction to keep the result within 128 bits.

**Step 3:** Derive $K_2$ from $K_1$ using the **same rule** (i.e., $K_2 = K_1 \cdot x$ in $GF(2^{128})$).

**CMAC Tag Generation (3 Marks):**

For a 2-block message $M = M_1 \Vert M_2$ (both 128 bits complete):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^{128} \\
C_1 &= \text{AES}_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = \text{AES}_K(M_1) \\
C_2 &= \text{AES}_K\big((M_2 \oplus K_1) \oplus C_1\big) \\
T &= C_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Why Sub-Keys $K_1$ and $K_2$ Are Needed (1 Mark):**

In plain CBC-MAC, the MAC is **insecure for variable-length messages** — an attacker can forge a valid MAC for a new message by combining known message-tag pairs. CMAC fixes this flaw by XORing either $K_1$ (for complete final block) or $K_2$ (for padded final block) before the final encryption, ensuring the MAC depends on the **exact length** of the message.

**Verification (1 Mark):**

The receiver applies the **identical CMAC procedure** to $M$ using the shared key $K$ and verifies:

$$V(M, T, K) = \text{Valid} \iff \text{CMAC}_K(M) = T$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Data Authentication Algorithm (DAA)** based on DES. With a clear diagram, show the CBC-MAC chain for a 3-block message. Why is the initial value (IV) fixed to zero in DAA?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Birthday Attack** on MAC algorithms. A 64-bit MAC tag is used in a banking application. Calculate the number of MAC queries an attacker needs to forge a valid tag with **50% probability** of success.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — DAA Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):** The Data Authentication Algorithm (DAA) is defined in **FIPS PUB 113 (1985)**. It uses **DES in CBC mode** with a fixed IV of zero. The MAC tag is the **last ciphertext block** of length **64 bits**.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to **Section 4.3** above for the Mermaid chain diagram.

**Algorithm for 3-Block Message (3 Marks):**

For $M = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert M_3$ with each $M_i$ being 64 bits:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^{64} \quad \text{(fixed IV for DAA)} \\
C_1 &= E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = E_K(M_1) \\
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) \\
C_3 &= E_K(M_3 \oplus C_2) \\
T &= C_3 \quad \text{(64-bit MAC tag)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why IV is Fixed to Zero in DAA (1 Mark):**

The IV is fixed to $0^{64}$ in the DAA standard because:
1. **Standardization Requirement:** Both sender and receiver must use the **same IV** to compute identical MACs. Using a random IV would require transmitting it, defeating the purpose.
2. **Determinism:** The MAC must be **deterministic** — the same $(M, K)$ must always produce the same $T$ for verification to work. A random IV would violate this.
3. **Security:** The cryptographic strength comes from the **secret key $K$** in the DES encryption, not from the IV.

**Verification (1 Mark):** The receiver recomputes the CBC chain using $C_0 = 0$, $K$, and the received message. Accepts iff the final $C_L'$ matches the received $T$.

**Limitations (Bonus 0.5 Mark):**
- Only 64-bit tag → vulnerable to birthday attack at $2^{32}$ queries.
- DES with 56-bit key is brute-forceable with modern hardware.
- **Replaced by CMAC-AES** in modern systems.

---

### Part (b) — Birthday Attack Analysis (7 Marks)

**Concept of Birthday Attack (2 Marks):**

The Birthday Attack exploits the **Birthday Paradox** from probability theory. Given a hash/MAC function producing $n$-bit output, an attacker can find a collision (two distinct inputs with the same output) in approximately $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ trials — much faster than brute-forcing the full $2^n$ output space.

**Mathematical Basis (2 Marks):**

The probability of at least one collision after $q$ random samples from a space of $2^n$ elements is approximately:

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-q(q-1) / (2 \cdot 2^n)}$$

For $P = 0.5$ (50% probability), solving:

$$1 - e^{-q^2 / (2^{n+1})} = 0.5 \implies q \approx 1.1774 \cdot \sqrt{2^n} \approx 2^{n/2}$$

**Numerical Calculation for 64-bit MAC (3 Marks):**

Given: $n = 64$ bits, target success probability $= 50\%$.

$$q \approx 2^{n/2} = 2^{64/2} = 2^{32}$$

Converting to decimal:

$$2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \approx 4.3 \times 10^9 \text{ MAC queries}$$

**Conclusion (Bonus 0.5 Mark):**

This is **computationally feasible in 2024** with a high-speed network and parallel processors (a few hours of computation). Therefore, **64-bit MAC tags are insecure** against birthday attacks in banking and other security-critical applications. The solution is to use a **MAC with at least 128-bit tag** (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256 truncated to 128 bits, or full CMAC-AES-128).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in MAC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HMAC with $H(K \Vert M)$:** HMAC is **NOT** a simple hash of key concatenated with message. It uses the **nested structure** $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M))$. Marks deducted for writing the wrong equation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize $K_0$:** If you write $H(K \Vert M)$ instead of normalizing $K_0$ to $B$ bytes, the **length-extension attack** vulnerability point is lost.
>
> 3. **DAA vs CBC-MAC Confusion:** Plain CBC-MAC is **insecure for variable-length messages**. DAA (FIPS 113) is a specific standardized variant, but it has known weaknesses. Always specify **DAA** or **CMAC** — never just "CBC-MAC" in design questions.
>
> 4. **IV in DAA:** Students often write $C_0 = \text{IV}$ as a random value. The IV is **mandatorily zero** in DAA. This is a common MCQ trap.
>
> 5. **CMAC Sub-Key Constant:** Forgetting the constant $0x87$ for AES-128 (or $0x1B$ for AES with 8-bit block size, though rare) — the $\text{msb}(L) = 1$ case will cost 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Tag Length Confusion:** Always state the tag length explicitly (e.g., 128 bits for CMAC-AES, 256 bits for HMAC-SHA-256). Examiners check this.
>
> 7. **Not Mentioning Constant-Time Comparison:** When asked about MAC verification, mentioning `memcmp` constant-time is worth a half-mark differentiator between Pass and First Class.
>
> 8. **Birthday Attack Formula:** Use $q \approx 2^{n/2}$, not $q \approx 2^n$. The square root is the entire point of the attack.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MAC (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** MAC = cryptographic checksum using shared symmetric key $K$ providing **integrity + authentication** (NOT confidentiality, NOT non-repudiation).
>
> ✅ **Equation:** $\text{MAC}_K : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ producing a **fixed-length tag** $T$.
>
> ✅ **Three Phases:** Key Establishment (out-of-band) → Tag Generation (sender) → Tag Verification (receiver).
>
> ✅ **HMAC Equation (memorize verbatim):** $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M))$
>
> ✅ **HMAC Parameters:** $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times; $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times.
>
> ✅ **HMAC Resistance:** Length-extension attack is mitigated by the **double-hash construction** with two different derived keys.
>
> ✅ **DAA:** FIPS PUB 113; uses DES in CBC mode; IV = 0; tag = last 64-bit block.
>
> ✅ **CMAC:** NIST SP 800-38B; uses AES-128; sub-keys $K_1, K_2$ derived via $GF(2^{128})$ multiplication; constant $0x87$.
>
> ✅ **Birthday Attack:** For $n$-bit tag, forgery in $2^{n/2}$ queries. **64-bit tags are obsolete.**
>
> ✅ **Comparison:** MAC is **symmetric, fast, no non-repudiation**; Digital Signature is **asymmetric, slower, has non-repudiation**.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Uses:** TLS 1.3, IPsec, SSH, JWT, AWS SigV4, WPA3, banking EMV.
>
> ✅ **Security Properties:** EUF-CMA, Key Recovery Resistance, Collision Resistance, Pseudorandomness.
>
> ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** DAA IV confusion, forgetting $0x87$ constant in CMAC, writing $H(K \Vert M)$ instead of HMAC equation, not using constant-time comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Cryptosystems (PKC) - Types of PKC, Trapdoor One-Way Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Public Key Cryptosystems (PKC) - Types of PKC, Trapdoor One-Way Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC)**, also called **Asymmetric Cryptosystem**, is a cryptographic framework in which a pair of mathematically related keys — a **Public Key** (advertised openly) and a **Private Key** (kept secret) — are used to perform encryption/decryption and digital signature operations. The security rests upon **computational infeasibility** of deriving the private key from the public key.

A PKC is formally defined as a **6-tuple** $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{T})$ where:
- $\mathcal{M}$ = Message (Plaintext) space
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Ciphertext space
- $\mathcal{K}$ = Key space (ordered pairs of public/private keys)
- $\mathcal{E}$ = Encryption algorithm
- $\mathcal{D}$ = Decryption algorithm
- $\mathcal{T}$ = Trapdoor function family

### 1.2 Trapdoor One-Way Function — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE] **Core Mathematical Definition**
> A **Trapdoor One-Way Function** $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ is a function that is:
> 1. **Easy to compute** — $\forall x \in \mathcal{X}$, $f(x)$ is computable in **polynomial time**.
> 2. **Hard to invert** — Given $y = f(x)$, finding $x$ is **computationally infeasible** without special knowledge.
> 3. **Easy to invert with trapdoor** — Given $y$ and a secret **trapdoor $t$**, $x = f^{-1}_{t}(y)$ is computable in **polynomial time**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Mailbox Analogy (PKC):**
Imagine a city with **thousands of transparent mailboxes** (Public Keys). Anyone can walk up and drop a letter inside. However, only the **owner** has the unique **physical key** (Private Key) to retrieve the letters. Even if an attacker knows the exact model of the mailbox (Public Key), he cannot forge the key without breaking the lock mechanism.

**Analogy 2 — The Paint Mixing Analogy (Trapdoor Function):**
Mixing **yellow** and **blue** paint to make **green** is easy (one-way function — easy to compute). Separating the green back into pure yellow and blue is practically **impossible**. But if you know the **exact recipe ratio** (Trapdoor), separation becomes trivial. The trapdoor is the secret ingredient!

**Analogy 3 — The Locked Diary with Combination:**
You can seal a diary with a public combination that everyone knows the *dial* to. Reading it requires the **secret PIN**. The dial is public, but the PIN sequence is the trapdoor.

### 1.4 Standard Cryptographic Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - **Key Length (n):** Measured in **bits**. Modern minimum: **2048 bits** for RSA, **256 bits** for ECC.
> - **Security Strength:** Measured in **bits of security** ($\lambda$). Example: AES-128 ≈ RSA-3072 in strength.
> - **Computational Complexity:** Expressed in **Big-O** polynomial time $O(n^k)$.
> - **Avalanche Effect:** A 1-bit input change must alter **≥ 50%** of output bits.
> - **NIST Recommended Key Sizes (2024):** RSA ≥ 2048 bits, ECC ≥ 224 bits, DSA ≥ 2048 bits.

### 1.5 Public Key vs. Symmetric Key — Quick Comparison

| Property | Symmetric Key | Public Key (Asymmetric) |
|----------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **Number of Keys** | 1 shared key | 2 (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Fast (100-1000x faster) | Slow (modular exponentiation) |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (needs secure channel) | Easy (public directory) |
| **Scalability** | $O(n^2)$ keys for $n$ users | $O(n)$ keys for $n$ users |
| **Primary Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures |
| **Example Algorithms** | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Public Key Cryptosystem

The PKC framework follows a strict sequence of operations:

**Step 1 — Key Generation Algorithm ($\mathcal{G}$):**
- Each user $U$ runs $\mathcal{G}(1^k)$ where $k$ = security parameter.
- Output: a key pair $(PK_U, SK_U)$ where $PK_U$ is the **public key** and $SK_U$ is the **secret private key**.
- Time complexity: **Polynomial** in $k$.

**Step 2 — Public Key Distribution:**
- $PK_U$ is published in a **digital certificate** (X.509 standard) or a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** repository.
- $SK_U$ never leaves the user's secure storage (HSM, TPM, or secure enclave).

**Step 3 — Encryption by Sender (Alice → Bob):**
- Alice retrieves Bob's $PK_B$ from the certificate.
- She computes $C = \mathcal{E}(PK_B, M)$ where $M$ is the plaintext.
- Only Bob, holding $SK_B$, can recover $M = \mathcal{D}(SK_B, C)$.

**Step 4 — Decryption by Receiver (Bob):**
- Bob applies $\mathcal{D}(SK_B, C)$ to recover $M$.
- Security guarantee: An eavesdropper Eve seeing $C$ and $PK_B$ cannot compute $M$ in polynomial time.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind PKC — Solving the Key Distribution Problem

In symmetric cryptography, if $n$ users need pairwise secure communication, they require $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ distinct keys. For $n = 1000$ users, that's **499,500 keys** — a logistical nightmare!

PKC reduces this to only **$2n$ keys** ($n$ public + $n$ private), making large-scale secure communication practical. This was the **revolutionary insight** of **Diffie & Hellman (1976)** in their seminal paper *"New Directions in Cryptography."*

### 2.3 Three Primary Applications of PKC

1. **Confidentiality (Encryption):** $C = \mathcal{E}(PK_B, M)$ — Only Bob's private key can decrypt.
2. **Authentication (Digital Signature):** $\sigma = \mathcal{S}(SK_A, H(M))$ where $H$ = hash function. Verifier checks $\mathcal{V}(PK_A, \sigma) = \text{True}$.
3. **Key Exchange:** Establish a **shared symmetric session key** $K_s$ over an insecure channel (e.g., Diffie-Hellman).

### 2.4 Types of Public Key Cryptosystems (KTU Exam Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Classification**
> The syllabus categorizes PKC based on the underlying **computational hardness assumption**.

| Type | Hardness Problem | Example Algorithm | Key Size (2024) |
|------|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Integer Factorization Based** | Factoring $n = p \cdot q$ | **RSA** (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman, 1977) | ≥ 2048 bits |
| **Discrete Logarithm Based (DL)** | Finding $x$ in $g^x \equiv y \pmod{p}$ | **ElGamal**, **DH**, **DSA** | ≥ 2048 bits |
| **Elliptic Curve Based (ECDLP)** | ECDLP on elliptic curve group | **ECDSA**, **ECDH**, **Ed25519** | ≥ 256 bits |
| **Lattice Based (Post-Quantum)** | Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) | **CRYSTALS-Kyber**, **NTRU** | Variable |
| **Knapsack Based** | Subset sum problem | **Merkle-Hellman (1978)** | Broken — historical only |
| **Hash Based Signatures** | Pre-image resistance of $H$ | **Lamport**, **XMSS**, **SPHINCS+** | Variable |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to prevent Markdown breakage!**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **PKC Core Equation** | $\mathcal{D}(SK, \mathcal{E}(PK, M)) = M$ | Fundamental decryption |
| **One-Way Function** | $f(x) = y$ easy; $f^{-1}(y) = x$ hard | Forward vs inverse |
| **Trapdoor Inverse** | $f^{-1}_{t}(y) = x$ easy with trapdoor $t$ | With secret knowledge |
| **RSA Encryption** | $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ | Ciphertext generation |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | Plaintext recovery |
| **RSA Key Relation** | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public-private key link |
| **Euler's Totient** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | For $n = p \cdot q$ |
| **Discrete Log** | $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$ | DL problem formulation |
| **Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret** | $K_{AB} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ | Session key establishment |
| **ElGamal Encryption** | $C_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$, $C_2 \equiv M \cdot y^k \pmod{p}$ | Probabilistic encryption |
| **Elliptic Curve Equation** | $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$ | ECC group definition |
| **ECDLP** | Given $P, Q = kP$, find $k$ | ECC hardness |
| **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(SK, H(M))$ | Authentication |
| **Signature Verification** | $\text{Verify}(PK, M, \sigma) = \text{True/False}$ | Integrity check |
| **Hash Length (SHA-256)** | **256 bits** output | Modern standard |
| **RSA Modulus** | $n = p \cdot q$ where $\vert p \vert \approx \vert q \vert \approx 1024$ bits | Secure RSA key |
| **Security Parameter** | $\lambda$ (bits of security) | Cryptographic strength |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS 1.3 Handshake:** Uses **X25519 (ECDH)** for key exchange and **RSA/ECDSA** for server authentication.
- **PGP/GPG Email Encryption:** Uses **RSA** + **AES** hybrid scheme.
- **Blockchain & Bitcoin:** Uses **ECDSA (secp256k1 curve)** for transaction signing.
- **SSH Authentication:** Uses **RSA/Ed25519** for host identity verification.
- **JWT Tokens:** Signed using **RS256 (RSA)** or **ES256 (ECDSA)** algorithms.
- **DNSSEC:** Uses **RSA** and **ECDSA** for DNS record authentication.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why RSA's Trapdoor Works (Mathematical Proof Sketch)

> [!NOTE] This derivation explains **why** RSA decryption recovers the original message using Euler's theorem.

**Starting Assumption:** For $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are distinct large primes, and $M$ is coprime to $n$:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

**This is Euler's Theorem.** Now let's derive the RSA decryption correctness.

**Step 1: Setup the Key Relation**
The public exponent $e$ and private exponent $d$ are chosen such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

**Step 2: Express the Key Relation Algebraically**
This means there exists an integer $k$ such that:

$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

**Step 3: Apply Decryption to a Ciphertext**
Bob receives $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ and computes:

$$C^d \equiv (M^e)^d \pmod{n}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the Exponent**
Using the law of exponents:

$$C^d \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Key Relation**
Substituting $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$:

$$C^d \equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 6: Apply Power Rules**

$$C^d \equiv M^1 \cdot M^{k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 7: Invoke Euler's Theorem**
Since $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$, we get $M^{k \cdot \phi(n)} \equiv 1^k \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$:

$$C^d \equiv M \cdot 1 \pmod{n}$$

**Step 8: Final Result**

$$C^d \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conclusion:** The derivation proves that **decryption with $d$ recovers $M$ exactly**. The trapdoor is the knowledge of the **prime factorization** of $n$ (which reveals $\phi(n)$), enabling computation of $d$ from $e$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Trapdoor Functions

| Function Type | Forward Computation (Easy) | Inversion (Hard) | Trapdoor (Secret) | Used By |
|---------------|----------------------------|------------------|--------------------|---------|
| **Multiplication** | $p \cdot q = n$ in $O(\log^2 n)$ | Factor $n$ into $p, q$ | Prime factors $p, q$ | **RSA** |
| **Modular Exponentiation** | $g^x \bmod p$ in $O(\log x \cdot \log^2 p)$ | Find $x$ from $g^x \bmod p$ | $\phi(p)$ or group order | **DH, ElGamal, DSA** |
| **Elliptic Curve Scalar Mult.** | $k \cdot P$ in $O(\log k)$ group ops | Find $k$ from $P, Q = kP$ | None practical (curve-dependent) | **ECDSA, ECDH** |
| **Knapsack Sum** | $\sum a_i x_i = S$ in $O(n)$ | Find $\{x_i\}$ from $S$ | Superincreasing sequence | **Merkle-Hellman** (broken) |
| **RSA-style Polynomial** | $f(x) = x^e \bmod n$ | Find $x$ from $y$ | $\phi(n)$ | **RSA family** |
| **Lattice Shortest Vector** | Encode message as lattice | Find closest lattice point | Trapdoor basis | **NTRU, Kyber** |

### 3.3 Derivation: Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret (Conceptual Steps)

**Step 1: Public Parameters (Global)**
Choose a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}^*_p$.

**Step 2: Alice's Private Operation**
Alice picks secret $a \in [1, p-2]$ and computes $A \equiv g^a \pmod{p}$.

**Step 3: Bob's Private Operation**
Bob picks secret $b \in [1, p-2]$ and computes $B \equiv g^b \pmod{p}$.

**Step 4: Public Exchange**
Alice sends $A$ to Bob; Bob sends $B$ to Alice. Both values are public.

**Step 5: Alice Computes Shared Secret**

$$K_{AB} \equiv B^a \equiv (g^b)^a \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}$$

**Step 6: Bob Computes Shared Secret**

$$K_{AB} \equiv A^b \equiv (g^a)^b \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}$$

**Step 7: Convergence**
Both arrive at the **same** $K_{AB}$. An eavesdropper seeing $A, B, g, p$ must solve the **Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH) problem**, which is equivalent to the **Discrete Logarithm** problem — believed to be hard for well-chosen parameters.

### 3.4 Worked Example: RSA Small Numerical Case

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example for KTU Board Exam Style**

**Given:** $p = 61, q = 53$, public exponent $e = 17$.

**Step 1: Compute Modulus**

$$n = p \cdot q = 61 \cdot 53 = 3233$$

**Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient**

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \cdot 52 = 3120$$

**Step 3: Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$**

$$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute Private Exponent $d$**
Find $d$ such that $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$:

$$d = 2753 \quad (\text{since } 17 \cdot 2753 = 46801 = 1 + 15 \cdot 3120)$$

**Step 5: Public Key** $= (e=17, n=3233)$; **Private Key** $= (d=2753, n=3233)$.

**Step 6: Encrypt $M = 65$**

$$C \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233} = 2790$$

**Step 7: Decrypt $C = 2790$**

$$M \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233} = 65 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.5 Complexity Analysis of Trapdoor Operations

| Operation | Best Known Algorithm | Complexity | Practical Security |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|--------------------|
| **Multiplying two primes** | Schoolbook | $O(n^2)$ | Trivial |
| **Factoring RSA modulus** | **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)** | $O(e^{(\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}})$ | Infeasible for $n \geq 2048$ bits |
| **Modular exponentiation** | Square-and-multiply | $O(\log x \cdot \log^2 p)$ | Trivial |
| **Discrete logarithm mod $p$** | **Number Field Sieve** | Sub-exponential | Infeasible for $p \geq 2048$ bits |
| **ECDLP** | **Pollard's rho** | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA security |
| **Lattice SVP** | **BKZ (Block Korkine-Zolotarev)** | $O(2^{0.292 \cdot n})$ | Post-quantum candidate |

### 3.6 Security Hierarchy of Hardness Problems

The relative difficulty ranking (from hardest to easiest to break) is:

$$\text{SIS} > \text{LWE} > \text{SVP} > \text{ECDLP} \approx \text{DLP} > \text{Integer Factorization} > \text{Diffie-Hellman} \approx \text{RSA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] This hierarchy is critical for **post-quantum cryptography** selection. Quantum computers via **Shor's Algorithm** break Factorization, DLP, and ECDLP in **polynomial time**, but lattice problems remain hard — hence the NIST PQC standardization.

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Public Key Cryptosystem — Full Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User A - Alice wants to send secure message] --> B[Alice retrieves Bobs Public Key PKB from PKI]
    B --> C[Alice computes C = E_PKB, M using Bob's public key]
    C --> D[Encrypted Ciphertext C transmitted over insecure channel]
    D --> E[Eve intercepts C and PKB - cannot decrypt without SKB]
    D --> F[Bob receives C and applies D_SKB, C]
    F --> G[Bob recovers original plaintext M]
    
    H[User B - Bob generates key pair] --> I[Key Generation: G produces PKB and SKB]
    I --> J[PKB published to PKI directory]
    I --> K[SKB stored securely in HSM or secure enclave]
    
    J --> B
    K --> F
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Y -->|Hard inversion without trapdoor| X1[Cannot recover X]
    Y -->|Easy inversion with trapdoor t| X2[Recovered X = f_inverse_t of Y]
    
    Y -.->|Eve observes Y| EV[Eve stuck - infeasible]
    Y -.->|Eve guesses t - infeasible| EV
    
    style X fill:#e1f5ff
    style Y fill:#fff4e1

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Choose two large primes p and q] --> S2[Step 2: Compute n = p times q]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Euler totient phi_n = p-1 times q-1]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Choose public exponent e coprime to phi_n]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute private exponent d such that e times d = 1 mod phi_n]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Publish Public Key = e, n]
    S5 --> S7[Step 7: Keep Private Key = d, n secret]
    S6 --> S8[Encryption: C = M to the power e mod n]
    S7 --> S9[Decryption: M = C to the power d mod n]
    S8 --> S9
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a Public Key Cryptosystem. List any two differences between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC)** is a cryptographic system that uses a pair of mathematically related keys — a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption (or vice versa for signatures). The public key is distributed freely, while the private key is kept secret.

**Two Differences:**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Public Key (Asymmetric) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **Number of Keys** | One shared key | Two keys (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slow (100–1000x slower) |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult problem | Solved via PKI |
| **Scalability** | $O(n^2)$ keys for $n$ users | $O(n)$ keys for $n$ users |

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each difference × 2 = 3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. What is a Trapdoor One-Way Function? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Trapdoor One-Way Function** $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ is a function that is:
- (1 mark) **Easy to compute** in the forward direction ($f(x)$ is polynomial-time computable).
- (1 mark) **Hard to invert** without the trapdoor (computationally infeasible).
- (1 mark) **Easy to invert with the trapdoor** $t$ (i.e., $f^{-1}_t(y)$ is polynomial-time computable given $t$).

**Example (1 mark integrated):**
**Integer multiplication vs. factorization.** Computing $n = p \cdot q$ is easy, but factoring a large $n$ back into $p$ and $q$ is infeasible. The trapdoor is the knowledge of the prime factors. This is the basis of RSA.
