| Prime | $p$ | $23$ | Given |
| Generator | $g$ | $5$ | Given |
| Private key | $x$ | $7$ | Secret |
| Public key | $y$ | $17$ | $5^7 \bmod 23$ |
| Ephemeral | $k$ | $3$ | Random, $\gcd(3,22)=1$ |
| Inverse | $k^{-1}$ | $15$ | $3 \cdot 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{22}$ |
| Sign part 1 | $r$ | $10$ | $5^3 \bmod 23$ |
| Sign part 2 | $s$ | $2$ | $15(10-70) \bmod 22$ |
| Verify $v_1$ | $y^r r^s$ | $9$ | $17^{10} \cdot 10^2 \bmod 23$ |
| Verify $v_2$ | $g^m$ | $9$ | $5^{10} \bmod 23$ |
| Result | $v_1 \equiv v_2$ | ✓ | Signature Valid |

## 3.3 Key Size & Security Analysis

The signature pair $(r, s)$ has total bit-length $2 \log_2 p$. For:
- $p \approx 2^{1024}$: signature is **2048 bits**.
- $p \approx 2^{2048}$: signature is **4096 bits**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The signature length of ElGamal is **twice** the bit-length of $p$, which is why DSA, Schnorr, and ECDSA were developed — they achieve similar security with shorter signatures (typically 320–512 bits for the same security level as 2048-bit ElGamal).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ElGamal Signing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Signer Inputs] --> B[Choose prime p]
    B --> C[Choose generator g]
    C --> D[Choose private key x in 1 to p-2]
    D --> E["Compute y = g^x mod p"]
    E --> F["Publish public key (p, g, y)"]
    F --> G[Message m received]
    G --> H[Choose random k with gcd k p-1 = 1]
    H --> I["Compute r = g^k mod p"]
    I --> J["Compute k inverse mod p-1"]
    J --> K["Compute s = k^-1 m - x r mod p-1"]
    K --> L["Output signature pair r, s"]
    L --> M[End: Send r, s with message m]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ElGamal Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Verifier Receives m, r, s] --> B[Retrieve public key p, g, y]
    B --> C{Check 1 leq r leq p-1}
    C -->|No| D[Reject Signature]
    C -->|Yes| E["Compute v1 = y^r times r^s mod p"]
    E --> F["Compute v2 = g^m mod p"]
    F --> G{v1 equals v2 mod p}
    G -->|No| D
    G -->|Yes| H[Accept Signature: Authentic and Untampered]
    D --> I[End: Verification Failed]
    H --> J[End: Verification Successful]
```

## 4.3 System-Level Interaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Signer Alice
    participant Channel as Public Channel
    participant Bob as Verifier Bob

    Alice->>Alice: Generate keys: p, g, x, y
    Alice->>Channel: Publish public tuple (p, g, y)
    Alice->>Alice: Choose random k, compute r, s
    Alice->>Channel: Send message m and signature (r, s)
    Bob->>Bob: Retrieve (p, g, y) from Alice
    Bob->>Bob: Compute v1 = y^r * r^s mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Compute v2 = g^m mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Check v1 == v2 mod p
    alt Valid
        Bob-->>Alice: Signature ACCEPTED
    else Invalid
        Bob-->>Alice: Signature REJECTED
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SKG["Signer Side"]
        A1["Key Generation Module"]
        A2["Signature Generation Module"]
    end
    subgraph VFL["Verifier Side"]
        B1["Signature Verification Module"]
        B2["Decision Output"]
    end
    A1 -->|Public key p g y| A2
    A2 -->|m, r, s| B1
    B1 -->|v1 v2| B2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the ElGamal Signature Scheme. List the three algorithmic phases and state the public parameters in each phase.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Key Generation (1 mark):** Signer chooses a large prime $p$, a primitive root $g$, a private key $x$ with $1 \leq x \leq p-2$, and computes the public key $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$. The public parameter set is $(p, g, y)$.
- **Signature Generation (1 mark):** For message $m$, the signer picks a random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ and computes $r \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$ and $s \equiv k^{-1}(m - xr) \pmod{p-1}$. The signature is the pair $(r, s)$.
- **Signature Verification (1 mark):** The verifier checks $1 \leq r \leq p-1$ and verifies $y^r \cdot r^s \equiv g^m \pmod{p}$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Why must the random value $k$ in the ElGamal Signature Scheme satisfy $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$? What happens if the same $k$ is reused for two different messages?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reason for coprimality (2 marks):** The condition $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ guarantees that the modular inverse $k^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$ exists. Without it, the computation of $s \equiv k^{-1}(m - xr) \pmod{p-1}$ would be undefined and the signature could not be produced.
- **Reuse of $k$ (1 mark):** If the same $k$ is used to sign two messages $m_1$ and $m_2$, an attacker who knows both signatures $(r, s_1)$ and $(r, s_2)$ can recover the private key $x$ by computing $x = (m_1 - m_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$, breaking the scheme completely.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, PCITT601, Q8(b)]**

**(a)** Describe the ElGamal Signature Scheme in detail. Explain the key generation, signature generation, and signature verification algorithms with the relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an ElGamal Signature Scheme, the public parameters are $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $y = 17$. The private key of the signer is $x = 7$. Compute the signature on the message $m = 10$ using the random value $k = 3$. Show all the steps of verification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Key Generation (2 marks):** The signer picks a large prime $p$, a primitive root $g$ mod $p$, a private key $x \in [1, p-2]$, and computes $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$. The public key is the triple $(p, g, y)$.
2. **Signature Generation (3 marks):** For message $m$, the signer selects a random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ and computes:
   - $r \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$
   - $s \equiv k^{-1}(m - xr) \pmod{p-1}$
   The signature is $(r, s)$.
3. **Signature Verification (2 marks):** The verifier checks $1 \leq r \leq p-1$, then verifies $y^r \cdot r^s \equiv g^m \pmod{p}$. Acceptance is given if and only if this congruence holds.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(Step 1 — Compute $r$):** $r \equiv g^k \equiv 5^3 \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$. **(1 mark)**

**(Step 2 — Find $k^{-1} \pmod{22}$):** $3 \cdot 15 = 45 \equiv 1 \pmod{22}$, so $k^{-1} = 15$. **(1 mark)**

**(Step 3 — Compute $s$):**
$s \equiv 15(10 - 7 \cdot 10) \pmod{22}$
$\equiv 15(10 - 70) \pmod{22}$
$\equiv 15(-60) \pmod{22}$
$\equiv -900 \pmod{22}$
$900 = 40 \cdot 22 + 20$, so $900 \equiv 20 \pmod{22}$, hence $-900 \equiv -20 \equiv 2 \pmod{22}$. **(2 marks)**

**Signature = $(10, 2)$.**

**(Step 4 — Verification):** Compute $v_1 \equiv y^r \cdot r^s \equiv 17^{10} \cdot 10^2 \pmod{23}$. Using repeated squaring, $17^{10} \equiv 4$ and $10^2 \equiv 8$, so $v_1 \equiv 32 \equiv 9 \pmod{23}$. Compute $v_2 \equiv g^m \equiv 5^{10} \pmod{23} \equiv 9 \pmod{23}$. **(2 marks)**

**(Step 5 — Conclusion):** $v_1 = v_2 = 9$, so the signature is **VALID**. **(1 mark)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary, PCITT601, Q9(a)]**

**(a)** Explain the security of the ElGamal Signature Scheme based on the Discrete Logarithm Problem. Discuss what happens if an attacker attempts existential forgery, and explain why the random nonce $k$ must never be reused. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given $p = 47$, $g = 5$, private key $x = 11$, message $m = 25$, and random $k = 13$, compute and verify the ElGamal signature. Show that reusing $k$ for two different messages exposes the private key. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Security foundation (2 marks):** Security rests on the hardness of the Discrete Logarithm Problem in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Recovering $x$ from $y = g^x \pmod{p}$ is computationally infeasible for large $p$.
2. **Existential forgery (2 marks):** An existential forger picks arbitrary $r, s$ values and tries to find an $m$ such that $y^r \cdot r^s \equiv g^m \pmod{p}$. This reduces to a discrete log problem in two variables, which is generally hard.
3. **Nonce reuse attack (3 marks):** If $k$ is reused across two signatures $(r, s_1)$ and $(r, s_2)$ on $m_1$ and $m_2$, then $s_1 - s_2 \equiv k^{-1}(m_1 - m_2) \pmod{p-1}$, which lets the attacker compute $k \equiv (m_1 - m_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$ and then $x \equiv k(r^{-1} \cdot \text{stuff}) \pmod{p-1}$, breaking the scheme.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(Step 1 — Compute $r$):** $r \equiv 5^{13} \pmod{47}$. Using $5^2 = 25$, $5^4 = 625 \equiv 14 \pmod{47}$, $5^8 \equiv 14^2 = 196 \equiv 8 \pmod{47}$, $5^{13} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 8 \cdot 14 \cdot 5 = 560 \equiv 560 - 11 \cdot 47 = 560 - 517 = 43 \pmod{47}$. So $r = 43$. **(1 mark)**

**(Step 2 — Compute $k^{-1} \pmod{46}$):** Solve $13u \equiv 1 \pmod{46}$. Using extended Euclidean: $46 = 3 \cdot 13 + 7$, $13 = 1 \cdot 7 + 6$, $7 = 1 \cdot 6 + 1$, back-substitution: $1 = 7 - 6 = 7 - (13 - 7) = 2 \cdot 7 - 13 = 2(46 - 3 \cdot 13) - 13 = 2 \cdot 46 - 7 \cdot 13$. So $u = -7 \equiv 39 \pmod{46}$. Check: $13 \cdot 39 = 507 = 11 \cdot 46 + 1 = 506 + 1$ ✓. So $k^{-1} = 39$. **(2 marks)**

**(Step 3 — Compute $s$):** $s \equiv 39(25 - 11 \cdot 43) \pmod{46}$. $11 \cdot 43 = 473$. $25 - 473 = -448$. $-448 \pmod{46}$: $448 = 9 \cdot 46 + 34$, so $-448 \equiv -34 \equiv 12 \pmod{46}$. Then $s \equiv 39 \cdot 12 = 468 \pmod{46}$. $468 = 10 \cdot 46 + 8 = 460 + 8$. So $s = 8$. **(2 marks)**

**Signature = $(43, 8)$.**

**(Step 4 — Verification (conceptual):** Verifier checks $y^{43} \cdot 43^8 \equiv 5^{25} \pmod{47}$ where $y = 5^{11} \pmod{47}$. (Full computation omitted here for brevity, but yields equality.) **(1 mark)**

**(Step 5 — Nonce reuse demonstration (1 mark):** If $k = 13$ is reused to sign $m' = 30$ producing $s' = 8 + k^{-1}(m' - m) = 8 + 39(5) = 8 + 195 \equiv 8 + 195 - 4 \cdot 46 = 8 + 195 - 184 = 19 \pmod{46}$, then $x = (m - m')(s - s')^{-1} \cdot (-1) \pmod{46}$ can be computed, exposing the private key.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in ElGamal Signature problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reduce modulo $p-1$ for $s$** (it is $\pmod{p-1}$, not $\pmod{p}$). This is the most common error worth 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Computing the modular inverse over the wrong modulus.** $k^{-1}$ is taken mod $p-1$, not mod $p$.
> 3. **Not verifying the coprimality condition** $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ explicitly — KTU awards half a mark for stating this.
> 4. **Mixing up encryption and signature equations** — examiners deduct a full mark for using the encryption formula in a signing problem.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — even in a signing problem, KTU requires the verification computation to be shown in full for full marks.
> 6. **Reusing $k$** — if a student does not mention the freshness of $k$, they lose 1 mark under the "design considerations" rubric.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 The ElGamal Signature Scheme is **non-deterministic** — same message and key produce different signatures due to random $k$.
- 🔑 The signature is a **pair $(r, s)$** where $r = g^k \bmod p$ and $s = k^{-1}(m - xr) \bmod (p-1)$.
- 🔑 The **verification equation** is the cornerstone: $y^r \cdot r^s \equiv g^m \pmod{p}$.
- 🔑 $k$ **must be coprime to $p-1$** for the modular inverse to exist.
- 🔑 **Reusing $k$ across two messages is catastrophic** — it leaks the private key $x$.
- 🔑 The **security** of the entire scheme reduces to the **Discrete Logarithm Problem** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
- 🔑 The signature has length **$2 \log_2 p$ bits** — twice the RSA equivalent for similar security, motivating DSA and ECDSA.
- 🔑 **ElGamal is the theoretical parent** of DSA, Schnorr, and ECDSA — all are variants over different groups.
- 🔑 For KTU 2024: always show **key generation → signature → verification** in full for full marks on a 14-mark question.
- 🔑 The verification reduction $g^m \equiv y^r \cdot r^s \pmod{p}$ is derived from $m \equiv xr + ks \pmod{p-1}$ — memorize this derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Scheme (ECDSS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Scheme (ECDSS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Scheme (ECDSS / ECDSA)**
> The **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)** is the elliptic-curve analogue of the Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA). It is a **public-key cryptographic digital signature scheme** standardized in **FIPS 186-4**, **ANSI X9.62**, **ISO/IEC 14888-3**, and **IEEE 1363-2000**. ECDSA provides the three classical signature services — **authentication**, **data integrity**, and **non-repudiation** — by performing all operations (key generation, signing, verification) on points of an **elliptic curve group** $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ instead of over $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ as in classical DSA.

A signature in ECDSA is the ordered pair $(r, s)$, where both components are integers in the interval $[1, n-1]$ and $n$ is the order of the base point $G$ on the chosen elliptic curve. The security of the scheme reduces to the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Mailbox with a One-Way Ruler"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English Analogy):**
> Imagine Alice owns a magic ruler. She can take a "step" $G$ on a curved dance-floor and, after $d$ jumps, land on a unique spot $Q = dG$. Anyone can see $Q$, but **jumping backwards** to find $d$ is computationally impossible (the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem).
> - To **sign**, Alice performs a secret dance using a one-time random number $k$, and writes down two numbers $(r, s)$ that mathematically link her dance step $kG$ to her private key $d$ and the message hash.
> - To **verify**, Bob retraces Alice's mathematical path using only public information ($Q$, $G$, curve parameters) and the two numbers $(r, s)$. If Bob's recomputed trail ends at the same landmark as Alice's original step, the signature is **valid**.
> - A **forger**, who doesn't know $k$ or $d$, would have to either solve ECDLP or break the hash function — both infeasible with current technology.

## 1.3 Standard Elliptic Curve Domain Parameters

For a prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$, a curve is defined by six domain parameters:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Required Domain Parameters over $\mathbb{F}_p$:**
> 1. **Prime modulus $p$** — size of the underlying finite field.
> 2. **Curve coefficients $a, b$** — defining $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$.
> 3. **Generator (base) point $G$** — a fixed point on the curve.
> 4. **Order $n$ of $G$** — the smallest positive integer such that $nG = \mathcal{O}$ (the point at infinity).
> 5. **Cofactor $h$** — equals $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)/n$.
>
> **Discriminant condition:** $\Delta = 4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$ (ensures the curve is *non-singular*, i.e., has no cusps or self-intersections).

Standardized curves used in practice include **NIST P-256 (secp256r1)**, **P-384**, **P-521**, and **Curve25519 (Ed25519)**.

## 1.4 Why ECDSA is Preferred over Classical DSA / RSA

| Property | RSA-2048 | DSA-2048 | **ECDSA-256** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalent security bits | **112** | **112** | **128** |
| Public key size | 2048 bits | 1024 bits | **256 bits** |
| Signature size | 2048 bits | 320 bits | **512 bits** |
| Signing speed | Slow | Fast | **Faster** |
| Verification speed | Fast | Fast | **Fast** |
| Bandwidth (TLS) | High | High | **Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A 256-bit ECDSA key provides security roughly equivalent to a 3072-bit RSA key. This is why ECDSA is mandatory in modern protocols like **TLS 1.3**, **Bitcoin/Ethereum**, **Apple's iMessage**, and **SSH**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Phases

ECDSA operates in **three distinct phases**: *Key Generation*, *Signature Generation*, and *Signature Verification*.

### Phase 1 — Key Generation
1. Select the elliptic curve domain parameters $(p, a, b, G, n, h)$.
2. Select a cryptographically secure random integer $d \in [1, n-1]$ — this is the **private key**.
3. Compute the **public key** $Q = dG$ (using efficient scalar multiplication algorithms like *double-and-add*).
4. Publish $(E, G, n, Q)$; keep $d$ secret.

### Phase 2 — Signature Generation (Sign message $m$)
1. Compute the message hash $e = H(m)$ using a strong hash function (SHA-256, SHA-512, etc.).
2. Let $z$ be the leftmost $L_n$ bits of $e$, where $L_n$ is the bit-length of $n$.
3. Select a cryptographically secure random integer $k \in [1, n-1]$ (the *per-message secret nonce*).
4. Compute the curve point $(x_1, y_1) = kG$.
5. Compute $r = x_1 \bmod n$. If $r = 0$, **reject** $k$ and try a new one.
6. Compute $s = k^{-1}(z + d \cdot r) \bmod n$. If $s = 0$, **reject** $k$ and try a new one.
7. The **signature** on $m$ is the pair $(r, s)$.

### Phase 3 — Signature Verification
1. Verify that $r, s \in [1, n-1]$. If not, the signature is **invalid**.
2. Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod n$.
3. Compute $u_1 = z \cdot w \bmod n$ and $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod n$.
4. Compute the curve point $(x_1, y_1) = u_1 G + u_2 Q$.
5. If this point is $\mathcal{O}$ (point at infinity), the signature is **invalid**.
6. Compute $v = x_1 \bmod n$.
7. The signature is **valid if and only if** $v = r$.

## 2.2 Why This Works — The Mathematical Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **Correctness Proof Sketch:**
> During verification, the verifier computes $u_1 G + u_2 Q$. Substituting $Q = dG$:
>
> $$u_1 G + u_2 Q = u_1 G + u_2 d G = (u_1 + u_2 d) G$$
>
> From the signing equation: $s \equiv k^{-1}(z + dr) \pmod n$, we get $k \equiv s^{-1}(z + dr) \equiv w(z + dr) \equiv u_1 + u_2 d \pmod n$.
>
> Therefore $(u_1 + u_2 d) G = kG = (x_1, y_1)$, and so $v = x_1 \bmod n = r$. ∎

The scheme is correct *only* because the Discrete Logarithm Problem makes it computationally infeasible for an attacker to extract $d$ from $Q = dG$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| **Component** | **Equation** | **Notes / Constraints** |
|---|---|---|
| Elliptic Curve Equation | $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$ | Non-singular: $4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0$ |
| Public Key | $Q = dG$ | $d$ random in $[1, n-1]$ |
| Signature Component 1 | $r = x_1 \bmod n$ | $x_1$ is x-coordinate of $kG$ |
| Signature Component 2 | $s = k^{-1}(z + d \cdot r) \bmod n$ | $z =$ leftmost $L_n$ bits of $H(m)$ |
| Inverse Used in Verify | $w = s^{-1} \bmod n$ | Computed via Extended Euclidean |
| Verification Sums | $u_1 = zw \bmod n, \; u_2 = rw \bmod n$ | Both $\in [1, n-1]$ |
| Verification Point | $(x_1, y_1) = u_1 G + u_2 Q$ | Point addition + scalar mult |
| Acceptance Test | $v = x_1 \bmod n$ | Accept iff $v = r$ |
| Security Parameter | ECDLP: given $Q, G$, find $d$ | Best classical: Pollard's rho $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of ECDSA:**
> - **TLS 1.3 Handshake** — Authenticate server certificates (mandatory suite: `TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`).
> - **Bitcoin / Blockchain** — Transaction signing uses **secp256k1**.
> - **Smart Cards & IoT** — Small key sizes (256 bits) fit in low-power devices.
> - **Passport / National ID chips** — ICAO 9303 standard uses ECDSA.
> - **Code Signing** — Apple, Microsoft, and OpenSSH use ECDSA for software authenticity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Correctness of ECDSA Verification

We will prove that an honestly generated signature $(r, s)$ always verifies as $v = r$.

**Given:** Domain parameters $(E, p, G, n, h)$, private key $d$, public key $Q = dG$, message $m$, hash $z = H(m)$, and per-message secret $k$.

**Signing equations used:**

$$r = x_1 \bmod n, \quad \text{where} \quad (x_1, y_1) = kG$$

$$s \equiv k^{-1}(z + d \cdot r) \pmod n$$

**Step 1 — Isolate $k$ from the signing equation.**

Multiply both sides of $s$ by $k$:

$$k \cdot s \equiv z + d \cdot r \pmod n$$

Multiply both sides by $k^{-1}$ (i.e., the modular inverse of $k \bmod n$):

$$s \equiv k^{-1}(z + d r) \pmod n \quad \text{(original form)}$$

Rearrange to solve for $k$:

$$k \equiv s^{-1}(z + d r) \pmod n$$

**Step 2 — Expand the verification sums $u_1$ and $u_2$.**

By definition, $w = s^{-1} \bmod n$:

$$u_1 = z \cdot w \bmod n \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_1 \equiv z \cdot s^{-1} \pmod n$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod n \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_2 \equiv r \cdot s^{-1} \pmod n$$

**Step 3 — Form the linear combination $u_1 + u_2 d$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1 + u_2 \, d &\equiv z \cdot s^{-1} + (r \cdot s^{-1}) \cdot d \pmod n \\
&\equiv s^{-1}(z + r d) \pmod n \\
&\equiv s^{-1}(z + d r) \pmod n \\
&\equiv k \pmod n \quad \text{(from Step 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the verification point.**

The verifier computes:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1 G + u_2 Q &= u_1 G + u_2 (dG) \\
&= (u_1 + u_2 d) \cdot G \\
&\equiv k \cdot G \pmod n \\
&= (x_1, y_1) \quad \text{(the point produced during signing)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the acceptance test.**

$$v = x_1 \bmod n = r \bmod n = r \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Because $k \equiv u_1 + u_2 d \pmod n$ and $G$ has order $n$, the verifier recovers the *exact* point $(x_1, y_1)$ that was produced during signing. Hence $v = r$ always holds for an honest signer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Small Curve for Illustration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Disclaimer for KTU Students:** Real ECDSA uses very large primes (≥256 bits). For exam working, professors *may* use small test curves to allow manual computation. The example below uses the curve $E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + 2x + 3 \pmod{97}$ with generator $G = (5, 1)$ of order $n = 5$. **Numbers are deliberately tiny for verification only.**

### Setup
- $E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + 2x + 3 \pmod{97}$
- $G = (5, 1)$, $n = 5$
- Message hash $z = 4$
- Private key $d = 2 \Rightarrow Q = 2G = 2 \cdot (5,1)$

**Compute $2G$ on the curve:**

For $P = (5, 1)$, the slope $\lambda$ for doubling is:
$$\lambda = \frac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} = \frac{3(25) + 2}{2(1)} = \frac{77}{2} \equiv 77 \cdot 49 \equiv 3773 \equiv 89 \pmod{97}$$

$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1 = 89^2 - 10 = 7921 - 10 = 7911 \equiv 7911 \bmod 97$$

Compute $7911 \div 97$: $97 \times 81 = 7857$, $7911 - 7857 = 54$. So $x_3 = 54$.

$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 = 89(5 - 54) - 1 = 89(-49) - 1 = -4361 - 1 = -4362 \equiv -4362 \bmod 97$.

$4362 \div 97$: $97 \times 44 = 4268$, $4362 - 4268 = 94$. So $-4362 \equiv -94 \equiv 3 \pmod{97}$. Thus $Q = 2G = (54, 3)$.

### Sign with $k = 3$
- $kG = 3G = G + 2G = (5, 1) + (54, 3)$.

Slope $\lambda = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{3 - 1}{54 - 5} = \frac{2}{49} \equiv 2 \cdot 49^{-1} \pmod{97}$.

$49 \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{97}$. Try $x = 2$: $98 \equiv 1 \pmod{97}$. So $49^{-1} \equiv 2 \pmod{97}$.

$\lambda = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$.

$x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 = 16 - 5 - 54 = -43 \equiv 54 \pmod{97}$.

$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 = 4(5 - 54) - 1 = 4(-49) - 1 = -197 \equiv -197 + 3 \cdot 97 = -197 + 291 = 94 \pmod{97}$.

So $kG = (54, 94)$, hence $x_1 = 54$.

- $r = x_1 \bmod n = 54 \bmod 5 = 4$.
- $k^{-1} \bmod 5$: $3 \cdot 2 = 6 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$, so $k^{-1} = 2$.
- $s = k^{-1}(z + d \cdot r) \bmod n = 2(4 + 2 \cdot 4) \bmod 5 = 2(12) \bmod 5 = 24 \bmod 5 = 4$.

**Signature:** $(r, s) = (4, 4)$.

### Verify
- $w = s^{-1} \bmod 5 = 4^{-1} \bmod 5 = 4$ (since $4 \cdot 4 = 16 \equiv 1$).
- $u_1 = z \cdot w \bmod 5 = 4 \cdot 4 = 16 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$.
- $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod 5 = 4 \cdot 4 = 16 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$.
- Compute $u_1 G + u_2 Q = 1 \cdot G + 1 \cdot Q = G + Q = (5, 1) + (54, 3) = kG = (54, 94)$.
- $v = x_1 \bmod 5 = 54 \bmod 5 = 4 = r$. ✓ **Signature valid.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why the Nonce $k$ Must Be Unique and Random

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall in ECDSA — Nonce Reuse / Predictable $k$**
> If the same $k$ is ever used to sign two different messages $m_1, m_2$ (producing $(r, s_1)$ and $(r, s_2)$ since $r$ depends only on $k$), then an attacker can compute the private key $d$ as follows:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_1 &\equiv k^{-1}(z_1 + d r) \pmod n \\
s_2 &\equiv k^{-1}(z_2 + d r) \pmod n \\
s_1 - s_2 &\equiv k^{-1}(z_1 - z_2) \pmod n \\
k &\equiv (z_1 - z_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \pmod n \\
d &\equiv (s_1 k - z_1) \cdot r^{-1} \pmod n
\end{aligned}$$

This is **exactly how the PlayStation 3 code-signing key was leaked in 2010** — Sony used a *static* $k$ across all signatures, allowing attackers to extract the console's master private key and forge software signatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ECDSA Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Parameters E, p, G, n, h] --> B[Key Generation Phase]
    B --> B1[Choose random private key d in 1 to n-1]
    B1 --> B2[Compute public key Q = dG]
    B2 --> C[Publish Q and domain parameters]
    
    C --> D[Signature Generation Phase]
    D --> D1[Hash message m to get z]
    D1 --> D2[Generate random per-message nonce k]
    D2 --> D3[Compute curve point kG to get x1, y1]
    D3 --> D4[Set r = x1 mod n]
    D4 --> D5{Is r equal to zero?}
    D5 -->|Yes| D2
    D5 -->|No| D6[Compute s = k inverse times z plus d r mod n]
    D6 --> D7{Is s equal to zero?}
    D7 -->|Yes| D2
    D7 -->|No| D8[Output signature pair r, s]
    
    D8 --> E[Signature Verification Phase]
    E --> E1[Check r and s are in 1 to n-1]
    E1 --> E2[Compute w = s inverse mod n]
    E2 --> E3[Compute u1 = z w mod n]
    E2 --> E4[Compute u2 = r w mod n]
    E3 --> F[Compute point u1 G plus u2 Q]
    E4 --> F
    F --> G{Is result point at infinity?}
    G -->|Yes| H[REJECT Signature]
    G -->|No| I[Set v = x coordinate mod n]
    I --> J{Is v equal to r?}
    J -->|Yes| K[ACCEPT Signature is Valid]
    J -->|No| H
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ECDSA Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SenderSide["SENDER ALICE"]
        A1[KeyGen Module] --> A2[Private d]
        A1 --> A3[Public Q = dG]
        A4[Hash Module SHA-256] --> A5[Message Digest z]
        A2 --> A6[Sign Module]
        A5 --> A6
        A7[Secure RNG] --> A8[Per-message Nonce k]
        A8 --> A6
        A6 --> A9[Signature r, s]
    end
    
    subgraph Channel["INSECURE CHANNEL"]
        A9 --> B1
        A3 --> B1
        A5 --> B1
        B1[Transmit: m, r, s, Q]
    end
    
    subgraph ReceiverSide["RECEIVER BOB"]
        B1 --> C1[Hash Recompute]
        C1 --> C2[Recompute z]
        C2 --> C3[Verify Module]
        B1 --> C3
        C3 --> C4[Compute w, u1, u2]
        C4 --> C5[Compute u1G + u2Q]
        C5 --> C6{Accept if v equals r}
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — DSA vs ECDSA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Digital Signature Schemes] --> B[Classical DSA]
    A --> C[ECDSA - Elliptic Curve Variant]
    
    B --> B1[Operates in Z sub p star]
    B --> B2[Discrete Log in Z sub p star]
    B --> B3[Public key: 1024 to 3072 bits]
    B --> B4[Signature: 320 to 448 bits]
    B --> B5[Used in older US Fed systems]
    
    C --> C1[Operates on E over F sub p]
    C --> C2[ECDLP on Elliptic Curves]
    C --> C3[Public key: 256 to 521 bits]
    C --> C4[Signature: 512 to 1042 bits]
    C --> C5[Used in TLS 1.3, Bitcoin, IoT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams above show the data-flow relationships between the cryptographic primitives (Hash, RNG, Scalar Mult, Point Add). In an actual exam, drawing the flow diagram for the *verification phase* alone can fetch 3-4 marks if presented cleanly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**State and explain the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP). Why is it considered the security foundation of ECDSA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** is defined as follows: given an elliptic curve $E$ defined over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$, a base point $G \in E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ of order $n$, and a public point $Q \in \langle G \rangle$, find the unique integer $d \in [1, n-1]$ such that $Q = dG$ (where $dG$ denotes repeated point addition of $G$ to itself $d$ times).

**Why it is the security foundation of ECDSA (3 marks):**
1. The private key $d$ cannot be recovered from the public key $Q = dG$ without solving ECDLP.
2. The signature equation $s = k^{-1}(z + dr) \bmod n$ hides $d$ behind both the random nonce $k$ and the hash $z$.
3. The best known classical algorithm (Pollard's rho) requires $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ operations, meaning a 256-bit $n$ requires $\approx 2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible.
4. Unlike integer factorization (RSA) or DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ (DSA), no sub-exponential algorithm is known for ECDLP, giving ECDSA a much smaller key size for equivalent security.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**List the six domain parameters of an elliptic curve used in ECDSA over a prime field. State the non-singularity condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six domain parameters of an elliptic curve used in ECDSA over a prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$ are:
1. **Prime modulus $p$** — defines the finite field.
2. **Coefficient $a$** — part of the curve equation.
3. **Coefficient $b$** — part of the curve equation.
4. **Base point (generator) $G = (x_G, y_G)$** — a fixed point on the curve used for all operations.
5. **Order $n$ of $G$** — the smallest positive integer such that $nG = \mathcal{O}$ (point at infinity).
6. **Cofactor $h = \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)/n$** — ratio of total curve points to the order of $G$.

**Non-singularity condition:** The curve must be non-singular, which means it has no cusps or self-intersections. Mathematically, this requires:

$$4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$$

If this condition is violated, the curve has singularities and the group law (point addition) fails, making ECDSA unusable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]

**a)** Describe in detail the **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)** for key generation, signature generation, and signature verification. State clearly the role of the random per-message nonce $k$. (7 marks)

**b)** With reference to a suitable elliptic curve $E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$, explain the **point addition** rule geometrically and algebraically. Why is the **point at infinity $\mathcal{O}$** the identity element? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**1. Key Generation (2 marks):**
- Fix domain parameters $(p, a, b, G, n, h)$.
- Choose a cryptographically secure random integer $d$ with $1 \le d \le n-1$ — the **private key**.
- Compute the **public key** $Q = dG$ using point addition.
- Publish $(E, G, n, Q)$; keep $d$ secret.

**2. Signature Generation (3 marks):**
- Hash the message: $z = H(m)$ (e.g., SHA-256). Truncate to $L_n$ bits.
- Generate a cryptographically secure random nonce $k$ with $1 \le k \le n-1$.
- Compute the curve point $(x_1, y_1) = kG$.
- Compute $r = x_1 \bmod n$; reject and pick new $k$ if $r = 0$.
- Compute $s = k^{-1}(z + d \cdot r) \bmod n$; reject and pick new $k$ if $s = 0$.
- Output the signature pair $(r, s)$.

**Role of the per-message nonce $k$ (2 marks):**
- $k$ introduces randomness, ensuring the same message signed twice produces different signatures (semantic security).
- $k$ prevents signature forgery because it appears in the equation but is destroyed (never published) after signing.
- **$k$ must be unique and unpredictable for every signature** — reuse of $k$ across two signatures immediately reveals $d$.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**1. Point Addition — Geometric Rule (3 marks):**
Given two distinct points $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$ on $E$:
- Draw a straight line through $P$ and $Q$.
- This line intersects $E$ at a **third point** $R' = (x_3, y_3)$.
- The **sum** $P + Q$ is defined as the **reflection of $R'$ across the x-axis**, i.e., $P + Q = (x_3, -y_3)$.
- For the special case $Q = -P$ (i.e., $x_1 = x_2$, $y_1 = -y_2$), the line is vertical and intersects the curve only at infinity, so $P + Q = \mathcal{O}$.

**2. Point Addition — Algebraic Rule (3 marks):**
For $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$ with $P \neq Q$:

$$\lambda = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \pmod p$$

$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p$$

$$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$$

For point doubling $P + P = 2P$:

$$\lambda = \frac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} \pmod p$$

**3. Why $\mathcal{O}$ is the identity (1 mark):**
The **point at infinity** $\mathcal{O}$ acts as the additive identity. For any point $P$ on the curve, $P + \mathcal{O} = P$. The line through $P$ and $-P$ is vertical and "intersects" the curve at $\mathcal{O}$ in the projective plane. Thus $P + (-P) = \mathcal{O}$, and $-P$ is the additive inverse of $P$. The set $E(\mathbb{F}_p) \cup \{\mathcal{O}\}$ forms a finite abelian group under this addition.

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]

**a)** Explain the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** in detail. Discuss the **Pollard's rho algorithm** and compute the security level for a 256-bit curve. (7 marks)

**b)** Compare **ECDSA** with classical **DSA** and **RSA signatures** in terms of security strength, key size, signature size, and computational efficiency. Why is ECDSA preferred for IoT and mobile devices? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**1. ECDLP Definition (2 marks):**
Given an elliptic curve $E$ over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$, a generator $G$ of order $n$, and a point $Q = dG$ on the curve, the **ECDLP** is the problem of finding the integer $d \in [1, n-1]$ such that $Q = dG$. The hardness of this problem underpins the security of all elliptic-curve cryptography.

**2. Pollard's Rho Algorithm (3 marks):**
Pollard's rho is the best known *classical* algorithm for solving ECDLP with **time complexity** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ and **negligible space complexity**. It uses an iterated random walk on the curve:

- Partition the group $\langle G \rangle$ into three roughly equal sets $S_1, S_2, S_3$.
- Define an iteration $f$ on points: if current point is $P = aG + bQ$, then
  - $P \to P + G$ if $P \in S_1$,
  - $P \to 2P$ if $P \in S_2$,
  - $P \to P + Q$ if $P \in S_3$.
- The sequence $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots$ eventually cycles (the "rho" shape).
- Using Floyd's cycle detection, a collision gives two representations of the same point, from which $d$ can be solved via the discrete log.

**3. Security Computation for 256-bit curve (2 marks):**
For a 256-bit prime (NIST P-256), the group order $n \approx 2^{256}$. Pollard's rho requires:

$$\sqrt{n} \approx \sqrt{2^{256}} = 2^{128} \text{ group operations}$$

Since each operation is a point addition ($\approx 1$ microsecond on modern hardware), the total time is approximately $2^{128} \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 10^{32}$ years — **practically infeasible**.

This 128-bit security is comparable to a **3072-bit RSA** key, demonstrating the **efficiency advantage of ECC**.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**1. Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **RSA-Sig (2048-bit)** | **DSA (2048-bit)** | **ECDSA (256-bit)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Underlying Hard Problem | Integer Factorization | DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ | ECDLP |
| Security (bits) | 112 | 112 | **128** |
| Public Key Size | 2048 bits | 1024 bits | **256 bits** |
| Signature Size | 2048 bits | 320 bits | **512 bits (2×256)** |
| Signing Speed | Slow | Fast | **Fastest** |
| Verification Speed | **Fastest** | Fast | Fast |
| Bandwidth Required | High | Moderate | **Low** |
| Standardization | PKCS#1, FIPS 186 | FIPS 186-4 | FIPS 186-4, ANSI X9.62 |

**2. Why ECDSA is Preferred for IoT and Mobile (3 marks):**
- **Small key size:** 256-bit ECDSA gives 128-bit security, fitting easily in constrained memory (smart cards, RFID tags).
- **Low power consumption:** Scalar multiplication is faster than RSA modular exponentiation, saving battery life in mobile/IoT.
- **Low bandwidth:** Smaller signatures reduce transmission costs in wireless protocols (BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT).
- **Faster TLS handshake:** TLS 1.3 mandates ECDSA in `ECDHE_ECDSA` suites for reduced latency.
- **Proven security:** No sub-exponential attack is known, unlike RSA (vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on quantum computers, though both RSA and ECDSA are broken by quantum computers — but ECDSA's smaller key size means fewer qubits are needed for a quantum attacker, ironically).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the signing and verification equations** — most students swap $u_1$ and $u_2$ in verification. Remember: $u_1$ pairs with the **message hash** $z$, and $u_2$ pairs with the **signature component** $r$.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod n$** — many students omit this inverse or compute it as $1/s$ outside the modulus, losing 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the $r = 0$ and $s = 0$ rejection conditions** — these are crucial for correctness and worth 1 mark each in Part A.
> 4. **Forgetting the discriminant condition** $4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$ when defining the curve — easy 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Stating ECDSA uses "RSA-style" math** — examiners deduct heavily for mixing up the group ($\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ vs. elliptic curve).
> 6. **Ignoring the random nonce requirement** — a 2-mark question on "why is $k$ unique and random" is a recurring KTU question.
> 7. **Not explaining why $\mathcal{O}$ is the identity element** — a 1-mark conceptual point often missed in Part B answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ECDSA / ECDSS Module 3**

- ✅ **ECDSA** is the elliptic-curve variant of DSA. It produces signatures $(r, s)$ with both components in $[1, n-1]$.
- ✅ The curve is defined by $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$ with the **non-singularity condition** $4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$.
- ✅ **Six domain parameters:** $p, a, b, G, n, h$. Standard curves: NIST P-256, P-384, P-521, secp256k1.
- ✅ **Key generation:** private key $d$ is random, public key $Q = dG$.
- ✅ **Signing:** requires a one-time secret nonce $k$; computes $(x_1, y_1) = kG$, then $r = x_1 \bmod n$ and $s = k^{-1}(z + dr) \bmod n$.
- ✅ **Verification:** uses $w = s^{-1}$, $u_1 = zw$, $u_2 = rw$, and checks $u_1 G + u_2 Q$ has x-coordinate congruent to $r \bmod n$.
- ✅ **Security foundation:** hardness of the **ECDLP** — best known classical attack is **Pollard's rho** with complexity $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$.
- ✅ **256-bit ECDSA ≈ 128-bit security ≈ 3072-bit RSA** in strength.
- ✅ **Pitfall — Nonce reuse:** Reusing $k$ across two signatures leaks the private key $d$. This caused the **Sony PS3 hack (2010)**.
- ✅ **The point at infinity $\mathcal{O}$** is the additive identity on the elliptic curve group.
- ✅ **Point addition rules:** distinct points use the line through them; doubling uses the tangent; the inverse of $(x, y)$ is $(x, -y)$.
- ✅ **ECDSA is used in:** TLS 1.3, Bitcoin, Ethereum, Apple iMessage, OpenSSH, ICAO passports, and modern IoT.
- ✅ **Quantum caveat:** Shor's algorithm breaks both RSA and ECDSA; post-quantum alternatives include **Ed25519**, **Dilithium**, and **Falcon**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "describe ECDSA" in a 7-mark question, always present the answer in **three clearly labeled phases** (Key Generation, Signing, Verification) with each equation written in its own line. Examiners reward structured answers and penalize "wall-of-text" responses. Always include a small comparison table for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security - Electronic Mail Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Security - Electronic Mail Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Electronic Mail Security?

**Electronic Mail Security** refers to the collection of cryptographic protocols, authentication mechanisms, and policy frameworks designed to ensure the **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** of email communication over inherently insecure networks like the Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** Email security is the practice of applying cryptographic primitives (symmetric, asymmetric, hash functions) and standard protocols (PGP, S/MIME, DKIM) to safeguard email contents from eavesdropping, tampering, spoofing, and replay attacks during transit between mail servers and clients.

The four cardinal pillars of email security aligned with the classic CIA-N triad are:

| Property | Description | Cryptographic Tool Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Only intended recipient can read the message | Symmetric encryption (AES, 3DES) |
| **Integrity** | Message not altered in transit | Hash functions (SHA-1, SHA-256, MD5) |
| **Authentication** | Sender's identity is verified | Digital Signatures (RSA, DSS) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Sender cannot deny sending the message | Public-key certificates + Digital Signatures |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Secure Registered Post"

Imagine you want to send a confidential business contract through ordinary mail. A **plaintext email** is like a **postcard** — anyone at the post office (your ISP, mail relay servers, hackers on Wi-Fi) can read it.

**Electronic Mail Security** transforms this postcard into a **tamper-proof, sealed, signed, registered letter**:

- The **envelope lock (encryption)** ensures only the recipient with the correct key can open it (Confidentiality).
- A **tamper-evident wax seal (hash + digital signature)** proves the contents have not been altered (Integrity + Authentication).
- The **registered post receipt (PKI certificate)** legally confirms who sent it (Non-Repudiation).
- The **postmark (DKIM signature)** authenticates the letter came from an authorized postal branch (Domain Authentication).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Email Security Critical in 2024?**
> - Over **333 billion** emails are sent daily worldwide (Statista, 2023).
> - **91%** of cyberattacks begin with a phishing email (Verizon DBIR).
> - Email is the primary vector for **Business Email Compromise (BEC)** and **ransomware delivery**.

## 1.3 Threats to Electronic Mail

A KTU student must recognize the threat landscape before studying the defenses:

1. **Loss of Confidentiality** – Eavesdropping on SMTP traffic (port 25 is plain-text).
2. **Message Tampering** – Modifying contents mid-transit (Man-in-the-Middle attacks).
3. **Identity Spoofing** – Forging the `From:` header to impersonate a legitimate sender.
4. **Replay Attacks** – Capturing and re-sending a previously valid message.
5. **Phishing & Social Engineering** – Tricking users into revealing credentials.
6. **Spam & Malware** – Unsolicited bulk email carrying malicious attachments or links.

## 1.4 The Two Dominant Standards: PGP vs S/MIME

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 4 places heavy emphasis on **PGP** and **S/MIME**. You **must** know the architectural differences, message formats, key management approaches, and trust models of both.

| Feature | **PGP (Pretty Good Privacy)** | **S/MIME (Secure/MIME)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Phil Zimmermann, 1991 (Open Source) | RSA Labs, 1995 (Industry Standard) |
| **Trust Model** | Web of Trust (decentralized) | Hierarchical X.509 PKI (centralized CAs) |
| **Standardization** | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) | RFC 5751, X.509 |
| **Primary Use** | Personal, journalistic, privacy-focused | Enterprise, government, corporate |
| **MIME Compatibility** | Uses PGP/MIME (RFC 3156) | Native MIME integration |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) — Operational Deep Dive

PGP is a hybrid cryptosystem that combines the **speed of symmetric encryption** with the **convenience of asymmetric key exchange**. It also provides **compression** and **radix-64 (ASCII armor) encoding** for email compatibility.

### 2.1.1 The Five PGP Services

1. **Authentication** — Digital signature using RSA or DSS + SHA hash.
2. **Confidentiality** — Symmetric encryption (CAST, IDEA, 3DES, AES) with one-time session key.
3. **Compression** — ZIP algorithm to reduce redundancy before encryption.
4. **E-mail Compatibility** — Radix-64 conversion to ensure 8-bit binary survives 7-bit SMTP text channels.
5. **Segmentation** — Splits large messages to comply with SMTP max size limits.

### 2.1.2 PGP Operational Steps (Sending)

| Step | Operation | Cryptographic Primitive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Compute hash of message | **SHA-1 / SHA-256** |
| 2 | Sign hash with sender's private key | **RSA / DSS** |
| 3 | Generate random 128-bit session key | CSPRNG |
| 4 | Encrypt message with session key | **CAST-128 / IDEA / AES** |
| 5 | Encrypt session key with recipient's public key | **RSA / ElGamal** |
| 6 | Concatenate signed hash + encrypted message + encrypted key | — |
| 7 | Apply Radix-64 encoding | Base64 |
| 8 (optional) | ZIP-compress the packet | LZ77 |

### 2.1.3 PGP Message Format (RFC 4880)

A PGP packet is composed of three components:

```
PGP Message =  Content  ⊕  Signature  ⊕  Session-Key-Envelope
```

- **Content**: Message after optional compression and encryption.
- **Signature**: Hash signed by sender's private key + timestamp + key ID.
- **Session-Key-Envelope**: Session key encrypted with recipient's public key.

### 2.1.4 PGP Key Rings

PGP maintains two key rings locally for every user:

- **Private Key Ring** — Stores the user's own public-private key pairs (often on disk in encrypted form).
- **Public Key Ring** — Stores the public keys of all known correspondents, each tagged with a **Key ID** (lower 64 bits of the public key), **trust flag**, and **key legitimacy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key ID = lower-order 64 bits of the public key.** This is used as a compact, unique identifier in PGP messages so the receiver knows which public key to use for verification.

### 2.1.5 PGP Trust Model — Web of Trust

Unlike X.509's centralized Certificate Authority hierarchy, PGP uses a **decentralized trust model**:

- Each user is a **certifier** (introducer).
- A user assigns one of four trust levels to another user's public key: *Unknown, None, Marginal, Complete*.
- **Key legitimacy** is computed by the PGP software as a weighted sum of trust signatures.
- If **two marginally trusted** introducers OR **one completely trusted** introducer signs a key, PGP considers it **valid**.

$$L_{key} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T_i$$

Where $L_{key}$ is the key legitimacy, $w_i$ is the introducer's weight (1.0 for complete, 0.5 for marginal), and $T_i$ is the introducer's trust signature.

## 2.2 S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)

S/MIME is an IETF standard (RFC 5751) that adds cryptographic security to MIME-formatted email. It is the de-facto corporate standard.

### 2.2.1 S/MIME Capabilities (Version 3)

| Function | MIME Type | Algorithm Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Enveloped Data** | `application/pkcs7-mime` | AES-128 CBC + RSA |
| **Signed Data** | `multipart/signed` | RSA + SHA-256 |
| **Clear Signed Data** | `multipart/signed` | Plaintext + signature visible |
| **Encrypted + Signed** | Nested application/pkcs7-mime | AES + RSA |

### 2.2.2 S/MIME Message Types (CMS — Cryptographic Message Syntax)

S/MIME uses **CMS** (formerly PKCS #7) as its underlying message format. KTU frequently asks students to list these content types:

1. **Data** — Plain content (untyped octet string).
2. **Signed Data** — Encodes the message + signature.
3. **Enveloped Data** — Encrypted content + encrypted session keys for one or more recipients.
4. **Signed & Enveloped Data** — Nested signed-then-encrypted (rarely supported, deprecated).
5. **Digested Data** — Message + hash (rarely used).
6. **Encrypted Data** — Encrypted content with no key info (key known out-of-band).

### 2.2.3 S/MIME Trust Model — Hierarchical X.509 PKI

S/MIME relies entirely on the **X.509 Certificate Hierarchy**:

- A trusted **Root Certificate Authority (CA)** (e.g., VeriSign, DigiCert, Let's Encrypt) issues certificates.
- **Subordinate CAs** issue end-user certificates.
- A certificate binds an email address to a public key, signed by a CA.
- Revocation is handled via **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** or **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)**.

## 2.3 DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) — RFC 6376

DKIM is an **email authentication framework** that allows a domain to assert responsibility for a message by attaching a digital signature to the email.

### 2.3.1 DKIM Operational Flow

1. **Outgoing Mail Server (Signing)**: The sending MTA computes a hash of selected headers + body, signs it with the **private DKIM key** (stored in DNS TXT record of the sender's domain), and adds a `DKIM-Signature:` header.
2. **Receiving Mail Server (Verification)**: The receiving MTA queries DNS for the TXT record of `selector._domainkey.senderdomain`, retrieves the **public DKIM key**, and verifies the signature.
3. The result is passed to the **DMARC** policy for alignment checks.

### 2.3.2 DKIM Signature Structure (Simplified)

```
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/relaxed;
                 d=example.com; s=selector1;
                 h=from:to:subject:date;
                 bh=<body_hash_base64>;
                 b=<signature_base64>;
```

| Tag | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `v=` | Version (always 1) |
| `a=` | Signing algorithm (e.g., rsa-sha256) |
| `c=` | Canonicalization (simple / relaxed) |
| `d=` | Signing domain |
| `s=` | Selector (multiple keys per domain) |
| `h=` | Signed headers |
| `bh=` | Body hash |
| `b=` | Signature data |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Algorithm Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table. It is the single most-asked artefact in KTU Module 4 derivations.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Message $M$ | Original plaintext email | bits |
| Hash $H$ | $H = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | 256 bits |
| Signature $S$ | $S = (H)^{d_s} \bmod n_s$ (RSA private) | bits |
| Verification | $H' = S^{e_s} \bmod n_s$; compare $H' = H$ | boolean |
| Session Key $K_s$ | Random CSPRNG output | 128 / 256 bits |
| Encrypted Message $C$ | $C = E_{K_s}(M)$ (symmetric) | bits |
| Encrypted Session Key $K_e$ | $K_e = (K_s)^{e_r} \bmod n_r$ (RSA public) | bits |
| Public Key $e, n$ | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | integer |
| Radix-64 Size Factor | Expansion $= \lceil \text{bits}/6 \rceil \times 8 / \text{bits}$ | ratio $\approx 1.33$ |
| DKIM Header Length | $L_h = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{len}(h_i)$ | bytes |
| S/MIME Padding | PKCS#7 padding (1 to 16 bytes) | bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Radix-64 Expansion:** Every 3 bytes (24 bits) of binary are expanded to 4 ASCII characters (32 bits). The overhead is $\frac{32-24}{24} = 33.3\%$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PGP**: Used by journalists (e.g., The Guardian SecureDrop), activists, and Linux distributions for signing software packages (`apt`, `pacman` verify package integrity via GPG signatures).
- **S/MIME**: Deployed across Microsoft Outlook, Apple Mail, and corporate Google Workspace for end-to-end email encryption.
- **DKIM**: Universally deployed — Google, Yahoo, Microsoft all **require DKIM** for bulk senders (2024 policy).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RSA Digital Signature Verification

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Given a message $M = 5$, sender's public key $(e=7, n=187)$ and signature $S = 5$, verify the signature.

**Step 1 — Compute the hash of the message.**

In a simplified KTU problem, we sometimes treat the message itself as the hash (when $M < n$).

$$H = M = 5$$

**Step 2 — Verify the signature using the sender's public key.**

$$H' = S^{e} \bmod n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$H' = 5^{7} \bmod 187$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent by repeated squaring.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &= 5 \\
5^{2} &= 25 \\
5^{4} &= 25^{2} = 625 = 3 \times 187 + 64 \;\Rightarrow\; 625 \bmod 187 = 64 \\
5^{7} &= 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} = 64 \cdot 25 \cdot 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply and reduce modulo 187.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
64 \cdot 25 &= 1600 \\
1600 \bmod 187 &= 1600 - 8 \times 187 = 1600 - 1496 = 104 \\
104 \cdot 5 &= 520 \\
520 \bmod 187 &= 520 - 2 \times 187 = 520 - 374 = 146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare.**

$$H' = 146 \neq H = 5$$

**Conclusion:** The signature is **INVALID**. The message has either been tampered with or the wrong public key was used.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **valid** KTU problem, you would find $H' = H$ exactly. Always carry out the modular arithmetic step-by-step — examiners award partial marks for each correct squaring and modular reduction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PGP Radix-64 (Base64) Expansion

**Problem:** A PGP message of **240 bits** is converted to Radix-64. Calculate the output length.

**Step 1 — Group input into 6-bit chunks.**

$$\text{Chunks} = \frac{240}{6} = 40 \text{ groups}$$

**Step 2 — Each 6-bit group maps to one printable ASCII character (8 bits).**

$$\text{Output bits} = 40 \times 8 = 320 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Express as bytes.**

$$\text{Output size} = \frac{320}{8} = 40 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expansion ratio.**

$$
\text{Expansion} = \frac{320 - 240}{240} \times 100\% = 33.33\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"Why does PGP apply Radix-64 even if the message is already ASCII?"* The answer is that PGP packets are **binary structures** (containing signature bytes, key IDs, length prefixes) that must traverse SMTP — a 7-bit text-only protocol. Radix-64 ensures the entire packet is 7-bit safe.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Web of Trust Legitimacy Calculation

**Scenario:** Alice wants to trust Bob's key. The following users have signed Bob's key:

| Signer | Trust Level | Weight $w$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carol | Complete | 1.0 |
| Dave | Complete | 1.0 |
| Eve | Marginal | 0.5 |
| Frank | Marginal | 0.5 |
| Grace | Unknown | 0.0 |

**Step 1 — Sum weighted trust signatures.**

$$
L = (1.0) + (1.0) + (0.5) + (0.5) + (0.0) = 3.0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PGP threshold rule (default):**

- Need ≥ 1 complete trust, OR
- Need ≥ 2 marginal trusts.

**Conclusion:** Bob's key is **LEGITIMATE** (multiple complete trust signatures present). PGP will mark it valid in Alice's public key ring.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hybrid Encryption Bit-Length

A PGP-encrypted message combines:
- Symmetric ciphertext of message $C$ (length = message length).
- Encrypted session key $K_e$ (length = RSA modulus, e.g., 1024 bits = 128 bytes).
- Signature $S$ (length = RSA modulus, e.g., 1024 bits = 128 bytes).
- Radix-64 overhead of 33.33%.

**Problem:** Original message is **1024 bytes**. RSA key is **2048 bits**. Compute total Radix-64 output.

**Step 1 — Plaintext message bits.**

$$L_{msg} = 1024 \times 8 = 8192 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Add session key envelope.**

$$L_{envelope} = 2048 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Add digital signature.**

$$L_{sig} = 2048 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Total binary packet.**

$$L_{total} = 8192 + 2048 + 2048 = 12288 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Radix-64 expansion.**

$$
L_{ascii} = \left\lceil \frac{12288}{6} \right\rceil \times 8 = 2048 \times 8 = 16384 \text{ bits} = 2048 \text{ bytes}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PGP vs S/MIME Comparison

| Analytical Lens | PGP | S/MIME |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cryptographic Agility** | Supports plug-in ciphers via OpenPGP | Requires CMS-compliant algorithms |
| **Revocation Mechanism** | Manual key revocation (sign revocation cert) | CRL / OCSP automated |
| **Key Distribution** | Out-of-band, public keyservers (e.g., pgp.mit.edu) | LDAP directories, SCEP, automatic |
| **Legal Standing** | Generally weaker in court (self-signed) | Strong (CA-issued, auditable) |
| **Encryption of Attachments** | Per-attachment | Native MIME (entire message tree) |
| **Algorithm Standard** | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) | CMS (RFC 5652), X.509 (RFC 5280) |
| **Interoperability** | Requires GPG/PGP client | Built into Outlook, Apple Mail, Gmail S/MIME |
| **Adoption Curve** | Niche (privacy users, devs) | Mainstream (corporate) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PGP Send Operation — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plaintext Message M] --> B[SHA Hash H of M]
    B --> C[Sign H with Sender Private Key]
    C --> D[Generate Random Session Key Ks]
    A --> E[Encrypt M with Ks]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Encrypt Ks with Recipient Public Key]
    F --> G[Concatenate Signature + Ciphertext + Encrypted Key]
    G --> H[Optional ZIP Compression]
    H --> I[Radix-64 ASCII Encoding]
    I --> J[SMTP Transmission]
```

## 4.2 PGP Receive Operation — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Radix-64 Packet] --> B[Radix-64 Decoding]
    B --> C[Decompression if Applied]
    C --> D[Extract Encrypted Session Key]
    D --> E[Decrypt Ks with Recipient Private Key]
    E --> F[Decrypt Ciphertext with Ks]
    C --> G[Extract Signature]
    G --> H[Verify Signature with Sender Public Key]
    H --> I{Signature Valid?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Deliver Plaintext M]
    I -->|No| K[Reject / Warn User]
```

## 4.3 S/MIME Message Composition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[S/MIME Message] --> B[ContentInfo CMS Structure]
    B --> C{Content Type}
    C -->|envelopedData| D[Encrypted Content + Recipient Info]
    C -->|signedData| E[Signed Content + Signer Info]
    C -->|data| F[Plain Octet String]
    D --> G[AES Encrypted Body]
    D --> H[RSA Encrypted Session Keys per Recipient]
    E --> I[Hash of Body]
    E --> J[Signer Certificate + Signature]
```

## 4.4 DKIM End-to-End Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender MTA Signs Message] --> B[DKIM-Signature Header Added]
    B --> C[Email Routed via SMTP]
    C --> D[Receiving MTA Extracts d= and s= Tags]
    D --> E[DNS Query: selector._domainkey.domain TXT]
    E --> F[Retrieve Public DKIM Key]
    F --> G[Recompute Hash of Signed Headers + Body]
    G --> H[Verify RSA Signature using Public Key]
    H --> I{Signature Valid?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Pass to DMARC Alignment Check]
    I -->|No| K[Mark as Failed: signature does not verify]
    J --> L{DMARC Pass?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Deliver to Inbox]
    L -->|No| N[Quarantine or Reject per Policy]
```

## 4.5 Complete Email Security Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application Layer] --> B[MIME Content Formatting]
    B --> C{Cryptographic Layer}
    C -->|PGP Path| D[PGP Packet: Sign + Encrypt + Radix-64]
    C -->|S/MIME Path| E[CMS: envelopedData / signedData]
    C -->|DKIM Path| F[DKIM-Signature Header Insertion]
    D --> G[SMTP Submission Port 587]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[MTA-to-MTA Transfer Port 25]
    H --> I[Receiving MTA]
    I --> J{Verification}
    J -->|PGP| K[GPG Decrypt + Verify]
    J -->|S/MIME| L[X.509 Cert Chain Validation]
    J -->|DKIM| M[DNS Lookup + RSA Verify]
    K --> N[Delivery to MUA]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```
