## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **S/MIME = MIME + CMS (RFC 5652) + X.509 PKI + Public-key crypto.**
- **S/MIME provides:** Confidentiality (encryption), Authentication (signature), Integrity (hash), Non-repudiation (signature).
- **Four functional types:** Enveloped, Signed, Clear-Signed, Signed-and-Enveloped.
- **Two cryptographic operations:** Asymmetric (RSA, ECDH) for key exchange/signatures; Symmetric (AES) for content encryption.
- **Encoding:** Base64 is mandatory because SMTP is 7-bit clean.
- **MIME foundations:** Five content types, five transfer encodings, four multipart subtypes.
- **CMS structure:** `ContentInfo` → `{ contentType (OID), content (ANY) }`.
- **Standard security order:** **Sign first, then encrypt** (prevents impersonation in plain-content delivery).
- **S/MIME vs PGP:** S/MIME is hierarchical & corporate; PGP is web-of-trust & individual.
- **Mandatory algorithms (RFC 5751):** RSAES-PKCS1-v1_5 (key encryption), RSA-SHA256 (signatures).
- **Recommended algorithms (2024):** RSA-2048+ / ECDSA-P256, AES-256-CBC, SHA-256/384/512.
- **ESS = optional** (RFC 2634): signed receipts, security labels, secure mailing lists, signing certificates.
- **Verification equation:** $h(M) \stackrel{?}{=} E_{pub,S}(\sigma)$.
- **RSA modulus size:** $n \geq 2048$ bits for security through 2030.
- **Exam-favorite:** "Compare S/MIME and PGP" — always mentions **trust model** first.
- **Exam-favorite:** "List the four S/MIME message types" — Enveloped, Signed, Clear-signed, Signed+Enveloped.
- **Exam-favorite:** "Why is S/MIME base64-encoded?" — Because SMTP is a **7-bit** text channel.
- **Exam-favorite:** "Sign-then-encrypt or encrypt-then-sign?" — **Sign first** (verifiable without decryption).
- **Examiner mantra:** Mention **RFC numbers** (5751 for S/MIME, 5652 for CMS, 2634 for ESS) for **bonus marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IP Security Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** IP Security Overview # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **IP Security (IPSec)** is a suite of protocols defined by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** under the **IPSec working group** to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, replay protection, and access control** at the **Internet Layer (Layer 3)** of the TCP/IP model. It secures communications over IP networks by operating directly on the IP datagram, making it transparent to upper-layer applications. IPSec is formally defined in the following core RFCs: - **RFC 4301** — Security Architecture for the Internet Protocol - **RFC 4302** — IP Authentication Header (AH) - **RFC 4303** — IP Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) - **RFC 4306** — Internet Key Exchange (IKEv2) > [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Highlight > In the 2024 scheme, the focus is on understanding *why* IP layer security exists, the *role of Security Associations (SA)*, the *difference between AH and ESP*, and the *two modes of operation* (Transport & Tunnel). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Diplomatic Pouch" Imagine you are sending a confidential business document through a regular courier. Anyone in the middle (postman, sorting office, delivery boy) can open the envelope, read the contents, or even tamper with the letter. **Without IPSec** = A standard envelope: the letter is exposed, the sender's identity is only a handwritten signature, and the recipient cannot verify if the letter was changed. **With IPSec** = A **sealed diplomatic pouch**: - The entire envelope is **sealed with wax and a unique stamp** (ESP encryption + integrity). - A **tamper-proof tag** is attached that breaks if anyone opens it (AH integrity check). - The pouch is registered in an **official diplomatic logbook** with a unique tracking number (Security Association & SPI). - A **trusted courier** verifies identity at both ends before acceptance (IKE authentication). So IPSec transforms a vulnerable, transparent IP packet into a **cryptographically sealed unit** that is opaque and verifiable end-to-end. ## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters | Parameter | Standard Value | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Protocol Number (AH)** | **51** | IP Protocol field identifier for AH | | **Protocol Number (ESP)** | **50** | IP Protocol field identifier for ESP | | **Default MTU** | **1500 bytes** | Max Transmission Unit over Ethernet | | **IPSec Encapsulation Overhead** | **50–57 bytes** (Transport), **70–77 bytes** (Tunnel) | Added bytes per packet | | **SPI Size** | **32 bits** | Security Parameter Index | | **Anti-Replay Window** | **64 bits** (default), 32 bits optional | Replay protection sliding window | | **HMAC-MD5 / HMAC-SHA-1** | 96-bit ICV | Integrity Check Value length | | **AES-CBC Block Size** | **128 bits** | Encryption block size | > [!TIP] Examiner Tip > Memorize the protocol numbers **50 (ESP)** and **51 (AH)**. They are frequently asked as 2-mark direct questions in KTU exams. # 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why IP Security? The Need for IPSec The IP protocol (IPv4 and IPv6) was originally designed for **trusted, closed networks**. It has several inherent weaknesses: 1. **No source authentication** — IP source addresses can be spoofed trivially. 2. **No payload integrity** — bits in transit can be modified without detection. 3. **No confidentiality** — payloads travel in plaintext. 4. **No replay defense** — old packets can be resent to impersonate legitimate sessions. IPSec addresses these issues by inserting a **security layer between IP and the transport/application layers**. ## 2.2 The Two Pillars of IPSec Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] Two Main Building Blocks > **1. IP Security Protocols (AH & ESP)** — The actual protection mechanisms applied to packets. > **2. Key Management (IKE / IKEv2)** — The negotiation and exchange of cryptographic keys. ## 2.3 IP Authentication Header (AH) — RFC 4302 The **Authentication Header (AH)** provides: - ✅ **Data origin authentication** - ✅ **Connectionless integrity** - ✅ **Anti-replay protection** - ❌ **No confidentiality** (no encryption) ### 2.3.1 AH Header Format (Mandatory KTU Diagram Topic) The AH is inserted between the standard IP header and the payload. Its structure is exactly **24 bytes** (fixed) plus a variable **ICV** (Integrity Check Value). | Field | Size (bits) | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Next Header** | 8 | Identifies the next protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.) | | **Payload Length** | 8 | Length of AH in 32-bit words minus 2 | | **Reserved** | 16 | Set to zero (for future use) | | **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | 32 | Identifies the Security Association (SA) | | **Sequence Number** | 32 | Monotonically increasing counter for anti-replay | | **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | Variable | MAC computed over the packet (e.g., 96 bits for HMAC-SHA-1) | > [!NOTE] Important: Mutable Fields > AH sets the **TTL**, **Header Checksum**, and **TOS** fields to zero during ICV calculation, because these change in transit and would invalidate the MAC. ## 2.4 IP Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) — RFC 4303 ESP is the **more powerful** and more commonly used protocol. It provides: - ✅ **Confidentiality** (via symmetric encryption: AES, 3DES) - ✅ **Data origin authentication** - ✅ **Connectionless integrity** - ✅ **Anti-replay protection** - ✅ **Limited traffic-flow confidentiality** (in tunnel mode) ### 2.4.1 ESP Packet Structure ESP uses a **trailer + trailer authentication** model: $$ \text{IP Header} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Header} \;\vert\; \text{Encrypted Payload} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Trailer} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Auth (ICV)} $$ The **ESP Trailer** contains: - **Padding** (0–255 bytes) — to align plaintext to block cipher size. - **Pad Length** (8 bits) — number of pad bytes added. - **Next Header** (8 bits) — type of payload (TCP, UDP, etc.). ## 2.5 Two Modes of Operation > [!IMPORTANT] KTU Favourite Question > **Transport Mode** vs **Tunnel Mode** — This comparison carries high marks every semester. | Feature | **Transport Mode** | **Tunnel Mode** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **IP Header** | Original IP header preserved | New outer IP header added | | **AH/ESP Placement** | After original IP header | Entire original packet encapsulated | | **Use Case** | Host-to-host communication | Gateway-to-gateway (VPN) or host-to-gateway | | **Overhead** | Lower (≈ 20–24 bytes) | Higher (≈ 20+24+20 = 64+ bytes) | | **IP Address Visibility** | Original IP addresses exposed | Original IPs hidden behind gateway | | **MTU Impact** | Minimal | May require fragmentation | ## 2.6 Security Association (SA) — The Foundation A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way logical relationship** between sender and receiver that defines the security parameters for a connection. > [!NOTE] One-Way Rule > A bidirectional communication requires **two SAs** (one in each direction). Each SA is identified by a unique triple: $$ \text{SA Identifier} = \{\,\text{Destination IP},\; \text{IPSec Protocol (AH/ESP)},\; \text{SPI}\,\} $$ The SPI is a **32-bit pseudo-random number** chosen by the receiver to identify the SA. It is stored in the **Security Association Database (SAD)**. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] Master this table. Every equation here has appeared in past KTU papers. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ICV (HMAC-SHA-1)** | $\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(\text{Packet}) \bmod 2^{96}$ | $K$ = secret key, truncated to 96 bits | Packet integrity check | | **Effective MTU (Tunnel)** | $\text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = \text{MTU}_{\text{link}} - \text{Overhead}_{\text{IPSec}} - \text{OuterIP}$ | $\text{Overhead}_{\text{IPSec}} \in [50, 77]$ | VPN path MTU discovery | | **AH Header Size** | $L_{\text{AH}} = 24 + L_{\text{ICV}}$ | $L_{\text{ICV}} \in \{96, 128, 192\}$ bits | Bandwidth calculation | | **ESP Header Size** | $L_{\text{ESP}} = 8 + L_{\text{ICV}} + L_{\text{Pad}} + 2$ | Headers + padding | Encryption block alignment | | **Block Padding** | $\text{Pad} = 16 - (\text{PayloadLen} \bmod 16)$ | AES = 16-byte block | Ensuring cipher alignment | | **Anti-Replay Window** | $W = 2^{32} - 1$ maximum, default 64 packets | 32-bit sequence counter | Replay protection range | | **SA Count (Bidirectional)** | $N_{\text{SA}} = 2 \times N_{\text{flows}}$ | $N_{\text{flows}}$ = number of sessions | Memory in SADB | | **SPI Range** | $\text{SPI} \in [1,\; 2^{32}-1]$ | 0 reserved for local use only | SA lookup key | # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Effective MTU for an IPSec Tunnel A frequently asked numerical problem in KTU is computing the **Effective MTU** for a tunnel-mode ESP packet using AES-CBC encryption with HMAC-SHA-1 integrity. ### 3.2 Problem Setup **Given:** - Original IP packet (TCP payload) size: $L_{\text{orig}} = 1400$ bytes - Encryption: AES-CBC (block size = 16 bytes) - Integrity: HMAC-SHA-1 (ICV = 12 bytes) - Tunnel mode ESP is used - Outer IP header size: $L_{\text{OIP}} = 20$ bytes - ESP Header size: $L_{\text{ESP-H}} = 8$ bytes - ESP Trailer (Pad Length + Next Header) size: $L_{\text{ESP-T}} = 2$ bytes **To find:** Effective MTU the upper layer can use (i.e., the max TCP payload that fits without fragmentation). ### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify total IPSec overhead.** The IPSec overhead consists of the new outer IP header, the ESP header, the ESP trailer, padding, and the ESP authentication ICV: $$ \text{Overhead}_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{OIP}} + L_{\text{ESP-H}} + L_{\text{ESP-T}} + L_{\text{pad}} + L_{\text{ICV}} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate padding requirement.** Before encryption, the payload + ESP trailer must be a multiple of the AES block size (16 bytes). The total data to be encrypted includes the original payload plus the ESP trailer (2 bytes): $$ \text{Data to encrypt} = L_{\text{orig}} + L_{\text{ESP-T}} = 1400 + 2 = 1402 \text{ bytes} $$ To make this a multiple of 16, padding is added: $$ L_{\text{pad}} = 16 - (1402 \bmod 16) $$ Compute the remainder: $$ 1402 \bmod 16 = 1402 - 87 \times 16 = 1402 - 1392 = 10 $$ Therefore: $$ L_{\text{pad}} = 16 - 10 = 6 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the total packet size.** $$ L_{\text{packet}} = L_{\text{OIP}} + L_{\text{ESP-H}} + (L_{\text{orig}} + L_{\text{ESP-T}} + L_{\text{pad}}) + L_{\text{ICV}} $$ Substituting the values: $$ L_{\text{packet}} = 20 + 8 + (1400 + 2 + 6) + 12 $$ $$ L_{\text{packet}} = 20 + 8 + 1408 + 12 = 1448 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the effective MTU for upper layers.** The standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. Subtracting the IPSec overhead gives the effective MTU: $$ \text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = 1500 - (L_{\text{OIP}} + L_{\text{ESP-H}} + L_{\text{ESP-T}} + L_{\text{pad}} + L_{\text{ICV}}) $$ $$ \text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = 1500 - (20 + 8 + 2 + 6 + 12) $$ $$ \text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = 1500 - 48 = 1452 \text{ bytes} $$ > [!NOTE] Standard Result > This is the famous **PPPoE + IPSec** result. In real-world VPNs, the effective MTU is often set to **1400 bytes** to be safe, but the precise computation above is what KTU expects. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode & Protocol Combinations The following table captures the **packet protection matrix** that KTU examiners love to test: | Mode | Protocol | Authentication Covers | Encryption Covers | Original IP Header Visible? | ICV Covers Outer IP? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Transport** | **AH** | IP payload + original IP header (excluding mutable fields) | Nothing (no encryption) | Yes | N/A | | **Transport** | **ESP** | IP payload + ESP header + ESP trailer | IP payload + ESP trailer | Yes | N/A | | **Tunnel** | **AH** | Entire original packet + new outer IP header (excluding mutable fields) | Nothing | No (hidden inside) | Partially (mutable fields excluded) | | **Tunnel** | **ESP** | ESP header + encrypted payload + ESP trailer | Entire original IP packet + ESP trailer | No (hidden inside) | N/A | ## 3.5 Step-by-Step: ICV Computation for AH When AH calculates the ICV, it includes almost the entire packet. The steps are: 1. The **TTL** field (8 bits) of the IP header is set to **0**. 2. The **Header Checksum** field (16 bits) is set to **0**. 3. The **TOS / DSCP / ECN** fields may also be zeroed (implementation dependent). 4. The MAC is computed over the **modified IP header + AH + payload**. 5. The result is **truncated** to 96 bits for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1. 6. The original (unmodified) IP header is restored for transmission. $$ \text{ICV} = \text{Trunc}_{96}\big(\text{HMAC\_SHA1}_K(\text{IP}_{\text{zeroed}} \,\vert\, \text{AH} \,\vert\, \text{Payload})\big) $$ # 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level IPSec Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Layer
HTTP, SMTP, FTP] --> B[Transport Layer
TCP / UDP] B --> C[IPSec Layer
AH / ESP Processing] C --> D[Internet Layer
IP Header Construction] D --> E[Data Link Layer
Ethernet / WiFi] E --> F[Physical Layer
Cables / Radio Waves] C -.uses.-> G[Security Association Database
SAD] C -.uses.-> H[Security Policy Database
SPD] C -.uses.-> I[Key Management
IKEv2 Protocol] G --> J[SPI Lookup] H --> K[Policy Decision
Protect / Bypass / Discard] I --> L[Negotiate Keys
ISAKMP Exchange] ``` ## 4.2 Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode Packet Encapsulation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TM1[Transport Mode ESP] A1[IP Header] --> A2[ESP Header] A2 --> A3[TCP Header] A3 --> A4[Data] A4 --> A5[ESP Trailer
Pad + PadLen + NextHdr] A5 --> A6[ESP ICV
96 bits] end subgraph TUM1[Tunnel Mode ESP] B1[New Outer IP Header] --> B2[ESP Header] B2 --> B3[Original IP Header] B3 --> B4[TCP Header] B4 --> B5[Data] B5 --> B6[ESP Trailer] B6 --> B7[ESP ICV] end ``` ## 4.3 IPSec Protocol Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Need Network Security?]) --> Q1{Confidentiality
required?} Q1 -- No --> AH[Use AH
Authentication Header] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Privacy of internal
IP addresses needed?} Q2 -- No --> ESP_T[Use ESP
Transport Mode] Q2 -- Yes --> ESP_TU[Use ESP
Tunnel Mode VPN] AH --> ModeA{Mode?} ESP_T --> ModeB{Mode?} ESP_TU --> ModeC{Mode?} ModeA -- Host to Host --> TM_AH[Transport AH] ModeA -- Network to Network --> TU_AH[Tunnel AH] ModeB --> TM_ESP[Transport ESP] ModeC --> TU_ESP[Tunnel ESP] ``` ## 4.4 Security Association (SA) Identification Triple ```mermaid flowchart LR SA[Security Association SA] --> SPI[SPI
Security Parameters Index
32 bits] SA --> DIP[Destination IP Address] SA --> PROTO[Protocol
AH or ESP] SA --> SADB[(SADB
Security Association Database)] SPI --> KEY[Encryption Key] SPI --> ALGO[Algorithms] SPI --> LIFETIME[Lifetime / Anti-Replay] SPI --> MODE[Mode: Transport / Tunnel] ``` ## 4.5 IP Packet Journey Through IPSec Gateway (Tunnel Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sender Host
192.168.1.10] -->|Plain IP Packet| GW1[IPSec Gateway A
203.0.113.5] GW1 -->|ESP Encrypted
New IP Header| NET((Public Internet)) NET --> GW2[IPSec Gateway B
198.51.100.7] GW2 -->|Decrypted
Original IP Restored| S2[Receiver Host
10.0.0.5] GW1 -.Uses.- SA1[SA1:
Encryption: AES-256
Integrity: SHA-256] GW2 -.Uses.- SA2[SA2:
Encryption: AES-256
Integrity: SHA-256] ``` # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define IPSec. List any four services provided by IPSec.** **Model Answer:** **IP Security (IPSec)** is a framework of open standards developed by the IETF to ensure **secure communication over IP networks** by operating at the **network layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model. **Four services provided by IPSec:** 1. **Data origin authentication** — Verifies the identity of the packet sender. 2. **Data integrity** — Ensures the packet has not been modified in transit. 3. **Confidentiality** — Protects payload from unauthorized disclosure (via ESP). 4. **Replay protection** — Detects and rejects duplicate or out-of-order packets using sequence numbers. *(Other acceptable services: Access control, Limited traffic-flow confidentiality)*. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is a Security Association (SA) in IPSec? Why is it one-way?** **Model Answer:** A **Security Association (SA)** is a **logical, one-way relationship** between two IPSec entities (hosts or gateways) that defines the **set of security parameters** (algorithms, keys, SPI, mode, lifetime) used to protect the communication. **Why one-way:** - A single SA defines the **parameters for traffic flowing in one direction only**. - A bidirectional conversation requires **two SAs** (one in each direction), because each direction may use different keys, different SPIs, and possibly even different algorithms. - This allows **asymmetric security policies** and simplifies rekeying. Each SA is uniquely identified by the triple: **``**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *IPSec Architecture & ESP in Depth* #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Explain the IPSec architecture in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the ESP packet format and explain each field.** **Model Solution:** **IPSec Architecture** has two main parts: 1. **IPSec Protocols**: AH (RFC 4302) and ESP (RFC 4303) — operate on the datagram. 2. **Key Management**: IKE / IKEv2 (RFC 7296) — negotiates SAs and keys. Supporting databases: - **SPD (Security Policy Database)** — decides whether a packet is *Protected, Bypassed, or Discarded*. - **SAD (Security Association Database)** — stores the active SAs indexed by SPI. - **PAD (Peer Authentication Database)** — stores authentication credentials. **ESP Packet Format:** | Field | Size | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **SPI** | 32 bits | Identifies the SA in the SAD | | **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter | | **Payload Data** | Variable | Encrypted original payload | | **Padding** | 0–255 bytes | Aligns plaintext to cipher block size | | **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Number of pad bytes | | **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload (e.g., TCP = 6) | | **ICV** | 32–128 bits | Integrity check over ESP header + payload + trailer | The encryption covers the **Payload + Padding + Pad Length + Next Header** fields, while authentication covers **ESP Header + Payload + ESP Trailer**. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode of IPSec. When would you prefer Tunnel Mode over Transport Mode?** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **IP Header** | Original preserved | New outer IP header added | | **Packet Encapsulation** | AH/ESP inserted after IP header | Entire original IP packet encapsulated | | **Overhead** | 20–24 bytes (ESP) | 50–77+ bytes | | **Use Case** | Host-to-host within trusted LAN | Gateway-to-gateway across Internet | | **NAT Compatibility** | Poor (IP addresses visible) | Excellent (private IPs hidden) | **Tunnel Mode is preferred when:** 1. Connecting two **separate private networks** across the Internet (**site-to-site VPN**). 2. Hiding the **internal IP addressing scheme** of an organization. 3. Communicating with **mobile users** through a VPN gateway. 4. When one or both endpoints are **security gateways** (e.g., Cisco ASA, FortiGate). 5. Implementing **remote access VPNs** where internal hosts lack IPSec software. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *AH, SAs, and MTU Calculations* #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **With a neat diagram, explain the Authentication Header (AH) format. How does it differ from ESP?** **Model Solution:** **AH Header Format (in bytes):** | Field | Size (bits) | | :--- | :--- | | Next Header | 8 | | Payload Length | 8 | | Reserved | 16 | | SPI | 32 | | Sequence Number | 32 | | ICV (variable) | 96 / 128 / 192 | **Differences between AH and ESP:** | Feature | AH | ESP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Confidentiality** | ❌ None | ✅ Yes (AES, 3DES) | | **Integrity** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Authentication** | ✅ Yes (covers IP header) | ✅ Yes (covers inner header only) | | **Replay Protection** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **NAT Traversal** | ❌ Breaks NAT | ✅ Possible with NAT-T | | **IP Header Protection** | ✅ Yes (excluding mutable fields) | ❌ No (in transport mode) | AH is suitable when **confidentiality is not required** (e.g., legal constraints on encryption) but **strong integrity of the entire IP header** is needed. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **An organization uses ESP in Tunnel Mode with AES-128-CBC encryption and HMAC-SHA-1 integrity. The original TCP segment is 1300 bytes. The outer IP header is 20 bytes, ESP header is 8 bytes, ESP trailer (Pad Len + Next Header) is 2 bytes, and ICV is 12 bytes. Calculate:** 1. **The padding required.** 2. **The total ESP-encapsulated packet size.** 3. **The effective MTU for upper layers** (assume link MTU = 1500 bytes). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate padding.** AES-CBC has a block size of **16 bytes**. The data to be encrypted = payload + ESP trailer: $$ \text{Data} = 1300 + 2 = 1302 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ 1302 \bmod 16 = 1302 - 81 \times 16 = 1302 - 1296 = 6 $$ $$ L_{\text{pad}} = 16 - 6 = 10 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate total packet size.** $$ L_{\text{total}} = 20 + 8 + 1300 + 2 + 10 + 12 $$ $$ L_{\text{total}} = 1352 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate effective MTU.** $$ \text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = 1500 - (20 + 8 + 2 + 10 + 12) $$ $$ \text{MTU}_{\text{eff}} = 1500 - 52 = 1448 \text{ bytes} $$ > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > **Common mistakes students make (deduct 2–3 marks each):** > 1. ❌ Forgetting to include the **ESP trailer (2 bytes)** when calculating padding requirement. > 2. ❌ Using **block size = 8** for AES (correct is **16 bytes**; 8 is for DES/3DES). > 3. ❌ Forgetting to subtract the ICV in the MTU calculation. > 4. ❌ Confusing **payload length** (1300 bytes) with **packet length** (includes headers). > 5. ❌ In AH vs ESP comparison, writing *"AH provides encryption"* — this is a fatal error; AH never encrypts. > 6. ❌ Forgetting that **SA is one-way** in 14-mark questions leads to loss of 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam ### 🔑 Core Concepts - IPSec operates at **Layer 3 (Network Layer)** — works for **all upper-layer protocols** (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.). - **Two protocols**: **AH (51)** = Authenticity + Integrity only; **ESP (50)** = + Confidentiality. - **Two modes**: **Transport** (host-to-host, original IP preserved) vs **Tunnel** (gateway-to-gateway, new IP added). - **One-way SA**, identified by triple: `{Destination IP, AH/ESP, SPI}`. - **IKEv2** (port 500/4500) negotiates SAs and keys. - **Replay protection** uses a **32-bit sequence number** and a **sliding window** (default 64). ### 📊 Numerical Must-Knows - AES block size = **16 bytes**; DES/3DES = **8 bytes**. - HMAC-SHA-1 ICV = **12 bytes (96 bits)**. - Tunnel mode overhead ≈ **50–77 bytes**. - Default anti-replay window = **64 packets**. ### 🚫 Frequent Negative Marks - Do **NOT** say AH provides encryption. - Do **NOT** say one SA is enough for bidirectional traffic. - Do **NOT** confuse the **SPI** (32 bits) with the **Sequence Number** (32 bits) — they are different fields. - Do **NOT** forget the **ESP trailer (2 bytes)** in padding calculations.

topics.title: IP Security Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IP Security Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏛️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IP Security Architecture (IPSec)** is a framework of open standards developed by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** that provides cryptographic security services for Internet Protocol (IP) networks at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model. It ensures end-to-end security for data traversing untrusted networks such as the public Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Direct from RFC 4301):** "IPSec provides security services at the IP layer by enabling a system to select required security protocols, determine the algorithms to be used, and put in place any cryptographic keys required to provide the requested services."

The architecture is formally defined in a series of **Request for Comments (RFCs)** published by the IETF IPSec Working Group, the principal ones being:
- **RFC 4301** – Security Architecture for the Internet Protocol
- **RFC 4302** – IP Authentication Header (AH)
- **RFC 4303** – IP Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)
- **RFC 4306 / RFC 7296** – Internet Key Exchange (IKEv1 / IKEv2)

## 1.2 Key Services Provided by IPSec

IPSec offers four primary security services, each addressable independently:

| Service | Mechanism | Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Origin Authentication** | MAC / HMAC | AH or ESP |
| **Data Integrity** | Hash / MAC verification | AH or ESP |
| **Confidentiality** | Symmetric encryption (DES, 3DES, AES) | ESP only |
| **Replay Protection (Anti-Replay)** | Sequence number / Sliding window | AH or ESP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Only **ESP** provides **confidentiality (encryption)**. AH provides authentication and integrity but **NOT** encryption. This is a classic 3-mark question trap.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏤

Imagine you are a **diplomat** sending a top-secret treaty document from Embassy A (New Delhi) to Embassy B (Washington D.C.):

| Diplomatic Step | IPSec Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You sign the document with your unique wax seal | **Data Integrity & Authentication (AH)** |
| You lock the document inside a tamper-proof briefcase with a key | **Confidentiality (ESP encryption)** |
| The courier tracks the dispatch number — no document is accepted twice with the same tracking number | **Anti-Replay Protection (Sequence Number)** |
| The embassy and your office agree *in advance* on the briefcase model, the key type, and the seal design | **Security Association (SA)** |
| A trusted courier service is hired to safely transport the briefcase | **IPSec Encapsulation & Tunnel Mode** |

In this analogy, the **IPSec framework** is the entire diplomatic courier protocol, ensuring that even if the package is intercepted en route, no one can read it (confidentiality), tamper with it (integrity), impersonate the sender (authentication), or reuse an old captured copy (anti-replay).

## 1.4 The "Big Picture" of IPSec

IPSec is not a single protocol — it is an **architecture** comprising three functional layers:

1. **Encapsulation Protocols** (AH, ESP) — perform the actual cryptographic operations on packets
2. **Security Association Management** (SA, SPD, SAD) — track state and policy
3. **Key Management** (IKE / IKEv2) — automate the creation, negotiation, and refresh of cryptographic keys

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Recall:** **"E-S-K"** → **E**ncapsulation (AH/ESP) → **S**ecurity Association (SA/SPD/SAD) → **K**ey Exchange (IKE). Use this to remember the architectural hierarchy.

# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of IPSec Architecture

### Pillar 1: Encapsulation Protocols

#### (a) Authentication Header (AH) — IP Protocol Number **51**

AH provides **connectionless integrity, data origin authentication, and optional anti-replay protection** for IP packets. It does **NOT** provide confidentiality (no encryption).

**AH Header Format (RFC 4302):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AH Header} = \big[ \text{Next Header} \,\|\, \text{Payload Len} \,\|\, \text{Reserved} \,\|\, \text{SPI} \,\|\, \text{Sequence Number} \,\|\, \text{ICV} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Next Header** | 8 | Identifies the next payload (e.g., TCP = 6, UDP = 17) |
| **Payload Length** | 8 | Length of AH in 32-bit words minus 2 |
| **Reserved** | 16 | Must be set to zero |
| **SPI** (Security Parameters Index) | 32 | Identifies the Security Association |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Monotonically increasing counter for anti-replay |
| **ICV** (Integrity Check Value) | 32–128 | MAC/HMAC output (e.g., HMAC-MD5-96, HMAC-SHA-1-96) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AH authenticates **the entire IP packet including the outer IP header** (except for mutable fields like TTL and Header Checksum). This is why AH cannot traverse NAT (which modifies IP addresses).

#### (b) Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) — IP Protocol Number **50**

ESP provides **confidentiality, data origin authentication, integrity, and anti-replay protection**. It is the more comprehensive and widely used protocol of the two.

**ESP Packet Format (RFC 4303):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ESP Packet} = \big[ \text{ESP Header} \,\|\, \text{Encrypted Payload} \,\|\, \text{ESP Trailer} \,\|\, \text{ESP Auth} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **ESP Header** | Contains SPI and Sequence Number (in cleartext) |
| **Payload Data** | **Encrypted** transport segment (TCP/UDP) or entire IP packet |
| **ESP Trailer** | Padding, Pad Length, Next Header (for decryption alignment) |
| **ESP Auth** | ICV over header + ciphertext + trailer (integrity check) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two integrity checks in ESP?** ESP authenticates only the **encrypted portion** (not the outer IP header). This makes ESP **NAT-friendly** because NAT modifies the outer IP header without breaking the integrity check.

### Pillar 2: Security Association (SA)

A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way (simplex) logical connection** between two IPSec endpoints that defines the security parameters for protecting traffic. A full duplex connection therefore requires **two SAs** (one in each direction).

**SA is uniquely identified by a 3-tuple:**

$$
\text{SA Identifier} = \langle \text{Destination IP Address, SPI, Protocol (AH or ESP)} \rangle
$$

**Key SA Parameters (stored in SAD):**
- **AH/ESP transform** (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256, AES-128-CBC)
- **Encryption & authentication keys**
- **SA lifetime** (bytes or seconds)
- **Anti-replay window size**
- **SA mode** (transport or tunnel)

> [!TIP]
> **SA is one-way** — this is the most commonly missed point in KTU exams. If the question says "establish an SA between A and B," it implies **two SAs** are needed.

#### Security Policy Database (SPD)

The SPD specifies the **policies** that determine whether a packet should be:
- **Discarded** (no traffic allowed)
- **Bypassed** (IPSec skipped — normal IP forwarding)
- **Protected** (apply IPSec using a specific SA)

#### Security Association Database (SAD)

The SAD contains the **active state** for every currently established SA — keys, sequence numbers, lifetimes, etc.

### Pillar 3: Key Management — Internet Key Exchange (IKE)

IKE is a **two-phase** protocol that automates SA negotiation and key exchange:

| Phase | Purpose | Message Exchanges |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | Mutual authentication, establish ISAKMP SA, derive keying material | Main Mode (6 msgs) or Aggressive Mode (3 msgs) |
| **Phase 2** | Negotiate IPSec SAs (AH/ESP) using the secure channel from Phase 1 | Quick Mode (3 msgs) |

**IKE uses UDP Port 500** (or **4500** with NAT-Traversal).

**IKEv2 (RFC 7296)** simplifies this to a single 4-message exchange (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH).

## 2.2 The Two Operational Modes of IPSec

### Mode 1: Transport Mode

**Use Case:** Host-to-host communication between two **end systems** that are both IPSec-aware.

**Operation:** Protects only the **payload** of the IP packet. The original IP header is preserved (with minor modifications).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Packet} &= \big[ \text{IP Header} \,\|\, \text{TCP/UDP} \,\|\, \text{Data} \big] \\
\text{Transport Mode (ESP)} &= \big[ \text{IP Header} \,\|\, \text{ESP Header} \,\|\, \text{Encrypted (TCP/UDP + Data)} \,\|\, \text{ESP Trailer} \,\|\, \text{ESP Auth} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages:**
- Lower overhead (no extra IP header)
- Suitable for end-to-end host communication

**Disadvantage:**
- Exposes the real source/destination IP addresses (no privacy on routing)

### Mode 2: Tunnel Mode

**Use Case:** Gateway-to-gateway (e.g., Site-to-Site VPN) or host-to-gateway (Remote Access VPN). Either endpoint can be a non-IPSec-aware device behind a Security Gateway.

**Operation:** The **entire original IP packet** is encapsulated inside a new IP packet with a fresh outer IP header.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tunnel Mode (ESP)} &= \big[ \text{New IP Header} \,\|\, \text{ESP Header} \,\|\, \text{Encrypted (Original IP + TCP/UDP + Data)} \,\|\, \text{ESP Trailer} \,\|\, \text{ESP Auth} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages:**
- Hides the original source/destination IPs
- Enables VPN through firewalls/NAT (using ESP with NAT-T)

**Disadvantage:**
- Adds **20+ bytes** of extra IP header overhead per packet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Mode for VPNs:** **Tunnel mode** is the default for most commercial VPN deployments (e.g., site-to-site IPsec VPN between branch offices). AH in transport mode is the **default** when only integrity is needed between hosts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet 📋

| Concept | Key Value / Rule | Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Protocol Number — AH** | **51** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **IP Protocol Number — ESP** | **50** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **IKE Port (UDP)** | **500** (4500 for NAT-T) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ESP Header Fields** | SPI (32) + Sequence Number (32) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **AH Header Fields** | Next Hdr + Payload Len + Reserved + SPI + Seq# + ICV | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SA is** | **One-way (simplex)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SA Identifier (Triple)** | $\langle \text{DestIP, SPI, Protocol} \rangle$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Confidentiality provided by** | **ESP only** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **AH authenticates** | Entire IP packet + payload | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ESP authenticates** | ESP header + ciphertext + ESP trailer (NOT outer IP) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **IKE Phases** | Phase 1 (ISAKMP SA) → Phase 2 (IPSec SA) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Default VPN Mode** | Tunnel Mode | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **AH + NAT** | **Incompatible** (mutates IP headers) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ESP + NAT** | **Compatible** (via NAT-T, UDP 4500) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Table Rule:** I have used `\vert` / `\mid` in place of `|` everywhere in the SA triple. Forgetting the **3-tuple composition** for an SA identifier is one of the most common KTU answer-sheet mistakes.

## 2.4 Why IPSec Matters in Engineering Practice

| Application Domain | IPSec Use |
|---|---|
| **Enterprise IT** | Site-to-site VPNs between branch offices, remote worker access |
| **Cloud Computing** | Securing traffic between on-premise data centers and AWS/Azure VPCs |
| **Government & Defense** | Classified network communications (e.g., MPLS/IPSec hybrid) |
| **IoT** | Lightweight IPsec profiles (RFC 7296) for embedded systems |
| **Telecommunications (5G)** | Securing backhaul between gNB and core network elements |

# 🔍 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Processing Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: ESP in Tunnel Mode Step-by-Step

This is the **most asked IPSec diagram** in KTU exams. Below is the exhaustive packet transformation trace.

### Step 1: Original (Unprotected) Packet Construction

An end-host application generates a normal IPv4 packet:

$$
P_{\text{orig}} = \big[\, H_{\text{IP}} \,\|\, H_{\text{TCP}} \,\|\, D \,\big]
$$

Where:
- $H_{\text{IP}}$ = Original IP Header (20 bytes minimum), contains Source IP, Dest IP, TTL, Protocol = 6 (TCP)
- $H_{\text{TCP}}$ = TCP header (Source Port, Dest Port, Seq, Ack, Flags, Checksum)
- $D$ = Application payload data (e.g., HTTP request, file transfer)

**Total original packet size (example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert P_{\text{orig}} \rvert &= \lvert H_{\text{IP}} \rvert + \lvert H_{\text{TCP}} \rvert + \lvert D \rvert \\
&= 20 + 20 + 100 = 140 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: ESP Header Construction (at the Sender)

The IPSec module constructs the ESP header (8 bytes):

$$
H_{\text{ESP}} = \big[\, \text{SPI}_{32} \,\|\, \text{SeqNum}_{32} \,\big]
$$

- **SPI** = 32-bit value identifying the SA, e.g., $0x0000C0FF$ (arbitrary)
- **SeqNum** = monotonically increasing counter, e.g., $0x00000001$ (first packet)

### Step 3: ESP Trailer Construction

To align ciphertext to the encryption block size, padding is added:

$$
T_{\text{ESP}} = \big[\, \text{Pad} \,\|\, \text{PadLength}_{8} \,\|\, \text{NextHeader}_{8} \,\big]
$$

- **Pad** = zero or more padding bytes (0–255)
- **PadLength** = 8-bit count of pad bytes
- **NextHeader** = 8-bit protocol of original payload (e.g., 6 for TCP)

### Step 4: Encryption of Payload + Trailer

The sender encrypts the original packet + ESP trailer using the negotiated cipher:

$$
C = E_K(\, H_{\text{TCP}} \,\|\, D \,\|\, T_{\text{ESP}} \,)
$$

Where $E_K$ denotes the symmetric encryption algorithm (e.g., **AES-128-CBC**) and $K$ is the session encryption key. The SPI and SeqNum are **not** encrypted.

### Step 5: ESP Authentication (ICV Generation)

The sender computes the Integrity Check Value over the authenticated portion:

$$
\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K^{\text{auth}}(\, H_{\text{ESP}} \,\|\, C \,)
$$

Typical algorithms: **HMAC-SHA-256-128** (128-bit ICV) or **HMAC-SHA-1-96** (96-bit ICV).

### Step 6: Outer (New) IP Header Construction

A new IP header is prepended for tunnel mode:

$$
H_{\text{IP}}^{\text{new}} = \big[\, \text{SrcIP}_{\text{gw1}} \,\|\, \text{DstIP}_{\text{gw2}} \,\|\, \text{Protocol}=50 \,\|\, \text{TTL} \,\big]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The outer IP header contains the **Security Gateway addresses** (e.g., firewall at branch A and firewall at branch B), not the original end-host IPs. This is the essence of tunnel-mode privacy.

### Step 7: Final Tunneled ESP Packet

$$
P_{\text{ESP-tunnel}} = \big[\, H_{\text{IP}}^{\text{new}} \,\|\, H_{\text{ESP}} \,\|\, C \,\|\, \text{ICV} \,\big]
$$

**Total packet size (example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert P_{\text{ESP-tunnel}} \rvert &= 20 + 8 + \lvert C \rvert + \lvert \text{ICV} \rvert \\
&= 20 + 8 + (20+100+3) + 16 = 167 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming 3 bytes of padding to reach a 16-byte AES block boundary, and a 16-byte ICV.)

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: End-to-End Processing at Sender and Receiver

| Stage | Sender Action | Receiver Action | Key Storage Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Policy Lookup** | Query SPD for outgoing packet | Query SPD for incoming packet | SPD (Policy DB) |
| **2. SA Lookup** | Check SAD for matching SA | Check SAD using $\langle \text{DestIP, SPI, Protocol} \rangle$ | SAD (Association DB) |
| **3. SA Negotiation** | Trigger IKE if no SA exists | Respond to IKE proposals | IKE / ISAKMP |
| **4. Sequence Number** | Increment counter (anti-replay) | Check seq# is within sliding window | SAD |
| **5. Encryption** | $C = E_K(\text{payload} + \text{trailer})$ | $P = D_K(C)$ | SAD (encryption key) |
| **6. Authentication** | $\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}(\text{header} \,\|\, C)$ | Verify $\text{HMAC}$ matches; reject if invalid | SAD (auth key) |
| **7. Packet Assembly** | Prepend new IP header (tunnel) or reuse original (transport) | Strip ESP header, forward decrypted payload | — |

## 3.3 ESP Processing Logic — Exhaustive Algorithm Trace

**Outbound (Sender) Processing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{IF (SPD lookup returns "PROTECT") THEN} \\
& \text{  IF (no matching SA in SAD) THEN trigger IKE} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Increment SeqNum; Reject if counter overflows } 2^{32} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{Compute } C = E_K(\text{payload} + \text{trailer}) \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Compute } \text{ICV} = \text{MAC}_{K_{\text{auth}}}(H_{\text{ESP}} + C) \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Assemble: } [\text{NewIP} + H_{\text{ESP}} + C + \text{ICV}] \rightarrow \text{Network}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inbound (Receiver) Processing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Look up SA using } \langle \text{DestIP, SPI, Protocol=50} \rangle \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Verify SeqNum in sliding window (replay defense)} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{Recompute } \text{MAC}_{K_{\text{auth}}}(H_{\text{ESP}} + C) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{ICV} \\
& \text{  IF mismatch → Drop packet, log auth failure} \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Decrypt } P = D_K(C) \text{ to obtain original payload} \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Remove padding, deliver payload to upper layer}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Order of Receiver Checks:** Always verify **integrity FIRST, then decrypt** in ESP. This is cryptographically correct (avoids padding oracle attacks) and is what IKEv2 mandates.

## 3.4 Comparative Mathematical Cost Analysis

| Mode | Added Overhead (bytes) | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **AH Transport** | 24 bytes (header) | Host-to-host integrity only |
| **AH Tunnel** | 24 + 20 = 44 bytes | Rare (gateway with auth only) |
| **ESP Transport** | 8 (header) + 2 (trailer min) + 12 (ICV min) ≈ 22 bytes | Host-to-host encryption |
| **ESP Tunnel** | 22 + 20 (new IP) ≈ 42 bytes | **Most common (VPN)** |

**Packet Overhead Ratio Formula:**

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{\lvert P_{\text{protected}} \rvert - \lvert P_{\text{orig}} \rvert}{\lvert P_{\text{orig}} \rvert} \times 100\%
$$

For a 100-byte payload in ESP tunnel mode:

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{(20+8+100+3+16) - 100}{100} \times 100\% = 47\%
$$

## 4.1 IPSec Architecture — Top-Level Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer HTTP, FTP, SMTP]
    TCP[TCP UDP Layer]
    IPSEC[IPSec Module]
    IP[IP Layer]

    APP --> TCP
    TCP --> IPSEC

    subgraph SG1[IPSec Architecture]
        direction TB
        EP[Encapsulation Protocols]
        SAM[Security Association Management]

        subgraph EP1[Encapsulation Protocols]
            AH[AH Protocol 51]
            ESP[ESP Protocol 50]
        end

        subgraph SAM1[SA Management]
            SA[Security Association SA]
            SPD[Security Policy Database SPD]
            SAD[Security Association Database SAD]
        end

        subgraph KM1[Key Management]
            IKE[IKE IKEv1 or IKEv2]
            ISAKMP[ISAKMP SA Phase 1]
        end
    end

    IPSEC -. uses .-> AH
    IPSEC -. uses .-> ESP
    IPSEC -. consults .-> SPD
    IPSEC -. stores in .-> SAD
    IPSEC -. negotiates via .-> IKE
    IKE -. establishes .-> ISAKMP
    ISAKMP -. secures .-> SA
    SA -. indexed in .-> SAD
```

## 4.2 AH Header Format — Bit-Level Schematic

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 8
    block:ah1["0-7 Next Header"]:1
    block:ah2["8-15 Payload Length"]:1
    block:ah3["16-31 Reserved"]:2
    block:ah4["32-63 SPI"]:4
    block:ah5["64-95 Sequence Number"]:4
    block:ah6["96-127+ ICV Variable"]:5
```

**AH Header Field Legend:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NH[Next Header 8 bits Protocol of payload e.g. 6 TCP]
    PL[Payload Length 8 bits AH length in 32-bit words minus 2]
    RS[Reserved 16 bits Set to 0]
    SPI[SPI 32 bits Security Parameters Index]
    SN[Sequence Number 32 bits Anti-replay counter]
    ICV[ICV 32 to 128 bits MAC value]
    NH --> PL --> RS --> SPI --> SN --> ICV
```

## 4.3 ESP Packet Format — Full Layout with Encryption Boundary

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 10
    block:esp1["ESP Header (Cleartext)"]:4
    block:esp2["SPI 32 bits"]:4
    block:esp3["SeqNum 32 bits"]:4
    block:esp4["Payload Data (ENCRYPTED)"]:8
    block:esp5["ESP Trailer (ENCRYPTED)"]:2
    block:esp6["Padding + PadLen + NextHdr"]:2
    block:esp7["ESP Auth (Cleartext ICV)"]:4
```

**Encryption Boundary Visualization:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IP Header] --> B[ESP Header SPI + SeqNum]
    B --> C[Encrypted Region]
    C --> D[Original TCP UDP Payload]
    C --> E[Data]
    C --> F[ESP Trailer]
    F --> G[ESP Auth ICV]
    G --> H[Forwarded to Network]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shaded "Encrypted Region" includes the original payload AND the ESP trailer. The ESP Auth (ICV) is computed over ESP Header + Encrypted Region — **the outer IP header is excluded** in ESP.

## 4.4 Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode — Side-by-Side Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TM[Transport Mode]
        direction LR
        TM1[Original IP Header SrcIP, DstIP]
        TM2[AH or ESP Header]

        TM5[ESP ICV if ESP]

    end

    subgraph TUM[Tunnel Mode]
        direction LR
        TUM1[NEW IP Header Gateway IPs]

    end
```

## 4.5 IKE Two-Phase Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Initiator as Initiator Host A
    responder as Responder Host B

    Note over Initiator,responder: Phase 1 Establish ISAKMP SA
    Initiator->>responder: Msg 1 SA Proposal + Nonce
    responder-->>Initiator: Msg 2 Chosen Proposal + Nonce
    Initiator->>responder: Msg 3 Encrypted Auth Material
    responder-->>Initiator: Msg 4 Encrypted Auth Material
    Initiator->>responder: Msg 5 Encrypted Verification
    responder-->>Initiator: Msg 6 Encrypted Verification

    Note over Initiator,responder: Phase 2 Negotiate IPSec SAs
    Initiator->>responder: Quick Msg 1 SA + Nonce + Key IDs
    responder-->>Initiator: Quick Msg 2 SA + Nonce + Key IDs
    Initiator->>responder: Quick Msg 3 Hash Confirmation

    Note over Initiator,responder: IPSec SAs Established Data Transfer Begins
```

## 4.6 SAD / SPD Interaction with Packet Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PKT[IP Packet Arrives] --> SPDQ{SPD Lookup}
    SPDQ -->|DISCARD| DROP[Drop Packet]
    SPDQ -->|BYPASS| NORM[Normal IP Forwarding]
    SPDQ -->|PROTECT| SADQ{SA Exists in SAD}

    SADQ -->|No| IKE[Trigger IKE Negotiation]
    IKE --> NEWS[Create New SA and Update SAD]
    SADQ -->|Yes| APPLY[Apply AH or ESP Transform]

    NEWS --> APPLY
    APPLY --> OUT[Protected Packet Forwarded]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Sequence to remember for KTU:** **SPD is checked FIRST** for every packet. If the policy says "PROTECT," IPSec then looks for an SA in the SAD. If absent, IKE is triggered. This is the standard control flow.

# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define IP Security. List the services provided by IPSec. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

**IP Security (IPSec)** is a framework of open standards developed by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** that provides security services — authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and anti-replay protection — at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model for IPv4 and IPv6 communications.

**Services Provided by IPSec:**

1. **Data Origin Authentication** — Verifies the identity of the sender using cryptographic MAC/HMAC.
2. **Data Integrity** — Ensures the packet has not been modified in transit using hash/MAC.
3. **Confidentiality** — Protects the payload from unauthorized disclosure using symmetric encryption (DES, 3DES, AES) — *provided by ESP only*.
4. **Anti-Replay Protection** — Detects and rejects duplicate or replayed packets using a monotonically increasing sequence number and a sliding receiver window.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list "non-repudiation" as an IPSec service. This is **incorrect** — IPSec provides authentication but **not non-repudiation** (because symmetric keys are shared). For non-repudiation, digital signatures are required.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between AH and ESP. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **AH (Authentication Header)** | **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Protocol Number** | 51 | 50 |
| **Confidentiality (Encryption)** | ❌ **Not provided** | ✅ **Provided** (DES, 3DES, AES) |
| **Integrity & Authentication** | ✅ Provided | ✅ Provided |
| **Anti-Replay** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Coverage of Authentication** | Authenticates **entire packet** (IP header + payload), except mutable fields | Authenticates **only the ESP header, payload, and trailer** (excludes outer IP header) |
| **NAT Compatibility** | ❌ **Incompatible with NAT** (because IP addresses are authenticated) | ✅ **Compatible with NAT** (using NAT-T, UDP 4500) |
| **Typical Use** | Integrity-only scenarios, host-to-host | VPN, full security services |
| **IPSec Protocol Reference** | RFC 4302 | RFC 4303 |

**Key Mnemonic:** **"AH = Authenticity, but Hidden = No"** ; **"ESP = Everything Secured Properly"**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A: Explain the IP Security Architecture in detail. Discuss the AH and ESP protocols with their header formats. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: IPSec Architecture Overview

**Model Answer:**

The **IP Security Architecture (IPSec)**, standardized under **RFC 4301**, is an IETF framework that secures IP communications at Layer 3. The architecture is organized into three major functional blocks:

**(1) Encapsulation Protocols** — Provide the actual cryptographic protection:
- **Authentication Header (AH)** — RFC 4302, IP protocol 51
- **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** — RFC 4303, IP protocol 50

**(2) Security Association Framework** — Manages state and policy:
- **Security Association (SA)** — A one-way logical connection that defines the security parameters (algorithm, keys, mode) for a conversation
- **Security Policy Database (SPD)** — Stores the rules specifying whether each packet should be **discarded, bypassed, or protected**
- **Security Association Database (SAD)** — Stores the current state of every active SA

**(3) Key Management** — Automates the creation and refresh of cryptographic keys:
- **Internet Key Exchange (IKE)** — RFC 4306 (IKEv1) / RFC 7296 (IKEv2)
- Operates over **UDP port 500** (or **UDP 4500** with NAT-Traversal)
- Two-phase: **Phase 1** establishes an ISAKMP SA; **Phase 2** negotiates IPSec SAs

**IPSec also operates in two modes:**
- **Transport Mode** — Original IP header preserved; only the payload is protected (host-to-host)
- **Tunnel Mode** — Entire original IP packet is encapsulated inside a new IP header (gateway-to-gateway VPN)

The architecture diagram is as shown in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: AH and ESP Header Formats

**AH Header Format (RFC 4302):**

$$
H_{\text{AH}} = \big[\, \text{NextHeader}_{8} \,\|\, \text{PayloadLen}_{8} \,\|\, \text{Reserved}_{16} \,\|\, \text{SPI}_{32} \,\|\, \text{SeqNum}_{32} \,\|\, \text{ICV}_{n} \,\big]
$$

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Next Header | 8 bits | Protocol of the payload following AH (e.g., 6 = TCP) |
| Payload Length | 8 bits | Length of AH in 32-bit words minus 2 |
| Reserved | 16 bits | Must be 0 |
| SPI | 32 bits | Identifies the Security Association |
| Sequence Number | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter |
| ICV | Variable (32–128 bits) | MAC/HMAC digest over the packet |

**ESP Packet Format (RFC 4303):**

$$
P_{\text{ESP}} = \big[\, \text{SPI}_{32} \,\|\, \text{SeqNum}_{32} \,\|\, \text{EncryptedPayload} \,\|\, \text{Pad, PadLen}_{8}, \text{NextHdr}_{8} \,\|\, \text{ICV}_{n} \,\big]
$$

The SPI and Sequence Number are sent in **cleartext** for routing and anti-replay processing. The **payload and trailer are encrypted** using a symmetric algorithm (AES, 3DES). The **ICV is computed** over the SPI, Sequence Number, encrypted payload, and trailer (NOT the outer IP header).

**Critical Differences:**
- AH authenticates the **entire IP packet** (including outer IP header, excluding mutable fields), making it **NAT-incompatible**
- ESP authenticates only the **ESP-protected portion** (header + ciphertext + trailer), making it **NAT-compatible**

---

### 🔹 Question B: Explain the concept of Security Association (SA) in IPSec. Discuss the different modes of IPSec with neat diagrams. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Security Association (SA)

**Model Answer:**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way (simplex) logical connection** between an IPSec sender and receiver that defines the complete set of security parameters and cryptographic material to be used for protecting traffic flowing in **one direction**.

**Key Properties of an SA:**

1. **Simplex Nature** — An SA protects traffic in only one direction. For a bidirectional IPSec session, **two separate SAs** must be established (one in each direction).

2. **SA Identifier (Triple)** — Each SA is uniquely identified by the 3-tuple:

$$
\text{SA}_{\text{ID}} = \langle \text{Destination IP Address, SPI, Protocol (AH or ESP)} \rangle
$$

   - **Destination IP** — The endpoint of the SA
   - **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** — A 32-bit value chosen by the receiver to identify the SA, carried in the AH/ESP header
   - **Protocol** — Either AH (51) or ESP (50)

3. **SA Parameters (Stored in SAD):**
   - Sequence Number Counter (for anti-replay)
   - Sequence Number Overflow Flag
   - Anti-Replay Window (32-bit or 64-bit sliding window)
   - AH/ESP Information — Algorithm, keys, IV, mode
   - SA Lifetime — Either time-based (e.g., 28,800 seconds) or byte-count-based
   - IPSec Protocol Mode — Transport or Tunnel
   - Path MTU — Maximum transmission unit for fragmentation handling

4. **SA Management** — SAs are created either **manually** (small static networks) or **automatically** via IKE (recommended for production).

**Security Policy Database (SPD):**
The SPD defines the **policies** that govern packet processing. For every packet, the SPD returns one of three actions:
- **DISCARD** — Do not allow this traffic
- **BYPASS** — Allow the packet without IPSec
- **PROTECT** — Apply a specific SA (must be present in SAD)

**Security Association Database (SAD):**
The SAD stores the **active state** of every currently valid SA — keys, sequence counters, algorithms, lifetimes, etc. The IPSec module consults the SAD on every packet to determine how to process it.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: IPSec Modes with Diagrams

**Transport Mode:**

In transport mode, **only the payload of the IP packet is protected**; the original IP header is retained (with the protocol field changed to 50 for ESP or 51 for AH).

$$
\text{Transport Mode} = \big[\, \text{Original IP Header} \,\|\, \text{AH/ESP Header} \,\|\, \text{Protected Payload} \,\|\, \text{Trailer} \,\|\, \text{ICV} \,\big]
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block:tm1["IP Header SrcIP, DstIP, Proto=50/51"]:1
    block:tm2["AH/ESP Header SPI, SeqNum"]:1
    block:tm3["TCP/UDP + Data (Encrypted if ESP)"]:1
    block:tm4["ESP Trailer Pad, NextHdr"]:1
    block:tm5["ESP ICV"]:1
```

**Use Cases:** Host-to-host communication where both endpoints implement IPSec directly (e.g., server-to-server communication in a trusted LAN).

**Tunnel Mode:**

In tunnel mode, the **entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encapsulated** inside a new IP packet with a fresh outer IP header. The original inner IP header is hidden from intermediate routers.

$$
\text{Tunnel Mode} = \big[\, \text{New IP Header (Gateway IPs)} \,\|\, \text{AH/ESP Header} \,\|\, \text{Original IP Packet} \,\|\, \text{Trailer} \,\|\, \text{ICV} \,\big]
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 6
    block:tum1["New IP Header Gateway1 → Gateway2"]:1
    block:tum2["AH/ESP Header SPI, SeqNum"]:1
    block:tum3["Original IP Header End-host IPs"]:1
    block:tum4["TCP/UDP + Data"]:1
    block:tum5["ESP Trailer"]:1
    block:tum6["ESP ICV"]:1
```

**Use Cases:** Site-to-site VPN between branch offices; remote-access VPN (host-to-gateway); traffic through untrusted networks.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Header** | Original preserved | New outer IP header added |
| **Payload Protected** | TCP/UDP + Data | Entire original IP packet |
| **Overhead** | ~22 bytes (ESP) | ~42 bytes (ESP) |
| **Privacy of End-Host IPs** | Exposed | Hidden |
| **Typical Use** | Host-to-host | Gateway-to-gateway / VPN |
| **NAT Compatibility** | AH: No, ESP: Yes | AH: No, ESP: Yes (with NAT-T) |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks Each in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Writing "IPSec provides non-repudiation"** — *WRONG.* IPSec uses symmetric keys, so it cannot provide true non-repudiation. Always say "data origin authentication" only.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "3-tuple" for SA** — Many students write only $\langle \text{DestIP, SPI} \rangle$, missing the **Protocol** (AH/ESP) field. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Saying "SA is bidirectional"** — *WRONG.* SA is **simplex** (one-way). A full duplex IPSec session needs **two SAs**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Protocol Numbers** — Mixing up AH (51) and ESP (50). Remember: ESP = **50** = "**5**0 = **5**ecured **P**rotocol" (mnemonic trick).
>
> 5. **Drawing Tunnel Mode without the NEW outer IP header** — The defining feature of tunnel mode is the **new outer IP header** with gateway addresses. Drawing only AH/ESP wrapping the payload is a transport-mode mistake.
>
> 6. **Listing "digital signatures" as AH's authentication method** — AH uses **MAC/HMAC** (symmetric), not digital signatures.
>
> 7. **Omitting the ICV field** in AH/ESP diagrams — The **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** must be shown as the last field.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 🎯

### ✅ Must-Remember Core Concepts

- [x] **IPSec = Layer 3 security** by IETF (RFC 4301)
- [x] **AH = Protocol 51** (no encryption); **ESP = Protocol 50** (with encryption)
- [x] **Services**: Authentication, Integrity, Confidentiality (ESP only), Anti-Replay
- [x] **SA is one-way (simplex)**; identified by 3-tuple: $\langle \text{DestIP, SPI, Protocol} \rangle$
- [x] **SPD** holds policy (DISCARD / BYPASS / PROTECT)
- [x] **SAD** holds active SA state (keys, counters, algorithms)
- [x] **IKE** operates over **UDP 500** (or **4500** for NAT-T)
- [x] **IKE Phase 1** → ISAKMP SA; **Phase 2** → IPSec SAs
- [x] **Transport Mode** → Host-to-host, original IP header preserved
- [x] **Tunnel Mode** → Gateway-to-gateway VPN, new outer IP header added
- [x] **AH + NAT = Incompatible** (IP header is authenticated)
- [x] **ESP + NAT = Compatible** (via NAT-T, UDP 4500)
- [x] **ICV** is computed using **HMAC** (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256), NOT digital signatures
- [x] **Anti-replay** uses a **monotonic sequence number** + **sliding window** at the receiver
- [x] **IPSec supports both IPv4 and IPv6** (mandatory in IPv6)

### 🧠 Memory Aids for Exam Hall

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **"50 = ESP, 51 = AH"** | Protocol numbers (ESP is lower, comes "first" in numbering) |
| **"E-S-K Architecture"** | Encapsulation (AH/ESP) → SA Management → Key (IKE) |
| **"AH = Authenticity Here (no encryption)"** | AH provides authentication only |
| **"ESP = Everything Secured Properly"** | ESP provides all four services |
| **"Tunnel adds a new IP hat"** | Tunnel mode = new outer IP header added |

### 🎯 Likely 14-Mark KTU Questions for 2024 Scheme

1. Explain IPSec Architecture in detail (AH, ESP, SA, IKE)
2. Explain Security Association (SA), SPD, and SAD
3. Compare Transport and Tunnel modes with diagrams
4. Explain AH and ESP header formats with processing logic
5. Explain IKE phases and key management
6. Explain anti-replay protection mechanism

### 📖 Recommended Additional Reading (RFCs)

- **RFC 4301** – Security Architecture for IP
- **RFC 4302** – IP Authentication Header
- **RFC 4303** – IP Encapsulating Security Payload
- **RFC 4306** – IKEv2 Protocol
- **RFC 7296** – IKEv2 Updates

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU board papers, always draw the **packet structure diagram** (e.g., for ESP tunnel mode) before writing the explanation. Examiners award 1–2 extra marks for **clean, labeled diagrams** with field sizes and offset positions.

---

> **End of Module 4 — IP Security Architecture Notes** 🎓

topics.title: Authentication Header and Encapsulating Security Payload
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication Header and Encapsulating Security Payload

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IPsec: The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication Header (AH)** and **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** are the two core security protocols of the **IPsec (Internet Protocol Security) framework** defined in **RFC 4302 (AH)** and **RFC 4303 (ESP)**. They operate at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model, securing every packet flowing between two communicating end-points.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
IPsec is a suite of protocols that provide **data origin authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and anti-replay protection** for IP datagrams. It does this by adding a special header after the standard IPv4/IPv6 header (or by encapsulating the entire datagram), using cryptographic techniques negotiated through a **Security Association (SA)** identified by a unique **Security Parameter Index (SPI)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Secure Postal System"

Imagine you are sending a sealed letter (your IP packet) through a public postal service (the Internet):

| Postal Service Component | Real-World Action | IPsec Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Tamper-proof wax seal** | Anyone breaking it knows the letter was opened | **AH (Authentication Header)** — ensures integrity & authentication |
| **Lockable, opaque steel safe** | Even the postal worker cannot read the contents | **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** — ensures confidentiality + integrity |
| **Registered post with a tracking number** | Sender gets proof of dispatch and receipt | **SPI (Security Parameter Index)** — identifies the Security Association |
| **Sequential serial numbers on receipts** | Prevents a thief from re-using an old receipt | **Anti-replay sequence numbers** |

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of IPsec Security

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight:**
> - **AH** = Provides **Authentication, Integrity, Anti-Replay**. Does **NOT** provide **Confidentiality** (no encryption).
> - **ESP** = Provides **Authentication, Integrity, Anti-Replay, AND Confidentiality** (encryption). It is the more powerful and widely deployed protocol.

## 1.4 The Security Association (SA) — The Contract First

Before AH or ESP can work, the two end-points must establish a **Security Association (SA)** — a one-way logical "security contract" containing:
- The **SPI (32-bit)** — a unique tag for the SA
- The **IPsec Protocol type** (AH = 51, ESP = 50)
- The **Destination IP address**
- The **Encryption algorithm** (e.g., AES-256) and key
- The **Integrity algorithm** (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256) and key
- The **SA lifetime**
- The **Mode** (Transport or Tunnel)

A **bidirectional** secure session requires **two SAs** (one in each direction), maintained in a database called the **SAD (Security Association Database)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Authentication Header (AH) — RFC 4302

The AH is inserted between the standard IP header and the payload (transport mode) or it becomes the outer layer protecting the entire inner packet (tunnel mode). Its job is to authenticate **everything except mutable fields** of the IP header.

### 2.1.1 AH Header Format (Bit-Level Breakdown)

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Next Header** | 8 | Type of payload following AH (e.g., 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 4=IPv4) |
| **Payload Length** | 8 | Length of AH in 32-bit words, minus 2 |
| **Reserved** | 16 | Set to zero (for future use) |
| **SPI (Security Parameter Index)** | 32 | Identifies which SA to use |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Monotonically increasing counter; prevents replay attacks |
| **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | Variable (multiple of 32 bits) | MAC (Message Authentication Code) — the cryptographic signature |

### 2.1.2 ICV Computation Logic

$$\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(\text{Immutable IP Header Fields} \;\|\; \text{AH Header} \;\|\; \text{Upper Layer Data})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why some IP header fields are NOT authenticated:**
> Fields like **TTL, Header Checksum, and ToS** change in transit. Authenticating them would invalidate the packet. AH computes the ICV over a "**predicted**" version of these fields (assumed unchanged from sender's value).

### 2.1.3 Services Provided by AH

1. **Data Origin Authentication** — proves the sender's identity
2. **Data Integrity** — proves the packet was not modified
3. **Anti-Replay Service** — uses the sequence number + sliding window
4. **NO Confidentiality** — payload is in cleartext

## 2.2 Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) — RFC 4303

ESP is more powerful because it can both **encrypt AND authenticate**. It has a three-part structure: **Header → Encrypted Payload → Trailer**, with an **ICV** appended at the end.

### 2.2.1 ESP Packet Format

| Sub-Section | Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Header** | SPI | 32 bits | Identifies the SA |
| **Header** | Sequence Number | 32 bits | Anti-replay |
| **Header** | **Payload Data (Variable)** | Variable | The **encrypted** upper-layer data (ciphertext) |
| **Header** | **Padding** | 0–255 bytes | Aligns data to cipher block size |
| **Header** | **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Tells receiver how many pad bytes to strip |
| **Header** | **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload (e.g., 6=TCP) |
| **Trailer** | **ICV** | 32 bits × n | MAC over ESP Header + ciphertext + ESP Trailer |

### 2.2.2 ESP Encryption & Authentication Scope

$$\text{Encrypted Portion} = \text{Original Payload} \;\|\; \text{Padding} \;\|\; \text{Pad Length} \;\|\; \text{Next Header}$$

$$\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(\text{ESP Header} \;\|\; \text{Encrypted Payload} \;\|\; \text{ESP Trailer})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In ESP, the **authentication covers the ESP header and the encrypted payload**, but does **not** authenticate the outer IP header (unlike AH).

## 2.3 The Two Operating Modes

### 2.3.1 Transport Mode

- AH/ESP header is inserted **after the original IP header**.
- **Original IP header is preserved.**
- Used for **host-to-host** communication.
- Adds minimal overhead.
- **AH Transport Mode** authenticates: IP payload + original IP header (immutable parts).
- **ESP Transport Mode** encrypts: IP payload (and ESP trailer). Original IP header is **visible**.

### 2.3.2 Tunnel Mode

- **The entire original IP datagram is encapsulated** as the payload.
- A **new outer IP header** is added.
- Used for **gateway-to-gateway** (e.g., VPN concentrators) or **host-to-gateway** communication.
- Provides full traffic flow confidentiality — even the original IPs are hidden.
- **AH Tunnel Mode** authenticates: inner IP + inner payload + (immutable parts of) outer header.
- **ESP Tunnel Mode** encrypts: entire inner IP + inner payload. **Outer IP is visible** (needed for routing).

## 2.4 Anti-Replay Protection: The Sliding Window

Both AH and ESP use a 32-bit sequence number. The receiver maintains a **sliding window** of received sequence numbers (typically W = 32 or 64 packets).

- If a new packet's sequence number is **> right edge of window**: accept and slide right.
- If it falls **inside the window**: check if already received. If yes → discard (replay). If no → accept.
- If it is **< left edge of window**: discard (too old).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| ICV length | $\text{Length}_{\text{ICV}} = 96$ bits (HMAC-MD5) or $128$ bits (HMAC-SHA-256) | Often asked in numericals |
| AH Payload Length | $\text{Length} = \left( \dfrac{\text{Total AH bytes}}{32} \right) - 2$ | Confuses students — minus 2 not 1 |
| SPI Size | $32$ bits $= 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ possible values | Theoretical question |
| Anti-replay window | Default size W = 32 packets (RFC 4303) | Direct question |
| IP Protocol Number — AH | $51$ | Frequently asked |
| IP Protocol Number — ESP | $50$ | Frequently asked |
| Combined AH+ESP overhead | $\text{Transport} \geq 24$ bytes, $\text{Tunnel} \geq 44$ bytes | Network design problems |
| Encryption Block Alignment | Padding = (BlockSize − (PayloadLen mod BlockSize)) mod BlockSize | Cryptographic operation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corporate VPNs**: Cisco AnyConnect, FortiGate, and Palo Alto firewalls use **ESP in Tunnel Mode** with IKEv2 for site-to-site and remote-access VPNs.
- **5G Core Networks (N3/N4 interfaces)**: IPsec/ESP is mandatory per 3GPP TS 33.501.
- **AWS VPC Peering & Transit Gateway**: Uses IPsec ESP with AES-GCM-128.
- **Military / Government**: Uses **AH** when confidentiality is not required but strict authentication is (e.g., command-and-control traffic).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AH Header Length Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Given an AH packet using HMAC-SHA-1 (which produces a 160-bit = 20-byte ICV), compute the value of the **Payload Length** field of the AH header.

**Step 1:** Identify the total fields in AH excluding the 20-byte ICV.

The fixed fields of AH are:
- Next Header: $1$ byte
- Payload Length: $1$ byte
- Reserved: $2$ bytes
- SPI: $4$ bytes
- Sequence Number: $4$ bytes
- **Subtotal = 12 bytes**

**Step 2:** Add the ICV length.

$$\text{Total AH Length} = 12 \text{ bytes (fixed)} + 20 \text{ bytes (ICV)} = 32 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to 32-bit words.

$$\text{Number of 32-bit words} = \frac{32 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/word}} = 8 \text{ words}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the AH specification rule.

> The **Payload Length** field is defined as: *"The length of AH in 32-bit words, **minus 2**."* This is because the length field itself occupies 1 word (2 bytes for Length+Reserved), and per RFC convention the count excludes these metadata words.

$$\text{Payload Length} = 8 - 2 = 6$$

**Result:** The Payload Length field should be set to **6** (decimal), or in binary:

$$\text{Payload Length (binary)} = 0000\;0110$$

## 3.2 Derivation: ESP Padding for Block Cipher

**Problem Statement:** A TCP segment of **37 bytes** is to be encrypted using **AES-CBC** (block size = 16 bytes). Compute the number of padding bytes required in the ESP trailer, and the value to be placed in the **Pad Length** field.

**Step 1:** Recall the ESP format. Padding is required so that the encryption block boundary aligns correctly. The total data to be encrypted includes:
- Original payload
- Padding
- Pad Length (1 byte)
- Next Header (1 byte)

**Step 2:** Compute the unpadded block alignment.

$$\text{Bytes to align} = 37 \text{ (TCP)} + 1 \text{ (Pad Length)} + 1 \text{ (Next Header)} = 39 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3:** Find padding so that the total is a multiple of 16.

$$\text{Padding} = (16 - (39 \bmod 16)) \bmod 16$$

$$\text{Padding} = (16 - 7) \bmod 16 = 9 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4:** Verify the alignment.

$$37 + 9 + 1 + 1 = 48 \text{ bytes} = 3 \times 16 \text{ bytes} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** The **Pad Length** field stores the number of padding bytes, which is **9**.

$$\text{Pad Length} = 9$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 9 and not 7?** Even though the TCP segment is only 7 bytes short of 48, the **Pad Length and Next Header fields themselves** occupy 2 bytes. Without these 2 bytes, the data would still misalign, so padding must "absorb" both the segment remainder AND the metadata overhead.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AH vs ESP — Full Comparison

| Property | Authentication Header (AH) | Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Protocol Number** | $51$ | $50$ |
| **Defining RFC** | RFC 4302 | RFC 4303 |
| **Data Origin Authentication** | **Yes** | **Yes** (optional, but typically used) |
| **Data Integrity** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Confidentiality (Encryption)** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Anti-Replay** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Covers IP Header** | **Yes** (entire, except mutable fields) | **No** (only ESP header + payload) |
| **NAT Traversal** | Fails (breaks with NAT) | Works (with ESP-in-UDP encapsulation) |
| **Overhead (Transport)** | $24$ bytes (no ICV) | $24$ bytes + ICV |
| **Overhead (Tunnel)** | $44$ bytes (no ICV) | $44$ bytes + ICV |
| **Usage Recommendation** | Rare today; used when authentication without encryption is mandatory | **Default for all modern VPNs** |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Anti-Replay Window Logic

Consider a receiver with window size $W = 32$ and right edge $R = 1000$.

| Scenario | Incoming Sequence Number $N$ | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| New packet ahead | $N = 1001$ | **Accept**, slide window to $R = 1001$ | $N > R$ |
| Old duplicate | $N = 900$ | **Discard** | $N < R - W = 968$ (left edge) |
| Inside window, already seen | $N = 985$ (bit in window = 1) | **Discard** | Replay attack detected |
| Inside window, new | $N = 995$ (bit in window = 0) | **Accept**, set bit | Genuine delayed packet |

The window can be modeled as a **bitmap** of size $W$:

$$\text{Position in bitmap} = N - (R - W + 1)$$

For $N = 985, R = 1000, W = 32$:

$$\text{Position} = 985 - (1000 - 32 + 1) = 985 - 969 = 16$$

This is the $17^{\text{th}}$ bit of the bitmap (indexing from 0).

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Security Association Database (SAD) Lookup

When an outbound IP packet matches an SPD (Security Policy Database) rule, IKE performs the following SA lookup:

**Step 1:** Extract the **5-tuple** of the packet:

$$(SrcIP,\; DstIP,\; Protocol,\; SrcPort,\; DstPort)$$

**Step 2:** Search the SPD for a matching policy (PROTECT, BYPASS, or DISCARD).

**Step 3:** For PROTECT, locate the SA in the SAD using:

$$\text{SA Lookup Key} = (DstIP,\; IPsec Protocol,\; SPI)$$

**Step 4:** If no SA exists, invoke **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** to negotiate one (Phase 1 = ISAKMP SA, Phase 2 = IPsec SA).

**Step 5:** Apply the AH or ESP transformation in the chosen mode, and emit the protected packet.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: IPsec Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outbound IP Packet] --> B[SPD Policy Lookup]
    B --> C{Policy Decision}
    C -->|BYPASS| D[Send in Cleartext]
    C -->|DISCARD| E[Drop Packet]
    C -->|PROTECT| F[SAD Lookup]
    F --> G{SA Exists?}
    G -->|No| H[Invoke IKE Negotiation]
    H --> I[Create New SA in SAD]
    G -->|Yes| I
    I --> J{Mode Decision}
    J -->|Transport| K[Insert AH/ESP after IP Header]
    J -->|Tunnel| L[Encapsulate Entire Datagram with New IP Header]
    K --> M[Apply Cryptographic Transform]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Protected IPsec Packet Sent to Network]
```

## 4.2 Packet Format Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A1[AH Transport Mode]
        A1A[Original IP Header] --> A1B[AH Header] --> A1C[TCP/UDP Payload]
    end
    subgraph A2[AH Tunnel Mode]
        A2A[New Outer IP Header] --> A2B[AH Header] --> A2C[Original IP Header] --> A2D[TCP/UDP Payload]
    end
    subgraph A3[ESP Transport Mode]
        A3A[Original IP Header] --> A3B[ESP Header] --> A3C[Encrypted Payload and ESP Trailer] --> A3D[ESP ICV]
    end
    subgraph A4[ESP Tunnel Mode]
        A4A[New Outer IP Header] --> A4B[ESP Header] --> A4C[Encrypted Original IP Packet and ESP Trailer] --> A4D[ESP ICV]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: AH vs ESP Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph P[IPsec Protocol Stack]
        direction LR
        A[AH Protocol 51] --> A1[Authentication and Integrity]
        A --> A2[Anti Replay]
        A --> A3[No Confidentiality]
        B[ESP Protocol 50] --> B1[Confidentiality via Encryption]
        B --> B2[Authentication and Integrity]
        B --> B3[Anti Replay]
    end
    A1 --> C[ICV covers IP Header plus Payload]
    B1 --> D[Encryption covers Payload only in Transport]
    B1 --> E[Encryption covers Entire Inner Packet in Tunnel]
```

## 4.4 Anti-Replay Sliding Window Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    L[Left Edge Old] -->|Discard if N less than L| W[Window of size 32]
    W --> R[Right Edge Newest]
    R -->|Discard if already seen bit equals 1| W
    R -->|Accept and slide if N greater than R| N[New packet accepted]
    W -->|Accept if inside and bit equals 0| D[Delayed but legitimate]
```

## 4.5 Security Association Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Host A
    participant IKE_A as IKE Daemon A
    participant Network as Internet
    participant IKE_B as IKE Daemon B
    participant Bob as Host B
    Alice->>IKE_A: Request to send secure packet
    IKE_A->>IKE_B: IKE Phase 1 ISAKMP SA Negotiation
    IKE_B-->>IKE_A: Agreed Algorithms and Keys
    IKE_A->>IKE_B: IKE Phase 2 IPsec SA Proposal with SPI
    IKE_B-->>IKE_A: Accepted SA with SPI Value
    IKE_A->>Alice: SA installed in SAD
    IKE_B->>Bob: SA installed in SAD
    Alice->>Bob: Protected packet with AH or ESP header
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between Authentication Header (AH) and Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) in IPsec. State the IP protocol numbers for both.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AH (RFC 4302) | ESP (RFC 4303) |
|---|---|---|
| IP Protocol Number | **51** | **50** |
| Confidentiality | Not provided | Provided via encryption |
