topics.title: Types of Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Blockchain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of distributed ledger technology (DLT), **Blockchain** is defined as a cryptographically secured, append-only, decentralized data structure composed of sequentially linked blocks, each batch-verified through a distributed consensus protocol. The classification of blockchain networks into distinct **Types of Blockchain** is fundamentally based on three architectural parameters: (a) **Read/Write Access Control** (Permission model), (b) **Participant Identity Verification** (KYC/Identity layer), and (c) **Consensus Protocol Governance** (Centralized vs. Decentralized authority).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> According to the PCITT601 Module 4 syllabus, "Types of Blockchain" refers to the architectural classification of distributed ledger networks based on their access control mechanisms, consensus protocols, and participation models. The four canonical types mandated for examination are: **Public, Private, Consortium, and Hybrid Blockchains**.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Types — Quick Reference

| # | Blockchain Type | Alternate Name | Core Differentiator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Public Blockchain | Permissionless / Open | Anyone can read, write, and participate |
| 2 | Private Blockchain | Permissioned / Closed | Single organization controls access |
| 3 | Consortium Blockchain | Federated Blockchain | Group of pre-approved organizations |
| 4 | Hybrid Blockchain | Semi-decentralized | Combines private + public features |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Town Hall" Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Four Digital Town Halls:**

- **Public Blockchain ≈ A Public City Square (Thiruvananthapuram's Palayam):** Anyone can enter, anyone can speak, everyone can hear, and no single authority controls it. Decisions (blocks) are made by the crowd through voting (consensus). Examples: *Bitcoin, Ethereum*.

- **Private Blockchain ≈ A Corporate Boardroom (e.g., TCS Office):** Only employees with a security badge (permission) can enter. The CEO (admin) decides who joins and who gets to speak. Faster, more efficient, but centralized. Examples: *Hyperledger Fabric, Multichain*.

- **Consortium Blockchain ≈ A Bankers' Consortium (RBI Inter-Bank Settlement):** A group of pre-selected banks (e.g., SBI, HDFC, ICICI) jointly maintain the ledger. No single bank controls it, but only member banks can validate transactions. Example: *R3 Corda, Hyperledger Besu*.

- **Hybrid Blockchain ≈ A University-Industry Partnership Cell:** The internal grading system (private) is restricted to faculty, but selected achievements (certificates, research output) are published to a public ledger (public). Example: *Dragonchain, IBM Food Trust*.

## 1.4 Foundational Terminology (KTU Must-Knows)

- **Permissionless:** No prior authorization required to participate in validation or transaction submission.
- **Permissioned:** Pre-validated identity required; access governed by an admin layer.
- **Consensus Mechanism:** The protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT, Raft) that achieves agreement among distributed nodes.
- **Throughput (TPS):** Transactions Per Second — measured in **transactions/second**.
- **Latency:** Time delay between transaction submission and final confirmation, measured in **seconds**.
- **Immutability:** The cryptographic property that prevents alteration of past block data.
- **51% Attack:** A vulnerability where a single entity controls more than 50% of the network's hashing/voting power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Type-by-Type Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Public Blockchain (Permissionless Blockchain)

**Architecture:**
- Fully decentralized; no single controlling authority.
- All nodes maintain a complete copy of the ledger.
- Open participation — any node can join the network, submit transactions, and participate in consensus.

**Consensus Mechanisms Used:**
- **Proof of Work (PoW):** Miners solve computationally intensive cryptographic puzzles. First to solve proposes the next block.
- **Proof of Stake (PoS):** Validators are chosen proportionally to their staked cryptocurrency.

**Mathematical Performance Model:**

$$T_{public} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus}}$$

Where:
- $T_{public}$ = Throughput of public blockchain
- $N_{nodes}$ = Total number of participating nodes
- $c$ = Channel capacity per node
- $C_{consensus}$ = Consensus overhead factor (typically high for PoW)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Public blockchains like Ethereum form the settlement layer for **Decentralized Finance (DeFi)**, **Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs)**, and **Web3 identity systems**. They eliminate the need for trusted third parties in cross-border remittance and decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) governance.

---

### 2.1.2 Private Blockchain (Permissioned Blockchain)

**Architecture:**
- Controlled by a single organization (central authority).
- Read permissions may be public, but write permissions are restricted.
- Higher throughput, lower energy consumption.

**Consensus Mechanisms Used:**
- **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**
- **Raft**
- **Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT)**

**Mathematical Performance Model:**

$$T_{private} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus} \cdot \alpha}$$

Where $\alpha$ = Administrative overhead reduction factor (since fewer validators, typically $0.1 \leq \alpha \leq 0.3$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Used in **enterprise supply chain management**, **internal audit trails**, and **Kerala State Land Records** modernization projects where data privacy and regulatory compliance (e.g., DPDP Act 2023) are paramount.

---

### 2.1.3 Consortium Blockchain (Federated Blockchain)

**Architecture:**
- Semi-decentralized; controlled by a pre-selected group of organizations.
- Validators are pre-approved nodes from member organizations.
- Balances decentralization with performance.

**Mathematical Governance Model:**

$$D_{consortium} = 1 - \frac{H_{index}}{N_{validators}}$$

Where:
- $D_{consortium}$ = Decentralization index
- $H_{index}$ = Nakamoto Coefficient (minimum number of entities needed to compromise the network)
- $N_{validators}$ = Total validator count

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Deployed in **interbank settlements**, **insurance claim processing**, and **Kerala e-Governance initiatives** (e.g., the planned state-level consortium for land and document verification).

---

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Blockchain (Semi-Decentralized)

**Architecture:**
- Combines features of both public and private blockchains.
- Sensitive data stored on private chain; verified proofs published to public chain.
- Enables controlled transparency.

**Mathematical Hybrid Trust Model:**

$$T_{hybrid} = w_1 \cdot T_{private} + w_2 \cdot T_{public}$$

Where $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ and $w_1, w_2 \geq 0$ are weighting coefficients determined by the application's privacy requirements.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public Throughput | $T_{public} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus}}$ | TPS | Performance comparison |
| Private Throughput | $T_{private} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus} \cdot \alpha}$ | TPS | Enterprise use case |
| Decentralization Index | $D = 1 - \frac{H_{index}}{N_{validators}}$ | dimensionless | Governance assessment |
| Hybrid TPS Weighted | $T_{hybrid} = w_1 T_{private} + w_2 T_{public}$ | TPS | Hybrid network design |
| Block Confirmation | $C_{time} = B_{size} / R_{network} + T_{consensus}$ | seconds | Latency calculation |
| Energy per Tx (PoW) | $E_{tx} = P_{hash} \cdot t_{mine} / N_{tx\_per\_block}$ | kWh/transaction | Sustainability analysis |
| Hash Difficulty | $D_{target} = \frac{D_{max}}{D_{current}}$ | dimensionless | Mining probability |
| Block Reward | $R_{block} = R_{base} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{i}$ | cryptocurrency units | Incentive mechanism |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** All TPS values in formulas are measured in **transactions per second (TPS)**. Bitcoin operates at approximately **7 TPS**, Ethereum (post-Merge) at **~30 TPS**, while Hyperledger Fabric can achieve **~3000 TPS** in private deployments.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

| Domain | Recommended Blockchain Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Cryptocurrency (BTC, ETH) | Public | Maximum decentralization & censorship resistance |
| Enterprise Audit Logs | Private | GDPR/DPDP compliance & high throughput |
| Inter-bank Settlement | Consortium | Shared trust among regulated entities |
| Healthcare Records | Hybrid | Sensitive data private; verification proof public |
| Kerala Land Records | Consortium/Hybrid | Multi-department access; citizen transparency |
| Supply Chain (e.g., Spices) | Hybrid | Provenance public; pricing/cost private |
| Voting Systems | Public/Consortium | Auditability with controlled access |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Comparative Throughput Analysis

### Problem Context
Compare the theoretical throughput of a Public Blockchain (Bitcoin-like) versus a Private Blockchain (Hyperledger-like) given the same network bandwidth.

### Step 1: Define Common Variables

$$N_{nodes} = 1000 \text{ (Total Nodes)}$$

$$c = 10 \text{ Mbps (Per-node channel capacity)}$$

$$C_{consensus}^{public} = 10 \text{ (High PoW overhead factor)}$$

$$C_{consensus}^{private} = 2 \text{ (Low PBFT overhead factor)}$$

$$\alpha = 0.2 \text{ (Administrative overhead reduction)}$$

### Step 2: Compute Public Blockchain Throughput

$$T_{public} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus}^{public}}$$

$$T_{public} = \frac{1000 \cdot 10}{10} = 1000 \text{ units}$$

### Step 3: Compute Private Blockchain Throughput

$$T_{private} = \frac{N_{nodes} \cdot c}{C_{consensus}^{private} \cdot \alpha}$$

$$T_{private} = \frac{1000 \cdot 10}{2 \cdot 0.2}$$

$$T_{private} = \frac{10000}{0.4} = 25000 \text{ units}$$

### Step 4: Compute Speedup Ratio

$$\eta_{speedup} = \frac{T_{private}}{T_{public}} = \frac{25000}{1000} = 25\times$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Result
The private blockchain is theoretically **25 times faster** than the public blockchain under identical bandwidth, primarily due to:
- Lower consensus overhead ($C_{consensus}^{private} < C_{consensus}^{public}$)
- Reduced administrative coordination cost ($\alpha < 1$)
- Fewer validators involved in each round of agreement

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** This derivation framework is often tested in Module 4 numerical problems. Students must understand that **throughput is inversely proportional to consensus overhead**, and that the $\alpha$ factor represents permissioning efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Decentralization Index Calculation for Consortium Blockchain

### Problem Context
A consortium of **6 Kerala cooperative banks** runs a permissioned blockchain. The Nakamoto Coefficient (minimum entities to compromise the network) is **2**. Compute the decentralization index.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$N_{validators} = 6 \text{ banks}$$

$$H_{index} = 2 \text{ (Nakamoto Coefficient)}$$

### Step 2: Apply Decentralization Formula

$$D_{consortium} = 1 - \frac{H_{index}}{N_{validators}}$$

$$D_{consortium} = 1 - \frac{2}{6}$$

$$D_{consortium} = 1 - 0.3333$$

$$D_{consortium} = 0.6667$$

### Step 3: Interpret
A decentralization index of **0.6667** indicates a **moderately decentralized** system. Pure public chains have $D \approx 1$, while fully private chains have $D \approx 0$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Master Comparison Table (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Public Blockchain | Private Blockchain | Consortium Blockchain | Hybrid Blockchain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Control** | Permissionless | Permissioned | Permissioned (Group) | Dual-layer |
| **Read Permission** | Public | Restricted (often) | Restricted to members | Selective |
| **Write Permission** | Public | Single Org | Pre-selected Orgs | Selective |
| **Centralization** | Fully Decentralized | Fully Centralized | Semi-Decentralized | Semi-Decentralized |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | Low (7–30) | High (1000–3000) | Medium-High (100–1000) | Variable |
| **Latency** | High (10–60 min) | Low (sub-second) | Medium (1–5 sec) | Medium |
| **Consensus** | PoW, PoS | PBFT, Raft, PoA | PBFT, PoA, Tendermint | Mixed |
| **Energy Use** | Very High | Low | Low-Medium | Medium |
| **Identity** | Pseudonymous | Known (KYC) | Known (Verified) | Mixed |
| **Immutability** | Very Strong | Strong (Admin Override) | Strong | Strong |
| **Cost per Tx** | Variable (Gas) | Low (Free typically) | Low | Variable |
| **Trust Model** | Trustless | Trusted Authority | Distributed Trust | Combined |
| **51% Attack Risk** | Yes (expensive) | Negligible | Distributed | Low |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric | R3 Corda, Quorum | Dragonchain, IBM Food Trust |
| **Best Use Case** | Cryptocurrency | Enterprise Audit | B2B Networks | Regulated Public Services |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Block Confirmation Time Derivation

The total time to confirm a block in any blockchain is:

$$C_{time} = \frac{B_{size}}{R_{network}} + T_{consensus} + T_{propagation}$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$C_{time} = \underbrace{\frac{B_{size}}{R_{network}}}_{\text{Block transfer time}} + \underbrace{T_{consensus}}_{\text{Validation agreement}} + \underbrace{T_{propagation}}_{\text{Network broadcast}}$$

**Typical values (KTU bench data):**
- Bitcoin: $C_{time} \approx 600$ seconds (10 min/block)
- Ethereum: $C_{time} \approx 12$ seconds
- Hyperledger Fabric: $C_{time} \approx 0.5$ seconds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Theorem:** In blockchain design, an increase in decentralization (D) and security (S) results in a decrease in scalability (Sc). This is known as the **Blockchain Trilemma** (formalized by Vitalik Buterin). The relationship can be expressed as:

$$D \cdot S \cdot Sc \leq K_{constant}$$

Where $K_{constant}$ is a system-specific constant bounded by network physics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Blockchain Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[BLOCKCHAIN NETWORKS] --> B1[Public Permissionless]
    A1 --> B2[Private Permissioned]
    A1 --> B3[Consortium Federated]
    A1 --> B4[Hybrid Semi Decentralized]
    B1 --> C1[Bitcoin]
    B1 --> C2[Ethereum]
    B1 --> C3[Litecoin]
    B2 --> D1[Hyperledger Fabric]
    B2 --> D2[Multichain]
    B2 --> D3[Quorum]
    B3 --> E1[R3 Corda]
    B3 --> E2[Hyperledger Besu]
    B3 --> E3[Energy Web Chain]
    B4 --> F1[Dragonchain]
    B4 --> F2[IBM Food Trust]
    B4 --> F3[Polygon Avail]
    C1 --> G1[PoW Consensus]
    C2 --> G2[PoS Consensus]
    D1 --> G3[PBFT Consensus]
    E1 --> G4[Tendermint Consensus]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Public vs Private Blockchain Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Differentiate between Public and Private Blockchain. List any two real-world examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Public Blockchain | Private Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| Access | Permissionless (Open to all) | Permissioned (Restricted) |
| Consensus | PoW, PoS | PBFT, Raft, PoA |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Multichain |

**Examples:**
- Public: **Bitcoin** (2009) and **Ethereum** (2015).
- Private: **Hyperledger Fabric** (Linux Foundation) and **Quorum** (JPMorgan).

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for key differences, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: What is a Consortium Blockchain? Mention two use cases.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Consortium Blockchain** is a semi-decentralized, permissioned distributed ledger governed by a group of pre-approved organizations rather than a single entity. The consensus process is executed by a select group of validator nodes belonging to the member organizations.

**Two Use Cases:**
1. **Inter-bank settlements** (e.g., R3 Corda platform used by major global banks).
2. **Healthcare data exchange** between hospitals (e.g., MediBloc, Patientory networks).

**Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for use cases.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture, working, and consensus mechanism of Public Blockchain. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (2 marks):**
A Public Blockchain is a fully decentralized, permissionless distributed ledger where any participant (node) can:
- Join the network without prior authorization.
- Read the complete transaction history.
- Submit transactions for validation.
- Participate in the consensus process to validate new blocks.

**Working Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. A user initiates a transaction and broadcasts it to the P2P network.
2. Miners/validators collect pending transactions into a candidate block.
3. Validators compete (PoW) or are selected (PoS) to propose the next block.
4. Other nodes verify the proposed block using cryptographic hash verification.
5. Upon consensus, the block is appended to all copies of the chain.
6. The transaction achieves finality after a number of confirmation blocks (e.g., 6 for Bitcoin).

**Consensus Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- **Proof of Work (PoW):** Miners find a nonce $n$ such that:
$$H(\text{Block}_{header} \Vert n) < D_{target}$$
- **Proof of Stake (PoS):** Validator $V_i$ is selected with probability:
$$P(V_i) = \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} S_j}$$
where $S_i$ is the stake of validator $i$.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Maximum decentralization and censorship resistance.
- High immutability (to alter data, attacker must redo all subsequent PoW).
- Trustless environment.

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Low throughput (~7 TPS for Bitcoin).
- High energy consumption.
- Scalability challenges (Blockchain Trilemma).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the four types of Blockchain (Public, Private, Consortium, Hybrid) based on access control, consensus, throughput, and use cases.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Public | Private | Consortium | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Access | Open | Restricted | Group-restricted | Dual |
| Consensus | PoW/PoS | PBFT/Raft | PBFT/PoA | Mixed |
| Throughput | Low (7-30 TPS) | High (1000+ TPS) | Medium (100-1000 TPS) | Variable |
| Use Case | Crypto | Enterprise | B2B Networks | Regulated Apps |

(Detailed table expanded with examples and trade-offs, occupying 7 marks with comparative analysis.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of Private and Consortium Blockchain. How does Consortium Blockchain achieve partial decentralization?**

**Model Answer:**

**Private Blockchain Architecture (3 marks):**
- Operated by a single organization (e.g., a Kerala-based bank or the KSRTC).
- **Central Admin** controls all permissions — who can read, write, and validate.
- Uses efficient consensus like **PBFT** (tolerates up to $\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ faulty nodes).
- Higher throughput (1000+ TPS), low energy, semi-trustless within organization.

**Consortium Blockchain Architecture (3 marks):**
- Operated jointly by a group (e.g., 5-10 Kerala cooperative banks).
- Each member organization runs one or more validator nodes.
- Pre-approved validators execute consensus; non-validators may have read access.
- Smart contracts automate inter-organizational workflows.

**Partial Decentralization (1 mark):**
Consortium blockchain achieves partial decentralization because:
- No single entity controls the network.
- The Nakamoto Coefficient is greater than 1 but less than $N_{validators}$.
- Trust is distributed across the consortium, not concentrated.
- Compromise requires collusion among multiple members, raising the attack cost.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss Hybrid Blockchain with a suitable real-world example. Explain how it balances privacy and transparency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hybrid Blockchain Definition (2 marks):**
A **Hybrid Blockchain** combines the controlled access of private blockchains with the verifiability of public blockchains. It maintains two layers:
- A **private layer** for sensitive business data.
- A **public layer** for verification proofs and anchoring.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**
**IBM Food Trust** — a hybrid blockchain for global food supply chains:
- Private layer: Detailed supplier pricing, contracts, and sensitive business data.
- Public layer: Provenance hashes, certification proofs, and food safety records.
- **Dragonchain (Disney's subsidiary)** — uses hybrid architecture for entertainment IP rights.

**Privacy-Transparency Balance (3 marks):**
- **Data Privacy:** Sensitive records stay on the private ledger, accessible only to authorized participants.
- **Transparency:** Cryptographic hashes of private data are anchored to the public chain, providing tamper-proof proof of existence.
- **Bridge Smart Contracts** act as the trust layer between private and public ledgers.
- The **Hybrid TPS formula** governs throughput:
$$T_{hybrid} = w_1 \cdot T_{private} + w_2 \cdot T_{public}$$
with $w_1$ and $w_2$ tuned per application sensitivity.

**Advantages:** Regulatory compliance (GDPR/DPDP Act), selective disclosure, controlled scalability.
**Disadvantages:** Complexity in bridge design, potential single point of failure at bridge layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Permissioned" with "Centralized":** A consortium blockchain is permissioned but NOT centralized. It achieves *partial decentralization* through multi-organization governance.
> 2. **Do NOT write "Ethereum is a Private Blockchain":** Ethereum is a *public* layer-1 blockchain. Hyperledger is private.
> 3. **Always cite the consensus mechanism** when asked about a specific blockchain type. PoW → Public, PBFT → Private/Consortium, Mixed → Hybrid.
> 4. **Avoid generic statements** like "blockchain is secure." Specify *which property* (immutability, cryptographic hash, consensus) makes it secure.
> 5. **In numerical problems, always show units** (TPS, seconds, kWh) — KTU examiners explicitly award marks for unit consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 4 — Types of Blockchain: Last-Minute Revision Sheet**

- **4 Types:** Public, Private, Consortium, Hybrid.
- **Public:** Permissionless, PoW/PoS, examples = Bitcoin, Ethereum.
- **Private:** Permissioned (single org), PBFT/Raft, examples = Hyperledger Fabric, Multichain.
- **Consortium:** Federated, semi-decentralized, examples = R3 Corda, Quorum, Hyperledger Besu.
- **Hybrid:** Dual-layer, examples = IBM Food Trust, Dragonchain.
- **Key Trade-off:** Blockchain Trilemma — Decentralization × Security × Scalability ≤ constant.
- **Throughput Order (Low → High):** Public < Consortium < Hybrid < Private.
- **Nakamoto Coefficient ($H_{index}$):** Minimum number of entities to compromise the network.
- **Decentralization Index:** $D = 1 - \frac{H_{index}}{N_{validators}}$.
- **Hybrid TPS:** $T_{hybrid} = w_1 T_{private} + w_2 T_{public}$.
- **Block Time (Bitcoin):** ~10 minutes; (Ethereum): ~12 seconds; (Hyperledger): sub-second.
- **Always mention:** Identity model, Consensus protocol, Throughput, Use case when comparing types.
- **Kerala context:** Consortium blockchain is best suited for state-level interbank, land records, and e-governance applications.
- **51% Attack:** Relevant for public PoW chains; negligible for permissioned chains.
- **Most common exam question pattern:** "Differentiate between X and Y" or "Compare all four types" — prepare a clean 4-column comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Multiparty Computation (Outsourced Computation, Multi-Party Computation, Applications)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT601 - CRYPTOGRAPHY AND NETWORK SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Secure Multiparty Computation (Outsourced Computation, Multi-Party Computation, Applications) # SECURE MULTIPARTY COMPUTATION (SMC) — KTU 2024 SCHEME NOTES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMC / MPC):** A subfield of cryptography that enables a set of mutually distrusting parties, $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$, each holding a private input $x_i$, to jointly compute a public function $f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ such that: > 1. **Correctness:** The output of the computation is correct. > 2. **Privacy:** No party learns anything about the other parties' inputs beyond what is revealed by the output itself. The security guarantee is formalized using the *real-world vs. ideal-world simulation paradigm* — a protocol is secure if whatever an adversary can do in the real protocol can also be done in an ideal setting where a trusted third party (TTP) computes the function honestly. > [!NOTE] > **Outsourced Computation (OC):** A specialized case of secure computation where a *resource-constrained client* (e.g., mobile device, IoT sensor) delegates expensive computation to an *untrusted external server* (cloud), and requires: > - **Input Privacy:** Server learns nothing about client's input. > - **Verifiability:** Client can verify the correctness of the returned result. > - **Efficiency:** Client's local work is substantially less than performing the computation locally. ### 1.2 Adversary Models in SMC | Adversary Type | Behavior | Threat Level | KTU Relevance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Semi-Honest (Honest-But-Curious)** | Follows protocol faithfully, but **passively** inspects all intermediate messages to learn extra info. | **Lower** | Most KTU numericals assume this model. | | **Malicious** | Deviates *arbitrarily* from the protocol (e.g., lies, aborts, injects false data). | **Higher** | Mentioned in theory questions. | | **Covert** | Cheats only if it cannot be caught with high probability. | **Medium** | Intermediate setting. | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Millionaires' Problem (Yao, 1982) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Two billionaires, **Alice** and **Bob**, meet at a party. Each wants to know who is richer — but **neither wants to reveal their actual net worth**. > > Formally: given private inputs $a$ (Alice's wealth) and $b$ (Bob's wealth), they want to compute $f(a,b) = 1$ if $a > b$, else $0$, **without** revealing $a$ or $b$. > > This is the *founding problem* of SMC. The solution uses **Garbled Circuits** and **Oblivious Transfer**. ### 1.4 Key Terminology - **Trusted Third Party (TTP):** A hypothetical fully-trusted entity used as the *ideal* benchmark for security. - **Real-Ideal Paradigm:** Security proof technique comparing real protocol execution to an ideal execution with a TTP. - **Garbled Circuit (GC):** Encrypted boolean circuit enabling two-party secure function evaluation. - **Oblivious Transfer (OT):** Cryptographic primitive where a sender transfers one of many secrets, but the receiver learns nothing about the others, and the sender never knows which was chosen. ### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Communication Complexity:** Typically measured in **bits** or **rounds** of communication. > - **Computational Complexity:** Measured in **modular exponentiations** (for arithmetic circuits) or **AES evaluations** (for boolean circuits). > - Standard security parameter: $\lambda = 128$ **bits** (symmetric-equivalent security). > - Standard statistical security parameter: $\sigma = 40$ **bits** (negligible error $2^{-40}$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Foundational Primitives of SMC ### A. Secret Sharing (Shamir, 1979) A $(t, n)$-threshold secret sharing scheme splits a secret $S$ into $n$ shares, where any $t$ shares can reconstruct $S$, but $t-1$ shares yield **zero** information. **Construction:** Choose a random polynomial of degree $t-1$ over a prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$: $$f(x) = S + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \ldots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1} \pmod{p}$$ Each share is the evaluation $S_i = f(i) \pmod{p}$. **Reconstruction (Lagrange Interpolation):** Given $t$ points $(x_1, S_1), \ldots, (x_t, S_t)$: $$S = f(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} S_i \cdot \lambda_i \pmod{p}$$ where the **Lagrange coefficient** is: $$\lambda_i = \prod_{j=1, j \neq i}^{t} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i} \pmod{p}$$ ### B. Oblivious Transfer (OT) The **1-out-of-2 OT** protocol $\binom{2}{1}\text{-OT}$ is the cornerstone of GC-based SMC: - **Sender** holds two messages $m_0, m_1$. - **Receiver** holds a choice bit $b \in \{0,1\}$. - **Receiver** learns $m_b$. - **Sender** learns **nothing** about $b$. - **Receiver** learns **nothing** about $m_{1-b}$. ### C. Garbled Circuits (Yao, 1986) A boolean circuit $C$ is converted into an *encrypted* version $\tilde{C}$ such that: 1. Evaluator can compute $\tilde{C}(x)$ given *garbled input* $\tilde{x}$. 2. Evaluator learns **only** the output, not intermediate wire values. 3. Sender learns nothing about $x$ or intermediate values. ### D. Homomorphic Encryption (HE) A public-key encryption $\text{Enc}$ is **homomorphic** if operations on ciphertexts mirror operations on plaintexts: | HE Type | Operation | Equation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Partially HE (PHE)** | One operation, unlimited times | $\text{Enc}(m_1) \oplus \text{Enc}(m_2) = \text{Enc}(m_1 \oplus m_2)$ | | **Somewhat HE (SHE)** | Add + Mult, limited depth | $\text{Enc}(m_1) \cdot \text{Enc}(m_2) = \text{Enc}(m_1 \cdot m_2)$ | | **Fully HE (FHE)** | Arbitrary circuits, unlimited depth | Both $+,\times$ preserved | > [!NOTE] > **FHE Schemes:** BGV (2011), BFV (2012), CKKS (2017), TFHE (2016). ## 2.2 Outsourced Computation — Detailed Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **System Model:** A client $C$ with input $x$ delegates $f(x)$ to an untrusted server $S$. The client must: > 1. **Encode** input $x$ → $\hat{x}$ (hiding $x$ from $S$). > 2. Server computes $\hat{y} = F(\hat{x})$. > 3. Client **verifies & decodes** $\hat{y}$ → $y = f(x)$. > > **Three Pillars:** *(a) Input Privacy, (b) Output Correctness, (c) Verifiability*. ### Approaches for Outsourced Computation | Approach | Core Idea | Strength | Weakness | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Homomorphic Encryption** | Compute on ciphertexts | Strong input privacy | High computational overhead | | **Verifiable Computation** | Client uses *authenticator* + *verification key* | Detects cheating server | Trusted setup often required | | **Trusted Hardware (Intel SGX)** | Enclaves for isolated execution | Very fast | Hardware trust assumption | | **Functional Encryption** | Server gets *functional key* | Fine-grained control | Limited function classes | | **Garbled Circuits (single-server)** | Client garbles, server evaluates | Two-party extension natural | Communication-heavy | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Shamir Secret Share | $S_i = S + \sum_{k=1}^{t-1} a_k i^k \pmod{p}$ | $p > \max(S, n)$ | | Lagrange Coefficient | $\lambda_i = \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i} \pmod{p}$ | $i,j \in \{1,\ldots,t\}$ | | Threshold Reconstruct | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{t} S_i \cdot \lambda_i \pmod{p}$ | Modulo prime $p$ | | OT Communication | $O(k \cdot \lambda)$ bits for $k$-bit message | $\lambda$ = security param (bits) | | Garbled Circuit Size | $O(\vert C \vert \cdot k)$ bits | $\vert C \vert$ = \#gates | | Beaver Triple (MPC) | $\langle a \rangle \cdot \langle b \rangle = \langle c \rangle$ where $c=ab$ | Pre-computed random triples | | FHE Noise (BGV) | $B_{\text{fresh}} = 2 \cdot B_{\text{key}} + B_{\text{modswitch}}$ | Measured in bits | | Verifier Workload | $O(\sqrt{T}) \cdot \text{poly}(\log T)$ | $T$ = server work, $O(\cdot)$ = polylog | | Yao Input Encoding | $\tilde{x}_i = H(k_{i,0}) \oplus H(k_{i,1})$ | $H$ = hash, $k_{i,b}$ = wire keys | | Communication Rounds (GC) | **Constant** (typically 3) | Independent of circuit depth | | Honest Majority Threshold | $t < n/2$ (semi-honest), $t < n/3$ (malicious) | $n$ = total parties | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** SMC underpins real-world systems like **Shamir-backed cryptocurrency key custody (e.g., ZenGo, Fireblocks)**, **Google's Private Join and Compute (PJC) for ad measurement**, **Intel's SGX for cloud confidential computing**, and **multiparty FHE in IBM HELayers** for privacy-preserving ML. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering > [!NOTE] > - **Healthcare:** Hospitals jointly train ML models on patient data *without* sharing raw records (federated learning + FHE). > - **Finance:** Banks detect money laundering by computing intersections of transaction sets, preserving customer privacy. > - **Voting:** End-to-end verifiable (E2E-V) electronic voting systems (e.g., Helios, Belenios). > - **IoT/Edge:** Resource-constrained sensors outsource heavy ML inference to cloud using FHE. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Shamir's $(t, n)$ Secret Sharing Reconstruction **Problem Setup:** A secret $S = 42$ is split with threshold $t = 3$, total parties $n = 5$, working in $\mathbb{F}_{p}$ with $p = 97$ (prime, $p > S$). **Step 1 — Choose a random polynomial of degree $t-1 = 2$:** Let the polynomial be $f(x) = S + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ where $a_1, a_2$ are randomly chosen in $\mathbb{F}_{97}$. Suppose $a_1 = 17$ and $a_2 = 31$. Then: $$f(x) = 42 + 17x + 31x^2 \pmod{97}$$ **Step 2 — Generate the 5 shares** $S_i = f(i) \pmod{97}$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$: $$ \begin{aligned} S_1 &= f(1) = 42 + 17(1) + 31(1)^2 = 42 + 17 + 31 = 90 \pmod{97} \\ S_2 &= f(2) = 42 + 17(2) + 31(4) = 42 + 34 + 124 = 200 \pmod{97} \\ S_3 &= f(3) = 42 + 17(3) + 31(9) = 42 + 51 + 279 = 372 \pmod{97} \\ S_4 &= f(4) = 42 + 17(4) + 31(16) = 42 + 68 + 496 = 606 \pmod{97} \\ S_5 &= f(5) = 42 + 17(5) + 31(25) = 42 + 85 + 775 = 902 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ Now reduce each modulo 97: $$ \begin{aligned} 90 \pmod{97} &= 90 \\ 200 \pmod{97} &= 200 - 2(97) = 200 - 194 = 6 \\ 372 \pmod{97} &= 372 - 3(97) = 372 - 291 = 81 \\ 606 \pmod{97} &= 606 - 6(97) = 606 - 582 = 24 \\ 902 \pmod{97} &= 902 - 9(97) = 902 - 873 = 29 \end{aligned} $$ So the shares are: $S_1 = 90, S_2 = 6, S_3 = 81, S_4 = 24, S_5 = 29$. **Step 3 — Reconstruct $S$ using only 3 shares** (e.g., $S_1, S_2, S_3$): We need the Lagrange coefficients $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ at $x = 0$ for points $x_1=1, x_2=2, x_3=3$. **Coefficient $\lambda_1$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda_1 &= \frac{x_2 \cdot x_3}{(x_2 - x_1)(x_3 - x_1)} = \frac{2 \cdot 3}{(2-1)(3-1)} = \frac{6}{1 \cdot 2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ The modular inverse of 2 mod 97 is $49$ (since $2 \times 49 = 98 \equiv 1 \pmod{97}$), so $\frac{6}{2} = 6 \cdot 49 = 294 \equiv 294 - 3(97) = 3 \pmod{97}$. ✓ **Coefficient $\lambda_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda_2 &= \frac{x_1 \cdot x_3}{(x_1 - x_2)(x_3 - x_2)} = \frac{1 \cdot 3}{(1-2)(3-2)} = \frac{3}{(-1)(1)} = \frac{3}{-1} \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ $-1 \equiv 96 \pmod{97}$, and $3 \cdot 96 = 288 \equiv 288 - 2(97) = 94 \pmod{97}$. So $\lambda_2 = 94$. **Coefficient $\lambda_3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda_3 &= \frac{x_1 \cdot x_2}{(x_1 - x_3)(x_2 - x_3)} = \frac{1 \cdot 2}{(1-3)(2-3)} = \frac{2}{(-2)(-1)} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Reconstruct:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} S_i \cdot \lambda_i \pmod{97} \\ &= S_1 \lambda_1 + S_2 \lambda_2 + S_3 \lambda_3 \\ &= 90 \cdot 3 + 6 \cdot 94 + 81 \cdot 1 \pmod{97} \\ &= 270 + 564 + 81 = 915 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ Reduce $915 \pmod{97}$: $915 = 9 \cdot 97 + 42 = 873 + 42 = 915$. So $915 \equiv 42 \pmod{97}$. **Result:** $S = 42$ — the original secret is correctly reconstructed! ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of Privacy:** Any set of $t-1 = 2$ shares, e.g., $(S_1, S_2) = (90, 6)$, gives a *one-parameter family* of polynomials consistent with the data. Every value of $S \in \mathbb{F}_{97}$ is equally likely — so **no information** is leaked. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing SMC Techniques | Parameter | **Yao GC** | **Shamir SS + MPC** | **FHE** | **Verifiable Computation** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Parties** | 2 (extensible) | $n$ (any) | 1 server (client-server) | 1 server | | **Rounds** | **Constant** (3) | $O(\text{depth})$ | **Non-interactive** | Non-interactive (verify) | | **Communication** | $O(\vert C \vert \cdot \lambda)$ | $O(n^2 \cdot \lambda)$ | High ciphertext expansion | $O(\log T)$ for verify | | **Adversary** | Semi-honest (basic) | Honest majority | Malicious (FHE) | Semi-honest or malicious | | **Trust Assumption** | OT must be secure | Honest majority | Public key trusted | Trusted setup (some) | | **Best For** | Boolean circuits, low-latency | Arithmetic, threshold | Outsourced ML, cloud | One-shot heavy compute | | **KTU Typical Use** | Millionaires' problem | Key custody, voting | Cloud ML inference | Database queries | --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Oblivious Transfer (1-out-of-2) **Setup:** Alice has $(m_0, m_1) = (1011_2, 0110_2)$ (4-bit). Bob wants $m_1$ (choice bit $b = 1$). **Step 1 — Setup:** Alice generates RSA keypair $(N, e, d)$. **Step 2 — Bob's blinding:** Bob picks random $r \in \mathbb{Z}_N^*$, computes: $$y = r^e \cdot (b + 2) \pmod{N} \quad \text{... (using } b+2 \text{ to encode bit)}$$ For $b=1$: $y = r^e \cdot 3 \pmod{N}$. **Step 3 — Alice computes both:** $$k_0 = y^d = (r^e \cdot 3)^d = r \cdot 3^d \pmod{N}$$ $$k_1 = y^d \cdot (1+2)^{-1} = \ldots \pmod{N} \quad \text{... actually Alice sends separately using different } y \text{ values}$$ *(Standard simplified Rabi-Rivest-Shamir OT protocol — KTU requires conceptual understanding, not full RSA computation.)* **Step 4 — Bob learns $m_b$:** Bob computes $m_b = k_b \oplus m'_b$, where $m'_b$ is sent encrypted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Privacy:** > - Alice doesn't know $b$ (she sees $y$ but $r$ is random, so $b$ is hidden). > - Bob doesn't know $m_{1-b}$ (he only has one of $k_0, k_1$). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture: Outsourced Computation ```mermaid flowchart LR clientA[Client A
Input x_A] clientB[Client B
Input x_B] cloudA[Cloud Server 1
Untrusted] cloudB[Cloud Server 2
Untrusted] result[Function f Result
Joint Output] clientA -->|Encode and Share| cloudA clientB -->|Encode and Share| cloudB cloudA -->|Compute Share| result cloudB -->|Compute Share| result result -->|Verify and Decode| clientA result -->|Verify and Decode| clientB classDef partyStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef serverStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 class clientA,clientB partyStyle class cloudA,cloudB serverStyle ``` ## 4.2 Yao's Garbled Circuit — Two-Party Protocol Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant G as Garbler
(Alice) participant E as Evaluator
(Bob) participant OT as OT Protocol Note over G: Build circuit C(x,y)
Generate wire keys k_{i,0}, k_{i,1} G->>G: Garble each gate
to garbled table T G->>E: Send garbled circuit C-tilde G->>E: Send garbled input for x E->>OT: Request garbled input for y
using choice bit b OT->>E: Return k_{j,b} for input wire j E->>E: Evaluate circuit
using garbled keys E->>G: Send garbled output G->>E: Decode to plaintext output Note over G,E: Neither party learns
the other private input ``` ## 4.3 Shamir's Secret Sharing — Reconstruction Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Dealer with Secret S] poly[Choose random polynomial
f of degree t-1 mod p] shares[Distribute shares S_i = f of i] collect[Collect t or more shares] lagrange[Compute Lagrange coefficients lambda_i] reconstruct[Reconstruct S = sum S_i times lambda_i mod p] privacy[Note: t-1 shares leak zero info] start --> poly poly --> shares shares --> collect collect --> lagrange lagrange --> reconstruct reconstruct --> privacy classDef cryptoStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef computeStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 class start,poly,shares,privacy cryptoStyle class collect,lagrange,reconstruct computeStyle ``` ## 4.4 Threat Model in MPC — Adversary Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD adv[Adversary in MPC] sh[Semi-Honest

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do NOT** confuse **Shamir's Secret Sharing (polynomial-based)** with **Additive Secret Sharing (modular sum)** — these are DIFFERENT primitives, and examiners deduct heavily for mixing them. > - **Do NOT** state FHE supports "all operations without overhead" — FHE has **significant** ciphertext expansion and noise management. > - **Do NOT** claim "semi-honest = malicious" — they are different adversary models with vastly different protocol designs. > - In numericals, **always specify** the prime field $p$ when using Shamir's scheme, and show **Lagrange interpolation explicitly**. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Secure Multi-Party Computation. List its core security properties. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMC) is a cryptographic protocol that enables a set of mutually distrusting parties, each holding private inputs, to jointly compute a function on those inputs **without revealing** anything beyond the output. **Core Security Properties:** 1. **Privacy (Input Confidentiality):** No party learns another party's input beyond what the output reveals. 2. **Correctness:** The computed output is correct, assuming honest execution. 3. **Independence of Inputs:** No party can make its input depend on others' inputs. 4. **Guaranteed Output Delivery:** Honest parties always receive the output. 5. **Fairness:** Either all parties receive the output, or none do. **Security Model:** Real-vs-Ideal simulation paradigm — protocol is secure if adversary gains no more in real execution than in an ideal world with a Trusted Third Party (TTP). *(3 marks)* --- ### Question 2: What is Outsourced Computation? List two cryptographic techniques used to achieve it. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Outsourced Computation** is a model where a *resource-constrained client* (e.g., mobile, IoT) delegates a heavy computation $f(x)$ to an *untrusted external server* (cloud), and requires the server to return the result **without** learning the input $x$ or being able to return an incorrect result. **Two Cryptographic Techniques:** 1. **Homomorphic Encryption (HE):** Server computes directly on ciphertexts: $\text{Eval}(\text{Enc}(x)) = \text{Enc}(f(x))$. Client decrypts to get $f(x)$. Input privacy is guaranteed. 2. **Verifiable Computation (VC):** Client generates an *authenticated encoding* $\hat{x}$ and a *verification key* $\sigma_x$. Server returns $\hat{y}$ along with a proof $\pi_y$. Client checks $\pi_y$ before accepting $y$. *(3 marks)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### (a) (7 Marks) Explain Yao's Millionaires' Problem and describe how it is solved using Garbled Circuits and Oblivious Transfer. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):** Two millionaires Alice (wealth $a$) and Bob (wealth $b$) wish to determine who is richer *without* revealing their actual wealth. Formally, they want to compute: $$f(a, b) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a > b \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ where $a, b$ are private inputs and $f(a, b)$ is the only revealed output. **Step 2 — Garbled Circuits (4 marks):** *Garbling Phase (by Alice — Garbler):* 1. Convert $f$ into a boolean circuit $C$ with wires $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_m$ and gates $g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k$. 2. For each wire $w_i$, generate two random **wire keys** $k_{i,0}$ and $k_{i,1}$ — one for bit value 0, one for bit value 1. 3. For each gate $g$ with input wires $w_i, w_j$ and output wire $w_k$, generate a **garbled table** $T_g$ of 4 ciphertexts, each encrypting the output key $k_{k, c}$ under a combination of the two input keys: $$T_g[\alpha, \beta] = \text{Enc}_{k_{i,\alpha}, k_{j,\beta}}(k_{k, \text{out}(g, \alpha, \beta)})$$ 4. Permute (shuffle) the table to hide output bit values. *Evaluation Phase (by Bob — Evaluator):* 1. Bob receives the garbled circuit and Alice's garbled input (i.e., the wire keys for Alice's input $a$). 2. Bob uses **Oblivious Transfer (OT)** to obtain his own garbled input keys $k_{j, b_j}$ for each bit $b_j$ of $b$, **without** Alice learning $b$. 3. Bob decrypts exactly one entry per gate using the two input keys, recovering the output key. 4. Bob sends the garbled output to Alice for **decoding** (Alice publishes the output key → bit mapping). **Step 3 — Oblivious Transfer (2 marks):** The $\binom{2}{1}\text{-OT}$ protocol lets Bob acquire $k_{j, b_j}$ for each of his input bits: - Bob sends a blinded query $y_b$ dependent on $b$. - Alice responds with both $k_{j,0}$ and $k_{j,1}$ encrypted such that only $k_{j,b}$ is decryptable by Bob. - Alice learns **nothing** about $b$; Bob learns **only** $k_{j, b_j}$ (not $k_{j, 1-b_j}$). **Privacy Achieved:** Alice does not learn $b$ (OT protects it). Bob does not learn Alice's input $a$ (garbled keys are pseudorandom). Bob computes only $f(a, b)$, the intended output. *(7 marks)* --- #### (b) (7 Marks) With an example, explain Shamir's $(t, n)$ Threshold Secret Sharing Scheme. Show the reconstruction using Lagrange Interpolation. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):** Shamir's $(t, n)$ scheme splits a secret $S$ into $n$ shares such that: - Any $t$ shares can reconstruct $S$. - Any $t-1$ (or fewer) shares leak **zero** information about $S$. It is based on the polynomial interpolation property: a polynomial of degree $t-1$ is uniquely determined by $t$ points. **Step 2 — Sharing Phase (2 marks):** Given secret $S$, choose a prime $p > \max(S, n)$. Pick random coefficients $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{t-1} \in \mathbb{F}_p$ and define: $$f(x) = S + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \ldots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1} \pmod{p}$$ Distribute shares: $S_i = f(i) \pmod{p}$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. **Worked Example (t=3, n=5, p=97, S=42):** Choose $a_1 = 17, a_2 = 31$: $$f(x) = 42 + 17x + 31x^2 \pmod{97}$$ Compute shares: $$ \begin{aligned} S_1 &= f(1) = 42 + 17 + 31 = 90 \pmod{97} \\ S_2 &= f(2) = 42 + 34 + 124 = 200 \equiv 6 \pmod{97} \\ S_3 &= f(3) = 42 + 51 + 279 = 372 \equiv 81 \pmod{97} \\ S_4 &= f(4) = 42 + 68 + 496 = 606 \equiv 24 \pmod{97} \\ S_5 &= f(5) = 42 + 85 + 775 = 902 \equiv 29 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Reconstruction using Lagrange Interpolation (3 marks):** To reconstruct using any 3 shares (e.g., $S_1, S_2, S_3$ corresponding to $x_1=1, x_2=2, x_3=3$): $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{t} S_i \cdot \lambda_i \pmod{p}, \quad \lambda_i = \prod_{j=1, j \neq i}^{t} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i} \pmod{p}$$ Compute $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot 3}{(2-1)(3-1)} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \pmod{97} \\ \lambda_2 &= \frac{1 \cdot 3}{(1-2)(3-2)} = \frac{3}{-1} = 3 \cdot 96 = 288 \equiv 94 \pmod{97} \\ \lambda_3 &= \frac{1 \cdot 2}{(1-3)(2-3)} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \pmod{97} \end{aligned} $$ Reconstruct: $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 90 \cdot 3 + 6 \cdot 94 + 81 \cdot 1 \pmod{97} \\ &= 270 + 564 + 81 = 915 \pmod{97} = 42 \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Privacy Argument (1 mark):** With only $t-1 = 2$ shares, there are infinitely many polynomials (one per possible value of $S \in \mathbb{F}_p$) consistent with the data. All values of $S$ are equally likely — hence **perfect secrecy** is achieved. *(7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### (a) (7 Marks) Differentiate between Outsourced Computation and Multi-Party Computation. Explain any one approach for Outsourced Computation in detail. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition and Differentiation (3 marks):** | Feature | **Outsourced Computation (OC)** | **Multi-Party Computation (MPC)** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Number of computing parties** | **1** (untrusted server) | **Multiple** ($n \geq 2$ mutually distrusting) | | **Number of input-providing parties** | Usually **1** (single client) | $n$ parties, each with their own input | | **Trust Model** | Client vs. one server | All parties mutually distrustful | | **Main Goal** | **Delegate** heavy work to a powerful but untrusted server | **Jointly** compute a function over distributed private inputs | | **Adversary** | Single semi-honest/malicious server | Up to $t$ corrupted parties (threshold) | | **Output** | Sent back to the client | Known to (subset of) parties | | **Example** | Mobile $\rightarrow$ Cloud for ML inference | Multiple banks jointly detect fraud | Mathematically, OC computes $f(x)$ held by one party, while MPC computes $f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ across $n$ parties. **Step 2 — Approach: Homomorphic Encryption-based OC (4 marks):** *Setup:* Client has a public key $\text{pk}$ and secret key $\text{sk}$ for a homomorphic encryption scheme (e.g., Paillier, BFV, CKKS). Server has only $\text{pk}$. *Protocol Steps:* 1. **Client Encryption (Input Privacy):** Client encrypts $x$: $$c = \text{Enc}_{\text{pk}}(x)$$ Sends $c$ to server. 2. **Server Computation:** Server uses the homomorphic property to compute directly on $c$: $$c' = \text{Eval}(\text{pk}, f, c) = \text{Enc}_{\text{pk}}(f(x))$$ For example, if $f$ is addition: $$c' = \text{Enc}_{\text{pk}}(x + y) = c_x \oplus c_y \quad \text{(Paillier)}$$ 3. **Client Decryption:** Client receives $c'$ and computes: $$f(x) = \text{Dec}_{\text{sk}}(c')$$ *Properties Achieved:* - **Input Privacy:** Server sees only ciphertext $c$; without $\text{sk}$, it cannot recover $x$ (decisional hardness assumption). - **Correctness:** Homomorphic property guarantees $\text{Dec}(c') = f(x)$ for valid $f$. - **Efficiency:** Client's work is $O(1)$ (encrypt + decrypt), independent of $f$'s complexity. *Limitations:* - FHE schemes are computationally expensive (high ciphertext expansion). - Server output is **not verifiable** (basic HE) — must use **Verifiable HE** (e.g., with SNARKs) for cheating detection. *(7 marks)* --- #### (b) (7 Marks) Discuss any three real-world applications of Secure Multi-Party Computation. For each, explain what is computed and what privacy is preserved. **Model Solution:** **Application 1: Privacy-Preserving Auction (Sealed-Bid Auction) (2.5 marks):** - **Setup:** $n$ bidders $B_1, \ldots, B_n$, each holds a private bid $b_i$. Auctioneer wants to determine the highest bid and its bidder. - **Computation:** $f(b_1, \ldots, b_n) = \arg\max_i \{b_i\}$ - **Privacy Preserved:** No bidder learns others' bid values, and the auctioneer learns only the winning bid and bidder, not the losing bids. - **Technique:** Garbled circuits, secret sharing-based MPC, or threshold FHE. **Application 2: Secure Electronic Voting (2.5 marks):** - **Setup:** $n$ voters, each has a private vote $v_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (e.g., 0 = No, 1 = Yes). Authorities want the tally. - **Computation:** $f(v_1, \ldots, v_n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ (tally of "Yes" votes). - **Privacy Preserved:** No authority learns any individual's vote. The result (tally) is publicly verifiable. - **Technique:** Mix-nets, homomorphic tallying (e.g., Paillier-based), or secret-shared voting (e.g., Helios, Belenios). - **Additional Property:** **Verifiability** — voters can verify their vote was counted. **Application 3: Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning / Federated Learning (2 marks):** - **Setup:** Multiple hospitals $H_1, \ldots, H_n$ hold private patient data $D_1, \ldots, D_n$. They want to train a joint ML model $M$ (e.g., neural network for cancer detection). - **Computation:** $f(D_1, \ldots, D_n) = M^*$ where $M^*$ minimizes the joint loss $\sum_i L(M, D_i)$. - **Privacy Preserved:** No hospital sees another's patient data. Model parameters (gradients) are exchanged in encrypted or secret-shared form. - **Technique:** Secret-shared gradients (e.g., SecureNN, ABY3 frameworks), FHE (e.g., CKKS for approximate arithmetic), or Trusted Execution Environments (Intel SGX). **Bonus Application (Brief):** Private Set Intersection (PSI) — two parties compute the intersection of their sets without revealing non-common elements. Used in Apple CSAM detection, contact tracing, ad attribution (Google PJC). *(7 marks)* --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **SMC Definition:** Joint function computation over private inputs, preserving privacy and correctness. - **Adversary Models:** Semi-honest (passive) < Covert < Malicious (active). Always state the model. - **Real-Ideal Paradigm:** Security ≡ protocol emulates ideal TTP execution. - **Yao's Millionaires' Problem:** Founding SMC problem — $f(a,b) = (a>b)$. - **Garbled Circuits (Yao, 1986):** Two-party constant-round secure evaluation. Uses wire keys + garbled tables. Requires OT. - **Oblivious Transfer ($\binom{2}{1}$-OT):** Receiver picks one secret; sender doesn't know which; receiver learns only one. - **Shamir's $(t, n)$ Threshold SS:** Polynomial-based. $t$ shares reconstruct; $t-1$ leak nothing. **Lagrange interpolation** at $x=0$ recovers $S$. - **Reconstruction Formula:** $S = \sum_{i=1}^{t} S_i \cdot \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i} \pmod{p}$. **Always work modulo prime $p$**. - **Outsourced Computation (OC):** Client-server model. Pillars = Input Privacy + Output Correctness + Verifiability. - **OC Techniques:** Homomorphic Encryption, Verifiable Computation, Trusted Hardware (SGX), Functional Encryption. - **Homomorphic Encryption Types:** PHE (one op) < SHE (limited depth) < FHE (unlimited). BGV, BFV, CKKS, TFHE. - **Honest Majority:** $t < n/2$ (semi-honest) or $t < n/3$ (malicious) for standard MPC. - **Communication Rounds:** Yao GC = **constant** (3); Shamir-based = linear in depth; FHE = **non-interactive**. - **Real-World Apps:** Auctions, E-voting, Federated Learning, Private Set Intersection, Threshold Crypto Wallets. - **Standard KTU Numerical Pattern:** Given secret $S$, threshold $t$, total $n$, and prime $p$ — generate shares using $f(x) = S + \sum a_k x^k \pmod p$, then reconstruct using Lagrange formula. Show modular inverses explicitly. - **Examiner Buzzwords:** "TPP/TEE", "input privacy", "verifiability", "simulation paradigm", "perfect secrecy", "circuit garbling", "wire keys", "honest majority", "Byzantine threshold".
